AI.motivation: Multiple rounds of post-training are needed for VLA model adaptation, but human supervision is expensive and limited. The goal is to maximize policy improvement and task throughput per unit of human labor and time.
AI.method: (1) A division-of-labor pipeline with Teleoperator (remote interventions) and Floor Operator (monitoring/resets) to enable few humans supervising many robots. (2) VLAC-CUT, an automatic tool that segments trajectories into progress-making, idle, failure-inducing, and recovery portions for efficient data curation and reuse.
AI.result: Final policies achieve 80%-95% success rates across four real-world manipulation tasks. Task throughput improves 1.7x-4.2x over the base model. VLAC-CUT guided rollout reuse outperforms HITL-only training under the same human-intervention budget.
AI.conclusion: Role specialization and automatic trajectory curation significantly improve human efficiency in VLA post-training, enabling scalable robot supervision with limited human labor.

title: Reference-Based Distillation Detection in LLMs
summary: Model distillation -- training on outputs from stronger third-party models -- is widely used to boost performance, but raises concerns about unfair advantages and policy violations. This motivates a fundamental question: can we detect whether a model was distilled from another? We show that, while identifying a teacher model from a student in isolation is highly challenging, it becomes tractable in a reference-based setting: given a model and an earlier-generation checkpoint from the same lineage, we can identify the teacher model used to train the later checkpoint. We introduce a distillation detection method based on reference-based membership inference. By comparing how strongly a student model preferentially aligns with outputs from different candidate teachers relative to a reference checkpoint, our method identifies the most likely teacher and detects evidence of distillation. To handle unknown distillation pipelines such as hidden prompts, we infer proxy prompt templates directly from model outputs. We additionally identify a distinctive glyph-level signal specific to o1/o3 models. Evaluating distillation detection is challenging because modern model lineages are already heavily entangled. To address this, we develop a hybrid evaluation spanning both controlled distillation experiments and real-world models. Across both settings, our approach recovers the true teacher with near-perfect accuracy in single-teacher distillation scenarios, even when the underlying distillation pipeline is largely unknown. We further introduce statistical tests for both teacher attribution and distillation detection, and extend our framework to open-world settings where no teacher is guaranteed to be present among the candidates. Applying our method to contemporary models yields new evidence regarding potential distillation relationships involving QwQ, DeepSeek-R1, and GPT-OSS.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a reference-based membership inference method to detect whether a model was distilled from another, achieving near-perfect accuracy in identifying teacher models and uncovering potential distillation relationships among contemporary models like QwQ, DeepSeek-R1, and GPT-OSS.
AI.motivation: Model distillation is widely used but raises concerns about unfair advantages and policy violations, motivating the need to detect whether a model was distilled from another.
AI.method: A reference-based membership inference approach that compares how strongly a student model aligns with outputs from different candidate teachers relative to a reference checkpoint, using proxy prompt templates for unknown distillation pipelines and identifying glyph-level signals specific to o1/o3 models.
AI.result: The method achieves near-perfect accuracy in identifying the true teacher in single-teacher distillation scenarios across both controlled experiments and real-world models, with statistical tests for attribution and detection.
AI.conclusion: The approach successfully detects distillation relationships in contemporary models, providing new evidence regarding potential connections involving QwQ, DeepSeek-R1, and GPT-OSS, and extends to open-world settings where no teacher may be present.

title: Towards Real-World Wearable Motion Reconstruction
summary: The modern-day surge in popularity of wearable devices poses a fundamentally unique motion capture problem: reconstructing full-body movement from any set of sensing hardware worn at a given moment. Yet, most research efforts assume fixed sensor configurations (e.g. IMU suits or HMD-centric rigs) and cannot generalize across them. In contrast, we argue that motion capture should prioritize unobtrusive and lightweight devices such as smartphones, smartwatches, smart glasses, and smart insoles, and study the interplay between them. To this end, we make three contributions. First, we present a large-scale multi-modal dataset synchronizing these consumer-grade sensors with ground-truth 3D motion, spanning 50 diverse activities including everyday tasks, sports, and social interactions. Second, we propose WHIP, a baseline generative model that reconstructs motion from arbitrary subsets of available sensors, robustly handling missing modalities and producing physically plausible motions. Third, we conduct a systematic study of sensor complementarity, quantifying how different modalities complement one another. Code and dataset are available at https://vcai.mpi-inf.mpg.de/projects/WHIP/

AI.tldr: This paper introduces WHIP, a generative model for full-body motion capture that works with arbitrary subsets of consumer-grade wearable sensors (smartphones, smartwatches, smart glasses, smart insoles), along with a large-scale multi-modal dataset of 50 activities, demonstrating robust handling of missing sensor modalities and systematic analysis of sensor complementarity.
AI.motivation: Current motion capture research assumes fixed sensor configurations (e.g., IMU suits or HMD-centric rigs) and cannot generalize across different wearable device setups. The authors argue for prioritizing unobtrusive, lightweight consumer devices and studying their interplay for flexible motion capture.
AI.method: 1) A large-scale multi-modal dataset synchronizing consumer-grade sensors (smartphones, smartwatches, smart glasses, smart insoles) with ground-truth 3D motion across 50 activities. 2) WHIP, a baseline generative model that reconstructs motion from arbitrary subsets of available sensors, robustly handling missing modalities. 3) A systematic study of sensor complementarity to quantify how different modalities complement each other.
AI.result: A baseline generative model (WHIP) that produces physically plausible motions from arbitrary sensor subsets, robustly handling missing modalities, along with quantitative analysis of sensor complementarity across different consumer-grade wearable configurations.
AI.conclusion: Motion capture should prioritize lightweight consumer devices over fixed sensor rigs, and the proposed dataset and WHIP model enable flexible, generalizable full-body reconstruction from any available sensor set.

title: Closed-Loop Control with Rule-Aligned Small Language Models and Multi-Agent Self-Correction
summary: A key step toward autonomous industrial operation is the ability to create and reconfigure control policies from natural-language requirement specifications, with minimal or no manual redesign. In this setting, policy generation by AI agents can be a credible path when paired with a plant-aware validator (e.g., a digital twin) that can check generated candidate actions before execution. However, practical deployment is constrained by inference latency and compute footprint: large cloud-based models are often too slow, opaque, or data-sensitive for edge closed-loop use. This work investigates whether a compact Small Language Model (SLM) can be retrained for control reasoning and embedded in a validator-guided correction loop. We use a Qwen2.5-1.5B model aligned via Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO), combined with (i) an action agent, (ii) a symbolic/digital-twin-style validation layer, and (iii) a reprompting agent that iteratively steers outputs toward valid actions. In randomized thermal-control simulations (30 experiments with 500 steps each), the framework achieves 91.5% average action-alignment accuracy (86.3%--100% across cases) at 3.84\,s mean inference latency. Under symbolic re-mapping, it maintains a 95% in-range rate, indicating robust physical regulation despite reduced token-level agreement. These results support SLM+validator architectures as a practical path toward reconfigurable autonomous control at the edge.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a compact Small Language Model (Qwen2.5-1.5B) aligned via GRPO and embedded in a validator-guided correction loop for autonomous industrial control from natural-language requirements. The framework achieves 91.5% action-alignment accuracy with 3.84s mean latency, demonstrating practical viability for edge deployment.
AI.motivation: To enable autonomous industrial operation where control policies can be created and reconfigured from natural-language specifications with minimal manual redesign, addressing the constraints of inference latency, compute footprint, and data sensitivity that make large cloud-based models unsuitable for edge closed-loop use.
AI.method: Uses a Qwen2.5-1.5B SLM aligned via Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO), combined with three components: (i) an action agent, (ii) a symbolic/digital-twin-style validation layer, and (iii) a reprompting agent that iteratively steers outputs toward valid actions.
AI.result: In randomized thermal-control simulations (30 experiments, 500 steps each), achieves 91.5% average action-alignment accuracy (range 86.3%-100%) at 3.84s mean inference latency. Under symbolic re-mapping, maintains 95% in-range rate.
AI.conclusion: SLM+validator architectures offer a practical path toward reconfigurable autonomous control at the edge, balancing accuracy, latency, and physical regulation robustness.

title: The Universal Language of CSI:Unifying Wireless Sensing Across Devices and Environments
summary: WiFi sensing based on Channel State Information (CSI) promises ubiquitous, device-free perception, yet current research remains trapped in a Tower of Babel - fragmented into isolated silos where models are tailored to specific hardware dialects, fixed environments, and narrow tasks. The primary bottleneck is the Heterogeneity Gap: the disparity in signal dimensions, sampling rates, and semantic labels that prevents cross-system understanding. To bridge this gap, we propose a foundation-model framework that treats CSI not merely as raw signals but as a structured language with a learnable universal grammar. We first curate and standardize a large collection of heterogeneous real-world CSI datasets, establishing a unified infrastructure that allows incompatible signal formats to be treated as a single corpus. Second, we introduce a modular architecture that acts as a universal translator where lightweight dataset-specific adapters tokenize diverse signal inputs into a shared latent vocabulary, while a shared self-supervised Transformer backbone learns the temporal syntax of human motion and environmental dynamics. This design decouples sensing semantics from hardware syntax. Extensive evaluations show that by mastering this universal language, our approach consistently outperforms task-specific baselines and exhibits strong generalization capability in new environments, achieving superior efficiency in few-shot scenarios. By effectively absorbing heterogeneity, the framework offers a path toward robust, general-purpose wireless sensing, mirroring the linguistic generalization observed in Large Language Models. The code implementation is available at: https://github.com/cjychenjiayi/WiLLM.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a foundation-model framework (WiLLM) that treats WiFi CSI as a structured language with a universal grammar, using dataset-specific tokenizers and a shared self-supervised Transformer backbone to bridge the heterogeneity gap across different hardware, environments, and tasks in wireless sensing.
AI.motivation: Current WiFi sensing research is fragmented into isolated silos where models are tailored to specific hardware, fixed environments, and narrow tasks, primarily due to the Heterogeneity Gap - disparities in signal dimensions, sampling rates, and semantic labels that prevent cross-system understanding.
AI.method: (1) Curation and standardization of heterogeneous real-world CSI datasets into a unified infrastructure; (2) A modular architecture with lightweight dataset-specific adapters (tokenizers) that convert diverse signal inputs into a shared latent vocabulary; (3) A shared self-supervised Transformer backbone that learns the temporal syntax of human motion and environmental dynamics, decoupling sensing semantics from hardware syntax.
AI.result: The approach consistently outperforms task-specific baselines, exhibits strong generalization in new environments, and achieves superior efficiency in few-shot scenarios by effectively absorbing heterogeneity.
AI.conclusion: By treating CSI as a universal language, this framework offers a path toward robust, general-purpose wireless sensing that mirrors the linguistic generalization observed in Large Language Models, effectively bridging the heterogeneity gap.

title: MUX-USCT: A Noise-Robust Neural Network for Ultrasound Computed Tomography
comment: 10 pages, 6 figures, 3 tables. Accepted at MICCAI 2026. This is the author's accepted manuscript; the final version will appear in Springer LNCS. Code: https://github.com/TheYuchen/mux-usct-miccai2026
summary: Deep neural networks (DNNs) have shown strong potential for ultrasound computed tomography (USCT) reconstruction in ideal noise-free environments, yet existing DNNs are vulnerable to the noisy conditions in clinical practice, as they equally treat inputs that suffer mild, moderate, or severe noise. More challenging, the distributions of noise shift along with the environment, indicating the less effectiveness of noise-aware training, which injects a specific noise distribution into the training data. We rethink these challenges and observe that the DNN models can become more robust to noise if we know the noise sources and filter them out. This filtering operation is very alike the Multiplexers (or MUX), a fundamental combinational circuit in digital logic design. However, the challenge here is that noise can happen randomly during inference; as a result, the manually predefined MUX cannot work. To address these challenges, we propose MUX-USCT, a novel encoder-decoder DNN architecture that encodes the known acoustic acquisition geometry with an "adaptive MUX" that can automatically identify and filter noise, where the attention mechanism is applied in reconstructing the speed-of-sound map. On the OpenPros benchmark, MUX-USCT reaches 6.88 m/s MAE with 17% fewer parameters than the leading baseline with 7.65 m/s of MAE. Under simulated clinical noise, it remains stable across diverse degradation types that cause geometry-agnostic baselines to fail. Results show that the attention distributions in MUX-USCT provide interpretable indicators of the signal quality between pairs of transducers.

AI.tldr: MUX-USCT is a novel encoder-decoder DNN that uses an adaptive multiplexer mechanism to filter noise in ultrasound computed tomography reconstruction, achieving 6.88 m/s MAE with 17% fewer parameters than the leading baseline, and remaining stable under clinical noise conditions.
AI.motivation: Existing DNNs for USCT are vulnerable to noisy clinical environments because they treat all noise levels equally, and noise distribution shifts make noise-aware training ineffective. The authors observe that noise filtering (like a MUX circuit) can increase robustness, but manually predefined MUX fails because noise occurs randomly.
AI.method: Proposes MUX-USCT, an encoder-decoder DNN with an "adaptive MUX" that automatically identifies and filters noise by encoding known acoustic acquisition geometry. Attention mechanisms are applied to reconstruct the speed-of-sound map.
AI.result: On OpenPros benchmark: 6.88 m/s MAE with 17% fewer parameters vs. leading baseline's 7.65 m/s MAE. Under simulated clinical noise, remains stable across diverse degradation types where geometry-agnostic baselines fail.
AI.conclusion: The attention distributions in MUX-USCT provide interpretable indicators of signal quality between transducer pairs, and the adaptive MUX framework successfully addresses the challenge of random noise during inference in clinical USCT.

title: Faster Closest-Point Algorithms for the $E_6^*$ and $E_7^*$ Lattices
summary:
The dual lattices $E_6^*$ and $E_7^*$ are of particular interest in source coding and data compression applications. Among all known lattices in dimensions six and seven they attain the smallest normalized second moments, i.e., the smallest average quantization error. Their use in practice requires fast closest-point (nearest-lattice-point) algorithms. The known approach, due to Conway and Sloane and completed for $E_6$ and $E_6^*$ by Takizawa, Yagi, and Kawabata (TYK), decodes these lattices as unions of cosets of root lattices $A_n$: each coset is decoded separately, and the best result is kept. This requires four coset decodings for $E_7^*$ and six for $E_6^*$, together with explicit distance computations.
  This paper shows that all these coset decodings can be collapsed into a single sweep. Reformulated in terms of glue vectors, the TYK decompositions state that $E_7^*$ is the union of the even glue classes of $A_7^*$, and that $E_6^*$ is a parity-matched sublattice of $A_1^*\oplus A_5^*$. The candidate chain constructed by the closest-point algorithm of McKilliam, Clarkson, and Quinn (MCQ) for $A_n^*$ visits every glue class of $A_n$ exactly once and is optimal within each class. Consequently, one sorted sweep per coordinate block yields the closest points of all glue cosets simultaneously, and $E_6^*$ and $E_7^*$ are decoded at roughly the cost of a single $A_5^*$ or $A_7^*$ quantization. Rough operation counts indicate a $4$--$6\times$ reduction for $E_6^*$ and $3$--$4\times$ for $E_7^*$ relative to coset-by-coset decoding. We also discuss further constant-factor improvements available from recent refinements of the $A_n^*$ algorithms, and an open question concerning sort-free linear-time decoding.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a unified, single-sweep closest-point algorithm for the dual lattices E6* and E7* by collapsing multiple coset decodings into one, achieving 3-6x speedup over prior coset-by-coset methods.
AI.motivation: E6* and E7* lattices achieve optimal quantization error in dimensions 6 and 7, but practical use requires efficient closest-point algorithms. Prior methods required separate decoding of multiple cosets (4 for E7*, 6 for E6*), which is computationally expensive.
AI.method: The paper reformulates TYK decompositions using glue vectors (E7* as even glue classes of A7*; E6* as parity-matched sublattice of A1*⊕A5*). It leverages the MCQ algorithm for A_n*, whose candidate chain visits every glue class exactly once and is optimal within each class. This allows collapsing all coset decodings into a single sorted sweep per coordinate block.
AI.result: E6* and E7* are decoded at roughly the cost of a single A5* or A7* quantization, yielding 4-6x reduction for E6* and 3-4x for E7* relative to coset-by-coset decoding. Further constant-factor improvements from refined A_n* algorithms are also discussed.
AI.conclusion: The proposed unified approach significantly accelerates lattice quantization for E6* and E7*, making them more practical for source coding. An open question remains about sort-free linear-time decoding.

title: A Comprehensive Survey and Systematic Real-World Evaluation of Embodied Vision-and-Language Navigation
comment: This paper has been accepted by IEEE TASE 2026
summary: Navigation is a fundamental capability of autonomous systems, yet most existing approaches rely on highly structured models and strong prior assumptions, limiting their robustness in open and uncertain real-world environments. Vision-and-Language Navigation (VLN) offers a promising direction by enabling robots to integrate natural language understanding with visual perception in a data-driven manner. Although VLN has attracted increasing research attention, systematic methodological taxonomy and real-world validation remain limited. This survey presents a comprehensive review of VLN research. Specifically, state-of-the-art methods are organized along two orthogonal dimensions: action paradigms, including hierarchical and monolithic frameworks, and model paradigms, including discriminative and generative approaches. A critical analysis of their respective strengths and limitations is provided. Additionally, we conduct a systematic real-world evaluation of representative VLN system configurations on a physical robotic platform. Experiments across ten diverse real-world scenes show a substantial performance gap between simulation and real-world deployment under the tested configurations: a representative monolithic RGB-only method achieves 61% success in simulation but drops to 22% in real-world deployment, while a hierarchical framework achieves a higher real-world success rate of 51%, suggesting stronger robustness in our evaluation setting. Finally, we highlight key challenges in perception, decision-making, and control that must be addressed in future research.

AI.tldr: This survey reviews Vision-and-Language Navigation (VLN) research, categorizing methods along action paradigms (hierarchical vs. monolithic) and model paradigms (discriminative vs. generative). Real-world robotic experiments across ten scenes reveal a significant simulation-to-reality performance gap (e.g., 61% to 22% for a monolithic RGB-only method), with hierarchical frameworks showing better robustness (51% real-world success). Key challenges in perception, decision-making, and control are identified.
AI.motivation: Existing autonomous navigation approaches rely on highly structured models and strong priors, limiting robustness in open real-world environments. Although VLN integrates language with vision, systematic methodological taxonomy and real-world validation remain limited.
AI.method: The survey organizes VLN methods along two orthogonal dimensions: action paradigms (hierarchical vs. monolithic frameworks) and model paradigms (discriminative vs. generative approaches). It conducts systematic real-world evaluation of representative VLN system configurations on a physical robotic platform across ten diverse scenes.
AI.result: A monolithic RGB-only method achieves 61% success in simulation but drops to 22% in real-world deployment. A hierarchical framework achieves 51% real-world success rate, demonstrating stronger robustness.
AI.conclusion: There is a substantial performance gap between simulation and real-world deployment for VLN systems. Hierarchical frameworks show better robustness. Key challenges in perception, decision-making, and control must be addressed in future research.

title: Depth-Entropy Guided Sampling for Training-Free LLM Reasoning
summary: Reinforcement learning (RL) has become the dominant paradigm for improving the reasoning capabilities of large language models, but it requires expensive training, curated data, and reward signals. Recent work shows that sampling from sharpened base-model distributions at test time recovers much of the RL gain, yet existing methods rely solely on output-layer likelihoods and ignore the transformer's internal forward-pass dynamics. We introduce Depth-Entropy Guided Sampling (DEGS), a training-free, test-time method that exploits layer-wise entropy collapse as an intrinsic quality signal. We observe that stronger reasoners -- including RL-posttrained variants -- exhibit a distinctive "late collapse": logit-lens decoded entropy stays elevated until deeper layers before converging. We define a per-sequence collapse depth $D(\mathbf{x})$ and a joint objective $π(\mathbf{x}) \propto p(\mathbf{x})^α\exp(βD(\mathbf{x}))$ that combines sequence likelihood with this depth-entropy structure, instantiated inside an MCMC power-sampling framework (DEGS-MCMC). Across three open-weight models and four reasoning benchmarks, this near-chance per-candidate signal compounds over the sampling trajectory into state-of-the-art training-free accuracy, with gains largest out of domain and on the harder splits -- exactly where likelihood alone falls short -- at single-digit-percent wall-clock overhead. DEGS narrowly trails an in-house GRPO reference on the math splits GRPO was trained for, yet surpasses it out of domain on GPQA for all three models, without any training, reward model, or labeled data.

AI.tldr: DEGS is a training-free, test-time method that uses layer-wise entropy collapse in transformers as an intrinsic quality signal to improve reasoning, achieving state-of-the-art accuracy without RL training, rewards, or labeled data.
AI.motivation: RL improves LLM reasoning but requires expensive training, curated data, and reward signals. Existing test-time methods only use output-layer likelihoods, ignoring internal transformer dynamics. The goal is to recover RL-like gains without any training.
AI.method: DEGS defines a per-sequence collapse depth D(x) from logit-lens decoded entropy, then combines sequence likelihood with this depth-entropy signal in an MCMC power-sampling framework (DEGS-MCMC) via π(x) ∝ p(x)^α exp(βD(x)).
AI.result: Across 3 models and 4 benchmarks, DEGS achieves state-of-the-art training-free accuracy with single-digit-percent wall-clock overhead. It nearly matches GRPO on in-domain math splits and surpasses it out-of-domain on GPQA for all three models.
AI.conclusion: Internal entropy dynamics are a powerful, underutilized signal for reasoning quality. DEGS demonstrates that effective reasoning improvement is possible without any training, rewards, or labeled data, especially for out-of-domain generalization.

title: Compression Asymmetry and Trajectory Binding in Noise-Anchored Diffusion Inversion
summary: Real-image diffusion inversion is governed by a tight quality-cost trade-off, with costs incurred in computation, storage, or per-image optimization. We study this trade-off through the forward Gaussian noise anchor that defines a diffusion trajectory and isolate two mechanisms behind effective stored-noise inversion. First, diffusion noise exhibits an element-wise compression asymmetry: int8 full-dimensional anchors preserve reconstruction, whereas low-dimensional subspace summaries are much less reliable, often collapsing even at comparable or smaller payloads; the element-wise over subspace ordering persists across five stored-noise inversion methods. Second, inversion is trajectory-bound and score-prior coupled: the matched forward anchor and a trained score network are both necessary, arguing against a purely algebraic-identity explanation. Together, these findings specify what to store and how to use it. They lead to Noise-Anchored Reverse Correction (NARC), a training-free inversion primitive that stores a single int8 latent anchor and reuses it with a fixed, noise-level-dependent anchor-weight schedule: strong anchoring when the reverse trajectory is noise-dominated, then relaxed anchoring as image detail emerges. On PIE-Bench++ with Stable Diffusion 1.5, NARC outperforms five modern non-exact baselines and improves PSNR by +3.24 dB over PnP DirectInv while using about 400x less inversion storage than PnP DirectInv. The compression asymmetry, anchor specificity, and editing plug-in also transfer to SDXL 1024^2.

AI.tldr: This paper identifies two key mechanisms (compression asymmetry and trajectory-bound score-prior coupling) behind effective stored-noise inversion for diffusion models, leading to NARC—a training-free method that stores a single int8 latent anchor and uses a noise-level-dependent weighting schedule, achieving +3.24 dB PSNR over PnP DirectInv with ~400x less storage.
AI.motivation: Real-image diffusion inversion suffers from a tight quality-cost trade-off (computation, storage, or per-image optimization). The paper aims to understand what makes stored-noise inversion effective and how to exploit these mechanisms to improve the trade-off.
AI.method: The authors isolate two mechanisms: (1) element-wise compression asymmetry—int8 full-dimensional anchors preserve reconstruction well, but low-dimensional subspace summaries collapse; (2) trajectory-bound, score-prior coupled inversion—both the matched forward anchor and trained score network are necessary. Based on these insights, they propose NARC (Noise-Anchored Reverse Correction), which stores a single int8 latent anchor and applies a noise-level-dependent anchor-weight schedule (strong anchoring early, relaxed anchoring later).
AI.result: On PIE-Bench++ with Stable Diffusion 1.5, NARC outperforms five modern non-exact baselines, improves PSNR by +3.24 dB over PnP DirectInv, and uses ~400× less inversion storage. The findings also transfer to SDXL 1024² for compression asymmetry, anchor specificity, and editing plug-in.
AI.conclusion: Effective stored-noise inversion requires full-dimensional int8 anchors (not subspace summaries) and depends on both the forward anchor and score network. NARC provides a practical, training-free primitive that dramatically improves the quality-cost trade-off for diffusion inversion and editing.

title: Feedback-Coupled Memory Systems in Continuous Time
comment: 19 pages, 4 figures. Extends arXiv:2603.11560 to continuous time. Code: github.com/stevefatz95/fcms-continuous
summary: The Feedback-Coupled Memory Systems (FCMS) architecture formalizes closed-loop coordination through four abstract operators, two of which - the agent update operator $f_i$ and the environmental update operator $Ψ$ - are left axiomatically undefined in the original framework. To address this, $f_i$ is defined by Mechanism-Based Intelligence (MBI), where agents update locally through a decentralized price mechanism and economic principles, and $Ψ$ is defined by the Coupled Memory Graph Process (CMGP), a non-Markovian framework where the environment is treated as a physical substrate that records and responds to trajectory history coherently without external forcing. The resulting continuous-time FCMS instantiation achieves Lyapunov global dissipativity governed by the computable threshold $4β^2 < 2ημγ^2$. This generalizes both the discrete FCMS stability condition $4ηβ^2 < γ$ and CMGP's physical bifurcation threshold $α_c = 1/K$, confirming that memory dissipation must outpace feedback gain as a universal organizing principle. Numerical simulation with $N=2$ agents and mean-field validation at $N=10^6$ confirm the stability threshold and the self-reinforcing coordination cascade that emerges when it is violated.

AI.tldr: This paper defines previously undefined operators in FCMS using MBI and CMGP, proves a unified Lyapunov stability condition, and validates it with simulations from N=2 to N=10^6 agents.
AI.motivation: The original FCMS framework left two core operators ($f_i$ and $Ψ$) axiomatically undefined, limiting its practical applicability.
AI.method: $f_i$ is defined via Mechanism-Based Intelligence (MBI) with decentralized price mechanisms, and $Ψ$ via Coupled Memory Graph Process (CMGP) as a non-Markovian physical substrate. Lyapunov analysis derives the stability condition $4β^2 < 2ημγ^2$.
AI.result: The derived condition generalizes both discrete FCMS and CMGP thresholds. Numerical simulations (N=2 and N=10^6 mean-field) confirm the stability boundary and cascade behavior.
AI.conclusion: Memory dissipation must outpace feedback gain as a universal organizing principle for stable closed-loop coordination in FCMS.

title: Multimodal EEG-IMU Fusion for Motor Assessment: Leveraging Task-Dependent Complementarity for Robustness
summary: Movement disorders such as Parkinson's disease require comprehensive motor assessment, but reliable digital assessment pipelines integrating multiple sensing modalities across diverse motor tasks remain insufficiently characterized. We present a proof-of-concept study evaluating task-specific modality performance and multimodal fusion across ten motor activities. Synchronized EEG-IMU data were recorded from six participants (52 recording pairs). We evaluated an EEGNet + Transformer model for 16-channel EEG (125 Hz) and XGBoost on hand-crafted accelerometer and gyroscope features (25 Hz). Under 5-fold cross-validation in a subject-dependent setting, IMU achieved 94.41+/-0.58% accuracy and outperformed EEG on 7 of 10 activities, while EEG achieved 92.82+/-1.45% and showed lower error for rhythmic cycling (4.03% vs. 12.10%). Late fusion via logistic regression reached 98.68+/-0.32%, giving an 81.5% error reduction versus EEG alone and improving worst-task accuracy from approximately 87% for a single modality to 96.76%. Fusion also reduced cross-task performance variance from approximately 3% to 1.06% (paired t-test, p < 0.001, df = 4; p-values approximate given fold dependence), showing more uniform reliability across the assessment battery. Although the small sample limits generalizability, these results suggest that EEG and IMU provide asymmetric, task-dependent strengths and that late fusion can leverage this complementarity to improve assessment reliability. This study provides methodological and empirical motivation for larger-scale clinical validation in movement disorder populations.

AI.tldr: This proof-of-concept study shows that fusing EEG and IMU data via late fusion (logistic regression) achieves 98.68% accuracy across 10 motor tasks, reducing error by 81.5% compared to EEG alone and improving reliability for movement disorder assessment, though limited by small sample size (N=6).
AI.motivation: Movement disorders like Parkinson's require comprehensive motor assessment, but reliable digital assessment pipelines that integrate multiple sensing modalities across diverse motor tasks remain insufficiently characterized.
AI.method: Six participants performed 10 motor activities with synchronized EEG-IMU data (52 recording pairs). EEGNet + Transformer processed 16-channel EEG (125 Hz); XGBoost handled hand-crafted accelerometer/gyroscope features (25 Hz). Late fusion used logistic regression.
AI.result: IMU: 94.41% accuracy (outperformed EEG on 7/10 tasks). EEG: 92.82% accuracy (better for rhythmic cycling: 4.03% vs 12.10% error). Late fusion: 98.68% accuracy, 81.5% error reduction vs EEG alone, worst-task accuracy improved to 96.76%, cross-task variance reduced from ~3% to 1.06% (p<0.001).
AI.conclusion: EEG and IMU provide asymmetric, task-dependent strengths, and late fusion leverages this complementarity to improve assessment reliability. The findings motivate larger-scale clinical validation in movement disorder populations.

title: Diffusion MRI preprocessing affects ADC estimation and automatic PI-RADS v2.1 classification in bi-parametric prostate MRI
comment: 19 pages, 10 figures, ISMRM Diffusion workshop 2025, ESMRMB 2025
summary: Diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI) is acquired as part of bi-parametric prostate MRI, but suffers from artifacts that degrade downstream quantitative and diagnostic performance. While DWI preprocessing is standard in brain imaging, its adoption in prostate imaging remains limited and lacks standardized pipelines. This study investigated the effect of different DWI preprocessing strategies on apparent diffusion coefficient (ADC) estimation and automatic Prostate Imaging Reporting and Data System (PI-RADS) classification. 268 cases were derived from the fastMRI prostate cohort by sequentially applying denoising, Gibbs-ringing correction, and diffeomorphic registration for susceptibility distortion correction. ADC maps were compared using linear least squares (LLS) and iteratively-weighted LLS (IWLLS). A 3-class DenseNet classifier was trained to predict PI-RADS scores from multi-channel MRI inputs. ADC analysis revealed statistically significant differences across preprocessing pipelines, with LLS and IWLLS producing numerically equivalent maps. Linear relationships between ADC values were preserved across most datasets (PCC ~0.99), while distortion correction realigned DWI to T2w anatomy and altered ADC values accordingly (PCC ~0.90). Classification showed the best AUROC and sensitivity for high-risk PI-RADS classes in the fully processed dataset. False-negative analysis revealed this dataset produced the least overconfident incorrect predictions on high-risk classes, which is a desirable property for clinical triage. DWI preprocessing, particularly distortion correction, enhances both ADC map quality and the predictive power of deep learning models for PI-RADS classification, supporting the need for optimized preprocessing pipelines in prostate MRI.

AI.tldr: This study investigates the impact of DWI preprocessing strategies (denoising, Gibbs-ringing correction, distortion correction) on ADC estimation and automatic PI-RADS classification in prostate MRI, finding that preprocessing—especially distortion correction—improves ADC quality and deep learning model performance.
AI.motivation: DWI preprocessing is standard in brain imaging but lacks standardized pipelines in prostate imaging, leading to artifacts that degrade quantitative and diagnostic performance.
AI.method: 268 cases from fastMRI prostate cohort underwent denoising, Gibbs-ringing correction, and diffeomorphic registration; ADC maps were computed via LLS and IWLLS; a 3-class DenseNet classifier predicted PI-RADS scores.
AI.result: Significant ADC differences across pipelines; LLS and IWLLS produced numerically equivalent maps; distortion correction realigned DWI to T2w anatomy (PCC ~0.90); fully processed dataset achieved best AUROC and sensitivity for high-risk PI-RADS classes with least overconfident false negatives.
AI.conclusion: DWI preprocessing, especially distortion correction, improves ADC map quality and deep learning model predictive power, supporting optimized preprocessing pipelines in prostate MRI.

title: Differentially Private Consistent Release of Counting Queries
summary:
We study the problem of releasing counting-query outputs through a stochastic mechanism that is both consistent and \((ε,δ)\)-differentially private. Consistency requires the released value to lie within the feasible range of the query, while utility is measured by the worst-case probability of error. We first derive a closed-form expression for the minimum achievable error probability and obtain an explicit optimal mechanism. By exploiting the active differential privacy constraints satisfied by this mechanism, we then characterize the entire class of optimal mechanisms via a propagation argument, identifying the structural properties shared by all optimizers.
  We next extend the framework to the setting in which the privacy mechanism is cascaded with an arbitrary fixed stochastic transformation representing a predetermined portion of the communication medium between the source and the destination. We first establish necessary and sufficient conditions under which this partial fixation of the medium incurs no loss in utility. We then derive upper and lower bounds on the optimal achievable performance based on convex mixing and spectral perturbation. Finally, we apply the theory to (M)-ary phase-shift keying (PSK) transmission over an additive white Gaussian noise (AWGN) channel and show that uncoded transmission is effectively optimal in the high-privacy regime.

AI.tldr: This paper derives optimal differentially private mechanisms for releasing counting queries with consistency constraints, characterizes all optimal mechanisms, and extends the analysis to cascaded privacy-communication systems, showing uncoded PSK transmission is near-optimal under high privacy.
AI.motivation: To design stochastic mechanisms for releasing counting queries that simultaneously satisfy differential privacy, consistency (outputs within feasible range), and minimize worst-case error probability.
AI.method: Closed-form expression for minimum achievable error probability and explicit optimal mechanism; propagation argument to characterize entire class of optimal mechanisms; extension to cascaded systems with convex mixing and spectral perturbation analysis.
AI.result: Derived closed-form optimal mechanism and characterized all optimizers; established conditions for no utility loss under cascaded transformations; showed uncoded M-ary PSK over AWGN is effectively optimal in high-privacy regime.
AI.conclusion: Optimal mechanisms for differentially private counting queries can be fully characterized, and the framework extends naturally to privacy-communication cascades where uncoded transmission becomes optimal under strong privacy guarantees.

title: A Biomimetic Myoelectric Tentacle Prosthesis with Sensorless Object Detection and Vibrotactile Feedback
summary: This paper presents the design and evaluation of a myoelectric tentacle-shaped prosthesis integrating electromyographic (EMG) control, sensorless object detection, and vibrotactile feedback. The objective was to develop a responsive and intuitive assistive device that adapts to various object shapes while providing sensory feedback to the user. The system relies on EMG signals to control the motion of a flexible, biomimetic structure whose curling geometry follows a logarithmic spiral, enabling it to coil around objects. To ensure stable control, the EMG signal is normalized and filtered, and a threshold-based method identifies user intention. Object contact is detected through a slope-based analysis of motor current, eliminating the need for external sensors, and a haptic feedback strategy based on cumulative vibrotactile stimulation conveys spatial information about the tentacle's configuration. The system was evaluated through quantitative and qualitative tests. The results demonstrate a low response time (77 ms on average), enabling smooth real-time interaction; an object-detection success rate above 90%, confirming robustness despite EMG variability; and an effective haptic feedback strategy that allowed users to reliably identify the folding zone of the tentacle. The proposed biomimetic design promotes further investigation of expressive artificial limbs by prioritizing expressive functionality over adherence to a predefined, anthropomorphic form factor.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a myoelectric tentacle-shaped prosthesis using EMG control, sensorless object detection via motor current analysis, and vibrotactile feedback. It achieved 77ms response time, >90% detection accuracy, and effective haptic feedback identification.
AI.motivation: To develop a responsive, intuitive assistive device that adapts to various object shapes while providing sensory feedback, prioritizing expressive functionality over anthropomorphic form.
AI.method: EMG signals control a logarithmic spiral-based biomimetic tentacle; sensorless object detection via slope-based motor current analysis; cumulative vibrotactile feedback for spatial configuration awareness.
AI.result: 77ms average response time, >90% object-detection success rate, effective haptic feedback for folding zone identification.
AI.conclusion: The biomimetic tentacle design is viable for expressive prosthetics, promoting further investigation into non-anthropomorphic artificial limbs.

title: Low-Rank Attention Residuals
summary: Attention Residuals replace the fixed residual sum with depthwise attention over previous sub-layer outputs in large language models (LLMs), but use each output as both a full-dimensional key and value. This couples routing with representation and makes depth-routing scores scale with the hidden width $d$. We propose Low-Rank Attention Residuals (LR-AttnRes), which keep full-dimensional residual values while using $r$-dimensional keys, with $r \ll d$, for routing. Projected LR-AttnRes emits learned low-rank keys from existing output projections, decoupling routing from residual content and achieving the best validation loss among the variants tested. Sliced LR-AttnRes uses the last $r$ dimensions of each value as the routing key, removing the auxiliary key-projection path and reducing residual-side FLOPs while still improving performance. Comprehensive sweeps show that depthwise routing can be effective with far fewer dimensions than the model width. We release code and models to facilitate future research.

AI.tldr: LR-AttnRes decouples routing from representation in attention residual networks by using low-rank keys (r << d) instead of full-dimensional keys, achieving better validation loss and reduced FLOPs.
AI.motivation: Standard Attention Residuals use full-dimensional keys for depthwise routing, which couples routing with representation and causes depth-routing scores to scale with hidden width d, limiting efficiency and flexibility.
AI.method: Proposes two variants: (1) Projected LR-AttnRes - learns low-rank r-dimensional keys from existing output projections, (2) Sliced LR-AttnRes - uses the last r dimensions of each value as the routing key, removing auxiliary projection paths.
AI.result: Low-rank routing (r << d) achieves the best validation loss; Sliced variant reduces residual-side FLOPs while still improving performance.
AI.conclusion: Depthwise routing can be effective with far fewer dimensions than model width, decoupling routing from content improves efficiency.

title: Lifelong Representations: A Survey on Continual Self-Supervised Learning for Vision Models
comment: This work has been accepted for publication in IEEE EAIS 2026
summary: Traditionally, continual learning has assumed access to labeled data, yet many real-world applications -- such as lifelong robotics -- require models to adapt continuously from unlabeled streams. This has led to the development of continual self-supervised learning (CSSL), a rapidly growing area that lacks a dedicated, systematic review. In this work, we present a comprehensive survey of CSSL for vision, with connections to emerging vision-language settings. First, we analyze existing evaluation protocols and highlight inconsistencies that hinder fair comparison. We then examine why self-supervised objectives exhibit improved robustness to catastrophic forgetting, relating this to task-agnostic representations and smoother loss landscapes. Next, we organize existing methods into a unified taxonomy based on their forgetting-mitigation strategies, including distillation, replay, regularization, architectural approaches, model merging, and objective-level adaptation. Finally, we identify open challenges such as scalability and the need for fast adaptability. We argue that advancing CSSL requires moving beyond small-scale benchmarks towards continual pre-training paradigms for large-scale systems.

AI.tldr: A comprehensive survey of continual self-supervised learning (CSSL) for vision, covering evaluation protocols, forgetting-mitigation strategies, and open challenges while advocating for large-scale continual pre-training.
AI.motivation: Real-world applications like lifelong robotics require models to adapt from unlabeled data streams, but CSSL lacks a dedicated systematic review despite its rapid growth.
AI.method: The authors (1) analyze evaluation protocols and highlight inconsistencies, (2) examine why self-supervised objectives resist catastrophic forgetting, (3) organize methods into a unified taxonomy (distillation, replay, regularization, architectural approaches, model merging, objective-level adaptation), and (4) identify open challenges.
AI.result: Identified key insights: self-supervised objectives exhibit improved robustness to forgetting due to task-agnostic representations and smoother loss landscapes. Established a unified taxonomy of CSSL methods.
AI.conclusion: CSSL research should move beyond small-scale benchmarks toward continual pre-training paradigms for large-scale systems, addressing scalability and fast adaptability challenges.

title: AGM-like Paraconsistent Partial Meet Abductive Expansion Operation
summary: In his 1996 doctoral thesis, Maurice Pagnucco created the first AGM-like abductive expansion operation. Taking his operation as a basis, as well as a taxonomy -- inspired by Atocha Aliseda -- responsible for highlighting and formalizing the main components of abductive reasoning, the main aim of this paper is to present a new paraconsistent AGM-like abductive expansion operation -- capable of assimilating contradictory explanatory hypotheses without trivialization and the consequent absurd epistemic state -- with its postulates and its transitively relational partial meet construction. To a large extent, the formal development presented in this paper was only made possible by the recent creation of the paraconsistent logic RCbr, an LFI (Logics of Formal Inconsistencies) that establishes properties especially relevant to belief revision contexts, in particular, the ability to be self-extensional -- i.e., to satisfy the replacement property. This is the first of two papers: the paraconsistent abductive expansion operation announced here -- which is part of a new system called AGMpabd -- despite bringing many interesting features, does not assign any relevant epistemic role to the paraconsistent operators of negation and consistency. Only in a second paper will an analogous paraconsistent abductive expansion operation -- which is part of another new system, AGMcircabd -- be enhanced in this direction. Nevertheless, to the best of my knowledge, the operation developed in this paper is the first of its kind in the AGM literature.

AI.tldr: This paper presents the first paraconsistent AGM-like abductive expansion operation (AGMpabd), built on Pagnucco's work and Aliseda's taxonomy, using the paraconsistent logic RCbr to handle contradictory explanatory hypotheses without trivialization.
AI.motivation: To create a paraconsistent abductive expansion operation that can assimilate contradictory explanatory hypotheses without trivialization, which existing AGM-based operations cannot handle.
AI.method: The operation is built upon Pagnucco's AGM-like abductive expansion, Aliseda's taxonomy of abductive reasoning, and the paraconsistent logic RCbr (an LFI) which satisfies the replacement property (self-extensionality). Postulates and a transitively relational partial meet construction are provided.
AI.result: The AGMpabd system is introduced as the first paraconsistent AGM-like abductive expansion operation in the literature, capable of handling contradictions without trivialization. However, it does not yet assign epistemic roles to paraconsistent operators of negation and consistency.
AI.conclusion: This is the first of two papers; the current operation (AGMpabd) is foundational. A follow-up paper will enhance it into AGMcircabd, which will integrate paraconsistent negation and consistency operators with epistemic significance.

title: MorphologyFM: A Foundation Model for Morphology-Aware Representation Learning from ECG and Pulse Oximetry Waveforms
summary: Foundation models have recently emerged as a powerful paradigm for learning transferable representations from large scale biomedical data, yet existing approaches for physiological waveforms primarily optimize reconstruction or forecasting objectives that do not explicitly preserve clinically meaningful waveform morphology. Electrocardiograms (ECGs) and pulse oximetry (SpO2) waveforms encode rich cardiovascular and hemodynamic information through their morphological structure. In this work, we introduce MorphologyFM, a multimodal foundation model pretrained on paired ECG and SpO2 waveforms from the MIMIC critical care database using a morphology aware self supervised learning objective. MorphologyFM combines morphology guided masking, cross modal representation learning, and contrastive latent alignment to learn representations that capture clinically relevant physiological structure without requiring manual annotations. We evaluate MorphologyFM across multiple downstream prediction tasks, including arrhythmia classification, hypoxemia prediction, mortality prediction, and length of stay estimation, demonstrating consistent improvements over representative self supervised learning methods, including Masked Autoencoders (MAE), contrastive learning, Barlow Twins, and Joint Embedding Predictive Architectures (JEPA). Furthermore, we show that jointly modeling ECG and SpO2 waveforms produces more transferable representations than single modality pretraining. Our results establish waveform morphology as a powerful inductive bias for self supervised physiological representation learning and introduce MorphologyFM as a general purpose foundation model for continuous physiological monitoring.

AI.tldr: MorphologyFM is a multimodal foundation model pretrained on paired ECG and SpO2 waveforms using morphology-aware self-supervised learning, outperforming existing methods on multiple clinical prediction tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing foundation models for physiological waveforms optimize reconstruction or forecasting objectives that do not explicitly preserve clinically meaningful waveform morphology, limiting their utility for downstream clinical tasks.
AI.method: Combines morphology-guided masking, cross-modal representation learning, and contrastive latent alignment on paired ECG and SpO2 waveforms from MIMIC critical care database.
AI.result: Outperforms MAE, contrastive learning, Barlow Twins, and JEPA on arrhythmia classification, hypoxemia prediction, mortality prediction, and length of stay estimation. Joint ECG+SpO2 pretraining yields better transferable representations than single-modality pretraining.
AI.conclusion: Waveform morphology serves as a powerful inductive bias for self-supervised physiological representation learning, and MorphologyFM can serve as a general-purpose foundation model for continuous physiological monitoring.

title: On the Performance of Integrated Satellite-Terrestrial Maritime Communications
summary: In this paper, we present an integrated terrestrial and satellite maritime communication system, where a shorebased terrestrial base station (TBS) and a low Earth orbit (LEO) satellite cooperatively provide wide-area communication services to maritime users. We conduct performance analysis for the integrated satellite-terrestrial maritime communication system. Specifically, we analyze the transmission rate and coverage probability of near-shore and off-shore users respectively according to the maritime communication environment. Besides, in order to better understand the impact of some key parameters, we also make asymptotic analysis in some special cases. Further, we design an optimization algorithm to maximize the coverage probability of near-shore users by adjusting the transmission power of TBS and the LEO satellite, while ensuring the both off-shore and near-shore users can meet the minimum communication rate requirements. Finally, extensive numerical analysis results verify the accuracy of the theoretical results and the effectiveness of the proposed optimization algorithm in the maritime communication system.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes and analyzes an integrated terrestrial-satellite maritime communication system where a shore-based base station and LEO satellite cooperatively serve near-shore and off-shore users, including performance analysis, asymptotic analysis, and an optimization algorithm for coverage probability.
AI.motivation: To provide wide-area communication services to maritime users by leveraging both terrestrial and satellite networks, addressing coverage limitations of each individual system.
AI.method: The authors analyze transmission rate and coverage probability for near-shore and off-shore users separately, perform asymptotic analysis for key parameters, and design an optimization algorithm that adjusts transmission power of TBS and LEO satellite to maximize near-shore coverage probability while meeting minimum rate requirements for both user types.
AI.result: Numerical analysis verifies the accuracy of theoretical results and confirms the effectiveness of the proposed optimization algorithm in the maritime communication system.
AI.conclusion: The integrated terrestrial-satellite approach effectively enhances maritime communication performance, and the proposed power optimization algorithm successfully balances coverage and rate requirements.

title: RASR: Range-Aware Scale Recovery for Metric UAV Navigation
comment: 5 pages, 4 figures. Technical report for the UAVM 2026 PairUAV Challenge
summary: Under Global Navigation Satellite System (GNSS) denial, a UAV controller still needs a distance and heading command it can execute, making accurate metric last-meter navigation essential. Dense pair-geometry foundation models transfer relative structure well, yet the distance scale of their raw metric outputs remains poorly calibrated. Under the relative error metric of PairUAV, correcting only the average scale can still leave costly, distance-dependent residuals near the goal. To address this scale mismatch, Range-Aware Scale Recovery (RASR) separates a transferable scale-recovery core from a protocol-specific calibration module in a per-pair system fixed at inference. The core compresses frozen Matching And Stereo 3D Reconstruction (MASt3R)-style geometry into a compact descriptor and uses global calibration to recover the dominant metric signal. Range-bucket residual correction and command-grid alignment stay inside the calibration module, so they match the command format and evaluation protocol of PairUAV. On the UAVs in Multimedia 2026 PairUAV online evaluation, RASR reaches a total score of 0.003189. Under the PairUAV protocol, frozen pair geometry thus yields stable per-pair distance and heading estimates, while every protocol-specific adjustment stays confined to a calibration module fixed before inference. Code and materials are available at https://github.com/lht-research/rasr-pairuav.

AI.tldr: RASR corrects metric scale errors in GNSS-denied UAV last-meter navigation by separating a frozen scale-recovery core from a protocol-specific calibration module, achieving strong PairUAV evaluation scores.
AI.motivation: Under GNSS denial, UAV controllers need accurate metric distance and heading for last-meter navigation, but dense pair-geometry foundation models output poorly calibrated distance scales, and average-scale correction alone leaves distance-dependent residuals near the goal.
AI.method: RASR separates a transferable scale-recovery core (compressing frozen MASt3R-style geometry into a compact descriptor with global calibration) from a protocol-specific calibration module (containing range-bucket residual correction and command-grid alignment) fixed at inference.
AI.result: RASR achieves a total score of 0.003189 on the UAVs in Multimedia 2026 PairUAV online evaluation, demonstrating stable per-pair distance and heading estimates.
AI.conclusion: Frozen pair geometry with RASR yields stable per-pair distance/heading estimates, while all protocol-specific adjustments stay confined to a pre-fixed calibration module, effectively solving the scale mismatch problem.

title: FedCausal-Dyn: A Causal-Dynamic Paradigm for Federated Learning under Dynamic Feature Drift
summary: This paper addresses the challenging problem of dynamic feature drift in federated learning, where data distributions evolve across clients and over time -- a common scenario in real-world applications like financial technology. Existing approaches often assume static drift, limiting their effectiveness in non-stationary environments. To overcome this, we propose \textbf{FedCausal-Dyn}, a novel federated learning framework built on a causal-dynamic paradigm. Its key innovation is \textit{causal-domain feature separation}, which disentangles domain-invariant causal features from spurious, domain-specific variations via specialized projection heads and adversarial training. This enables \textit{reliable and dynamic prototype aggregation}, weighting local class prototypes by estimated reliability before global aggregation. We further introduce \textit{causal-feature guided collaborative regularization}, unifying prototype contrastive alignment and domain invariance into a cohesive objective. Extensive experiments on three federated domain generalization benchmarks demonstrate that FedCausal-Dyn consistently achieves state-of-the-art performance, with the highest average accuracy and the most stable results. Ablation studies confirm each component's critical contribution. Our work provides a robust and principled solution for federated learning under dynamic feature drift.

AI.tldr: FedCausal-Dyn is a federated learning framework that handles dynamic feature drift by separating causal features from spurious ones using adversarial training, enabling reliable prototype aggregation and achieving state-of-the-art results on benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing federated learning methods assume static data drift, which fails in real-world non-stationary environments (e.g., fintech) where data distributions evolve across clients and over time.
AI.method: Proposes FedCausal-Dyn with three key components: (1) causal-domain feature separation via projection heads and adversarial training, (2) reliable dynamic prototype aggregation weighting local prototypes by estimated reliability, (3) causal-feature guided collaborative regularization unifying contrastive alignment and domain invariance.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on three federated domain generalization benchmarks with highest average accuracy and most stable results; ablation studies confirm each component's critical contribution.
AI.conclusion: FedCausal-Dyn provides a robust and principled solution for federated learning under dynamic feature drift, effectively handling non-stationary environments.

title: Adversarially Guided Diffusion for LiDAR Range Image Synthesis
comment: Accepted at the 1st Workshop on Secure and Trustworthy AI (STAI 2026), co-located with the European Conference on Machine Learning and Principles and Practice of Knowledge Discovery in Databases (ECML PKDD 2026)
summary: LiDAR semantic segmentation is a key perception task in autonomous driving, where false predictions can affect downstream planning and safety-critical decision-making. Although adversarial attacks, and specifically adversarial examples, have been widely studied for image classification and 3D point cloud segmentation, unrestricted adversarial examples remain largely unexplored in the space of 2D range images, which are projections of 3D point clouds. The proposed method is, to the best of our knowledge, the first diffusion-based unrestricted adversarial attack against 2D range-image segmentation, using adversarial guidance from a segmentation loss. By applying guidance directly during sampling, the method produces unrestricted adversarial examples that remain close to the learned LiDAR data manifold while inducing structured segmentation errors. Experiments on the SemanticKITTI dataset using RangeNet++ and CENet segmentation networks demonstrate that the attack provides adjustable degradation across guidance strengths and transfers across segmentation architectures. Compared with norm-bounded FGSM and SegPGD baselines, the proposed attack offers a distinct effectiveness-realism trade-off, achieving controllable white-box and transfer degradation while maintaining competitive distributional and visual realism.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes the first diffusion-based unrestricted adversarial attack against 2D range-image segmentation for LiDAR data, using adversarial guidance from segmentation loss to generate realistic yet misleading examples.
AI.motivation: Unrestricted adversarial examples remain largely unexplored for 2D range images (projections of 3D point clouds), which are important for autonomous driving safety. Existing norm-bounded attacks may not produce realistic adversarial examples for this domain.
AI.method: A diffusion-based unrestricted adversarial attack that applies adversarial guidance from a segmentation loss directly during the sampling process, generating examples that stay on the LiDAR data manifold while inducing structured segmentation errors.
AI.result: Experiments on SemanticKITTI with RangeNet++ and CENet show adjustable degradation across guidance strengths, transferability across architectures, and a better effectiveness-realism trade-off compared to FGSM and SegPGD baselines.
AI.conclusion: The proposed attack offers controllable white-box and transfer degradation with competitive distributional and visual realism, highlighting a new direction for adversarial robustness research in LiDAR-based perception.

title: Coresets Before Score Sets: Evaluation-Unsupervised Prompt Subset Selection for LLM Benchmarks
summary: We study LLM benchmark coreset selection: selecting a small subset of prompts over multiple benchmarks whose induced model scores and rankings approximate those obtained from the full benchmark suite. In evaluation-unsupervised benchmark coreset selection (our approach), the selection algorithm uses no model evaluation outcomes, and operates on a fine granularity by producing subsets of prompts over multiple benchmarks rather than producing a sub-collection of entire benchmarks. We use submodular subset selection, and we develop and evaluate many different submodular functions for this purpose, including determinantal point process (DPP) based approaches, submodular mutual information functions, and facility location-based functions. On a new large-scale suite of 35 heterogeneous benchmarks spanning five different capability categories, 18 frontier LLMs, and over 61K prompts, we find that the facility location (FL) function operating exclusively on inexpensive semantic prompt embeddings preserves LLM scores better than twelve separate score-based and diversity-based baselines, across a range of coreset budgets. Moreover, we show our proposed objective is not limited to the evaluation-unsupervised regime: in the setting where only a handful of whole benchmarks must be selected and a large amount of model scores are available, the same objective matches or outperforms state-of-the-art baselines on the MMLU and MTEB leaderboards, while being substantially cheaper to compute. Together, our results suggest that submodularity, in general, is a strong and reliable tool for benchmark compression.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes using submodular subset selection (especially facility location function) to compress LLM benchmarks by selecting a small subset of prompts that preserves model rankings and scores, without needing model evaluation outcomes.
AI.motivation: Evaluating LLMs on large benchmark suites is computationally expensive. The authors aim to select a small but representative subset of prompts across multiple benchmarks that can approximate full-benchmark evaluation results, reducing cost while maintaining fidelity.
AI.method: The paper formulates benchmark coreset selection as a submodular optimization problem in an evaluation-unsupervised setting (no model scores needed). They compare multiple submodular functions—determinantal point process (DPP), submodular mutual information, and facility location (FL)—using inexpensive semantic embeddings. The FL function selects diverse and representative prompts across benchmarks.
AI.result: On a large suite of 35 benchmarks, 18 LLMs, and 61K prompts, the facility location function outperforms 12 baselines in preserving LLM scores across coreset budgets. It also matches/outperforms SOTA on MMLU and MTEB leaderboards in supervised settings while being cheaper to compute.
AI.conclusion: Submodularity, particularly the facility location function, is a reliable and efficient tool for LLM benchmark compression in both unsupervised and supervised settings.

title: Data-Driven Forward and Inverse Modeling of V-Beam Thermal Sensors
summary: This paper presents a machine learning framework for data-driven inverse design of V-beam thermal sensors. The goal is to determine the optimal sensor geometry: beam inclination angle, beam length and beam width that achieves a target displacement under a given temperature. The design should also provide the geometry with minimum structure volume and minimum mechanical stress the sensor must support. This problem is ill-posed as for a given displacement there are multiple possible geometric configurations, causing direct regression methods to fail. We document a series of five exploratory trials that progressively revealed the nature of the problem culminating in a two-phase solution: a neural network forward model trained to map geometry and material constants to sensor responses, a gradient-descent inverse optimization over the frozen forward model, minimizing stress and volume simultaneously. The proposed pipeline utilizes a 3000-sample dataset and achieves a MAPE of 4.76% for predicting the displacement, more than 70% of predictions having MAPE of under 5%.

AI.tldr: A two-phase ML framework (forward NN + gradient-descent inverse optimization) for designing V-beam thermal sensors, achieving 4.76% MAPE on displacement prediction with 70%+ predictions under 5% error.
AI.motivation: To determine optimal V-beam sensor geometry (angle, length, width) achieving target displacement under temperature while minimizing structural volume and mechanical stress, addressing an ill-posed inverse problem where multiple geometries map to the same displacement.
AI.method: Five exploratory trials leading to a two-phase solution: (1) Train a neural network forward model mapping geometry+material constants to sensor responses; (2) Freeze the forward model and perform gradient-descent inverse optimization to minimize stress and volume simultaneously. Uses a 3000-sample dataset.
AI.result: MAPE of 4.76% for displacement prediction; more than 70% of predictions have MAPE under 5%.
AI.conclusion: The proposed two-phase ML pipeline effectively handles the ill-posed inverse design problem for V-beam thermal sensors, producing accurate displacement predictions and enabling multi-objective optimization of stress and volume.

title: Label-Free Target-Domain Adaptation for Unconstrained Event-Image Feature Matching via Dual-Stage Distillation
comment: Accepted to ACM MM 2026. The source code and benchmark will be made publicly available at https://github.com/ZhonghuaYi/nexus2-official
summary: Building pixel-level correspondence between event and image data is a fundamental task for multi-sensor systems. However, existing cross-modal matching methods are largely restricted by their reliance on either matching labels or strictly aligned hardware, which limits them to unlabeled and unconstrained real-world scenarios where neither matching ground truth nor prior sensor relationships are available. To address this, we propose a novel two-stage training paradigm. First, we leverage large-scale data to perform label-agnostic distillation pretraining, upgrading optimization objectives with distribution-based and contrastive losses to learn highly generalizable representations. Second, to tackle unlabeled and unconstrained downstream data, we introduce an epipolar-guided self-distillation framework. By utilizing consistency verification to isolate robust matches and incorporating geometric confidence derived from an external epipolar prior, our model can effectively self-evolve directly on target domains without any supervision. Furthermore, we introduce a rigorous cross-modal evaluation benchmark based on TUM-VIE, featuring physically separated cameras with distinct intrinsic parameters and resolutions. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our proposed method achieves state-of-the-art performance on both MVSEC and TUM-VIE pose estimation tasks. The source code and benchmark will be made publicly available at https://github.com/ZhonghuaYi/nexus2-official.

AI.tldr: A two-stage training paradigm (label-agnostic distillation pretraining + epipolar-guided self-distillation) for establishing pixel-level correspondence between event and image data without relying on matching labels or aligned hardware, achieving SOTA on MVSEC and TUM-VIE pose estimation.
AI.motivation: Existing cross-modal matching methods are limited by reliance on matching labels or strictly aligned hardware, making them unsuitable for unlabeled and unconstrained real-world scenarios.
AI.method: (1) Label-agnostic distillation pretraining using distribution-based and contrastive losses on large-scale data. (2) Epipolar-guided self-distillation framework with consistency verification and geometric confidence from external epipolar prior for unsupervised self-evolution on target domains.
AI.result: State-of-the-art performance on both MVSEC and TUM-VIE pose estimation tasks.
AI.conclusion: The proposed method effectively handles unlabeled, unconstrained cross-modal matching without requiring matching ground truth or prior sensor relationships, and introduces a new rigorous benchmark for the community.

title: Joint Communication and Sensing Design for Integrated Satellite-Terrestrial Maritime Systems
summary: Joint communication and sensing has been a key technology in 6G. By integrating sensing into maritime communications, ships can communicate with the base station while sensing the surrounding environment to ensure safe navigation. In this paper, we introduce an integrated satellite-terrestrial maritime system (ISTMS) with joint communication and sensing based on the same radio-frequency signals. Specifically, the terrestrial base station (TBS) and low Earth orbit (LEO) satellite provide communication services for near-shore users (NSUs) and off-shore users (OSUs), respectively, while simultaneously performing target sensing. Based on a differential evolution method (DE), we propose a sensing algorithm, which can enhance the location accuracy and reduce resource consumption. Furthermore, we derive the key performance metrics for both communication and sensing. Through joint beamforming optimization of the TBS and LEO satellite, we maximize the sum rate of maritime users while satisfying target localization accuracy requirements and transmit power constraints. Finally, extensive simulation results demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed algorithms in terms of location accuracy and transmission rate compared with the baseline algorithms.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes an integrated satellite-terrestrial maritime system (ISTMS) for joint communication and sensing, using a differential evolution-based sensing algorithm and joint beamforming optimization to enhance location accuracy and sum rate for maritime users.
AI.motivation: To enable safe maritime navigation by integrating sensing capabilities into 6G maritime communications, allowing ships to communicate while sensing the surrounding environment.
AI.method: Proposes an ISTMS where TBS serves near-shore users and LEO satellites serve off-shore users, both performing sensing. Uses a differential evolution (DE) based sensing algorithm for accurate localization, and joint beamforming optimization between TBS and LEO satellite to maximize sum rate under localization accuracy and power constraints.
AI.result: Simulation results show the proposed DE-based sensing algorithm and joint beamforming optimization achieve higher location accuracy and transmission rate compared to baseline algorithms.
AI.conclusion: The ISTMS framework effectively integrates communication and sensing in maritime environments, improving both target localization and communication performance.

title: TS-Mask VLA: 2D Temporal-Spatial Masking for Vision-Language-Action Model with Effective Bridging
comment: 9 pages, 5 figures, accepted to the 2026 IEEE/RSJ International Conference on Intelligent Robots and Systems (IROS 2026)
summary: Vision-language-action (VLA) models aim to understand natural-language instructions and visual observations, and to generate and execute corresponding actions as embodied agents. Recently, autoregressive token-based action generation has driven the development of many representative VLA models. However, this paradigm often reduces action generation to next-token prediction, thereby lacking explicit modeling of the spatiotemporal structure of action sequences and the disentanglement between vision-language representations and actions, which can limit performance in long-horizon and complex scenarios. In this paper, we propose TS-Mask VLA, a vision-language-action framework for robot manipulation. TS-Mask VLA is built upon two key designs: (1) a Discrete Diffusion Action Expert equipped with a Bridge Attention conditioning bridge, which enables multi-layer conditioning from the VLM and facilitates more accurate and stable action generation; and (2) a temporal-spatial 2D masking strategy for discrete action tokens that strengthens the model's understanding of cross-time dependencies and inter-dimensional coupling, leading to more structurally consistent action sequences. We conduct extensive experiments on simulation benchmarks and real-world tasks. On LIBERO, TS-Mask VLA achieves a 95.7 percent average success rate with only 0.5B parameters, outperforming significantly larger models. On CALVIN, it attains the best average sequence length of 4.19 and strong long-horizon performance. Comprehensive analyses and ablations further validate the effectiveness of our design.

AI.tldr: TS-Mask VLA proposes a discrete diffusion action expert with bridge attention and temporal-spatial 2D masking for robot manipulation, achieving 95.7% success on LIBERO with only 0.5B parameters.
AI.motivation: Existing autoregressive VLA models lack explicit modeling of spatiotemporal structure in action sequences and disentanglement between vision-language representations and actions, limiting performance in long-horizon/complex tasks.
AI.method: (1) Discrete Diffusion Action Expert with Bridge Attention conditioning bridge for multi-layer VLM conditioning. (2) Temporal-spatial 2D masking strategy for discrete action tokens to model cross-time dependencies and inter-dimensional coupling.
AI.result: 95.7% average success rate on LIBERO (0.5B params, outperforming larger models); best average sequence length of 4.19 on CALVIN with strong long-horizon performance.
AI.conclusion: TS-Mask VLA's diffusion-based action generation with temporal-spatial masking effectively improves action structural consistency and long-horizon performance, validated by extensive ablations.

title: Mitigating Early Training Collapse in CTR Models
summary: Deep neural models for click-through rate prediction often exhibit a sharp decline in validation performance immediately after the first training epoch despite continued improvement in training loss. This instability restricts effective learning and limits model performance. In this study, we analyze this behavior using large-scale industrial datasets and evaluate practical mitigation strategies. While reducing the learning rate provides only incremental gains, controlling feature sparsity yields substantial improvements.
