This work evaluates both approaches for multi-agent sUAS separation under adversarial GNSS degradation. Both architectures first estimate a worst-case traffic state consistent with bounded observation uncertainty, then diverge: action filtering constrains policy outputs via discrete-time control barrier functions evaluated at the worst-case state, while observation filtering presents the worst-case state directly to the policy as corrected input. Experimental results show that action filtering provides negligible safety improvement, while observation filtering reduces near mid-air collisions by 90% and remains robust to the barrier function's tradeoff between separation distance and closing rate. These results suggest that, for policies with learned safety behaviors, preserving the policy's decision authority outperforms overriding its actions with hand-designed constraints.

AI.tldr: This paper compares action filtering vs. observation filtering for sUAS separation assurance under GNSS degradation, finding that observation filtering reduces near mid-air collisions by 90% while action filtering provides negligible improvement. The key insight is that preserving the learned policy's decision authority outperforms overriding its actions with hand-designed constraints.
AI.motivation: Learned separation policies for sUAS assume accurate GNSS data, which fails in urban environments due to multipath, signal blockage, and interference. The paper investigates how to deploy these policies under GNSS degradation — specifically whether to filter the policy's actions or its observations at runtime.
AI.method: Both architectures estimate a worst-case traffic state consistent with bounded observation uncertainty, then diverge: (1) action filtering uses discrete-time control barrier functions evaluated at the worst-case state to constrain policy outputs; (2) observation filtering presents the worst-case state directly as corrected input to the policy.
AI.result: Action filtering provides negligible safety improvement, while observation filtering reduces near mid-air collisions by 90% and remains robust to the tradeoff between separation distance and closing rate in the barrier function.
AI.conclusion: For policies with learned safety behaviors, preserving the policy's decision authority (via observation filtering) outperforms overriding its actions (via action filtering) with hand-designed constraints.

title: The Silent Freeze: Predicting When Low-Precision Training Stops Learning
summary: Training in reduced floating-point precision can silently halt learning: when a gradient-descent weight update falls below half the unit in the last place (ULP) of the weight, it rounds away and that coordinate freezes while its gradient is still nonzero. The freeze is deterministic, governed by a per-coordinate half-ULP condition, and predictable from a high-precision trajectory and the target mantissa length alone, without low-precision data. In a small GPT trained under the standard AdamW-plus-cosine recipe with bf16-equivalent stored weights, training proceeds normally and then permanently freezes just past mid-run, within four steps of the a-priori prediction. In a $124$-million-parameter GPT-2 transformer whose weights are constrained to the $8$-bit floating-point grid after every optimizer step, with no master weights, the dense weights freeze at initialization in both fp8 formats -- predicted \emph{a priori} from an fp32 reference -- and validation loss plateaus while full precision keeps improving. Stochastic rounding removes the persistent freeze, and the same reference predicts that too. The condition transfers across frozen-feature regression, a mantissa-truncation emulator spanning $128\times$ in precision, small networks, and a CNN on MNIST: a computable axis of low-precision training, not diffuse noise.

AI.tldr: Training in reduced floating-point precision can cause deterministic weight freezing when gradient updates fall below half the unit in the last place (ULP) of the weight, permanently halting learning in that coordinate. This is predictable from high-precision trajectories alone, and stochastic rounding can prevent it.
AI.motivation: To understand and predict the silent failure mode where low-precision training causes weights to freeze deterministically, even though gradients remain nonzero, which can halt learning unexpectedly.
AI.method: The authors analyze the half-ULP condition for weight freezing, predict freeze steps from high-precision reference trajectories, and validate predictions across GPT, GPT-2 (124M parameters), frozen-feature regression, mantissa-truncation emulators, small networks, and CNN on MNIST. They also test stochastic rounding as a remedy.
AI.result: Weight freezing occurs deterministically at predicted steps: GPT (bf16-equivalent) freezes just past mid-run within 4 steps of prediction; 124M-parameter GPT-2 with 8-bit float weights freezes at initialization; validation loss plateaus while full precision continues improving. Stochastic rounding removes the persistent freeze as predicted.
AI.conclusion: Low-precision weight freezing is a computable, deterministic phenomenon (not diffuse noise) that can silently halt training, but stochastic rounding can prevent it.

title: ShapKO: Shapley-Adaptive Modality Knockout for Robust Multimodal Learning
summary: Multimodal medical models often degrade when inputs are missing, a common scenario in real-world clinical workflows. Separately, even when all modalities are present, modality dominance is observed during training, where optimization over-relies on a highly predictive modality and undertrains complementary sources, resulting in poor robustness under partial availability. While training-time modality knockout improves missing-modality robustness, existing approaches use static masking rates that cannot adapt to evolving modality utility during training. We introduce ShapKO (Shapley-Adaptive Modality Knockout), a dynamic training strategy that learns modality-specific knockout probabilities based on validation utility. ShapKO periodically evaluates performance across modality subsets, estimates modality importance via Shapley values, and updates masking probabilities to suppress dominant modalities more frequently. This adaptive process promotes complementary representations, while requiring no architectural modifications. We evaluate ShapKO on three datasets covering multitask clinical classification, survival prediction, and cancer detection. ShapKO consistently improves performance under modality absence and yields interpretable trajectories of learned masking behavior. Code is available at: https://github.com/sumona00/ShapKO

AI.tldr: ShapKO dynamically adjusts modality knockout probabilities during training using Shapley values to mitigate modality dominance and improve robustness under missing modalities in multimodal medical models.
AI.motivation: Multimodal medical models suffer from performance degradation when inputs are missing in real-world clinical workflows, and modality dominance during training leads to poor robustness under partial modality availability.
AI.method: ShapKO periodically evaluates model performance across modality subsets, estimates each modality's importance via Shapley values, and updates knockout probabilities to suppress dominant modalities more frequently during training.
AI.result: Evaluated on three datasets (multitask clinical classification, survival prediction, cancer detection), ShapKO consistently improves performance under modality absence and produces interpretable trajectories of learned masking behavior.
AI.conclusion: ShapKO offers a dynamic, adaptive training strategy that improves missing-modality robustness without architectural changes, outperforming static masking approaches across diverse medical tasks.

title: Norm Enforcement for AI Agents: Robustly Shaping Behavior in Multi-Agent Systems
comment: ICML2026 Trustworthy AI for Good Workshop
summary: AI agents are increasingly deployed in shared environments where they pursue diverse goals and compete for rewards. This multi-agent competition can lead to behaviors that serve individual gains at collective cost -- for instance, marketing agents may post misleading content as a result of competing for engagement on social media. Human societies address such problems through norms that constrain acceptable behavior, supported by enforcement mechanisms that detect and penalize violations. Motivated by this, we study norm enforcement mechanisms for language model agents. We find that simple enforcement mechanisms are exploited by misaligned agents for competitive advantage, even when they are not explicitly trained or prompted to do so. We thus turn our attention to designing more robust mechanisms, and identify two key ingredients: estimating each agent's reliability over time, and updating this estimate with escalating penalties for repeated misbehavior. Across three simulated environments and a variety of agent populations, mechanisms built on these principles resist exploitation, while still penalizing norm violations at comparable or lower cost than baselines. Our results position norm enforcement mechanisms as scalable levers for shaping agents' behavior, but only when designed to anticipate becoming part of the system they govern. Our code and data are available at https://yaowenye.com/norm-enforcement.

AI.tldr: The paper studies norm enforcement mechanisms for language model agents in multi-agent competitive environments, finding that simple mechanisms are easily exploited by misaligned agents. The authors propose robust mechanisms combining reliability estimation and escalating penalties, which resist exploitation across multiple simulated environments.
AI.motivation: AI agents deployed in shared environments pursue competing goals, leading to individually beneficial but collectively costly behaviors (e.g., marketing agents posting misleading content). Human societies address such issues through norms with enforcement mechanisms, motivating the study of similar approaches for language model agents.
AI.method: The authors identify two key ingredients for robust norm enforcement: (1) estimating each agent's reliability over time, and (2) updating this estimate with escalating penalties for repeated misbehavior. They test these mechanisms across three simulated environments with diverse agent populations.
AI.result: Simple enforcement mechanisms are exploited by misaligned agents even without explicit training or prompting. The proposed mechanisms resist exploitation while penalizing norm violations at comparable or lower cost than baselines across all three simulated environments and various agent populations.
AI.conclusion: Norm enforcement mechanisms can serve as scalable levers for shaping agent behavior, but only when designed to anticipate becoming part of the system they govern. The approach effectively deters exploitation while maintaining fairness.

title: Networked ISAC Enabled Target Recognition Towards Low-Altitude Economy
summary: In this paper, we propose a low-altitude target (LAT) recognition scheme based on multi-base station (BS) collaboration and multi-scale feature fusion for integrated sensing and communications (ISAC) network. Firstly, we formulate the motion equations, echo channels, and echo signals for unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV), bird, vehicle, and pedestrian under multi-BS collaborative monitoring scenario. Then we extract the velocityresolution-preferred time-frequency spectrum, time-resolutionpreferred time-frequency spectrum, and the velocity-transfer time-frequency spectrum observed by each BS from echo signals. We collectively refer to these three types of time-frequency spectrum as the multi-scale feature of the LAT. Next, we design a multi-BS and multi-scale feature fusion enabled LAT recognition network with Swin Transformer, which employs the visualized images of multi-scale feature to jointly recognize the target through deep feature extraction, intra-BS feature interaction, inter-BS feature interaction, and target recognition output. We generate a massive echo signal dataset comprising 1,440,000 samples for LAT recognition within ISAC network. This dataset can serve as a public benchmark to evaluate our proposed scheme and facilitate future research. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed scheme realizes high recognition accuracy and robust unseen-subtype generalization, confirming the effectiveness of multi-scale feature fusion and the additional gains brought by multi-BS collaboration. The project page is available at: https://alivn999.github.io/COSMOS-Networked-ISAC-Enabl ed-Target-Recognition-Towards-Low-Altitude-Economy/.

AI.tldr: A low-altitude target recognition scheme using multi-BS collaboration and multi-scale feature fusion with Swin Transformer for ISAC networks, achieving high accuracy and robust generalization.
AI.motivation: To address the challenge of recognizing low-altitude targets (UAVs, birds, vehicles, pedestrians) in ISAC networks by leveraging multi-base-station collaboration and multi-scale time-frequency features for improved accuracy and robustness.
AI.method: Formulates motion/echo models for four target types; extracts three types of time-frequency spectra (velocity-resolution-preferred, time-resolution-preferred, velocity-transfer) as multi-scale features; designs a Swin Transformer-based recognition network with intra-BS and inter-BS feature interaction; generates a large-scale dataset (1.44M samples).
AI.result: High recognition accuracy and robust unseen-subtype generalization; multi-scale feature fusion and multi-BS collaboration provide additional performance gains.
AI.conclusion: The proposed scheme effectively integrates multi-BS collaboration and multi-scale features for reliable LAT recognition in ISAC networks, with a public benchmark dataset released for future research.

title: Plug-and-Play Reweighting for Resilient Collaborative Decision-Making in Connected Autonomous Driving
comment: 8 pages, 4 figures, 2 tables. Accepted to the 2026 IEEE/RSJ International Conference on Intelligent Robots and Systems (IROS 2026)
summary: Collaborative decision-making is a fundamental capability in multi-robot systems, such as connected autonomous vehicles. However, perceptual noise and adversarial attacks in collaborators can severely affect decision reliability. Overall, existing methods typically rely on retraining with attack-specific defenses or on restrictive perturbation assumptions to improve resilience, which limits their practicality. In this paper, we propose a novel Resilient Collaborative Decision-Making (RCDM) framework that consists of an attention-based encoder for extracting individual robot perceptual embeddings and an attention-based decoder for fusing collaborator perceptions and making decisions. To improve resilience to corrupted observations, we design a novel plug-and-play reweighting module that down-weights the influence of corrupted inputs by analyzing the consistency of neighborhood points relative to the local structure and assigning smaller weights to points that deviate strongly from the local median. This module can be seamlessly integrated into attention-based collaborative decision-making without requiring additional training. We evaluate our method in high-fidelity simulations, considering perceptual noise and five types of attacks across diverse accident-prone scenarios. Experimental results demonstrate that our approach consistently outperforms existing methods by up to 26% and achieves state-of-the-art resilient performance.

AI.tldr: Proposes RCDM, a plug-and-play reweighting module for attention-based multi-robot decision-making that improves resilience to corrupted observations by down-weighting outliers based on local structural consistency, achieving SOTA performance without retraining.
AI.motivation: Existing collaborative decision-making methods rely on retraining with attack-specific defenses or restrictive perturbation assumptions, limiting practicality in real-world multi-robot systems facing perceptual noise and adversarial attacks.
AI.method: RCDM framework: (1) attention-based encoder for individual perceptual embeddings, (2) attention-based decoder for collaborative fusion, (3) a plug-and-play reweighting module that down-weights corrupted inputs by analyzing neighborhood point consistency relative to local structure (deviation from local median).
AI.result: Outperforms existing methods by up to 26% across perceptual noise and five attack types in high-fidelity accident-prone simulations, achieving state-of-the-art resilient performance.
AI.conclusion: The proposed plug-and-play reweighting module effectively enhances resilience to corrupted observations in collaborative decision-making without needing additional training, as demonstrated across diverse attack scenarios.

title: Discovering Latent Response Laws in Forced Physical Systems
comment: 23 pages, 5 figures and 2 tables. Supplementary Information provided as an ancillary file
summary: Governing equations provide compact descriptions of physical systems, yet the variables in which they are simple are often hidden in high-dimensional measurements. This challenge is sharper for forced systems, whose responses depend on both intrinsic dynamics and time-dependent inputs. Here we introduce FLARE, a forced latent autoencoder for response equations that learns compact response coordinates, identifies sparse input-dependent latent dynamics and decodes equation rollouts to full responses. By estimating latent dimension from data and separating state estimation from external forcing, FLARE enables forecasts to be initialized from past responses and driven by prescribed future inputs. Across known dynamical systems, application-scale forced responses and visual observations, FLARE recovers compact forced dynamics and predicts long-horizon high-dimensional responses under inputs not used for training. By turning learned coordinates into a dynamical interface, FLARE extends equation discovery to systems whose effective states are hidden within complex observations, providing a route for interpretable modelling and prediction of high-dimensional responses in forced dynamical systems.

AI.tldr: FLARE is a forced latent autoencoder that discovers compact governing equations from high-dimensional measurements of forced dynamical systems, enabling prediction of responses under unseen inputs.
AI.motivation: Governing equations for physical systems often involve hidden variables in high-dimensional measurements, and this challenge is amplified for forced systems where responses depend on both intrinsic dynamics and time-dependent inputs.
AI.method: FLARE uses a forced latent autoencoder to learn compact response coordinates, identify sparse input-dependent latent dynamics, and decode equation rollouts back to full responses. It estimates latent dimension from data and separates state estimation from external forcing.
AI.result: Across known dynamical systems, application-scale forced responses, and visual observations, FLARE recovers compact forced dynamics and predicts long-horizon high-dimensional responses under inputs not used during training.
AI.conclusion: FLARE extends equation discovery to systems whose effective states are hidden within complex observations, providing a route for interpretable modeling and prediction of high-dimensional responses in forced dynamical systems.

title: Bridging the Catalog-to-Real Gap: Scalable Product Recognition via Multi-Stage Contrastive Learning
summary: Automated product recognition is a cornerstone of modern retail intelligence; however, accurately matching real-world, in-store images against extensive corporate catalogs remains a major scalability bottleneck for large-scale applications. In this work, we address this challenge by reformulating the task as an embedding-based cross-domain retrieval problem rather than a standard closed-set classification task. Specifically, we define the objective as retrieving the most corresponding catalog reference image for a given real-world product query crop from an expansive inventory. To bridge the severe domain gap between pristine studio packshots and noisy in-store queries, we introduce a novel catalog-to-real multi-stage contrastive learning paradigm (Cat2Real). This framework fine-tunes a vision backbone by systematically exploiting both item-level and image-level similarities to drive targeted hard negative mining. Extensive empirical evaluations demonstrate that our paradigm scales seamlessly to unseen products and categories, yielding outstanding zero-shot generalization performance even in the complete absence of real-world training images for novel inventory.

AI.tldr: Cat2Real is a multi-stage contrastive learning framework that reformulates product recognition as an embedding-based cross-domain retrieval task, enabling zero-shot generalization from studio catalog images to real-world in-store photos without requiring real-world training data for new products.
AI.motivation: The scalability bottleneck of matching in-store images against large corporate catalogs in retail intelligence, due to the severe domain gap between pristine studio packshots and noisy real-world photos.
AI.method: A novel catalog-to-real (Cat2Real) multi-stage contrastive learning paradigm that fine-tunes a vision backbone using item-level and image-level similarities with targeted hard negative mining to bridge the domain gap.
AI.result: The paradigm scales seamlessly to unseen products and categories with outstanding zero-shot generalization performance, even without real-world training images for novel inventory.
AI.conclusion: Reformulating product recognition as an embedding-based cross-domain retrieval task with Cat2Real effectively overcomes scalability bottlenecks in large-scale retail applications.

title: Verification of Adaptive Agentic Controllers through Finite Rule Revision
summary: Industrial agentic AI systems increasingly exhibit a gap between prototype capability and production deployment. In particular, adaptive agents may generate plausible outputs while remaining difficult to verify under non-determinism, confidentiality constraints, limited context, and weak observability. This paper formulates a bounded verification protocol for adaptive agentic controllers represented by finite symbolic rules, explicit diagnostic predicates, explanation logs, and held-out re-evaluation. The central research question is: when an adaptive agentic controller is represented through finite rules, explicit diagnostic predicates, explanation logs, and held-out re-evaluation, which classes of controller failure can be detected, locally repaired, or rejected without relying on unrestricted human-in-the-loop judgment? The proposed framework treats the controller as a finite revisable object. Diagnostic failures are mapped to predefined rule-level edits, including rule addition, rule deletion, and priority revision. Repaired controllers are then evaluated on held-out simulation seeds or cloned initial states. Experiments in a stylized financially constrained inventory-control benchmark show three outcomes: resource-induced failures that remain non-repairable by one rule edit, partial repairs that are rejected because they violate thresholds or guardrails, and a local one-step repair of an order-volatility failure induced by removing a smoothing rule. The contribution is methodological and provides a simulation-compatible procedure for testing whether specific controller-level failures can be made observable, explainable, locally revisable, and empirically re-tested under controlled conditions.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a bounded verification protocol for adaptive agentic controllers that uses finite symbolic rules, diagnostic predicates, explanation logs, and held-out re-evaluation to detect, locally repair, or reject controller failures without unrestricted human oversight, validated on an inventory-control benchmark.
AI.motivation: Industrial agentic AI systems suffer a gap between prototype capability and production deployment due to issues like non-determinism, confidentiality constraints, limited context, and weak observability, making verification difficult despite plausible outputs.
AI.method: The framework treats the controller as a finite revisable object, maps diagnostic failures to predefined rule-level edits (addition, deletion, priority revision), and evaluates repaired controllers on held-out simulation seeds or cloned initial states.
AI.result: Experiments show three outcomes: non-repairable resource-induced failures, partial repairs rejected by thresholds/guardrails, and a successful one-step repair of an order-volatility failure via smoothing rule removal.
AI.conclusion: The contribution is a simulation-compatible procedure for testing whether controller-level failures are observable, explainable, locally revisable, and empirically re-testable under controlled conditions.

title: Non-Reciprocal Dynamic Metasurface Antenna: Practical Multiport-Network Modeling and Optimization for Multi-User Interference Resilience
summary: Channel reciprocity fundamentally limits full-duplex (FD) base stations due to multi-user co-channel interference. We examine the potential of deploying a non-reciprocal dynamic metasurface antenna (NR-DMA) at the base station to overcome this limitation. Our NR-DMA architecture connects a single circulator to three feed ports of a multi-feed DMA with strong mutual coupling (MC) between its seven feeds and 96 1-bit-programmable meta-elements. We model our system with multiport network theory, using experimentally estimated proxy parameters of a fabricated 19-GHz DMA and the measured circulator response. Our NR-DMA's reconfigurability is captured by a diagonal tunable scattering matrix, showing that non-reciprocal DMAs and RISs need not require a "beyond-diagonal" tunable scattering matrix. We jointly optimize the DMA state, analog feed weights, circulator-port assignment, and circulation direction. Our optimized NR-DMA realizes distinct forward and reverse channel responses. In our interference-limited high-SNR case study, the NR-DMA improves the FD sum rate by about 60% over a reciprocal DMA benchmark. Comparisons with proxy objectives and MC-unaware optimization show that end-to-end FD optimization and MC-aware modeling are both essential.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a non-reciprocal dynamic metasurface antenna (NR-DMA) for full-duplex base stations, using a circulator with a multi-feed DMA and strong mutual coupling. The optimized NR-DMA achieves distinct forward/reverse channels, improving FD sum rate by ~60% over reciprocal DMA benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Channel reciprocity limits full-duplex base stations due to multi-user co-channel interference. The authors aim to overcome this fundamental limitation by deploying a non-reciprocal dynamic metasurface antenna.
AI.method: The NR-DMA connects a single circulator to three feed ports of a multi-feed DMA with 7 feeds and 96 1-bit-programmable meta-elements. The system is modeled using multiport network theory with experimentally estimated parameters. Joint optimization of DMA state, analog feed weights, circulator-port assignment, and circulation direction is performed.
AI.result: The optimized NR-DMA realizes distinct forward and reverse channel responses. In interference-limited high-SNR scenarios, the NR-DMA improves FD sum rate by about 60% over a reciprocal DMA benchmark. End-to-end FD optimization and MC-aware modeling are both essential.
AI.conclusion: Non-reciprocal DMAs/RISs can achieve non-reciprocal behavior using a diagonal tunable scattering matrix (no "beyond-diagonal" matrix required). MC-aware and end-to-end optimization are critical for realizing the full potential of NR-DMAs in full-duplex systems.

title: Direct Rotor Thrust Sensing and Feedback Control for Disturbance Rejection of Multirotors Using Load-cells
summary:
Gust disturbances, dynamic vertical inflow and ground effect are key adverse aerodynamic phenomena that induce variations in the forces acting on a multirotor and complicate its flight control. Miniature rotorcraft typically rely on simplified modelling of such effects to compute adjustments in thrust to counteract these forces. In the most basic case, disturbance force estimations are derived from the aircraft's motion and the generated thrust is assumed to exactly match that requested by the controller. However, such systems rely on the aircraft's trajectory to be affected before disturbances can be sensed and compensated. Numerous approaches presented over the last 15-20 years aim to reject external disturbances more quickly, but challenges remain.
  This paper presents a new approach in this category by measuring the instantaneous force of the rotors directly at the point of generation using load-cells and implementing high-speed control to accurately track the desired thrust. Measurements from load-cells were previously considered too noisy to provide meaningful input, but the experiments presented in the paper using purpose-built hardware from low-cost commodity components in single- and dual rotor see-saw models and a flying aircraft demonstrate both the feasibility and the effectiveness of the approach in the presence of complex aerodynamic phenomena.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a new approach for multirotor disturbance rejection by mounting load-cells directly at rotors to measure instantaneous force, enabling high-speed thrust control despite previous beliefs that such measurements were too noisy. Experiments on single/dual rotor testbeds and a flying aircraft demonstrate feasibility.
AI.motivation: Gust disturbances, dynamic vertical inflow, and ground effect cause force variations on multirotors that complicate flight control. Existing disturbance rejection methods rely on sensing disturbances through trajectory changes, which introduces delays.
AI.method: Direct measurement of instantaneous rotor force using load-cells mounted at the point of thrust generation, coupled with high-speed control to accurately track desired thrust.
AI.result: Experiments with purpose-built hardware from low-cost components on single-rotor, dual-rotor see-saw models, and a flying aircraft demonstrate feasibility and effectiveness against complex aerodynamic disturbances.
AI.conclusion: Load-cell-based direct force sensing, previously considered too noisy, is both feasible and effective for improving multirotor disturbance rejection.

title: Quota Marketplace: Dynamic Pricing for Efficient Allocation of ML Training Resources
summary:
The escalating demand for Machine Learning (ML) training resources in recent years has resulted in a substantial gap between the high demand and the available supply. Efficient allocation of these scarce and expensive resources is crucial for organizations to maximize their return on investment. Existing resource allocation mechanisms, like Karma [OSDI'23], are designed to guarantee Pareto efficiency and max-min fairness in settings with dynamic (time-varying) user demands, but fail to preserve these key properties in the presence of demands with heterogeneous values. Given the ubiquity and inevitability of heterogeneity in organizational values of different workloads, effective resource allocation policies must accommodate these variations.
  In this paper, we describe the design, implementation, deployment, and theoretical analysis of Quota Marketplace, a market-based mechanism to efficiently allocate ML training chips (like GPUs), explicitly addressing scenarios with demands of heterogeneous value. We detail the implementation of this mechanism within Google and present metrics that demonstrate its impact. We also discuss many business-critical requirements that the Quota Marketplace handles quite effectively, and document the gains and opportunities it has unlocked. We establish theoretically how this market-based approach achieves the essential properties of Pareto efficiency and max-min fairness by allowing the users to express the value of their workloads and enabling dynamic resource pricing based on supply and demand fluctuations. Ultimately, the market facilitates resource allocation that aligns with organizational priorities.

AI.tldr: Quota Marketplace is a market-based mechanism deployed at Google for efficiently allocating ML training chips (GPUs) to handle heterogeneous workload values, achieving Pareto efficiency and max-min fairness through dynamic pricing while addressing real-world business requirements.
AI.motivation: Existing resource allocation mechanisms (e.g., Karma) guarantee Pareto efficiency and max-min fairness for dynamic demands but fail when workloads have heterogeneous values. Given the growing scarcity of ML training resources and the inevitability of value heterogeneity across organizational workloads, new allocation policies are needed.
AI.method: The paper describes Quota Marketplace, a market-based mechanism that allows users to express the value of their workloads and enables dynamic resource pricing based on supply and demand fluctuations. The approach is theoretically analyzed, implemented within Google's infrastructure, and evaluated with production metrics.
AI.result: The mechanism was successfully deployed at Google, demonstrating impact through production metrics, handling business-critical requirements effectively, and unlocking new gains and opportunities for resource allocation.
AI.conclusion: The market-based approach achieves Pareto efficiency and max-min fairness in heterogeneous value settings by letting users express workload value and using dynamic pricing, ultimately aligning resource allocation with organizational priorities.

title: Do Transformer Temporal Heads and Post-Pooling Motion Gates Help CorrNet-based CSLR? An Empirical Study
summary: CorrNet is a strong baseline for continuous sign language recognition (CSLR) because it models inter-frame correlations inside the visual encoding stage. In this paper, we study two natural extensions of a reproduced CorrNet system: replacing the BiLSTM temporal head with a Transformer encoder, and injecting motion cues after temporal pooling. We find that the Transformer head does not outperform the BiLSTM baseline, even with a training strategy adjusted for the Transformer, and the two heads have almost the same computational and runtime cost. For the second extension, we design a lightweight module called MotionGate. In our experiments, MotionGate consistently collapses to an identity-like mapping: the gate loses motion selectivity, and the injected residual becomes a weak, non-selective perturbation of the pooled features. These results suggest that explicit motion injection after CorrNet's correlation-based encoding is largely redundant, and that natural-looking architectural extensions in CSLR should be tested carefully instead of being assumed to help.

AI.tldr: This paper tests two natural extensions to CorrNet for continuous sign language recognition: replacing BiLSTM with Transformer (which doesn't improve performance) and injecting motion cues via MotionGate (which collapses to identity mapping). Results show these intuitive extensions fail, highlighting the need for careful testing rather than assuming architectural changes will help.
AI.motivation: To investigate whether two seemingly natural architectural extensions to CorrNet (Transformer head and motion injection) actually improve CSLR performance, or if they are redundant given CorrNet's existing correlation-based encoding.
AI.method: The authors (1) replace the BiLSTM temporal head with a Transformer encoder with adjusted training strategy, and (2) design a lightweight motion injection module called MotionGate to inject motion cues after temporal pooling.
AI.result: Transformer head does not outperform BiLSTM with similar computational cost; MotionGate collapses to identity mapping, losing motion selectivity and producing weak non-selective perturbations.
AI.conclusion: Explicit motion injection after CorrNet's correlation-based encoding is largely redundant; natural-looking architectural extensions should be carefully tested rather than assumed beneficial in CSLR.

title: EvoCUA-1.5: Online Reinforcement Learning for Multi-turn Computer-Use Agents
summary: Computer-use agents must solve long-horizon tasks through repeated interaction with partially observable, multimodal desktop environments. Although imitation learning and offline trajectory refinement provide strong priors, static traces cannot cover the causal feedback loop of real computer use: each action changes the screen state, future action space, and recovery options. EvoCUA-1.5 extends self-evolving computer-use agents from offline experience learning to online reinforcement learning, where policies interact with executable sandbox environments and improve from verifiable task outcomes. Online RL in this setting requires more than directly reusing single-turn language-RL recipes. Multi-turn interaction introduces context-managed observations, sparse terminal rewards, variable-length trajectories, and slow environment feedback. EvoCUA-1.5 addresses these challenges with Step-Level Policy Optimization (STEPO), which preserves trajectory-level advantage balance after decomposition into step-level samples; policy-aware filtering and pass-rate calibration over verifiable synthesized tasks; Dynamic Tri-Adaptive Curriculum (DTAC), which combines learnable tasks, difficult positive replay, and controlled infeasible-task exposure; and a fully asynchronous RL infrastructure with staleness control and mini-group batching. Experiments show that these components improve training stability and downstream performance. EvoCUA-1.5 achieves 63.2\% success on OSWorld-Verified, outperforming comparable 32B/35B-scale open-weight baselines and even approaching models with significantly larger parameter counts. Overall, EvoCUA-1.5 provides a practical framework for scaling online RL in multi-turn computer-use agents.

AI.tldr: EvoCUA-1.5 extends computer-use agents from offline learning to online RL, introducing Step-Level Policy Optimization (STEPO), policy-aware filtering, Dynamic Tri-Adaptive Curriculum (DTAC), and an asynchronous RL infrastructure to handle multi-turn interaction challenges, achieving 63.2% success on OSWorld-Verified, outperforming comparable open-weight baselines.
AI.motivation: Static traces from imitation learning cannot cover the causal feedback loop of real computer use (actions change screen state, future action space, and recovery options), motivating the need for online RL that learns from verifiable task outcomes through interactive sandbox environments.
AI.method: EvoCUA-1.5 proposes: (1) Step-Level Policy Optimization (STEPO) for trajectory-level advantage balance from step-level samples; (2) policy-aware filtering and pass-rate calibration over verifiable synthesized tasks; (3) Dynamic Tri-Adaptive Curriculum (DTAC) combining learnable tasks, difficult positive replay, and controlled infeasible-task exposure; (4) fully asynchronous RL infrastructure with staleness control and mini-group batching.
AI.result: EvoCUA-1.5 achieves 63.2% success on OSWorld-Verified, outperforming comparable 32B/35B-scale open-weight baselines and approaching models with significantly larger parameter counts.
AI.conclusion: The proposed components improve training stability and downstream performance, providing a practical framework for scaling online RL in multi-turn computer-use agents.

title: SCOPE: Sidelobe-Controlled Off-grid Profile Estimation for Multiband Multistatic Target Localization in Upper Mid-Band ISAC Systems
comment: Accepted for presentation at the 2026 IEEE International Workshop on Signal Processing Advances in Wireless Communications (SPAWC)
summary: Multiband multistatic integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) in fragmented FR3 bands (7-24 GHz) enables high resolution localization via virtual wideband and spatial diversity. However, frequency anisotropy decorrelates target scattering across non-contiguous bands, while large inter-band frequency gaps generate severe grating lobes that create persistent ghost peaks. We propose sidelobe-controlled off-grid profile estimation (SCOPE), a robust localization algorithm that exploits multi-view consistency across distributed receivers and frequency bands to suppress grating-lobe ambiguities. At the transmitter, an iterative minimax precoder suppresses out-of-region sidelobes to reduce false peaks in the coarse likelihood map. At the receiver, SCOPE employs profile likelihood with Top-K inhibition-based peak selection to avoid trapping in ghost basins, followed by derivative-free off-grid refinement. Simulations demonstrate that SCOPE achieves sub-meter localization with 90% probability at -5 dB SNR and 3 mm root mean square error (RMSE) at 25 dB SNR.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes SCOPE, a robust localization algorithm for multiband multistatic ISAC in fragmented FR3 bands, which suppresses grating-lobe ambiguities via iterative minimax precoding and profile likelihood with Top-K inhibition, achieving sub-meter localization with 3 mm RMSE at high SNR.
AI.motivation: Fragmented FR3 bands (7-24 GHz) enable high-resolution localization through virtual wideband and spatial diversity, but suffer from frequency anisotropy decorrelation and severe grating lobes from large inter-band gaps, creating persistent ghost peaks that hinder accurate localization.
AI.method: SCOPE combines (1) an iterative minimax precoder at the transmitter to suppress out-of-region sidelobes in the coarse likelihood map, and (2) profile likelihood with Top-K inhibition-based peak selection at the receiver to avoid ghost basins, followed by derivative-free off-grid refinement.
AI.result: Simulations show sub-meter localization with 90% probability at -5 dB SNR, and 3 mm RMSE at 25 dB SNR.
AI.conclusion: Multi-view consistency across distributed receivers and frequency bands effectively suppresses grating-lobe ambiguities, enabling robust high-precision localization in fragmented FR3 bands.

title: TAC-LOCO: Unified Whole-Body Control for Quadrupedal TACtile-Informed LOCO-Manipulation
summary: Dynamic loco-manipulation requires legged robots to coordinate whole-body motion while maintaining stable physical interaction with grasped objects under uncertain external forces. While tactile sensing has been widely studied for robotic manipulation, its role in dynamic whole-body control remains largely unexplored. Existing works without tactile feedback commonly grasp firmly rather than regulate the grasp according to the interaction. We propose TAC-LOCO, a tactile-augmented unified reinforcement learning framework that encodes tactile array observations from compliant grippers into a compact latent representation and joins it with proprioception for unified control of the legs, arm, and gripper. With effective grasp stability reward design, the policy learns to simultaneously track body velocity and end-effector trajectories, moderate grasp force, and prevent object slip under both gradual load changes and sudden release events. We deploy the policy zero-shot on a Unitree Go2 with an Interbotix WidowX 250 arm and tactile gripper, demonstrating dynamic tactile-informed loco-manipulation under varying external interactions, achieving a 47% reduction in grasping force and an object drop rate of less than 1%.

AI.tldr: TAC-LOCO is a tactile-augmented RL framework for dynamic loco-manipulation that uses tactile sensing from compliant grippers to enable whole-body control, achieving 47% less grasping force and <1% drop rate on a real robot.
AI.motivation: Tactile sensing is widely used in manipulation but under-explored for dynamic whole-body loco-manipulation; existing methods grasp firmly without regulating grip based on interaction forces.
AI.method: A unified RL framework that encodes tactile array observations into a latent representation, joins it with proprioception, and learns unified control of legs, arm, and gripper with a grasp stability reward.
AI.result: Zero-shot deployment on Unitree Go2 with WidowX 250 arm and tactile gripper shows 47% reduction in grasping force and <1% object drop rate under varying external interactions.
AI.conclusion: Tactile feedback enables more adaptive, force-efficient grasping during dynamic loco-manipulation, reducing unnecessary grip force while maintaining stability.

title: Spectral Origins of the Self-Correction Blind Spot in Autoregressive Generation
summary: Large autoregressive language models exhibit a self-correction blind spot: they reliably fix identical errors when attributed to an external source yet fail to fix the same errors in their own outputs. Prior work has documented this phenomenon empirically, through controlled error injection, error-depth decompositions, RL-based verifier-corrector training, and intrinsic self-verification, but offers no formal model of why generating a token suppresses the ability to detect its error, no quantitative activation condition for correction markers, and no convergence guarantee for reinforcement-learning-based self-correction. We close these gaps with SPARC, a spectral-algebraic theory of self-correction in autoregressive generation. We define the error-propagation operator as the product of per-step attention Jacobians on the residual stream and prove that the blind spot arises if and only if the spectral radius of this operator is at least one. We derive a sharp activation threshold, given as a function of the spectral radius, that a correction marker must exceed, recovering the 89.3\% blind-spot reduction observed with a simple ``Wait'' marker. We further prove that RL-based verifier-corrector training converges at a rate proportional to the squared coupling strength over the square root of the number of samples if and only if the verifier-corrector coupling matrix has spectral norm below one, and that this criterion is invariant across residual-stream autoregressive modalities, unifying text LLMs and autoregressive image and video generation. Experiments across four backbones and a visual autoregressive probe validate every theorem, with spectral predictions matching measured blind-spot rates within 3.2\% RMSE.

AI.tldr: SPARC proposes a spectral-algebraic theory explaining why large autoregressive models fail to self-correct their own errors (the "blind spot"), deriving mathematical conditions for when and how self-correction works, with experiments validating predictions within 3.2% RMSE.
AI.motivation: Prior work documented the self-correction blind spot empirically but lacked a formal mathematical model for why generating a token suppresses error detection, what activation threshold correction markers must meet, and whether RL-based self-correction converges reliably.
AI.method: Defines an error-propagation operator as the product of per-step attention Jacobians on the residual stream, derives spectral-radius condition for blind spot existence, computes sharp activation threshold for correction markers, and proves convergence rate for RL-based verifier-corrector training based on spectral norm of the coupling matrix.
AI.result: Spectral predictions match measured blind-spot rates within 3.2% RMSE across four LLM backbones and a visual autoregressive probe; reproduces the 89.3% blind-spot reduction of the "Wait" marker; proves convergence criterion invariant across text, image, and video modalities.
AI.conclusion: The self-correction blind spot in autoregressive models is fundamentally a spectral phenomenon, yielding precise mathematical conditions for correction efficacy and convergence guarantees, unifying self-correction theory across autoregressive modalities.

title: Banshee: Target Switch Attacks on Gimbal-Stabilized Visual Tracking Systems via Acoustic Injection
comment: Accepted by the 47th IEEE Symposium on Security and Privacy (IEEE S&P 2026). 20 pages. Artifact: https://github.com/U1ltra/Banshee
summary: Gimbal-stabilized visual tracking is critical for modern autonomous systems such as Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs). While prior work shows acoustic signals can disturb gimbal internals, the impact of such attacks on real-world applications like UAV tracking and following remains underexplored. Existing demonstrations largely overlook practical challenges for real-world attacks, such as object-motion uncertainty and runtime latency. To bridge this gap, we present Banshee, the first physically realizable attack that induces target switching in UAV visual tracking systems by exploiting acoustic vulnerabilities in gimbal-camera systems. Banshee generates carefully crafted acoustic waveforms that induce optimized adversarial gimbal oscillations, causing directionally biased camera-view drifts that break inter-frame target associations. Consequently, the onboard tracker is driven to switch from the original target to an attacker-selected object with high probability, with occasional target loss. Banshee achieves a 93.6% success rate in simulation across two commercial gimbal systems and five trackers. Real-world benchtop and in-flight black-box attacks against a commercial drone across varied scenarios show an overall 95.5% attack success rate. Our results reveal a practical cross-domain vulnerability between acoustics and vision, highlighting the need for robust designs of gimbal systems and applications. Our code is available at: https://github.com/U1ltra/Banshee.

AI.tldr: Banshee is the first physically realizable acoustic attack that induces target switching in UAV visual tracking systems by exploiting gimbal-camera vulnerabilities, achieving 93.6% simulation and 95.5% real-world attack success rates.
AI.motivation: Prior work showed acoustic signals can disturb gimbal internals, but the impact on real-world UAV tracking applications (e.g., object-motion uncertainty, runtime latency) remains underexplored.
AI.method: Banshee generates crafted acoustic waveforms that induce optimized adversarial gimbal oscillations, causing biased camera-view drifts that break inter-frame target associations, forcing the tracker to switch to an attacker-selected object.
AI.result: 93.6% success rate in simulation across 2 commercial gimbal systems and 5 trackers; 95.5% success rate in real-world benchtop and in-flight black-box attacks against a commercial drone.
AI.conclusion: Reveals a practical cross-domain vulnerability between acoustics and vision, highlighting the need for robust gimbal system designs.

title: From Patterns to Maze Structures: SMT-Based Path Synthesis and 2D/3D Construction
summary: We present a pipeline for constructing maze structures from input patterns such as text or shapes. The central path-synthesis problem is encoded in Satisfiability Modulo Theories as global constraints on adjacency, continuity, and pattern-constrained coverage, allowing each fixed-bound instance to be solved in one call. The resulting path is either a planar, self-avoiding route or a layered traversal with prescribed over--under crossings, and it serves as a scaffold for constructing planar mazes and three-dimensional realizations of woven mazes. This report extends the published Bridges 2026 conference paper with more representative SMT-LIB examples and a fuller account of how synthesized paths become concrete maze constructions in planar and three-dimensional form.

AI.tldr: A pipeline using SMT (Satisfiability Modulo Theories) to synthesize maze paths from input patterns, enabling planar and 3D woven maze constructions from text or shapes.
AI.motivation: To generate maze structures from arbitrary input patterns (text/shapes) by encoding path-synthesis as a constraint satisfaction problem solvable in one call.
AI.method: Encodes path-synthesis as SMT constraints (adjacency, continuity, pattern-constrained coverage), solving fixed-bound instances in one call to produce planar or layered paths with over-under crossings.
AI.result: Able to produce planar self-avoiding routes or layered woven traversals from input patterns, serving as scaffolds for 2D and 3D maze constructions.
AI.conclusion: The SMT-based approach generalizes maze construction from arbitrary patterns, supporting both planar and 3D woven forms, with extended SMT-LIB examples provided.

title: Design and Experimental Validation of a Multiband Cross-Polarization Conversion (CPC) Metasurface for Radar Cross Section (RCS) Reduction
summary: Radar cross-section (RCS) reduction is a fundamental requirement in modern stealth technology, playing a critical role in the low-observable performance of aerial and naval platforms. Among the various passive RCS reduction strategies, including radar-absorbing materials, absorptive coatings, and artificially engineered surfaces, metasurface-based cross-polarization conversion has emerged as a compelling approach owing to its structural simplicity and low profile. In this work, a single-layer cross-polarization conversion (CPC) metasurface developed on a cost-effective FR4 dielectric substrate (relative permittivity 4.4, loss tangent 0.02) is proposed for multiband RCS reduction. The designed structure achieves a polarization conversion ratio (PCR) exceeding 95% at three distinct operating frequencies of 7.8 GHz, 11.7 GHz, and 18 GHz, spanning the C-, X-, and Ku-bands, which directly translates into a monostatic RCS reduction exceeding 10 dBsm at the corresponding bands. The metasurface further demonstrates stable polarization conversion performance under oblique incidence up to 60 degrees, confirming its suitability for wide-angle illumination conditions encountered in practical deployment scenarios. Experimental validation conducted in an anechoic chamber confirms close agreement with full-wave electromagnetic simulations, substantiating the reliability of the fabricated prototype. The proposed design offers a lightweight, low-cost, and high-performance candidate for multiband stealth and low-observable platform applications.

AI.tldr: A single-layer cross-polarization conversion metasurface on FR4 substrate achieves >95% PCR and >10 dBsm RCS reduction at three bands (7.8/11.7/18 GHz), with stable performance up to 60° oblique incidence, validated experimentally.
AI.motivation: To develop a lightweight, low-cost, and high-performance metasurface for multiband RCS reduction in stealth technology, addressing the need for simple structures with wide-angle stability.
AI.method: A single-layer cross-polarization conversion (CPC) metasurface was designed on cost-effective FR4 dielectric substrate, achieving polarization conversion via engineered surface patterning. Performance was evaluated via full-wave EM simulations and validated by anechoic chamber measurements.
AI.result: The metasurface achieves >95% PCR at 7.8, 11.7, and 18 GHz (C, X, Ku bands), corresponding to >10 dBsm monostatic RCS reduction. Stable performance is maintained under oblique incidence up to 60°, with close agreement between simulation and experiment.
AI.conclusion: The proposed CPC metasurface is a lightweight, low-cost, high-performance candidate for multiband stealth and low-observable platform applications, validated both numerically and experimentally.

title: Millimeter Wave Radar: From Synthetic Aperture to Probabilistic Mapping
summary: Robust probabilistic mapping is essential for autonomous robotic systems operating in challenging environments. While traditional sensors fail in adverse conditions such as smoke and fog, millimeter wave (mmWave) radar sensors offer reliable sensing in such conditions. However, creating accurate probabilistic maps from radar data presents significant challenges due to the inherently sparse and noisy characteristics of radio wave measurements and signal processing steps. In an attempt to address these issues, we establish a complete pipeline from raw radar signals to probabilistic occupancy maps, incorporating Synthetic Aperture Radar processing followed by a probabilistic modeling step. We conduct extensive validation across indoor environments, comparing our approach against different signal processing and probabilistic modeling approaches. We also evaluate mapping quality through downstream path planning performance analysis. Furthermore, we investigate the impact of key parameters and antenna array configuration on mapping performance. The experimental results demonstrate both the effectiveness and limitations of SAR-based probabilistic mapping for real-world robotic deployment. To facilitate future research and broader adoption, we contribute an open-source cascaded mmWave radar dataset with an accompanying GPU-accelerated signal processing pipeline available at https://github.com/rpl-cmu/rpm.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a complete pipeline for creating probabilistic occupancy maps from mmWave radar data using Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) processing and probabilistic modeling, validated across indoor environments with downstream path planning analysis.
AI.motivation: Robust probabilistic mapping is essential for autonomous robots in challenging environments where traditional sensors fail (e.g., smoke, fog). mmWave radar works in such conditions, but creating accurate probabilistic maps from radar data is difficult due to its sparse and noisy characteristics.
AI.method: The authors establish a complete pipeline from raw radar signals to probabilistic occupancy maps, combining SAR processing with probabilistic modeling, and evaluate against different signal processing and probabilistic modeling approaches.
AI.result: Extensive indoor validation shows both effectiveness and limitations of SAR-based probabilistic mapping. Key parameters and antenna array configurations impact mapping performance, and downstream path planning analysis confirms mapping quality.
AI.conclusion: SAR-based probabilistic mapping is viable for real-world robotic deployment in adverse conditions, though limitations remain. The authors contribute an open-source dataset and GPU-accelerated signal processing pipeline for future research.

title: RUBRIC: Realism--Utility Balanced Ranking for Imbalanced Classification
summary:
Class imbalance poses a fundamental challenge in risk-sensitive applications such as fraud detection and medical diagnosis, where minority-class samples are scarce yet critical for accurate classification. Existing oversampling methods generate synthetic samples to rebalance class distributions; however, they often produce large numbers of low-quality candidates that distort decision boundaries or introduce artifacts, leading to overfitting and degraded generalization.
  In this work, we introduce RUBRIC, a generator-agnostic filtering framework that formulates synthetic sample selection as a quality-over-quantity optimization problem. RUBRIC ranks candidates using a realism-utility trade-off: realism is quantified by a learned discriminator that distinguishes real samples from synthetic samples, while utility captures proximity to the decision boundary through a concave margin-based scoring function. We show that, under mild regularity conditions, the proposed filtering strategy monotonically tightens the generalization bound for margin-based classifiers by jointly reducing distribution shift and suppressing near-negative tail contributions.
  Through extensive experiments on credit-card fraud detection and other imbalanced benchmarks, we demonstrate that RUBRIC improves F1-macro and recall while maintaining comparable ROC-AUC across several generators. We also provide explicit lambda-sensitivity analysis to show how users can recover AUPRC when ranking quality is prioritized.

AI.tldr: RUBRIC is a filtering framework for selecting high-quality synthetic samples in imbalanced learning, improving generalization by optimizing a realism-utility trade-off and showing consistent gains on fraud detection benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Class imbalance hurts risk-sensitive applications; existing oversampling methods generate many low-quality synthetic samples that distort decision boundaries and cause overfitting.
AI.method: RUBRIC is a generator-agnostic filtering framework that selects synthetic samples via a realism-utility trade-off: realism via a discriminator distinguishing real vs. synthetic, utility via a concave margin-based scoring function. The filtering strategy tightens generalization bounds theoretically.
AI.result: RUBRIC improves F1-macro and recall while maintaining comparable ROC-AUC across multiple generators on credit-card fraud detection and other imbalanced benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Quality-over-quantity filtering with realism-utility trade-off effectively mitigates the pitfalls of oversampling, and the framework provides controllable lambda-sensitivity for recovering AUPRC.

title: UniPose9D: Universal Category-Agnostic Object Pose Estimation
summary: Object pose estimation is a fundamental problem in 3D vision. Although recent state-of-the-art approaches achieve strong performance, they often overfit to existing benchmarks and exhibit limited generalization to novel categories and unseen scenes. We propose UniPose9D, a category-agnostic foundation model for 9D object pose estimation: given an instance mask/ROI and either an RGB-D observation or an RGB image with predicted depth, the model estimates rotation, translation, and metric size without category labels, CAD models, mean-shape priors, or reference views. Specifically, UniPose9D samples point pairs from the observed object geometry and uses DINOv2 and PointNet features to predict NOCS coordinates for each pair. To improve accuracy, we introduce a point-pair-based RANSAC N-hop Kabsch--Umeyama algorithm with an adaptive threshold. We further employ flow matching to address symmetric ambiguities and construct a large-scale training set by curating and aligning pose annotations from existing public datasets. Experiments across six datasets show that a single unified model can match or surpass specialist methods while generalizing to unseen objects and in-the-wild scenarios. Our code and model are available on https://github.com/qq456cvb/UniPose9D.

AI.tldr: UniPose9D is a category-agnostic foundation model for 9D object pose estimation (rotation, translation, metric size) that works with RGB-D or RGB+predicted depth inputs, using DINOv2 and PointNet features with point-pair sampling and RANSAC N-hop Kabsch–Umeyama algorithm, achieving strong generalization across six datasets without category labels or CAD models.
AI.motivation: Existing object pose estimation methods overfit to benchmarks and show limited generalization to novel categories and unseen scenes. The authors aim to build a category-agnostic foundation model that eliminates the need for category labels, CAD models, mean-shape priors, or reference views.
AI.method: UniPose9D samples point pairs from observed object geometry, extracts DINOv2 and PointNet features to predict NOCS coordinates per pair, applies a novel point-pair-based RANSAC N-hop Kabsch–Umeyama algorithm with adaptive threshold for pose estimation, and uses flow matching to handle symmetric ambiguities. Training uses a large-scale curated dataset from public sources.
AI.result: Across six datasets, a single unified model matches or surpasses specialist methods and generalizes well to unseen objects and in-the-wild scenarios.
AI.conclusion: UniPose9D demonstrates that category-agnostic 9D pose estimation is feasible at a foundation-model level, achieving strong generalization without category-specific priors.

title: Length Penalties Make Chain-of-Thought Less Monitorable
summary: Length-penalized reinforcement learning can shorten chain-of-thought reasoning while hiding an influence that drives the model's answer. In our experiments, training with length penalties does not stop misleading hints from steering models, even though the models' chains of thought mention the hint much less often. A token-accuracy evaluation would count these runs as successful because they use fewer reasoning tokens with little accuracy loss; it would miss whether the remaining trace still shows what drove the answer. We train Qwen3-4B and Qwen3-14B variants with different target chain lengths, then evaluate them with biasing-hint interventions on held-out MMLU-Pro-R and four transfer benchmarks. Compression sharply cuts reasoning tokens, preserves most multiple-choice accuracy, and leaves hint influence near baseline. At the strongest target, lower-bound faithfulness falls to 63.1% of baseline for Qwen3-14B and 69.4% for Qwen3-4B; the raw rate at which a monitor catches hint use falls from 69% to 49% and from 60% to 48%. To separate length from content, we randomly delete sentences from uncompressed baseline chains until the remaining text matches the compressed length. Even after this length matching, compressed chains disclose the hint 7-35 percentage points less often than baseline chains that we shorten at random, for both Qwen3 sizes and all five evaluation distributions. Compression therefore does more than shorten reasoning, preferentially removing the cues a monitor needs to see what influenced the answer. Together, these results reveal a compression-monitorability frontier in which cheaper reasoning can preserve answers while making the influences behind them harder to detect.

AI.tldr: Length-penalized reinforcement learning shortens chain-of-thought reasoning but reduces monitorability of misleading hints. Compression preferentially removes cues needed to detect answer influences, creating a trade-off between reasoning efficiency and faithfulness transparency.
AI.motivation: To investigate whether length-penalized compression of chain-of-thought reasoning preserves model accuracy while hiding the influence of biasing hints, potentially undermining the interpretability benefits of explicit reasoning.
AI.method: Trained Qwen3-4B and Qwen3-14B variants with different target chain lengths. Evaluated with biasing-hint interventions on MMLU-Pro-R and four transfer benchmarks. Compared compressed chains to length-matched random deletion baselines to isolate length effects from content effects.
AI.result: Compression sharply cuts reasoning tokens with little accuracy loss, but faithfulness drops to 63.1% (Qwen3-14B) and 69.4% (Qwen3-4B) of baseline. Monitor detection of hint use falls from 69% to 49% and 60% to 48%. Compressed chains disclose hints 7-35 percentage points less than length-matched random baselines.
AI.conclusion: Compression does more than shorten reasoning—it preferentially removes cues needed to detect answer influences. This reveals a compression-monitorability frontier where cheaper reasoning preserves answers while hiding the influences behind them.

title: An X-Band Monopulse Direction-Finding Receiver Based on a Rat-Race Comparator and a $2\times2$ Antipodal Vivaldi Array
summary: This paper presents the design, fabrication, and experimental validation of a compact monopulse direction-finding (DF) receiver operating in the X-band. The receiver combines a $2 \times 2$ antipodal Vivaldi antenna array with a rat-race (180-degree hybrid) comparator network that simultaneously synthesizes the sum ($Σ$), azimuth-difference ($Δ_{az}$), and elevation-difference ($Δ_{el}$) channels required for monopulse processing, removing the need for mechanical scanning. The Vivaldi element provides good impedance matching across 8-12 GHz with a simulated realized gain of approximately 6.44 dBi, and the comparator exhibits the 180-degree phase balance at its difference ports required for angle encoding, with the fabricated prototype preserving the matched, balanced response measured across the band. Three LTC5564 envelope detectors convert the channel outputs to DC voltages that are digitized by an on-board microcontroller and processed in software to form the monopulse ratios and estimate the direction of arrival (DoA), with the result displayed in real time on a MATLAB graphical user interface. Azimuth direction finding is experimentally demonstrated over fourteen angles spanning -20 to 19 degrees at 11 GHz, yielding a root-mean-square error (RMSE) of 7 degrees; the architecture is directly extensible to elevation. The result is a passive, low-cost, and fully integrated receiver suitable for radar sensing, electronic warfare, and surveillance.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a compact X-band monopulse direction-finding receiver using a 2×2 Vivaldi antenna array and rat-race comparator network, achieving real-time DoA estimation with 7° RMSE without mechanical scanning.
AI.motivation: To develop a passive, low-cost, fully integrated monopulse DF receiver that eliminates the need for mechanical scanning, suitable for radar, electronic warfare, and surveillance applications.
AI.method: A $2 \times 2$ antipodal Vivaldi antenna array is combined with a rat-race 180° hybrid comparator to synthesize Σ, Δaz, and Δel channels. Three envelope detectors convert RF outputs to DC voltages, digitized by a microcontroller, and processed in MATLAB for real-time DoA estimation.
AI.result: The Vivaldi element achieves ~6.44 dBi gain across 8-12 GHz. Azimuth DoA testing at 11 GHz over -20° to 19° yields 7° RMSE. The comparator maintains 180° phase balance across the band.
AI.conclusion: The proposed receiver is a passive, low-cost, fully integrated solution for real-time monopulse direction finding, directly extensible to elevation, with demonstrated feasibility for radar sensing, EW, and surveillance.

title: From Non-Rigid to Rigid: Safe Acquisition of Rigid Communication Graphs under Limited Sensing
summary: Communication graph rigidity is a fundamental requirement in many multi robot formation control approaches. However, ensuring and maintaining a rigid communication topology becomes challenging in practice due to limited sensing ranges and dynamic operating conditions. This paper provides a method for achieving an inter robot collision free, rigid time varying communication graph, where communication links are established or broken according to limited sensing ranges, without assuming an initial rigid graph. In addition, the proposed approach guarantees the realization of a rigid graph for heterogeneous nonlinear multi robot systems. A computationally lean, distributed quadratic optimization-based controller is developed for a leader follower architecture, acquiring rigidity based on hierarchical second-order consensus among robots. Follower agents do not require global absolute positions of any agent, including their own. The proposed method is validated through both simulations and hardware experiments in a motion-capture environment, demonstrating reliable performance under the limited sensing capabilities of individual robots.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a distributed control method for achieving and maintaining rigid communication graphs in multi-robot formation control under limited sensing ranges, without requiring an initial rigid graph or global position information.
AI.motivation: Communication graph rigidity is essential for multi-robot formation control, but maintaining rigid topologies is challenging due to limited sensing ranges and dynamic conditions. Existing methods often assume an initial rigid graph, which is impractical.
AI.method: A distributed quadratic optimization-based controller with a leader-follower architecture is developed, using hierarchical second-order consensus to achieve rigidity. No global absolute positions are needed for followers.
AI.result: Validated through both simulations and hardware experiments in a motion-capture environment, demonstrating reliable performance under limited sensing capabilities.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach successfully achieves collision-free, rigid time-varying communication graphs for heterogeneous nonlinear multi-robot systems without initial rigidity assumptions.

title: Estimation, Prediction, and Assortment Optimization for Markov Chain Choice Models with Panel Data
summary: We propose a framework for the Markov chain (MC) choice model with panel data, including parameter estimation, personalized choice prediction, and personalized assortment optimization. In contrast to the traditional setting, which assumes that each transaction is independently drawn from a random utility model, our framework accounts for dependencies among transactions for the same customer in historical data, captured by partial-ordering preference information. To the best of our knowledge, our framework initiates the study of choice modeling with panel data under MC. As our primary result, we propose novel expectation-maximization (EM) algorithms for MC parameter estimation by incorporating partial-ordering-based customer preference information. On synthetic datasets and the sushi dataset, our EM algorithms outperform the traditional EM algorithm of Simsek and Topaloglu (Operations Research, 66, 2018) and multinomial-logit-based partial-order benchmarks adapted from Jagabathula and Vulcano (Management Science, 64, 2018). As our secondary contribution, we present hardness and computational results for conditional choice prediction and assortment optimization problems. These results complement our estimation framework and clarify the computational landscape of conditional choice and assortment optimization, which may be of independent interest.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a new framework for the Markov chain choice model using panel data, with EM algorithms that leverage partial-ordering preferences to outperform existing methods in estimation, prediction, and assortment optimization.
AI.motivation: Traditional choice models assume independent transactions per customer, ignoring dependencies in panel data. This work aims to capture those dependencies via partial-ordering preferences under the Markov chain model.
AI.method: The authors propose novel expectation-maximization (EM) algorithms that incorporate partial-ordering-based customer preferences for MC parameter estimation from panel data.
AI.result: On synthetic and sushi datasets, the proposed EM algorithms outperform the traditional EM algorithm (Simsek & Topaloglu, 2018) and multinomial-logit-based partial-order benchmarks (Jagabathula & Vulcano, 2018).
AI.conclusion: The framework initiates choice modeling with panel data under MC, and the accompanying hardness and computational results for conditional choice prediction and assortment optimization clarify the problem's computational landscape.

title: Model Guides You How to Draw: Adaptive Visual Gating for Unified Multimodal Reasoning
summary: Unified multimodal models (UMMs) with interleaved reasoning, which generate both textual and visual steps as part of intermediate reasoning traces, have demonstrated great potential for visual mathematical reasoning tasks. However, we identify a key insight in this paradigm: generating intermediate visual reasoning steps is not always beneficial and can even be harmful, as self-generated visual steps may introduce erroneous visual evidence that misleads subsequent reasoning. Moreover, frequently triggering visual steps during reasoning incurs substantial computational and memory overhead, degrading inference efficiency. To address these accuracy and efficiency challenges, we observe that the model's internal signals can indicate whether a visual step will benefit reasoning before the entire visual generation is completed. Specifically, this work identifies two internal signals: 1) Generation Intent, which reflects whether the model has a concrete textual plan for what to draw, and 2) Visual Fidelity, which measures whether the visual generation remains grounded in the original input image. Leveraging these internal signals, we propose AdaViG, a training-free adaptive visual gating method for unified multimodal reasoning. AdaViG dynamically evaluates each triggered visual step at an early visual generation stage and aborts it when both signals are weak, thereby preventing misleading visual evidence from entering the reasoning trace while avoiding unnecessary computation. Comprehensive experiments demonstrate that AdaViG improves accuracy by up to 5.7% while reducing visual generation FLOPs by 25.0%-91.0% and wall-clock latency by 15.4%-45.6%.

AI.tldr: AdaViG is a training-free adaptive method that uses internal model signals (Generation Intent and Visual Fidelity) to dynamically decide whether to abort intermediate visual reasoning steps in unified multimodal models, improving accuracy by up to 5.7% while reducing computation by 25-91%.
AI.motivation: Self-generated intermediate visual reasoning steps in UMMs can introduce erroneous visual evidence that misleads subsequent reasoning, and frequently triggering visual steps causes substantial computational/memory overhead, degrading inference efficiency.
AI.method: The paper identifies two internal signals—Generation Intent (whether the model has a concrete textual plan for drawing) and Visual Fidelity (whether visual generation remains grounded in the original image)—and proposes AdaViG, a training-free adaptive gating method that evaluates each triggered visual step at an early stage and aborts it when both signals are weak.
AI.result: AdaViG improves accuracy by up to 5.7%, reduces visual generation FLOPs by 25.0%-91.0%, and reduces wall-clock latency by 15.4%-45.6%.
AI.conclusion: Not all intermediate visual reasoning steps are beneficial; dynamically gating visual generation based on internal model signals can simultaneously improve both accuracy and efficiency in unified multimodal reasoning.

title: PHITSBench: an execution-scored benchmark for AI-assisted PHITS radiation-transport input generation using natural language
summary: We introduce PHITSBench, an execution-scored benchmark for the Monte Carlo Particle and Heavy Ion Transport code System (PHITS). PHITSBench comprises 282 transport-scorable tasks spanning three common workflow categories: parameter editing (Edit), syntax repair (Repair ), and complete simulation generation from natural-language descriptions (Reproduce). Each task is evaluated using a Composite Metric Score that combines execution success with agreement between generated and reference transport observables. Using PHITSBench, we evaluate five GPT-5.4-based configurations ranging from zero-shot prompting to knowledge-augmented and agentic workflows. Without domain-specific knowledge, the model performs well on editing and repair tasks (95% and 70% success, respectively) but fails to generate correct simulations from scratch (0% success on the Reproduce track). A structured, machine-readable PHITS knowledge catalog, supplied alongside the user manual, raises single-shot Reproduce-task success to 57%. Agentic execution provides a further improvement to 66-73%, but at increased computational cost. Failure analysis shows that the remaining errors are dominated by incorrect selection and configuration of physical observables rather than syntax generation. These results suggest that future progress in AI-assisted radiation-transport modeling will depend as much on machine-readable knowledge bases, curated domain-training datasets, and execution-grounded evaluation environments as on advances in foundation models themselves.
