It demonstrates effective linearization of a power amplifier using a 196.608 MHz OFDM signal, achieving up to 23 dB NMSE and 11 dB ACLR improvements.
AI.motivation: Current DPD algorithms for 5G/B5G require wideband testing, but existing lab hardware is expensive and inflexible; there is a need for an affordable, open-source DPD test platform.
AI.method: The system uses RFSoC 4x2 hardware with full control over frequency, sampling mode, output power, and attenuation up to 1 GHz bandwidth. Linearization is performed using memory polynomial and augmented real-valued time-delay neural network (ARVTDNN) DPD models.
AI.result: NMSE improvement up to 23 dB, ACLR improvement up to 11 dB when linearizing a PA with a 196.608 MHz OFDM 256-QAM signal.
AI.conclusion: OwnDPDLab is a flexible, low-cost, open-source DPD test solution capable of wideband signal processing, validated by significant linearization performance improvements.

title: Large Language Model Enhanced Differentiable Trajectory Planning for IoT-Enabled Autonomous Driving
summary: Autonomous driving planning is a key component of IoT-enabled intelligent transportation systems, requiring vehicles to generate safe, efficient, and executable trajectories in complex urban environments from multi-source contextual information. While imitation learning (IL) has shown promise on large-scale datasets, IL-based planners still suffer from limited coverage of complex long-tail interactions, weak consistency with downstream constrained refinement, and insufficient use of high level scene semantics under real time constraints. To address these issues, this paper proposes a large language model (LLM) enhanced differentiable trajectory planning framework for IoT-enabled autonomous driving. Specifically, we introduce a surrounding agent centric data augmentation strategy to reorganize sur rounding agent trajectories as additional planning supervision, thereby improving the training distribution without collecting additional raw data. We further design a complexity-aware asyn chronous LLM-based semantic enhancement module to extract scene-related high-level semantic features with controlled online overhead. In addition, a differentiable optimization module is incorporated to refine generated trajectories with explicit residual penalties while backpropagating optimization gradients to the upstream planner. Experiments show that the proposed method achieves the best overall scores of 83.63 and 78.29 on the nuPlan closed-loop nonreactive and reactive Hard20 benchmarks, respectively, and CARLA-ROS tests further verify its online deployment and real time closed-loop execution capability.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes an LLM-enhanced differentiable trajectory planning framework for autonomous driving that improves training distribution via agent-centric data augmentation, extracts high-level semantic features with asynchronous LLM enhancement, and refines trajectories through differentiable optimization, achieving top scores on nuPlan benchmarks and real-time CARLA-ROS deployment.
AI.motivation: Imitation learning-based planners for autonomous driving suffer from limited coverage of complex long-tail interactions, weak consistency with downstream constrained refinement, and insufficient use of high-level scene semantics under real-time constraints.
AI.method: (1) A surrounding agent-centric data augmentation strategy reorganizing agent trajectories for additional supervision. (2) A complexity-aware asynchronous LLM-based semantic enhancement module for high-level scene semantics with controlled overhead. (3) A differentiable optimization module refining trajectories with explicit residual penalties and backpropagating gradients to the upstream planner.
AI.result: Achieves best overall scores of 83.63 and 78.29 on nuPlan closed-loop non-reactive and reactive Hard20 benchmarks; verified real-time closed-loop execution capability via CARLA-ROS tests.
AI.conclusion: The proposed LLM-enhanced differentiable trajectory planning framework effectively addresses key IL limitations and demonstrates superior performance and real-time deployment feasibility for IoT-enabled autonomous driving.

title: Multimodal Routing for Interpretable, Robust, and Auditable Clinical Prediction
comment: Accepted at CHASE 2026. 12 pages, 6 figures, 4 tables
summary: Electronic health record (EHR) data are inherently multimodal, and leveraging multiple modalities can improve predictive performance. However, most existing approaches rely on deep fusion, which obscures how individual modalities contribute to predictions and limits the interpretability of multimodal reasoning. We propose an explicit multimodal routing framework for clinical prediction that enables interpretable, robust, and auditable reasoning across three EHR modalities: structured longitudinal variables (L), clinical notes (N), and chest X-rays (I). Our model constructs discrete unimodal, directional bimodal, and trimodal routes to capture both individual modality signals and asymmetric cross-modal interactions. To audit multimodal reasoning and assess robustness, we introduce inference-time route masking, which simulates missing modalities and reweights the remaining routes without retraining. We analyze changes in performance and routing weights under these scenarios to understand model decision-making. We evaluate our framework on multi-label phenotype prediction (K = 25) and binary ICU mortality prediction using trimodal patient stays from MIMIC-IV, revealing systematic differences in modality reliance across clinical condition groups. Overall, our framework offers a transparent, auditable, and practical approach to multimodal clinical prediction, providing interpretability, robustness, and insights into how different data sources drive model decisions.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes an explicit multimodal routing framework for clinical prediction using EHR data (structured variables, notes, chest X-rays). It constructs discrete unimodal, bimodal, and trimodal routes for interpretable reasoning, and introduces inference-time route masking to audit modality reliance and robustness without retraining.
AI.motivation: Existing multimodal EHR approaches use deep fusion, which obscures individual modality contributions and limits interpretability of multimodal reasoning.
AI.method: Explicit multimodal routing framework with discrete unimodal, directional bimodal, and trimodal routes across three modalities (L, N, I). Inference-time route masking simulates missing modalities and reweights remaining routes without retraining.
AI.result: Evaluated on multi-label phenotype prediction (K=25) and binary ICU mortality prediction using MIMIC-IV trimodal data, revealing systematic differences in modality reliance across clinical condition groups.
AI.conclusion: The framework offers transparent, auditable, and practical multimodal clinical prediction with interpretability, robustness, and insights into how different data sources drive model decisions.

title: BiLoG-Net: A Bi-Context Location-Guided Network for Breast Mass Segmentation and Malignancy Classification in Mammography
summary: Breast cancer remains the most commonly diagnosed malignancy among women worldwide, yet accurate detection and characterization of breast masses in mammography remain challenging due to subtle intensity variations, heterogeneous tissue densities, and indistinct lesion boundaries that complicate radiological interpretation. To address these limitations, we propose BiLoG-Net, a deep learning framework that jointly performs breast mass segmentation and malignancy classification through bi-context location-aware feature modeling and segmentation-guided attention mechanisms. Our architecture integrates a novel encoder-decoder paradigm with Fire-based feature extraction, lightweight global and local feature enhancement modules, and adaptive location-aware gating to simultaneously capture long-range contextual dependencies and fine-grained boundary-sensitive details. Unlike conventional multi-stage pipelines, our tightly coupled multi-task design enables mutual reinforcement between pixel-level localization and image-level diagnosis, reducing error propagation while producing spatially grounded malignancy predictions. Evaluated on CBIS-DDSM and INBreast benchmarks, BiLoG-Net achieves state-of-the-art performance with Dice scores of 94.20% and 93.10%, classification accuracies of 95.20% and 93.60%, and AUC values of 97.10% and 96.00%, respectively, substantially outperforming existing CNN and transformer-based baselines. By combining precise boundary delineation with reliable malignancy assessment in a single end-to-end model, this work holds strong potential for clinical computer-aided detection systems, helping radiologists prioritize suspicious cases and improve screening efficiency in busy clinical settings.

AI.tldr: BiLoG-Net is a deep learning framework for simultaneous breast mass segmentation and malignancy classification in mammography, achieving state-of-the-art results on CBIS-DDSM and INBreast datasets (Dice: 94.20%/93.10%, Acc: 95.20%/93.60%).
AI.motivation: Breast cancer detection in mammography is challenging due to subtle intensity variations, heterogeneous tissue densities, and indistinct lesion boundaries. Existing pipelines suffer from error propagation in multi-stage approaches.
AI.method: BiLoG-Net uses a novel encoder-decoder with Fire-based feature extraction, lightweight global/local feature enhancement modules, adaptive location-aware gating, and segmentation-guided attention for joint multi-task learning.
AI.result: On CBIS-DDSM: Dice 94.20%, Acc 95.20%, AUC 97.10%. On INBreast: Dice 93.10%, Acc 93.60%, AUC 96.00%. Outperforms CNN and transformer-based baselines.
AI.conclusion: The tightly coupled multi-task design reduces error propagation, produces spatially grounded malignancy predictions, and shows strong potential for clinical CAD systems to improve screening efficiency.

title: IdeaTrail: Full-Process Agent Trajectories for Scientific Ideation
summary: Scientific research is a complex, multi-stage workflow rather than a single act of text generation. The ideation process typically emerges through literature search, paper reading, tool use, claim checking, cross-paper synthesis, brainstorming, rejection of weak directions, and iterative writing. Existing resources capture individual components of this process, but datasets that jointly record tool use, evidence acquisition, intermediate artifact evolution, and idea- or proposal-level endpoints remain limited. This report introduces \method, a multi-turn process-trajectory dataset for scientific ideation and proposal generation. Each instance records a research process from evidence gathering to either idea selection or proposal construction. Rather than freely fabricating trajectories, \method starts from human-selected high-quality research papers and proposal artifacts and uses a Generator--Advisor synthesis loop. The Generator produces the visible trajectory through actions, observations, and artifact edits, while the Advisor has access to the full generation context and checks grounding, causal order, naturalness, and leakage from hidden targets. This reverse-to-forward procedure produces multi-turn research data that remains aligned with real scientific artifacts while approximating the uncertainty, evidence use, and staged convergence of research practice. \method provides both a dataset and a general recipe for synthesizing process-supervision data for scientific research agents.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces SciProcess, a multi-turn dataset that captures the full trajectory of scientific ideation (literature search, reading, tool use, claim checking, etc.) rather than just final outputs, using a Generator-Advisor synthesis loop aligned with real research artifacts.
AI.motivation: Existing resources only capture individual components of the scientific research workflow, but no dataset jointly records tool use, evidence acquisition, artifact evolution, and idea/proposal-level endpoints together.
AI.method: The method uses human-selected high-quality research papers and proposal artifacts as starting points, then employs a Generator-Advisor loop where the Generator produces visible trajectories (actions, observations, edits) and the Advisor checks for grounding, causal order, naturalness, and leakage from hidden targets.
AI.result: A multi-turn process-trajectory dataset (SciProcess) that remains aligned with real scientific artifacts while approximating the uncertainty, evidence use, and staged convergence of real research practice.
AI.conclusion: SciProcess provides both a dataset and a general recipe for synthesizing process-supervision data for scientific research agents.

title: Stochastic Analysis of Successive Interference Cancellation in a Narrow-Beam LEO Uplink
summary: We investigate SIR distributions and order statistics of user equipments (UEs) at a typical low Earth orbit satellite base station (LEO BS) with narrow Gaussian antenna beams in the uplink. We analyze SIR distributions for the three strongest UEs under successive interference cancellation (SIC), using a Gaussian mixture shadowing model. The UEs are distributed on Earth according to a Poisson point process (PPP). We show that SIC enables each LEO BS to serve multiple UEs per beam cell, achieving simultaneously a good average network throughput and user fairness.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes SIR distributions and order statistics for UEs at a typical LEO satellite base station with narrow Gaussian antenna beams in the uplink, using a Gaussian mixture shadowing model and showing that SIC enables serving multiple UEs per beam cell with good throughput and fairness.
AI.motivation: To understand how successive interference cancellation (SIC) can improve network throughput and user fairness in LEO satellite communication systems with narrow Gaussian beams.
AI.method: The authors analyze SIR distributions for the three strongest UEs under SIC, using a Gaussian mixture shadowing model, with UEs distributed on Earth according to a Poisson point process (PPP).
AI.result: SIC enables each LEO BS to serve multiple UEs per beam cell, achieving simultaneously good average network throughput and user fairness.
AI.conclusion: Successive interference cancellation is an effective technique for improving both throughput and fairness in uplink LEO satellite communication systems with narrow Gaussian beams.

title: SUREFlow: State-space Uncertainty-aware REsidual Flow Matching for Robust Robot Manipulation
comment: Accepted at IEEE/RSJ International Conference on Intelligent Robots & Systems (IROS) 2026, Pittsburgh, PA, USA
summary: Generative vision-language-action policies have advanced robot manipulation, but they often exhibit instability under noise, partial observability, and stochastic initial conditions. During extended rollouts, small velocity errors accumulate, degrading execution reliability. Existing diffusion and flow-based policies typically assume homoscedastic residuals and lack explicit uncertainty modeling within action generation, limiting robustness during iterative rollout. We propose SUREFlow, a state-space uncertainty-aware residual flow matching framework built on a Mamba backbone. The method jointly predicts action velocities and input-dependent residual uncertainty, enabling selective refinement of unreliable action dimensions without environment feedback while preserving computational efficiency. On LIBERO, SUREFlow achieves 92.5% average success rate (SR), outperforming the Mamba-based MaIL by 34.2%. On LIBERO-PRO, it attains around 49% SR using only 179M parameters, achieving performance comparable to large VLAs with 3-7B parameters. SUREFlow source code is available on: https://github.com/tanvirnwu/SUREFlow

AI.tldr: SUREFlow is a state-space uncertainty-aware residual flow matching framework using a Mamba backbone that jointly predicts action velocities and input-dependent residual uncertainty, achieving 92.5% success rate on LIBERO (outperforming MaIL by 34.2%) and ~49% on LIBERO-PRO with only 179M parameters (comparable to 3-7B parameter VLAs).
AI.motivation: Existing diffusion and flow-based policies suffer from instability under noise, partial observability, and stochastic conditions; small velocity errors accumulate during extended rollouts, and current methods lack explicit uncertainty modeling in action generation.
AI.method: SUREFlow uses a Mamba-based backbone for state-space uncertainty-aware residual flow matching, jointly predicting action velocities and input-dependent residual uncertainty, enabling selective refinement of unreliable action dimensions without environment feedback.
AI.result: 92.5% average success rate on LIBERO (34.2% improvement over MaIL); ~49% SR on LIBERO-PRO with only 179M parameters, matching large VLAs with 3-7B parameters.
AI.conclusion: SUREFlow demonstrates that explicit uncertainty modeling in flow matching policies significantly improves robustness and efficiency in robot manipulation, achieving strong performance with far fewer parameters compared to large vision-language-action models.

title: Vilya-1: An all-atom foundation model for macrocycle structure prediction and design
summary: Macrocyclic peptides are an increasingly important therapeutic modality, but existing computational methods for modeling their structures and properties are limited in scope and do not generalize well across the synthetically accessible chemical space. In this work, we introduce Vilya-1, a deep learning model that addresses two central challenges in macrocycle design: sampling biologically relevant conformations across arbitrary chemistries and predicting key developability properties such as membrane permeability. Vilya-1 operates on a uniform all-atom representation and is trained on heterogeneous structural datasets spanning diverse topologies and chemical classes. Across a broad set of macrocycles composed of canonical and non-canonical residues, Vilya-1 substantially improves geometric accuracy relative to physics-based methods, co-folding networks, and deep-learning conformer generators, while maintaining broad chemical coverage that extends to small molecules. Vilya-1 also supports generative applications, enabling the design of novel macrocycles with tailored chemical, structural, and property profiles. Together, these capabilities establish Vilya-1 as a foundation model for accelerating the development of next-generation macrocycle therapeutics.

AI.tldr: Vilya-1 is a deep learning foundation model for macrocyclic peptides that improves conformational sampling and property prediction (e.g., membrane permeability) across diverse chemistries, outperforming physics-based and deep-learning methods, and supports generative design of novel macrocycles.
AI.motivation: Existing computational methods for modeling macrocyclic peptide structures and properties are limited in scope and fail to generalize across the vast synthetically accessible chemical space, which hinders the development of this important therapeutic modality.
AI.method: Vilya-1 is a deep learning model operating on a uniform all-atom representation, trained on heterogeneous structural datasets covering diverse topologies and chemical classes (canonical and non-canonical residues, small molecules). It simultaneously addresses conformation sampling and developability property prediction.
AI.result: Vilya-1 substantially improves geometric accuracy over physics-based methods, co-folding networks, and deep-learning conformer generators, while maintaining broad chemical coverage. It also supports generative applications for designing novel macrocycles with tailored properties.
AI.conclusion: Vilya-1 establishes itself as a foundation model for macrocycle design, capable of accelerating the development of next-generation macrocycle therapeutics through accurate structure prediction and generative design.

title: ScratNet: A Swin-Based Multi-Scale Dilated Network with Precision Refinement for Semiconductor Scratch Segmentation
summary: Surface scratch defects in semiconductor manufacturing pose significant challenges due to their irregular shapes, low contrast, and varying scales. Traditional inspection methods often struggle to detect such defects reliably, especially in complex imaging scenarios. While deep learning approaches based on Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) have improved accuracy, they often fail to capture fine-grained edge details. To address these limitations, we propose ScratNet, a novel end-to-end scratch segmentation framework that integrates a modified Swin Transformer backbone with a tailored decoder. The decoder incorporates a Multi-Scale Dilated Aggregation (MDA) module to capture both local and global context, a Stem Integration Module (SIM) to restore spatial detail, and a Precision Refinement (PR) branch that enhances boundary sharpness using anisotropic convolutions. Through this stage-adaptive feature aggregation and boundary-aware refinement, ScratNet achieves superior accuracy on thin and irregular defects. Extensive experiments demonstrate that ScratNet consistently outperforms existing methods, providing a scalable and robust solution for automated scratch inspection in high-precision manufacturing.

AI.tldr: ScratNet integrates a modified Swin Transformer backbone with a specialized decoder (MDA, SIM, PR modules) to accurately segment thin, irregular surface scratch defects in semiconductor manufacturing, outperforming existing methods.
AI.motivation: Surface scratch defects in semiconductor manufacturing have irregular shapes, low contrast, and varying scales. Traditional and CNN-based methods struggle to detect them reliably, especially in capturing fine-grained edge details.
AI.method: ScratNet uses a modified Swin Transformer backbone + tailored decoder with: (1) Multi-Scale Dilated Aggregation (MDA) for local/global context, (2) Stem Integration Module (SIM) for spatial detail restoration, (3) Precision Refinement (PR) branch with anisotropic convolutions for boundary sharpness.
AI.result: ScratNet consistently outperforms existing methods in segmenting thin and irregular scratch defects, providing superior accuracy and robustness.
AI.conclusion: ScratNet offers a scalable and robust solution for automated scratch inspection in high-precision semiconductor manufacturing, effectively addressing the limitations of prior approaches.

title: UNIT: Unleash Large Language Models Potential for Graph Continual Learning
summary: In real-world multimodal web scenarios, graph-structured data often arrives in a streaming manner, making graph continual learning a crucial paradigm for continuously modeling such evolving structures. However, existing graph continual learning methods still face two fundamental challenges. 1) semantic-structural separation, where the graph-based methods excel at modeling topological relationships but neglect deep semantics. 2) imbalanced knowledge transfer, where existing models fail to effectively leverage general knowledge gained from early tasks to benefit subsequent new tasks. To address above issues, we propose a novel framework, \textbf{UN}leash Large Language Models PotentIal for Graph ConTinual Learning (UNIT). By fine-tuning large language model only on the first task, we bridge the distributional gap between the pre-trained LLM corpus and the target task dataset to enhance the adaptability of LLMs for graph-structured tasks. Meanwhile, we propose an uncertain-aware anchor generation mechanism to effectively preserve representative knowledge across tasks, avoiding the neglect of universal knowledge learned from previous tasks. Additionally, we introduce structural confluence modeling to explicitly integrates graph topology information into semantic information, enhancing the collaborative capabilities between semantic understanding and structural modeling. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our proposed method achieves state-of-the-art performance in the graph continual learning task.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes UNIT, a framework that leverages large language models for graph continual learning by fine-tuning LLMs on the first task, using uncertain-aware anchor generation for knowledge preservation, and integrating structural confluence modeling to combine topology with semantics, achieving SOTA results.
AI.motivation: Graph continual learning faces two challenges: 1) semantic-structural separation where graph methods neglect deep semantics, and 2) imbalanced knowledge transfer where general knowledge from early tasks fails to benefit new tasks.
AI.method: UNIT fine-tunes a large language model only on the first task to bridge distribution gaps, proposes uncertain-aware anchor generation to preserve representative knowledge across tasks, and introduces structural confluence modeling to integrate graph topology with semantic information.
AI.result: Extensive experiments demonstrate state-of-the-art performance in graph continual learning tasks.
AI.conclusion: UNIT effectively addresses semantic-structural separation and imbalanced knowledge transfer in graph continual learning by leveraging LLMs.

title: Scalable Rate-Splitting Precoding via Recurrent Structure-Preserving Graph Neural Networks
comment: Submitted to IEEE Transactions on Wireless Communications
summary: Graph neural network (GNN)-based precoding has demonstrated strong potential for scalable multi-user beamforming in multi-user multiple-input single-output (MU-MISO) systems under space division multiple access (SDMA). However, direct extension to rate-splitting multiple access (RSMA) is non-trivial due to the coupled common/private-stream structure inherent to RSMA, which requires a fundamentally different graph representation and permutation equivariance structure. Motivated by this, we propose a recurrent structure-preserving graph neural network (RS-GNN) for scalable RSMA precoding. RS-GNN constructs precoder-dependent graph features at every refinement layer, enabling closed-loop interference-aware message passing, and recovers the common and private precoders through an analytically grounded structure-based reconstruction via a differentiable linear solver. This design decouples the learnable parameters from fixed system dimensions, enabling generalization to unseen system sizes without retraining. We formally prove that RS-GNN satisfies mixed permutation equivariance with respect to both user and antenna orderings, and show that RS-GNN reduces to conventional SDMA precoding as a special case by deactivating the common-stream branch. Simulation results demonstrate that RS-GNN achieves near-WMMSE sum-rate performance with significantly lower online inference time, while generalizing robustly to unseen system sizes; its SDMA special case consistently outperforms existing GNN-based precoders across unseen antenna and user configurations, SNR regimes, and channel distributions.

AI.tldr: Proposes RS-GNN, a recurrent structure-preserving graph neural network for scalable precoding in RSMA systems, achieving near-optimal sum-rate performance with strong generalization across unseen system sizes.
AI.motivation: Existing GNN-based precoding works well for SDMA but cannot directly extend to RSMA due to the coupled common/private-stream structure requiring different graph representation and permutation equivariance properties.
AI.method: RS-GNN constructs precoder-dependent graph features per refinement layer for closed-loop interference-aware message passing, and recovers common/private precoders via a differentiable linear solver with analytically grounded structure-based reconstruction.
AI.result: Achieves near-WMMSE sum-rate performance with lower online inference time, generalizes robustly to unseen system sizes, and its SDMA special case outperforms existing GNN-based precoders across various settings.
AI.conclusion: RS-GNN provides a scalable, theoretically grounded solution for RSMA precoding that reduces to conventional SDMA as a special case, demonstrating strong generalization and computational efficiency.

title: SLIDER: Sparse History-Guided Aerial Robot Target Search using Sliding Local Maps
comment: Accepted by IEEE Robotics and Automation Letters (RA-L), 2026. https://github.com/Poaos/SLIDER
summary: Efficient exploration and target search in large-scale unknown environments remain challenging for aerial robots due to the demands of broad spatial coverage, fine-grained perception, and real-time decision-making. This paper presents SLIDER, a lightweight and memory-efficient framework that avoids reliance on globally dense maps by combining a local sliding map with sparse global history information. A novel observation quality evaluation method is proposed, leveraging historical poses and sensor models to assess point cloud data in real-time, enabling efficient frontier detection. To support scalable and responsive planning, an incremental viewpoint clustering strategy dynamically adapts to local updates, significantly reducing the number of candidate targets and decreasing computational load. A sparse global topological map is incrementally maintained to assist global planning and cost evaluation. Extensive simulations and real-world experiments demonstrate that the proposed system outperforms state-of-the-art methods in memory usage, decision latency, and search efficiency.

AI.tldr: SLIDER is a lightweight, memory-efficient framework for aerial robot exploration that uses a local sliding map and sparse global history to enable real-time frontier detection, incremental viewpoint clustering, and efficient target search without relying on globally dense maps.
AI.motivation: Efficient exploration and target search in large-scale unknown environments are challenging for aerial robots due to the need for broad spatial coverage, fine-grained perception, and real-time decision-making.
AI.method: Combines a local sliding map with sparse global history; proposes real-time observation quality evaluation using historical poses and sensor models; uses incremental viewpoint clustering for adaptive candidate target reduction; incrementally maintains a sparse global topological map.
AI.result: Outperforms state-of-the-art methods in memory usage, decision latency, and search efficiency in both simulations and real-world experiments.
AI.conclusion: SLIDER provides an effective lightweight solution for aerial robot exploration that balances efficiency and memory constraints without compromising performance.

title: MLPs are Hebbians: Constructing Efficient Fact-Storing MLPs for Transformers
summary: Large language models (LLMs) store factual knowledge in their parameters. While recent work has shown that this knowledge resides in MLP layers, existing constructive and mechanistic interpretability models of fact-storage in LLMs fail to explain the surprising empirical phenomenon that they store facts at an information-theoretically optimal rate. In this work, we develop a theoretical account of this phenomenon. We develop the first Transformer-compatible fact-storing MLP closed-form construction that satisfies the following three properties empirically observed in LLMs: it (i) attains optimal fact storage scaling, (ii) handles arbitrary input/output geometries, and (iii) works inside Transformers. Key to our work is to analyze the decoding margin of MLPs, whereas prior work only studies MLP fact storage. Under isotropic embeddings, our construction achieves information-theoretically optimal storage capacity scaling and requires $10$-$104\times$ fewer parameters at matched fact count than prior constructions. For arbitrary key and value embeddings, we show that our construction attains the same storage capacity scaling, up to penalization factors depending on the embedding geometries. Moreover, we demonstrate that our constructed MLPs can be used within Transformer blocks for factual recall tasks at optimal capacity scaling, requiring $15$-$63\times$ fewer parameters at matched fact count than prior constructions. Finally, as a proof-of-concept, we show that fact-storing MLPs enable modular fact editing by swapping a Transformer's MLP with a new one.

AI.tldr: This paper develops a theoretical framework showing that MLP layers in LLMs can store facts at information-theoretically optimal rates, with a novel closed-form construction that outperforms prior work by 10-104x parameter efficiency and enables modular fact editing.
AI.motivation: Existing mechanistic interpretability models fail to explain why LLMs store factual knowledge in MLP layers at an information-theoretically optimal rate. The authors aim to provide a theoretical account of this surprising empirical phenomenon.
AI.method: The authors develop the first Transformer-compatible MLP closed-form construction that achieves three key properties: optimal fact storage scaling, handling arbitrary input/output geometries, and working inside Transformers. They analyze the decoding margin of MLPs (going beyond prior work on MLP fact storage) and derive scaling laws under both isotropic and arbitrary embedding geometries.
AI.result: Under isotropic embeddings, the construction achieves optimal storage capacity with 10-104x fewer parameters than prior work. For arbitrary embeddings, it attains same scaling up to penalization factors. Inside Transformer blocks, it requires 15-63x fewer parameters at matched fact counts. The construction also enables modular fact editing via MLP swapping.
AI.conclusion: The authors provide a theoretical explanation for optimal fact storage in LLMs and demonstrate a practical construction that dramatically improves parameter efficiency while enabling modular editing of factual knowledge in Transformers.

title: PhenoEmbed: Self-Supervised Multispectral UAV Time-Series Embeddings for Individual Tree Crown Phenology
summary: Tree crowns are a challenging target for resilient AI because they are not static objects: their spectral response, internal texture, translucency, and apparent boundaries change substantially across the growing season. We develop PhenoEmbed, a self-supervised crown-centric temporal embedding model trained with contrastive and masked reconstruction objectives on HeideBench, an 18-date UAV multispectral time-series benchmark for forest crown phenology in D{ö}lauer Heide. The model treats seasonal crown dynamics as phenological appearance change driven by leaf emergence, canopy closure, senescence, and leaf-off conditions. Segmented tree crown polygons are retained as object anchors to extract aligned crown-centered crops through time, allowing one 256-dimensional vector summarizing seasonal crown appearance to be learned per tree. On 5,885 crop-safe crowns, the exported embeddings show structured low-dimensional organization, with the first two principal components explaining 25.1\% of variance and nearest-neighbor retrieval producing a median top-1 cosine similarity of 0.946. Compared with handcrafted temporal features and a learned mean-pooling baseline, PhenoEmbed yields substantially more compact nearest-neighbor structure, while ablations show that the contrastive loss, masked reconstruction loss, and explicit seasonal time features each affect the structure of the learned embedding space. These results support PhenoEmbed as a reusable forest crown representation learner and motivate future downstream tests of whether such features improve tree-level models under seasonal change.

AI.tldr: PhenoEmbed is a self-supervised temporal embedding model that learns 256-dimensional representations of tree crown appearance from multispectral UAV time-series data, capturing phenological changes across seasons. Trained on 5,885 crowns, it produces compact, well-structured embeddings with strong nearest-neighbor similarity (0.946 cosine), outperforming handcrafted and baseline approaches.
AI.motivation: Tree crowns are challenging for AI because they are dynamic—their spectral response, texture, translucency, and boundaries change substantially across the growing season. Existing methods do not effectively capture these seasonal phenological dynamics.
AI.method: PhenoEmbed uses contrastive and masked reconstruction objectives trained on HeideBench, an 18-date UAV multispectral time-series benchmark. It extracts aligned crown-centered crops from segmented tree crown polygons over time, learning a 256-dimensional embedding per tree that summarizes seasonal crown appearance.
AI.result: First two principal components explain 25.1% of variance; median top-1 cosine similarity of nearest-neighbor retrieval is 0.946. PhenoEmbed produces more compact nearest-neighbor structure than handcrafted temporal features and mean-pooling baselines. Ablations show contrastive loss, masked reconstruction loss, and seasonal time features each influence embedding structure.
AI.conclusion: PhenoEmbed serves as a reusable forest crown representation learner, with potential for downstream tasks to improve tree-level models under seasonal change.

title: GRATE: Temporal Extensions for Inductive KG Foundation Models via Gated Rotary Attention
comment: Accepted at the ICML 2026 Workshop on Graph Foundation Models: A New Era for Graph Machine Learning. 17 pages, 4 figures
summary: Knowledge graph foundation models such as Ultra and Trix achieve strong inductive transfer by learning relation-graph representations that generalise to unseen entities and relations. Extending this transferability to temporal knowledge graphs (TKGs) remains challenging: existing temporal models tie their parameters to dataset-specific entities, relations, or timestamps and are not designed to transfer to TKGs with disjoint vocabularies. We propose GRATE (Gated Rotary Attention for Temporal Encoding), an entity-side message function that adds no learnable parameters and encodes time through relative time differences by rotating each edge message according to its time gap to the query and applying a query-conditioned gate to select temporally relevant signals. GRATE integrates into NBFNet-style KG foundation models while preserving structural transferability. Existing TKG benchmarks evaluate within shared train/test vocabularies and cannot directly test cross-dataset temporal transfer; we therefore construct GDELTIndT and WIKIIndT, inductive transfer benchmark suites with disjoint entities, relations, and timestamps spanning both interpolation and extrapolation. Across these benchmarks and held-out forecasting datasets, a single jointly pretrained GRATE checkpoint improves over the static base model in most settings.

AI.tldr: GRATE is a parameter-free temporal encoding mechanism for KG foundation models that enables inductive transfer across temporal knowledge graphs with disjoint vocabularies, outperforming static baselines on new cross-dataset benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Extending inductive transfer capabilities of KG foundation models to temporal knowledge graphs remains challenging because existing temporal models tie parameters to dataset-specific entities, relations, or timestamps and cannot transfer across TKGs with disjoint vocabularies.
AI.method: GRATE (Gated Rotary Attention for Temporal Encoding) is an entity-side message function with no learnable parameters that encodes time via relative time differences, applying rotary position encodings to edge messages based on time gaps and using query-conditioned gating to select temporally relevant signals. It integrates into NBFNet-style KG foundation models.
AI.result: A single jointly pretrained GRATE checkpoint improves over the static base model in most settings across new inductive transfer benchmarks (GDELTIndT, WIKIIndT) and held-out forecasting datasets.
AI.conclusion: GRATE successfully extends structural transferability of KG foundation models to the temporal domain without adding learnable parameters, enabling cross-dataset temporal generalization.

title: DeepRT Engine: A Unified GPU-Parallel Ray-Tracing Framework with Hybrid SBR-IM Path Search for 6G Digital Twin Channel
summary: Digital twin channel (DTC) aims to establish a real-time digital counterpart of physical wireless channels for reproducing and predicting site-specific propagation characteristics. As a high-precision channel computation method for realistic propagation scenarios, ray tracing (RT) serves as a key enabler for DTC construction. However, conventional RT suffers from high complexity under serial path-searching workflows. This letter proposes DeepRT Engine (DeepRT-E), a parallel RT acceleration architecture with a three-stage physically-inspired pipeline for real-time DTC construction. Firstly, DeepRT-E constructs a bounding volume hierarchy (BVH) to partition the scene and reduce redundant ray-surface intersections. Secondly, the shooting and bouncing rays (SBR) algorithm is executed through a ray-level parallel tracing framework to identify candidate surface sequences and prune the search space of the image method (IM). Finally, a parallel batched IM solver refines the retained candidates for accurate propagation-path recovery. Simulation results show that DeepRT-E reduces runtime by 96.3% and achieves a converged error of only 0.001 dB, outperforming Wireless InSite and Sionna in efficiency and accuracy.

AI.tldr: DeepRT-E is a parallel ray tracing acceleration engine that reduces runtime by 96.3% with 0.001 dB error for real-time digital twin channel construction.
AI.motivation: Conventional ray tracing suffers from high complexity under serial path-searching workflows, making it inefficient for real-time digital twin channel construction.
AI.method: A three-stage physically-inspired pipeline: (1) BVH-based scene partitioning to reduce redundant intersections, (2) ray-level parallel SBR tracing to identify candidate surface sequences, (3) parallel batched IM solver for accurate path recovery.
AI.result: Reduces runtime by 96.3% and achieves converged error of only 0.001 dB, outperforming Wireless InSite and Sionna.
AI.conclusion: DeepRT-E enables real-time DTC construction with high efficiency and accuracy through parallelized ray tracing acceleration.

title: BucketKD: A Safety-Aware Bucket-Based Knowledge Distillation Framework for End-to-End Motion Planning
summary: End-to-end motion planning has emerged as a promising paradigm in autonomous driving, directly mapping raw sensor data to control commands via deep neural networks. Despite its advantages, its large model size hinders deployment in resource-constrained platforms. In this paper, we present BucketKD, a bucket-based knowledge distillation framework that yields compact and safety-aware end-to-end planners. Compared to the state-of-the-art approach, which relies on simplified planning state representations, BucketKD discretizes critical environmental variables into adaptive buckets that capture richer scene semantics while preserving efficiency. In addition, we design a safety-aware waypoint attention mechanism that evaluates each waypoint's risk level by accounting for both obstacle proximity and relative motion through a time-to-collision (TTC) formulation widely used in transportation research. This enables the student model to better retain safety-critical behaviors during distillation. Extensive experiments in CARLA using the Bench2Drive dataset show that BucketKD significantly outperforms the state-of-the-art in both planning accuracy and safety while maintaining strong compression ratios.

AI.tldr: BucketKD is a knowledge distillation framework for end-to-end motion planning in autonomous driving that discretizes environmental variables into adaptive buckets and uses a safety-aware waypoint attention mechanism with TTC formulation, significantly outperforming prior methods in accuracy and safety while maintaining strong compression.
AI.motivation: Large end-to-end motion planning models are difficult to deploy on resource-constrained platforms due to their size, and existing distillation methods use oversimplified planning state representations that lose critical scene semantics.
AI.method: BucketKD discretizes critical environmental variables into adaptive buckets to capture richer scene semantics, and introduces a safety-aware waypoint attention mechanism that evaluates risk via obstacle proximity and time-to-collision (TTC) formulation to help the student model retain safety-critical behaviors.
AI.result: Extensive experiments in CARLA using Bench2Drive dataset show BucketKD significantly outperforms state-of-the-art in both planning accuracy and safety while maintaining strong compression ratios.
AI.conclusion: BucketKD effectively produces compact yet safety-aware end-to-end planners through bucket-based discretization and safety-aware attention during knowledge distillation.

title: FlashTrie: A GPU-Accelerated Constrained Beam Search for Generative Retrieval
summary:
Constrained decoding is essential in generative retrieval, where document identifiers generated directly from a query must exactly match a predefined library of valid IDs. At scale, decoding is often constrained using a trie with beam search but most implementations run on CPU. Limited parallelism then makes trie traversal and candidate validation a serving bottleneck as beam width grows.
  We present FlashTrie, which addresses this limitation by optimizing constrained beam search on GPUs. It introduces an integer-aware succinct trie layout that uses bit compression to reduce memory footprint while keeping the full index in GPU high-bandwidth memory reducing memory stalls, and a cooperative CUDA kernel that performs beam expansion, validation, and pruning entirely on-device without per-step host orchestration. It further replaces CPU-style irregular lookup and heap maintenance with GPU-aware parallel primitives, improving warp utilization and reducing divergence.
  Together, these designs significantly reduce decoding latency and increase throughput while preserving retrieval quality. On a library of 800M keywords with beam widths up to 1000, FlashTrie reduces trie-search latency to under 3 ms, achieving up to 24x speedup over a highly optimized multi-threaded CPU baseline. These improvements enable FlashTrie to scale beam sizes by up to 5x in latency-critical applications such as sponsored search. In a large-scale online A/B experiment on a popular commercial search engine, it delivers a statistically significant +0.71% revenue lift, enabling real-time constrained decoding at a scale previously feasible only offline. The FlashTrie code will be publicly released after the review process.

AI.tldr: FlashTrie optimizes constrained beam search for generative retrieval by implementing a succinct integer-aware trie layout and a cooperative CUDA kernel that runs entirely on GPU, achieving up to 24x speedup over CPU baselines and enabling real-time decoding at scale.
AI.motivation: Constrained decoding in generative retrieval uses trie-based beam search, but existing implementations run on CPU with limited parallelism, causing trie traversal and candidate validation to become a serving bottleneck as beam width grows.
AI.method: FlashTrie introduces (1) an integer-aware succinct trie layout with bit compression to fit the full index in GPU high-bandwidth memory, and (2) a cooperative CUDA kernel performing beam expansion, validation, and pruning entirely on-device without host orchestration, using GPU-aware parallel primitives instead of CPU-style irregular lookup and heap maintenance.
AI.result: On a library of 800M keywords with beam widths up to 1000, FlashTrie reduces trie-search latency to under 3 ms (up to 24x speedup over a multi-threaded CPU baseline), enables 5x larger beam sizes in latency-critical applications, and delivers a statistically significant +0.71% revenue lift in a large-scale online A/B experiment on a commercial search engine.
AI.conclusion: FlashTrie enables real-time constrained decoding at a scale previously feasible only offline, demonstrating significant latency reduction, throughput improvement, and measurable business impact, with code to be publicly released.

title: CoSAG: Compact Semantic Anchor Gaussians via Training-Free Rate-Distortion Coding
summary: Open-vocabulary 3D scene understanding is commonly achieved by embedding 2D vision-language features such as CLIP into a 3D Gaussian Splatting scene, turning it into a text-queryable semantic field. However, attaching a high-dimensional feature to each of millions of Gaussians inflates a single scene to gigabytes, which makes storage and deployment the real bottleneck of these fields. Existing compact methods each learn and ship a per-scene codec, an autoencoder, a quantized codebook, or a distilled feature field, entangling field construction with field storage and never compressing the per-Gaussian assignment that holds the bulk of the cost. We argue that construction and storage should be decoupled, and that storage is a rate-distortion problem over the per-Gaussian binding to a small anchor table, a structure no prior open-vocabulary method compresses. We present CoSAG, which constructs the field without any per-scene training through a closed-form transmittance-weighted lift, spatially grounded semantic anchors, and multi-view denoising, and stores it with a spatially predictive entropy coder that ships no decoder. Because the anchors are spatially grounded, the binding is predictable and therefore highly compressible. The transmittance-weighted lift and multi-view denoising yield a clean, view-consistent assignment, so the entropy coder spends almost no rate on correcting noise and instead codes only the residual against its spatial prediction. CoSAG reaches sub-megabyte storage while matching or exceeding the state of the art across the 2D-rendered, 3D-selection, and dense-LSeg protocols, reducing field size by 37 to 76x relative to LangSplatV2 at higher accuracy.

AI.tldr: CoSAG decouples 3D semantic field construction from storage, reaching sub-megabyte size (37-76x smaller than LangSplatV2) via transmittance-weighted lift, spatially grounded semantic anchors, and multi-view denoising, with a predictive entropy coder—all without per-scene training.
AI.motivation: Existing open-vocabulary 3D fields attach high-dimensional features to each Gaussian, inflating a single scene to gigabytes. Prior compact methods entangle construction with storage and never compress the per-Gaussian assignment, which holds the bulk of the cost.
AI.method: CoSAG decouples construction from storage via (1) a closed-form transmittance-weighted lift, (2) spatially grounded semantic anchors, (3) multi-view denoising, and (4) a spatially predictive entropy coder (no decoder shipped).
AI.result: Sub-megabyte storage while matching/exceeding SOTA across 2D-rendered, 3D-selection, and dense-LSeg protocols; 37-76x field size reduction vs. LangSplatV2 at higher accuracy.
AI.conclusion: Construction and storage should be decoupled; storage is a rate-distortion problem over per-Gaussian binding to a small anchor table. CoSAG proves this approach yields massive compression without sacrificing accuracy.

title: KGCQual: An Interpretable Framework for Evaluating the Knowledge Graph Construction Quality from Text
comment: This paper is under review at a conference
summary: Knowledge Graphs (KGs) are increasingly constructed through automated extraction pipelines; however, such systems often introduce spurious or incomplete triples, which degrade downstream performance. Existing evaluation practices rely heavily on task-specific metrics or small-scale manual verification, offering limited insight into the structural and semantic fidelity of extracted graphs. We propose a novel, interpretable metric for intrinsic KG quality assessment that measures how closely an automatically extracted graph approximates an "ideal" graph capturing the key noun phrases, predicate relations, and basic linguistic phenomena such as negation expressed in the source text. Our framework integrates two complementary components: (1) an entity-level assessment that evaluates completeness, resolution quality, and connectivity, and (2) a relation-level assessment that judges predicate preservation and multiplicity using lexical similarity, dependency-parse alignment, and light-weight negation handling to ensure semantic faithfulness. We evaluate our metric across multiple state-of-the-art triple extraction systems and datasets, including WebNLG, TinyButMighty, and BenchIE, demonstrating that it reliably identifies omissions, redundancy, and structural deviations that existing metrics overlook. Our work offers a scalable, model-agnostic, and interpretable framework for comparing automated KG construction methods and provides a foundation for standardised evaluation. We further validate the metric through an ablation study isolating noun and verb components, and a downstream evaluation showing that KGCQual scores correlate significantly with link prediction performance on the same extracted KGs. The code repository is available at https://github.com/kracr/kg-quality-metric.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes KGCQual, a novel interpretable metric for intrinsic KG quality assessment that evaluates entity-level (completeness, resolution, connectivity) and relation-level (predicate preservation, multiplicity, negation handling) fidelity of automatically extracted knowledge graphs against source text, validated across multiple extraction systems and datasets.
AI.motivation: Existing KG evaluation relies on task-specific metrics or small-scale manual verification, which offer limited insight into structural and semantic fidelity of automatically extracted graphs.
AI.method: A two-component interpretable metric: (1) entity-level assessment (completeness, resolution quality, connectivity) and (2) relation-level assessment (predicate preservation, multiplicity, negation handling) using lexical similarity, dependency-parse alignment, and light-weight negation handling.
AI.result: The metric reliably identifies omissions, redundancy, and structural deviations overlooked by existing metrics; ablation study validates noun/verb components; KGCQual scores correlate significantly with link prediction performance.
AI.conclusion: Offers a scalable, model-agnostic, and interpretable framework for standardized evaluation of automated KG construction methods.

title: Detection of sUAS in Urban Environments using Multi-Antenna Micro-Doppler Radar
comment: 5 pages, 6 figures, accepted for presentation at IEEE VTC2026-Fall
summary: Sensing and early detection of small unmanned aerial systems (sUAS) are critically important in modern-day defense. In dense urban and indoor environments, detection becomes extremely challenging due to dense multipath, fading, low-altitude flight, and non-line-of-sight (NLOS) radio-frequency propagation. This paper presents a continuous-wave multiple-input multiple-output radar and a deep learning model for sUAS detection using NLOS signals. The radar operates at 2.47 GHz, and spectral correlation densities derived from rotational micro-Doppler signatures from the rotor blades are used as inputs to the deep learning model. Experimental results demonstrate an overall detection accuracy of $86.11\%$ across a dataset of five drone types, confirming the feasibility of sUAS detection in dense urban environments without direct line-of-sight conditions.

AI.tldr: A CW MIMO radar at 2.47 GHz combined with a deep learning model using spectral correlation densities from micro-Doppler signatures achieves 86.11% accuracy for detecting small drones in NLOS urban/indoor environments.
AI.motivation: To address the critical challenge of detecting small UAVs in dense urban and indoor environments where multipath, fading, low-altitude flight, and NLOS RF propagation severely degrade conventional sensing methods.
AI.method: Uses a continuous-wave MIMO radar operating at 2.47 GHz, extracts spectral correlation densities from rotational micro-Doppler signatures of rotor blades, and feeds these features into a deep learning model for classification.
AI.result: Achieves 86.11% overall detection accuracy across a dataset of five different drone types under NLOS conditions.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates the feasibility of detecting sUAS in dense urban environments without direct line-of-sight, validating the proposed radar+deep learning approach as a practical solution for modern defense needs.

title: Underwater Dead Reckoning with Deployable Situation-Triggered Covariance Scheduling
comment: 16 pages, 9 figures. Submitted to IEEE Journal of Oceanic Engineering
summary: Underwater dead reckoning estimates vehicle position when vision is unavailable and external positioning cannot be assumed. A single set of filter parameters can work well in many situations, but fixed tuning may be poorly matched during turns, motion transitions, or periods when sensor measurements are less reliable. This paper presents the Situation-Triggered Calibrated Adaptive Robust Extended Kalman Filter for a BlueROV2. An onboard probabilistic trigger identifies the current motion situation while one error-state filter runs continuously. When the trigger is confident, the filter changes only to the corresponding pre-calibrated process- and measurement-noise matrices; the state estimate, covariance history, dynamics, and measurement models are not reset or replaced. The trigger, noise profiles, and a one-time Doppler velocity log yaw-alignment correction are calibrated offline using sparse AprilTag-supervised pool runs. A separate validation set selects the scheduling policy, which is then fixed before held-out testing. Across four held-out pool runs, the method reduces label-weighted mean per-run translation root-mean-square error from 0.488 m to 0.471 m relative to the same filter backbone with one global noise profile, and every held-out run favors the scheduled method. A paired bootstrap over 10-second segments gives a candidate-minus-baseline difference of -0.017 m with a 95% confidence interval of [-0.024, -0.008] m, while orientation error remains essentially unchanged. These results indicate that situation-aware covariance scheduling provides a modest but consistent vision-free dead-reckoning improvement without switching estimators or resetting the filter.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a Situation-Triggered Calibrated Adaptive Robust Extended Kalman Filter for BlueROV2 underwater vehicles that switches between pre-calibrated noise profiles based on detected motion situations, achieving modest but consistent improvements in dead-reckoning accuracy (0.471m vs 0.488m RMSE) without resetting the filter.
AI.motivation: Fixed filter tuning performs poorly during turns, motion transitions, or when sensor measurements become unreliable in underwater dead reckoning scenarios where vision and external positioning are unavailable.
AI.method: An onboard probabilistic trigger identifies motion situations while one continuous error-state filter runs. When the trigger is confident, only the process- and measurement-noise matrices switch to pre-calibrated values without resetting state estimates, covariance history, or dynamics models. Noise profiles and trigger are calibrated offline using sparse AprilTag-supervised pool runs.
AI.result: Across four held-out pool runs, label-weighted mean per-run translation RMSE improved from 0.488 m to 0.471 m. Paired bootstrap over 10-second segments showed -0.017 m difference with 95% CI [-0.024, -0.008] m. Orientation error remained essentially unchanged. Every held-out run favored the scheduled method.
AI.conclusion: Situation-aware covariance scheduling provides a modest but consistent vision-free dead-reckoning improvement without switching estimators or resetting the filter.

title: Benchmarking Dynamic Affective Reasoning: A Viewer-Centric Video Emotion Dataset
summary: Video emotion analysis is typically framed as a static classification problem, treating each clip as an independent labeled unit. However, such a formulation overlooks a key psychological fact: emotions change as a result of cumulative reactions to consecutive causal events. To bridge this gap, we introduce Dynamic Affective Reasoning, the first large-scale benchmark for viewer-centric affect transitions and causal reasoning over consecutive video events. DAR contains 15,087 videos and 36,908 event-aligned affective segments annotated with 27 emotion categories. Unlike existing video-based emotion datasets, DAR presents a new viewer-centric perspective on fine-grained emotional expressions and transitions, and provides dense, temporally grounded, and causally explicit reasoning chains. Based on DAR, we formally define three challenging tasks: affective segmentation, fine-grained emotion classification, and affective reasoning. Complementing this benchmark, we propose DAR-R1, a two-stage framework that combines supervised fine-tuning with Group Relative Policy Optimization. Experiments across 10+ MLLMs show that DAR-R1 sets a new state-of-the-art for dynamic affective reasoning, in terms of both emotional localization and affective reasoning. Project page: https://github.com/Zhang-Zhiyan/DAR.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Dynamic Affective Reasoning (DAR), a large-scale benchmark with 15,087 videos and 36,908 event-aligned affective segments for viewer-centric emotion transitions and causal reasoning, along with DAR-R1, a two-stage SFT+GRPO framework that achieves SOTA on dynamic affective reasoning tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing video emotion analysis treats clips as static independent units, ignoring that emotions change as cumulative reactions to consecutive causal events. This gap between psychological reality and computational modeling motivates the need for a benchmark capturing affect transitions and causal reasoning.
AI.method: The authors curate DAR (15,087 videos, 36,908 affective segments, 27 emotion categories) with viewer-centric, temporally grounded, causally explicit reasoning chains. They define three tasks: affective segmentation, fine-grained emotion classification, and affective reasoning. They propose DAR-R1, a two-stage framework combining supervised fine-tuning (SFT) with Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO).
AI.result: Experiments across 10+ Multimodal Large Language Models show DAR-R1 achieves state-of-the-art performance on dynamic affective reasoning, excelling in both emotional localization and affective reasoning.
AI.conclusion: The DAR benchmark and DAR-R1 framework advance video emotion analysis beyond static classification to dynamic, causally-grounded affect transition modeling, setting new standards for the field.

title: When Are Sparse Feature Interventions Actually Localized? Matched Evaluation for SAE-Based Safety Control
comment: 11 pages, 5 figures, 4 tables. Preliminary version; extended multi-model study in progress
summary: We evaluate when sparse autoencoder (SAE) features act as localized control handles for safety-relevant behavior. This question is difficult because apparent success can arise from weak interventions, mismatched baselines, model robustness, or degenerate outputs that automated safety judges mark as unsafe without representing meaningful harmful compliance. We introduce a matched coherence-gated evaluation protocol for runtime safety interventions: methods are compared at matched target-effect points, and the primary target metric counts harmful compliance only when an output is both judge-unsafe and coherent. Applying this protocol to three prompt splits on Gemma-2-9B-it with a Gemma Scope layer-20 residual SAE, we find that SAE feature ablation has a narrow useful regime. SAE top800 reaches a low-to-mid target effect with lower total perturbation and competitive utility, but SAE top1600 loses utility relative to a matched dense refusal-direction baseline, and SAE top3200 primarily induces coherence collapse. Human audit confirms that coherence gating removes unsafe-only artifacts, and feature diagnostics show that the useful regime is driven by a stable head of refusal-aligned features whose activation separation decays rapidly with rank. These results argue that SAE-based safety interventions should be evaluated as regime-dependent control mechanisms rather than assumed to be uniformly localized.

AI.tldr: This paper evaluates sparse autoencoder (SAE) features as safety control handles, finding they work only in a narrow regime; they introduce a coherence-gated evaluation protocol to avoid false positives from unsafe-only artifacts.
AI.motivation: Current evaluations of SAE-based safety interventions may produce misleading successes due to weak interventions, mismatched baselines, model robustness, or degenerate outputs that automated judges incorrectly flag as unsafe.
AI.method: Introduces a matched coherence-gated evaluation protocol: methods are compared at matched target-effect points, and harmful compliance is counted only when outputs are both judge-unsafe and coherent. Applied to Gemma-2-9B-it with Gemma Scope layer-20 residual SAE across three prompt splits.
AI.result: SAE ablation has a narrow useful regime: top800 reaches low-to-mid target effect with low perturbation, top1600 loses utility versus a dense refusal-direction baseline, and top3200 primarily causes coherence collapse. Useful regime is driven by a stable head of refusal-aligned features with rapidly decaying activation separation.
AI.conclusion: SAE-based safety interventions should be treated as regime-dependent control mechanisms, not assumed to be uniformly localized.

title: Dual-Process Atomic Skill Learning: Decoupling Semantic Reasoning and Real-Time Control
summary: Language-conditioned Imitation Learning (IL) is essential for enabling robots to perform complex tasks following natural language instructions. However, generalizing to multi-step compositional tasks remains a significant challenge. While hierarchical approaches attempt to address this by decomposing tasks into atomic skills, existing methods often suffer from training instability and codebook collapse due to the tight coupling between high-level skill reasoning and low-level action generation in joint training paradigms. Inspired by the Dual-Process Theory of cognition, we propose Dual-Process Atomic Skill Learning (DASL), a novel asynchronous hierarchical imitation learning framework that decouples slow semantic reasoning from fast, real-time motion control. DASL comprises a Slow-Frequency Policy that predicts interpretable, discrete skills via Vector Quantization, and a High-Frequency Policy that leverages a latent diffusion model and a Decision Transformer to generate precise actions conditioned on these latent skills. By asynchronously coordinating these modules and utilizing diffusion to structure the latent space, our framework mitigates the skill codebook interference problem common in joint training paradigms. Evaluations across simulation benchmarks and experiment demonstrate that DASL significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, excelling in skill acquisition and compositional generalization to unseen instructions. GitHub page: https://github.com/Hatakekaka/DASL

AI.tldr: DASL is an asynchronous hierarchical imitation learning framework inspired by Dual-Process Theory that decouples slow semantic reasoning (via Vector Quantization) from fast motion control (via latent diffusion + Decision Transformer), significantly improving compositional generalization for language-conditioned robot tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing hierarchical IL methods suffer from training instability and codebook collapse due to tight coupling between high-level skill reasoning and low-level action generation in joint training paradigms.
AI.method: DASL uses a Slow-Frequency Policy with Vector Quantization for discrete skill prediction and a High-Frequency Policy combining latent diffusion model with Decision Transformer for precise action generation, asynchronously coordinated to mitigate codebook interference.
AI.result: Significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in skill acquisition and compositional generalization to unseen instructions across simulation benchmarks and real experiments.
AI.conclusion: Decoupling semantic reasoning from motion control via asynchronous coordination and latent diffusion structuring effectively resolves codebook collapse and improves compositional generalization in language-conditioned IL.

title: Error Aware Distribution Prediction for Lightweight Implicit Neural Representations
summary: Implicit neural representations (INRs) offer compact encoding of volumes, but as lossy approximators, inevitably have prediction errors. We consider INRs that can simultaneously encode relative error scales by predicting distributions using tools from uncertainty estimation. Typically, uncertainty estimation relies on computationally expensive approaches or on predefined parametric assumptions about the predictive distribution (e.g., Gaussian). In this study, we propose a lightweight method that reformulates regression-based INR training as a classification task by discretizing continuous targets into bins, enabling flexible distribution modeling to capture complex multimodal behaviors. We analyze the trade-off between regression and classification for INR training and demonstrate that the classification setting tends to achieve high reconstruction quality and competitive error awareness through uncertainty estimation, compared to regression-based approaches.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes converting regression-based implicit neural representation (INR) training into a classification task by discretizing continuous targets into bins, enabling flexible uncertainty estimation and distribution modeling without expensive computational costs or parametric assumptions.
AI.motivation: INRs as lossy approximators inevitably have prediction errors, but existing uncertainty estimation methods are computationally expensive or rely on restrictive parametric assumptions (e.g., Gaussian distributions).
AI.method: Reformulates regression-based INR training as a classification task by discretizing continuous target values into bins, enabling flexible distribution modeling that can capture complex multimodal behaviors.
AI.result: The classification-based approach achieves high reconstruction quality and competitive error awareness through uncertainty estimation compared to regression-based approaches.
AI.conclusion: Classification-based INR training offers a lightweight alternative to traditional regression-based methods for uncertainty-aware implicit neural representations, effectively balancing reconstruction quality and error estimation.

title: What Does Your Short-Answer VQA Score Actually Measure? Evaluator-Dependent Instability in Multimodal Short-Answer Benchmarks
summary: Short-answer VQA benchmarks conflate two distinct quantities: whether a model's answer is semantically correct, and whether that answer matches the surface form expected by the automatic evaluator. We study this conflation across six vision--language models and six benchmarks, using a human-validated semantic judge (97.6% precision) to audit over 37k official errors. A second text-only judge reproduces the same benchmark-level false-negative pattern, showing that the effect is not an artifact of a single audit model. On text-rich benchmarks, up to half of these errors are semantically acceptable answers penalized purely for surface-form mismatch. This instability is structured by answer type: extractive and multi-span answers are far more evaluator-sensitive than scalar answers. Benign prompt and context rewrites further destabilize official outcomes, flipping item-level correctness at substantial rates without changing the underlying task. A deterministic CPU-only contract repair confirms that the undercount is partially recoverable. These findings imply that official short-answer VQA scores should be accompanied by semantic audits and answer-type diagnostics to remain interpretable.

AI.tldr: Short-answer VQA benchmarks conflate semantic correctness with surface-form matching; up to half of reported errors are actually semantically acceptable answers penalized for format mismatch. The paper proposes semantic audits and answer-type diagnostics as necessary complements to official scores.
AI.motivation: Current short-answer VQA benchmarks conflate two distinct aspects: semantic correctness (whether the answer is right) and surface-form matching (whether the answer matches the expected format). This conflation creates systematic false-negative errors that undermine the interpretability of benchmark scores.
AI.method: The authors use a human-validated semantic judge (97.6% precision) to audit over 37k official errors across 6 vision-language models and 6 benchmarks. They also apply a text-only judge to replicate findings, and conduct perturbation experiments (prompt/context rewrites) and a CPU-only contract repair to test recoverability.
AI.result: Up to half of errors on text-rich benchmarks are semantically acceptable answers penalized for surface-form mismatch. Extractive and multi-span answers are more evaluator-sensitive than scalar answers. Benign prompt/context rewrites destabilize official outcomes at substantial rates. The undercount is partially recoverable via deterministic contract repair.
AI.conclusion: Official short-answer VQA scores should be accompanied by semantic audits and answer-type diagnostics to remain interpretable and reliable.

title: Behavioural Signatures of Risk-Sensitive Decision-Making in Large Language Models
summary: As large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used in decision support, it is important to understand whether their choices under uncertainty exhibit stable and interpretable behavioural regularities. Human decision-making combines relatively persistent risk preferences with context-dependent adjustment, yet it remains unclear whether analogous behavioural structure can be observed in LLM-based decision systems. Here we examine this question using a controlled multi-model framework based on no-limit Texas Hold'em, where behaviour is quantified by Participation, measuring voluntary engagement in uncertain opportunities, and Proactiveness, measuring pre-flop risk escalation. Across homogeneous self-play and heterogeneous mixed-model interactions, frontier LLMs exhibit stable, model-specific risk profiles, forming a spectrum from conservative to aggressive decision styles. These profiles remain largely robust under changing opponent composition, while the most conservative and most aggressive models diverge further in mixed settings. Under global risk pressure and personal resource constraint, models adapt in structured but heterogeneous ways, ranging from broad behavioural contraction to selective de-escalation and near-invariant behaviour. These findings suggest that LLMs differ not only in baseline risk disposition, but also in the risk signals they respond to and the flexibility with which they adjust, providing a behavioural basis for auditing risk-sensitive decision-making in interactive settings. Our code is publicly available at: https://github.com/XuankunRong/AgentTexasPoker.

AI.tldr: This paper examines whether LLMs exhibit stable, interpretable behavioral regularities under uncertainty by analyzing their decision-making in no-limit Texas Hold'em poker. Using Participation and Proactiveness metrics across self-play and mixed-model interactions, the authors find that frontier LLMs display model-specific risk profiles that are stable but adapt in structured ways under pressure, suggesting a behavioral basis for auditing risk-sensitive LLM decision-making.
AI.motivation: As LLMs are increasingly deployed in decision support roles, it is critical to understand whether their choices under uncertainty exhibit stable and interpretable behavioral patterns, analogous to human risk preferences that combine persistent traits with context-dependent adjustment.
AI.method: The authors use no-limit Texas Hold'em as a controlled multi-model framework, quantifying behavior via Participation (voluntary engagement in uncertain opportunities) and Proactiveness (pre-flop risk escalation). They analyze both homogeneous self-play and heterogeneous mixed-model interactions across frontier LLMs under varying opponent compositions, global risk pressure, and personal resource constraints.
AI.result: Frontier LLMs exhibit stable, model-specific risk profiles forming a spectrum from conservative to aggressive decision styles. These profiles remain robust under changing opponents, with extremes diverging further in mixed settings. Under risk pressure and resource constraints, models adapt in structured but heterogeneous ways—from broad contraction to selective de-escalation to near-invariant behavior.
AI.conclusion: LLMs differ not only in baseline risk disposition but also in which risk signals they respond to and how flexibly they adjust. This provides a behavioral basis for auditing risk-sensitive decision-making in interactive LLM applications.

title: World Models as Adversaries: Multi-Agent Self-Play Fine-Tuning for Robust Motion Planning
summary: Robust motion planning in dense traffic requires autonomous vehicles to interact in rare and safety-critical scenarios that are underrepresented in naturalistic driving data. Although adversarial training offers a feasible solution, existing methods often rely on external scenario generators, heuristic perturbations, or simulator-heavy rollouts, which makes them difficult to integrate with modern autoregressive planners. Here, we cast adversarially robust planner learning as a constrained min-max game and propose Adversarial World Modeling (AWM), a theoretically grounded multi-agent self-play fine-tuning framework. Since solving the exact game is intractable, AWM introduces a principled decoupled solver. In the inner minimization, the planner's predictive world model is converted into a role-conditioned adversary that learns sparse, scene-adaptive attack coalitions via counterfactual credit assignment. In the outer maximization, the ego planner optimizes a regret-aware robust best response against the frozen AWM, utilizing tail-risk weighting and reference-anchored trust regions to improve hard-case recovery while preserving nominal driving behavior. Experiments on the nuPlan and InterPlan benchmarks demonstrate that our method generates transferable adversarial interactions and yields a robust planner that achieves competitive closed-loop performance in both nominal and highly interactive long-tail scenarios. Theoretical analysis justifies the decoupled solver and the main optimization components.

AI.tldr: AWM is a self-play fine-tuning framework for robust motion planning in dense traffic, formulating adversarial training as a constrained min-max game with a decoupled solver that generates sparse adversarial coalitions and optimizes regret-aware robust responses, achieving competitive performance in both nominal and long-tail scenarios.
AI.motivation: Robust motion planning in dense traffic requires handling rare, safety-critical scenarios underrepresented in naturalistic driving data, but existing adversarial training methods rely on external generators, heuristic perturbations, or simulator-heavy rollouts that are difficult to integrate with modern autoregressive planners.
