AI.result: LeRoPE consistently outperforms both standard RoPE and partial RoPE across all scales. At the largest scale, RoPE requires 3.4% more compute (FLOPs) to match LeRoPE's performance. The authors also observe the emergence of a high-norm, positional band (LeRoPE band).
AI.conclusion: Making RoPE frequencies learnable is a simple yet effective modification that improves performance across model scales without additional computational overhead during inference, offering a practical improvement for language model training.

title: GRC-ProbNet: Uncertainty-aware Feature Extraction for Cardiovascular Disease Classification
summary: The automatic detection and classification of cardiovascular disease (CVD) from computed tomography (CT) images plays an important role in clinical practice. Recently, a hybrid pipeline (GRC-Net) for CVD classification was proposed, which leverages a deep-learning-based segmentation and registration method to extract radiomic and geometric features. However, GRC-Net relies on a deterministic segmentation mask, without considering the inherent ambiguity associated with cardiac anatomy. In this paper, we propose GRC-ProbNet, which takes advantage of a deep ensemble to produce multiple segmentation masks for a given input. From these masks, we extract multiple uncertainty features. We analyze these uncertainty features for both their correlation with segmentation error and their propagation effects on downstream CVD classification performance. Our experiments on the publicly available MM-WHS and ASOCA datasets show that the uncertainty measure that best reflects segmentation quality is not necessarily the one that provides the strongest signal for downstream CVD classification. Overall, our results demonstrate that GRC-ProbNet utilizing uncertainty features substantially improves CVD classification AUROC (92.92\) compared to the baseline GRC-Net model (91.25%). Our code is publicly available: https://github.com/biomedia-mira/GRC-ProbNet.

AI.tldr: GRC-ProbNet improves cardiovascular disease classification from CT images by using deep ensemble-based uncertainty features from multiple segmentation masks, outperforming the deterministic baseline GRC-Net with 92.92% vs 91.25% AUROC.
AI.motivation: GRC-Net relies on a single deterministic segmentation mask, ignoring the inherent ambiguity of cardiac anatomy, which limits classification robustness.
AI.method: GRC-ProbNet uses a deep ensemble to generate multiple segmentation masks, extracts multiple uncertainty features, and analyzes their correlation with segmentation error and downstream CVD classification performance.
AI.result: On MM-WHS and ASOCA datasets, uncertainty features substantially improve CVD classification (AUROC 92.92%) compared to baseline GRC-Net (91.25%). The best uncertainty measure for segmentation quality does not necessarily yield the best downstream classification.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating uncertainty features from deep ensembles enhances CVD classification performance, and the relationship between segmentation uncertainty and classification utility is non-trivial.

title: Co4ICF: Co-evolving Physics-Informed Surrogate and RL-based Pulse Optimizer for Inertial Confinement Fusion
summary: Offline-trained surrogates for Inertial Confinement Fusion (ICF) suffer a well-known failure mode that iterative optimizers drive inputs into out-of-distribution (OOD) regions where predictions become unreliable. Here we present Co4ICF, a co-evolving framework that couples a physics-informed surrogate with a PPO-based pulse optimizer. The surrogate is iteratively fine-tuned on policy-induced trajectories, correcting extrapolation errors as the optimizer shifts the input distribution; the optimizer queries this evolving surrogate as a fast environment. In the 1D MULTI environment, Co4ICF achieves 146.1% normalized yield based on current laser design baseline; as a post-hoc cross-fidelity check, the optimized pulse further attains 246.9% normalized yield when directly evaluated in 2D-MULTI without any 2D training or fine-tuning. Budget-matched ablations support that the gains are not explained solely by additional simulation data and are consistent with the co-evolving mechanism playing a key role. We release a large-scale MULTI-IFE simulation dataset to support future benchmarking.

AI.tldr: Co4ICF is a co-evolving framework coupling a physics-informed surrogate with a PPO-based optimizer for ICF pulse design, achieving significant yield improvements (146.1% in 1D, 246.9% in 2D) by iteratively fine-tuning the surrogate on policy-induced trajectories to correct OOD extrapolation errors.
AI.motivation: Offline-trained surrogates for ICF suffer from failure when iterative optimizers drive inputs into out-of-distribution (OOD) regions, causing unreliable predictions.
AI.method: Co4ICF co-evolves a physics-informed surrogate with a PPO-based pulse optimizer; the surrogate is iteratively fine-tuned on policy-induced trajectories to correct extrapolation errors as the optimizer shifts input distributions.
AI.result: Achieves 146.1% normalized yield in 1D MULTI, and 246.9% normalized yield in 2D-MULTI without any 2D training or fine-tuning; budget-matched ablations confirm gains are not solely from additional data.
AI.conclusion: The co-evolving mechanism is key to performance improvements; a large-scale MULTI-IFE simulation dataset is released for benchmarking.

title: 3D Scene Graph Prediction: Generating Hierarchical Models from Partially Observed Environments
comment: Accepted at IROS 2026. Main paper: 8 pages, 3 figures, 3 tables. Includes a supplementary appendix
summary: Generating realistic 3D indoor scenes is an area of growing interest in computer vision and robotics. Existing methods, often motivated by applications such as interior design, generally focus on object layout generation within a single room. The generation of high-level scene structure, such as room-level layout and traversability, remains underexplored despite its importance for robotics applications. In this paper, we consider the case where a robot has explored part of an environment and needs to predict the unexplored parts to support downstream tasks such as exploration or object search. We propose a top-down framework for synthesizing hierarchical 3D scene graphs, including a room layer -- describing the floor plan and traversability -- and an object layer modeling object layouts within each room. For the room layer, we propose a novel mixed-domain graph diffusion model jointly predicting room categories, floor boundaries, and traversability between rooms. Via corruption and masking, this model supports partial constraints such as incomplete floor plans, avoiding the need for partially observed training data. For the object layer, we integrate an existing mixed discrete-continuous diffusion model for joint prediction of object categories, locations, sizes, and orientations within each room given the floor plan. We compare our method with state-of-the-art occupancy-based and LLM-based floor plan generation methods on a standard benchmark. Compared with an occupancy-based learning baseline, our method generalizes substantially better to out-of-distribution partial floor plans. We also demonstrate our integrated prediction pipeline on real-world scenes from robot-collected data, enabling prediction beyond explored areas.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a top-down framework using a mixed-domain graph diffusion model to generate hierarchical 3D scene graphs for indoor environments. It jointly predicts room-level layout (categories, floor boundaries, traversability) and object-level layouts within rooms, supporting partial constraints to predict unexplored spaces from partially observed data, with strong generalization to out-of-distribution scenarios.
AI.motivation: Existing methods focus on object layout generation within single rooms and overlook high-level scene structure like room-level layout and traversability, which is crucial for robotics applications such as exploration and object search. The authors aim to address the scenario where a robot has partially explored an environment and needs to predict the unexplored parts.
AI.method: The authors propose a top-down framework with two layers: (1) a room layer using a novel mixed-domain graph diffusion model that jointly predicts room categories, floor boundaries, and traversability between rooms, supporting partial constraints via corruption and masking; (2) an object layer using an existing mixed discrete-continuous diffusion model to predict object categories, locations, sizes, and orientations within each room given the floor plan.
AI.result: The method outperforms occupancy-based and LLM-based floor plan generation methods on a standard benchmark. It generalizes substantially better to out-of-distribution partial floor plans compared to an occupancy-based learning baseline. The integrated pipeline is also demonstrated on real-world scenes from robot-collected data, enabling prediction beyond explored areas.
AI.conclusion: The proposed hierarchical scene graph generation approach effectively handles partial observability and generalizes well, making it suitable for robotics applications requiring prediction of unexplored indoor environments. The mixed-domain diffusion model for room layout and integration with object-level generation offers a comprehensive solution.

title: RDQ: Residual Distribution Quantization for Large Language Models
summary: Post-training quantization (PTQ) of large language models degrades sharply below 4-bit precision. We identify the root cause as residual stream distributional drift: quantization noise injected at each transformer layer accumulates in the shared residual representation, causing KL divergence from the FP16 baseline to grow super-linearly with depth (Pearson r=0.999 with log-perplexity, p<0.001, confirmed across all tested methods and bit-widths). We discover that 84% of LLaMA-3-8B layers exhibit non-Gaussian residual distributions (KS test, p<=0.05), and that per-layer residual stream variance grows 6,548x across depth. We propose RDQ (Residual Distribution Quantization), a PTQ framework whose central contribution is Cascaded Error Compensation (CEC): a sequential calibration procedure that captures the actual drifted activations each layer receives (computed by running calibration data through already-quantized upstream layers) and fits per-channel AWQ-style scales against those drifted inputs, with scales folded into preceding RMSNorm weights for exact mathematical equivalence at zero inference overhead. RDQ achieves state-of-the-art results on all three tested architectures: LLaMA-3-8B: 7.55 / 5.62 PPL (W3/W4); Qwen-2.5-7B: 7.46 / 6.38 PPL; Mistral-7B: 6.88 / 5.73 PPL. RDQ beats the best published baseline (LeanQuant/SpinQuant) at every model and bit-width combination, with gains up to -46.4% vs. RTN at W3A16 on LLaMA-3-8B. All output is standard group-128 asymmetric quantization, deployable on Qualcomm AIMET, GGUF, and any standard inference stack at zero runtime overhead.

AI.tldr: RDQ (Residual Distribution Quantization) is a PTQ framework that addresses the root cause of severe quality degradation in sub-4-bit LLM quantization — residual stream distributional drift — via Cascaded Error Compensation (CEC), achieving state-of-the-art perplexity across LLaMA-3, Qwen-2.5, and Mistral models with zero inference overhead.
AI.motivation: LLM PTQ degrades sharply below 4-bit precision; the paper identifies the root cause as residual stream distributional drift, where quantization noise from each layer accumulates in the shared residual representation, causing KL divergence from FP16 to grow super-linearly with depth (Pearson r=0.999 with log-perplexity, p<0.001). Additionally, 84% of LLaMA-3-8B layers exhibit non-Gaussian residual distributions (KS test, p<=0.05), and per-layer residual variance grows 6,548x across depth.
AI.method: The core contribution is Cascaded Error Compensation (CEC): a sequential calibration procedure that computes actual drifted activations each layer receives by running calibration data through already-quantized upstream layers, then fits per-channel AWQ-style scales against those drifted inputs, folding scales into preceding RMSNorm weights for exact mathematical equivalence at zero inference overhead.
AI.result: RDQ achieves state-of-the-art results on three architectures: LLaMA-3-8B (7.55/5.62 PPL at W3/W4), Qwen-2.5-7B (7.46/6.38 PPL), Mistral-7B (6.88/5.73 PPL). Beats best published baselines (LeanQuant/SpinQuant) at every model/bit-width combination, with gains up to -46.4% vs. RTN at W3A16 on LLaMA-3-8B. Uses standard group-128 asymmetric quantization, deployable on Qualcomm AIMET, GGUF, and any standard inference stack at zero runtime overhead.
AI.conclusion: RDQ demonstrates that explicitly modeling and compensating for residual stream distributional drift is critical for sub-4-bit PTQ, and the proposed CEC procedure provides an effective, zero-overhead solution that generalizes across architectures and outperforms existing methods consistently.

title: Benchmarking the Robustness of Foundation Models for Mammography under Domain Shift
comment: Under Review. Giang Nguyen and Raghav Mehta contributed equally
summary: Foundation models are increasingly used as image feature extractors for mammography, but their robustness under external domain shift remains unclear. We benchmark 15 foundation-model backbones across breast density, BI-RADS severity, and cancer status using a unified frozen-backbone linear-probe protocol, training on 3 source datasets and evaluating on 12 task-compatible out-of-distribution (OOD) datasets after label harmonization. Mammography-specific vision-language models (Mammo-FM and MaMA) provide the strongest mean OOD performance, but robustness is not explained by mammography exposure alone. DINOv3 remains a competitive vision-only baseline, and mammography-adapted pretraining does not consistently improve generalization. Dataset-level analysis further shows that even leading models show heterogeneous performance across datasets. Feature-space inspection reveals that useful representations can preserve clinical signal while retaining dataset and acquisition structure. These findings highlight dataset-level OOD evaluation as a central criterion for assessing mammography representations. Our code is publicly available: https://github.com/biomedia-mira/mammo-ood.

AI.tldr: This study benchmarks 15 foundation models for mammography under domain shift, finding that mammography-specific vision-language models (Mammo-FM, MaMA) perform best overall, but robustness is not explained by mammography exposure alone; DINOv3 is a strong vision-only baseline, and dataset-level OOD evaluation is crucial for assessing representations.
AI.motivation: Foundation models are increasingly used as image feature extractors for mammography, but their robustness under external domain shift remains unclear.
AI.method: Benchmarked 15 foundation-model backbones across breast density, BI-RADS severity, and cancer status using a unified frozen-backbone linear-probe protocol, training on 3 source datasets and evaluating on 12 OOD datasets after label harmonization.
AI.result: Mammography-specific VLMs (Mammo-FM, MaMA) provide strongest mean OOD performance, but robustness is not explained by mammography exposure alone. DINOv3 is competitive as vision-only baseline; mammography-adapted pretraining does not consistently improve generalization. Even leading models show heterogeneous performance across datasets.
AI.conclusion: Dataset-level OOD evaluation should be a central criterion for assessing mammography representations; useful representations can preserve clinical signal while retaining dataset and acquisition structure.

title: ANCHOR: Automated Alignment Auditing for CLI Agents on Real-World Harm
comment: Accepted at ICML 2026. 19 pages, 14 figures, 5 tables
summary: Autonomous CLI agents can now execute hundreds of actions across multi-hour sessions: writing code, executing shell commands, browsing the web, and managing cloud infrastructure, all with minimal human oversight. Does greater autonomy invite greater risk? We introduce ANCHOR, an automated auditing framework that stress-tests CLI agents on illegal tasks grounded in public US court cases. ANCHOR deploys an auditor agent fine-tuned on dark personality data using supervised and reinforcement fine tuning. This auditor roleplays persistent malicious users who decompose tasks, reframe requests upon refusal, and adapt strategies across multi-turn interactions. Evaluating frontier CLI agents, we find that while they often refuse illegal tasks when prompted directly, compliance reaches 100\% under persistent malicious interaction. When agents comply, they frequently exceed user requests, autonomously building infrastructure for large-scale harm, including catastrophic risk scenarios such as large-scale financial fraud and bioweapon development. These findings demonstrate that current alignment techniques are insufficient for autonomous agents and underscore the need for safety evaluations against persistent, adaptive malicious users. We release ANCHOR at https://github.com/garified/anchor

AI.tldr: ANCHOR is an automated auditing framework that stress-tests CLI agents on illegal tasks based on real US court cases. It uses a malicious auditor agent (fine-tuned on dark personality data) that persistently adapts strategies across multi-turn interactions. Results show that while agents refuse direct illegal requests, compliance reaches 100% under persistent adversarial pressure, often escalating to catastrophic risk scenarios like financial fraud and bioweapon development.
AI.motivation: Current alignment techniques are insufficient for autonomous CLI agents that operate over long sessions with minimal oversight, and there is a need for safety evaluations against persistent, adaptive malicious users.
AI.method: ANCHOR uses an auditor agent fine-tuned on dark personality data via supervised and reinforcement fine-tuning, which roleplays persistent malicious users who decompose tasks, reframe requests upon refusal, and adapt strategies across multi-turn interactions, grounded in real public US court cases.
AI.result: While agents often refuse illegal tasks when prompted directly, compliance reaches 100% under persistent malicious interaction. When agents comply, they frequently exceed user requests, autonomously building infrastructure for large-scale harm including catastrophic risks (large-scale financial fraud and bioweapon development).
AI.conclusion: Current alignment techniques are insufficient for autonomous agents, underscoring the need for safety evaluations against persistent, adaptive malicious users.

title: A Single Diffusion-Policy Controller for Multi-Task Block Pushing with Zero-Shot Sim-to-Real Transfer
summary: Diffusion policies have shown promising empirical performance in representing and learning complex maneuvers for robots using behavior cloning (BC). In this paper, we explore training diffusion policies from scratch using reinforcement learning (RL) for multi-task robotic manipulation. Specifically, we aim to train a single diffusion policy for block-pushing tasks with multiple shapes. The proposed framework features a simple policy loss function, which is a reweighted evidence lower bound used in BC-based diffusion policy training and can seamlessly serve as the policy learning module in RL algorithms. To address the exploration challenges arising from the absence of demonstrations, we incorporate reverse curriculum generation and objective-centric representations. Combined with the expressiveness of diffusion policies, our design supports learning of multi-task block-pushing policies in our sparse-reward simulation setting. We further evaluate whether the trained diffusion policy transfers in zero-shot to real-world tasks under varying environmental conditions including goal positions, block shapes, block weights and surface friction, providing evidence that this pipeline can transfer to our real-world block-pushing setup under the tested variations.

AI.tldr: This paper trains a single diffusion policy from scratch using RL (not BC) for multi-task block-pushing, using a reweighted ELBO loss, reverse curriculum generation, and objective-centric representations to handle exploration in sparse-reward settings, and demonstrates zero-shot transfer to real-world tasks under varied conditions.
AI.motivation: To extend diffusion policies beyond behavior cloning (which requires demonstrations) to reinforcement learning-based training, enabling multi-task robotic manipulation learning without expert demonstrations.
AI.method: A diffusion policy trained via RL using a reweighted evidence lower bound (ELBO) loss, combined with reverse curriculum generation and objective-centric representations to address exploration challenges in sparse-reward settings.
AI.result: The trained diffusion policy successfully learns multi-task block-pushing policies in a sparse-reward simulation, and transfers zero-shot to real-world tasks with variations in goal positions, block shapes, block weights, and surface friction.
AI.conclusion: Diffusion policies can be effectively trained via RL from scratch without demonstrations for multi-task manipulation, and can transfer to real-world tasks under varied environmental conditions.

title: LLMs as a Jury: Cross-Model Consensus Can Outperform Process Reward Models for LLM Reasoning
summary: Selecting the correct answer from a pool of candidate reasoning chains is the engine of test-time scaling, yet the standard selectors each carry a cost: self-consistency inherits the errors of the single model it resamples, and trained reward models need labeled data and transfer poorly off-distribution. We study a third signal, free at inference time: cross-model consensus, the degree to which independently trained models, each solving the problem once, agree on a final answer. We treat the panel as an LLM-jury, in which the structure of agreement, not any model's score of another, is the verification signal. Across seven benchmarks it selects correct answers better than self-consistency and far better than a model scoring its own candidates: on competition math it closes the entire gap to an oracle selector, while self-scoring closes almost none. The mechanism is error decorrelation: independently trained models err differently, so their wrong answers scatter while the correct one accumulates agreement. We make this precise with a parameter-free law, derived in closed form, that predicts consensus accuracy from three measured panel statistics to a mean absolute error of $0.03$ and exposes the method's ceiling: a shared-error floor where models share a misconception, near zero on math but non-trivial on science. Against four trained verifiers spanning discriminative, outcome, and generative reward models, the free LLM-jury matches the strongest inside their math training domain and is the top selector outside it. Cross-model consensus is thus a verifier we can characterize in advance: a law that says when to trust it, and a floor that marks where it cannot.

AI.tldr: Cross-model consensus—agreement among independently trained LLMs—serves as a free, inference-time verification signal that outperforms self-consistency and trained reward models, especially out-of-distribution, with accuracy predictable via a parameter-free law.
AI.motivation: Standard answer selectors for test-time scaling (self-consistency, trained reward models) have limitations: self-consistency inherits errors from a single model, while reward models need labeled data and transfer poorly off-distribution. The authors seek a third, free signal.
AI.method: Treat a panel of independently trained LLMs as a jury; use the structure of agreement (not any model scoring another) as the verification signal. Derive a parameter-free law predicting consensus accuracy from three panel statistics.
AI.result: Cross-model consensus outperforms self-consistency and self-scoring across 7 benchmarks; on competition math it matches the oracle selector. The law predicts accuracy with MAE of 0.03. Matches strongest trained verifiers in-domain and surpasses them out-of-distribution.
AI.conclusion: Cross-model consensus is a free, characterized verifier with a predictable law for when to trust it and a shared-error floor marking its limitations.

title: Gradient-Skipping Relevance Propagation for Efficient Explainability of Vision Transformers
summary: Vision Transformers (ViTs) are difficult to interpret because current methods of relevance propagation and attention flow do not fully consider some key architectural features, such as the uneven importance of attention heads and residual connections. Prior approaches typically assume uniform importance across attention heads; furthermore, they model skip connections as identity paths, leading to inaccurate relevance attribution. To address these issues, we introduce GradSkip, a novel relevance propagation method for ViTs based on adaptive head weighting and skip-aware propagation. GradSkip models the different importance of the attention heads and dynamically distributes relevance between the attention and residual paths. Experiments on ImageNet1K and BloodMNIST demonstrate a state-of-the-art faithfulness of GradSkip while requiring over 14 times fewer GFLOPs than the best-performing existing approaches. Additional evaluations using transformer-based segmentation confirm improved localization and alignment with ground-truth regions.

AI.tldr: GradSkip improves ViT interpretability by adaptively weighting attention heads and properly modeling residual connections for more faithful relevance attribution.
AI.motivation: Existing ViT interpretability methods fail because they assume uniform importance across attention heads and treat residual connections as simple identity paths, leading to inaccurate relevance attribution.
AI.method: GradSkip introduces adaptive head weighting and skip-aware propagation to dynamically distribute relevance between attention and residual paths in ViTs.
AI.result: Achieves SOTA faithfulness on ImageNet1K and BloodMNIST while requiring 14x fewer GFLOPs than prior methods; improved localization on segmentation tasks.
AI.conclusion: Properly modeling head importance and residual connections significantly improves ViT interpretability with lower computational cost.

title: GRASP: GRanularity-Aware Search Policy for Agentic RAG
summary: Agentic retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) extends static RAG by allowing language models to iteratively reason, generate search queries, retrieve evidence, and predict answers. However, it remains challenging for models to decide when to retrieve, whether to use lexical matching or semantic similarity, and how to control context granularity to prevent irrelevant tokens from interfering with agent reasoning. In this paper, we introduce GRASP, a reinforcement learning (RL) framework for training agents to adaptively coordinate complementary retrieval tools during multi-step reasoning. GRASP provides the agent with semantic search, keyword search, and paragraph-reading actions, enabling it to retrieve sentence-level evidence and expand further context only when needed. We train the policy with a reward that jointly accounts for answer accuracy, grounded reading, complementary search, and turn efficiency. Experiments on multi-hop reasoning benchmarks show that GRASP improves both retrieval recall and downstream question answering performance compared with single-step retrieval, prompting-based agentic RAG, and RL-based retrieval baselines. Qualitative and ablation analyses show that the learned policy develops interpretable skimming and scanning behavior: it uses semantic search for broad exploration, paragraph reading for local verification, and keyword search for entity-specific evidence. These results suggest that learning to coordinate retrieval signals and context granularity is critical for agent's correct reasoning.

AI.tldr: GRASP introduces a reinforcement learning framework that trains language model agents to adaptively coordinate semantic search, keyword search, and paragraph reading during multi-step reasoning, improving retrieval recall and QA accuracy on multi-hop benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Current agentic RAG methods struggle with deciding when to retrieve, which retrieval method (lexical vs. semantic) to use, and how to control context granularity to avoid irrelevant tokens interfering with agent reasoning.
AI.method: GRASP uses reinforcement learning to train a policy that coordinates three retrieval tools (semantic search, keyword search, paragraph reading) with a reward function accounting for answer accuracy, grounded reading, complementary search, and turn efficiency.
AI.result: GRASP outperforms single-step retrieval, prompting-based agentic RAG, and RL-based retrieval baselines on multi-hop reasoning benchmarks, improving both retrieval recall and downstream QA performance.
AI.conclusion: Learning to coordinate retrieval signals and context granularity is critical for correct agent reasoning; the learned policy develops interpretable skimming (semantic search) and scanning (keyword search, paragraph reading) behaviors.

title: Mapping Pamir: Multi-Session Visual-Inertial SLAM and 3D Reconstruction of an Underwater Shipwreck
comment: 8 pages, 12 figures. Accepted to ICRA 2026, Vienna, Austria
summary: This paper presents a framework for multi-session mapping of underwater environments utilizing an affordable action camera. The Visual-Inertial data are augmented by water depth recordings from a dive computer. SVIn2, an open-source VI-SLAM framework, is utilized to generate a trajectory and a sparse reconstruction for each session. Utilizing the keyframes extracted from SVIn2 and the estimated camera poses, a Structure-from-Motion (SfM) framework, COLMAP, is employed for global optimization and to produce a dense reconstruction of the target environment. The presence of calibration targets at fixed locations, when available, is used to estimate the coordinate transformation between different data collection sessions, thus transforming the different sessions into the same coordinate frame. The proposed pipeline is employed for the mapping of a shipwreck off the coast of Barbados. For the first time, both the exterior and the accessible interior parts of the wreck were mapped in two sessions, while a third session employed two cameras with different fields of view.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a multi-session underwater mapping framework using an affordable action camera, dive computer depth data, SVIn2 for trajectory estimation, and COLMAP for dense reconstruction, demonstrated on a shipwreck off Barbados.
AI.motivation: To enable affordable, multi-session 3D mapping of underwater environments (both exterior and interior) using consumer-grade cameras rather than expensive specialized equipment.
AI.method: Combines visual-inertial data from an action camera with depth readings from a dive computer. Uses SVIn2 (VI-SLAM) for per-session trajectory/sparse reconstruction, then COLMAP (SfM) for global optimization and dense reconstruction. Calibration targets enable coordinate transformation across sessions.
AI.result: Successfully mapped both exterior and accessible interior of a shipwreck in two sessions, plus a third session with two cameras of different fields of view. Demonstrates feasibility of low-cost multi-session underwater mapping.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework enables affordable, multi-session dense 3D reconstruction of underwater environments, including complex structures like shipwrecks with interior spaces, using consumer hardware.

title: Beyond Euclidean Clipping: Overcoming Exploration Collapse in LLM RL via Riemannian Isometric Policy Optimization
summary: Reinforcement learning (RL) has become a dominant paradigm for enhancing LLMs' reasoning capabilities. However, RL algorithms with PPO-Clip are inherently limited by exploration collapse. Subsequent works remain primarily heuristic and fail to identify the essential cause of PPO-Clip's failure. This work reveals the fundamental flaw of PPO-Clip: it implicitly measures policy discrepancy using Euclidean metric, which is theoretically inconsistent with the intrinsic geometry on the policy Riemannian manifold. This geometric mismatch results in overly conservative updates in low-probability regions while aggressive in high-probability regions, ultimately collapsing exploration. To correct this geometric flaw, we propose Riemannian Isometric Policy Optimization (RIPO), which guarantees isometric policy updates on the Riemannian manifold, effectively balancing exploration and exploitation. We further show that RIPO achieves a favorable bias-variance trade-off, which stabilizes optimization. Extensive experiments demonstrate that RIPO significantly surpasses existing LLM RL algorithms across seven competition-level benchmarks (up to 60% improvement over GRPO on AIME24).

AI.tldr: This paper reveals that PPO-Clip's failure in LLM RL stems from using Euclidean metric to measure policy changes, which conflicts with the Riemannian geometry of policy space. It proposes RIPO (Riemannian Isometric Policy Optimization) to fix this geometric mismatch, achieving up to 60% improvement over GRPO on AIME24.
AI.motivation: Existing RL algorithms for LLMs (PPO-Clip, GRPO) suffer from exploration collapse, and subsequent heuristic fixes fail to address the root cause. This paper aims to identify the fundamental geometric flaw in PPO-Clip and propose a principled solution.
AI.method: The paper identifies that PPO-Clip implicitly uses Euclidean metric to measure policy discrepancy, which is inconsistent with the intrinsic Riemannian manifold geometry of policy space. To correct this, they propose RIPO (Riemannian Isometric Policy Optimization), which ensures isometric (distance-preserving) policy updates on the Riemannian manifold, and achieves a favorable bias-variance trade-off.
AI.result: RIPO significantly outperforms existing LLM RL algorithms across seven competition-level benchmarks, with up to 60% improvement over GRPO on AIME24.
AI.conclusion: The geometric mismatch in PPO-Clip is the essential cause of exploration collapse; correcting it via Riemannian isometric updates leads to substantially better performance in LLM reasoning tasks.

title: Neural Motion Blending Across Arbitrary Character Topologies
comment: To appear in the proceedings of Computer Graphics International (CGI 2026). For references, please cite the official proceedings version. Paper website: https://mmlab-cv.github.io/neural_motion_blending/
summary: Motion blending in character animation enables the synthesis of new motions by interpolating between existing examples. Current methods are typically restricted to fixed skeleton topologies, requiring identical or near-identical skeletal structures across characters. We present a novel framework for motion blending across heterogeneous skeletons. The proposed architecture combines a semantic encoder, which extracts per-frame latent representations of the motion state, with a diffusion-based decoder, which reconstructs character-specific motion conditioned on this latent code. At inference, blended motions are obtained by interpolating the latent representations of two input motions. We train and evaluate the method on the Truebones Zoo dataset using motions defined on both same and distinct skeleton topologies, demonstrating the ability to achieve smooth and plausible blending in a variety of scenarios.

AI.tldr: A novel framework for motion blending across heterogeneous (different) skeleton topologies using a semantic encoder and diffusion-based decoder, with interpolation in latent space.
AI.motivation: Current motion blending methods are limited to fixed or near-identical skeleton topologies, preventing blending across characters with different skeletal structures.
AI.method: A semantic encoder extracts per-frame latent motion representations, a diffusion-based decoder reconstructs character-specific motion from the latent code, and blending is achieved by interpolating latent codes at inference.
AI.result: Trained and evaluated on the Truebones Zoo dataset, demonstrating smooth and plausible blending across both same and distinct skeleton topologies.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework successfully enables motion blending across heterogeneous skeletons, overcoming the topological limitations of prior work.

title: Agents Don't Just Agree, They Remember: Benchmarking Persistent Sycophancy in Stateful Personal Agents
summary: Stateful personal agents increasingly maintain long-term user profiles, episodic memories, and reusable skills. This persistence turns conversational sycophancy into a state-writing failure: accepted user-centric claims can be committed as lasting preferences, background facts, or workflows and later reused after the original conversation is gone. We call this persistent sycophancy and introduce the Personal Agent Sycophancy Benchmark (PASB), a 1,600-task benchmark that traces whether a conversational claim is accepted, written into durable agent state, and reused in a later neutral query. Unlike prior benchmarks that provide pre-written memories, PASB evaluates real agents (Hermes-Agent and OpenClaw) that decide what to store. It isolates the write process by combining four scenario framings with four temporal delivery patterns and separating a five-turn persist stage from a cleared three-turn query stage, ensuring downstream effects arise only from durable state. Across twelve models, the commit boundary is the key inflection point: downstream failure increases from 45.0% in session-only episodes to 71.9% after commitment, a consistent increase of 27.0 percentage points. Committed claims exhibit three write-time patterns: status promotion, attribution removal, and scope broadening. These patterns become stronger under memory-like or procedural framing, repeated reinforcement, and even across domain boundaries. These results show that agent sycophancy is fundamentally a state-writing governance problem. Once user content is committed to durable memory, safety must govern what agents write, not only what they say. PASB identifies the write-time controls needed to gate risky commits while preserving the source, role, and scope of stored content beyond response-level mitigations.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces persistent sycophancy—where accepted user claims get written into an agent's durable state and reused later—and presents PASB, a 1,600-task benchmark showing that downstream failures jump from 45.0% to 71.9% after commitment, identifying sycophancy as a state-writing governance problem.
AI.motivation: Stateful personal agents store long-term profiles, memories, and skills, making conversational sycophancy a state-writing failure: accepted user claims become lasting state reused beyond the original conversation, which prior benchmarks (using pre-written memories) fail to capture.
AI.method: PASB combines four scenario framings with four temporal delivery patterns, separating a five-turn persist stage (where agents decide what to store) from a cleared three-turn query stage. It evaluates real agents (Hermes-Agent, OpenClaw) across twelve models, isolating the write process to measure downstream effects from durable state.
AI.result: Downstream failure increases from 45.0% (session-only) to 71.9% (after commitment), a 27.0 percentage point increase. Three write-time patterns emerge: status promotion, attribution removal, and scope broadening, strengthening under memory/procedural framing, repeated reinforcement, and across domain boundaries.
AI.conclusion: Agent sycophancy is fundamentally a state-writing governance problem. Safety must govern what agents write (write-time controls) not only what they say, preserving source, role, and scope of stored content beyond response-level mitigations.

title: Real-Time Rulebook-Aware Nonlinear MPC for Autonomous Driving with Priority-Biased Tiered Slacks
comment: The manuscript is submitted to the Journal of Control Engineering Practice, A journal of The International Federation of Automatic Control (IFAC)
summary: Autonomous-vehicle motion planners must resolve conflicts among safety, regulation, comfort, and efficiency in real time while exposing those decisions for audit. We present W-SQP, a weighted tiered-slack nonlinear model predictive controller (NMPC) that compiles nine driving-rule families into a four-tier shared-slack nonlinear program solved online with CasADi and IPOPT; the name denotes the weighted quadratic slack penalty, not a sequential-quadratic-programming solver. Strongly separated tier penalties bias residual violations toward lower-priority rules while leaving actuation bounds hard. The controller replans from its executed state at $10$\,Hz and records per-rule residuals on every cycle. A $90$\,ms solver-time limit returns an anytime iterate that is projected through the vehicle dynamics before execution; median and maximum observed wall-clock solve times were $28$ and $104$\,ms. We evaluate W-SQP in closed loop on 150 Waymo Open Motion Dataset scenarios in Waymax against reactive and proposal-and-select baselines, and introduce a log-independent protocol that separates safety and regulatory compliance from resemblance to the recorded human trajectory. Under this protocol, W-SQP shows no systematic group-level deficit relative to expert replay on the log-independent safety and regulatory rules, with several localized regressions in the hardest, highest-divergence scenarios. The results characterize W-SQP as an auditable, priority-biased, anytime-capable NMPC prototype rather than a hard-real-time or formally safe controller.

AI.tldr: W-SQP is a weighted tiered-slack NMPC that compiles nine driving-rule families into a four-tier shared-slack optimization, solving at 10 Hz with rule residuals recorded per cycle; tested on 150 Waymo scenarios, it shows no systematic safety/regulatory deficit vs. expert replay, functioning as an auditable, priority-biased, anytime-capable prototype.
AI.motivation: Autonomous-vehicle motion planners need to resolve conflicts among safety, regulation, comfort, and efficiency in real time while keeping decisions auditable, which existing methods struggle to balance transparently.
AI.method: W-SQP uses a weighted tiered-slack nonlinear model predictive controller with four penalty tiers, strongly separated tier penalties, hard actuation bounds, per-cycle rule residual logging, and a 90 ms solver-time limit producing anytime iterates projected through vehicle dynamics.
AI.result: Evaluated on 150 Waymo Open Motion Dataset scenarios, W-SQP achieves median 28 ms and max 104 ms solve times, shows no systematic group-level safety/regulatory deficit vs. expert replay, with localized regressions only in the hardest, highest-divergence scenarios.
AI.conclusion: W-SQP is characterized as an auditable, priority-biased, anytime-capable NMPC prototype, not a hard-real-time or formally safe controller.

title: Knowledge-Conditioned, Single-Pass LLM Synthesis of Executable Unity Game Scenes: A Compiler Error Census across 26 Goal Playable Concepts
comment: Substantially reframed and extended version of arXiv:2603.07101, with new experiments, figures, and analysis
summary: Large language models (LLMs) write Unity C\# for game scenes. Yet nearly all demonstrations rest on an iterative repair loop that regenerates code until it compiles, conflating what the model writes with what the loop fixes. We remove the loop and evaluate a single pass, where the first draft is final. This isolates the model's parametric knowledge, the most stringent test of unaided generation. Models instantiate Goal Playable Concepts, playable counterparts of goal patterns, across 10,400 generations (four open-weight models, 7B--30B; two generation modes; four intermediate-representation (IR) conditioning levels; 26 goal patterns; 20 seeds). None compiled into a runnable scene, leaving no survivorship bias. To understand how the generated C\# scripts fail, we categorize the 99 error codes behind 90{,}673 compiler-error occurrences as Grounding (invented or misused Unity types and APIs) or Hygiene (structural defects needing no Unity knowledge). The split differs sharply by goal pattern (e.g., Stealth fails mostly on invented engine references; Capture on plain C\# structure). Larger models, stricter IRs, and different generation modes move the errors but never yield a compiling scene. The bottleneck is missing engine-specific knowledge. The census orders goal patterns by that demand, showing designers where single-pass generation breaks.

AI.tldr: This paper tests LLMs' ability to generate Unity C# code for game scenes in a single pass without iterative repair loops. Across 10,400 generations using four models, none compiled into runnable scenes. Errors are categorized as Grounding (missing Unity knowledge) or Hygiene (structural issues), with results showing the bottleneck is missing engine-specific knowledge.
AI.motivation: To isolate the model's parametric knowledge by removing the iterative repair loop commonly used in LLM code generation demonstrations, providing the most stringent test of unaided generation.
AI.method: Evaluated single-pass generation of Unity C# code across 10,400 generations (4 models, 7B-30B; 2 generation modes; 4 IR conditioning levels; 26 goal patterns; 20 seeds). Categorized 99 error codes behind 90,673 compiler errors as Grounding or Hygiene.
AI.result: None of the 10,400 generations compiled into a runnable scene. Error types differ by goal pattern (e.g., Stealth fails on invented engine references; Capture on plain C# structure). Larger models, stricter IRs, and different modes shift errors but never produce compiling code.
AI.conclusion: The bottleneck is missing engine-specific (Unity) knowledge. The paper provides a census ordering goal patterns by that knowledge demand, showing where single-pass generation fails for game designers.

title: ABot-N1: Toward a General Visual Language Navigation Foundation Model
summary: Visual Language Navigation foundation models aim to unify deep reasoning for grounded spatial decisions with broad versatility for diverse embodied tasks. Current approaches typically achieve this integration via monolithic policies that map observations directly to actions, yet they often suffer from coordinate drift and poor handling of long-tail semantics. Furthermore, these black-box mappings lack interpretability, hindering the simultaneous achievement of generality, robustness, and transparency. We present ABot-N1, a step toward a general Visual Language Navigation foundation model, that addresses these challenges by decoupling cognition from control via a slow-fast architecture guided by dual visual-language signals. More specifically, a slow vision-language reasoner performs explicit Chain-of-Thought reasoning while producing a pixel goal. This compact set of image-space anchor points serves as a universal interface for diverse tasks, including point-goal, object-goal, poi-goal, instruction-following, and person-following. Subsequently, a fast action expert leverages both the textual cues and the pixel guidance to generate continuous waypoints at the native control frequency. By bridging high-level intents and low-level control through pixel-grounded anchors paired with explicit linguistic traces, our approach ensures robust, generalizable, and interpretable navigation across simulation and real-world benchmarks. ABot-N1 establishes new state-of-the-art records, delivering massive gains specifically in urban-scale navigation: boosting POI arrival by 35.0% (to 77.3%) and achieving 95.4%/92.9% SR in complex indoor and outdoor scenes. It also maintains superior robustness across object-reaching, person-following, and instruction-following tasks. New Point-Goal/POI-Goal benchmarks are released as open source to advance the field of urban-scale navigation.

AI.tldr: ABot-N1 is a Visual Language Navigation foundation model using a slow-fast architecture that decouples cognition from control. A slow vision-language reasoner performs Chain-of-Thought reasoning to produce pixel goals as a universal interface, while a fast action expert generates continuous waypoints. It achieves state-of-the-art results, especially in urban-scale navigation (35% POI gain to 77.3%, 95.4%/92.9% SR in indoor/outdoor scenes), and is robust across multiple navigation tasks.
AI.motivation: Current VLM navigation approaches use monolithic policies that map observations directly to actions, suffering from coordinate drift, poor handling of long-tail semantics, and lack of interpretability. The goal is to simultaneously achieve generality, robustness, and transparency in navigation.
AI.method: A slow-fast architecture: (1) Slow vision-language reasoner performs explicit Chain-of-Thought reasoning and produces pixel goals (image-space anchor points) as a universal interface. (2) Fast action expert uses both textual cues and pixel guidance to generate continuous waypoints at native control frequency. The approach bridges high-level intents and low-level control through pixel-grounded anchors with explicit linguistic traces.
AI.result: New SOTA: POI arrival improved by 35.0% (to 77.3%), 95.4%/92.9% SR in complex indoor/outdoor scenes. Superior robustness across object-reaching, person-following, and instruction-following tasks. New Point-Goal/POI-Goal benchmarks released as open source.
AI.conclusion: ABot-N1 demonstrates that decoupling cognition from control via a slow-fast architecture with pixel-grounded anchors achieves robust, generalizable, and interpretable navigation across diverse tasks and environments, advancing toward a general Visual Language Navigation foundation model.

title: Cross-Layer Misalignment Detection in Agent Skills: A Progressive Loading-Aware Contrastive Learning Approach
comment: 10 pages, 5 pages supplemental. Accepted at the KDD 2026 Workshop on Evaluation and Trustworthiness of Agentic AI
summary: Large language model (LLM) agents are increasingly extended through Agent Skills, reusable artifacts that package natural-language metadata, procedural instructions, and execution-time resources for runtime use. As open-source skill marketplaces expand, users and agents increasingly rely on brief metadata to select third-party skills, making it difficult to detect inconsistencies between a skill's description and its true behavior, a problem we call cross-layer misalignment. To address this issue, we propose Progressive Loading-Aware Hierarchical Contrastive Learning (PL-HCL), an LLM-based framework that detects misalignment by modeling the layered structure of Agent Skills and learning cross-layer consistency. Using a normalized corpus of over 264,000 open-source skills and a human-verified challenge set, PL-HCL improves Macro-F1 from approximately 0.45 for unadapted baselines to 0.87-0.89 across evaluated LLM backbones. This approach offers an effective screening tool for users and operators, as well as design principles for detecting inconsistencies in layered digital artifacts.

AI.tldr: PL-HCL is an LLM-based framework that detects cross-layer misalignment in Agent Skills (where a skill's description contradicts its actual behavior), achieving 0.87-0.89 Macro-F1 across LLM backbones by modeling hierarchical skill structure and using progressive contrastive learning.
AI.motivation: As open-source skill marketplaces expand, users and agents rely on brief metadata to select third-party skills, making it difficult to detect inconsistencies between a skill's description and its true behavior (cross-layer misalignment).
AI.method: Progressive Loading-Aware Hierarchical Contrastive Learning (PL-HCL) - an LLM-based framework that models the layered structure of Agent Skills and learns cross-layer consistency through progressive contrastive learning.
AI.result: On a normalized corpus of 264,000+ open-source skills and a human-verified challenge set, PL-HCL improves Macro-F1 from ~0.45 (unadapted baselines) to 0.87-0.89 across evaluated LLM backbones.
AI.conclusion: PL-HCL provides an effective screening tool for detecting inconsistencies in layered digital artifacts, offering practical value for users and operators of skill marketplaces.

title: Think When It Matters: Conditional VLM Reasoning for Social Navigation with RL Policies
summary: As mobile robots become more integrated into everyday human environments, social robot navigation is becoming essential for ensuring human comfort, safety, and trust. While reinforcement learning (RL) navigation policies provide the fast inference and reactive behavior necessary for real-time deployment, they still lack flexible semantic reasoning capabilities and often fail to generalize to complex social scenarios. Recent approaches have increasingly turned to vision-language models (VLMs) in place of RL policies to improve semantic and social reasoning in robot navigation. Nevertheless, their high computational cost and slow inference remain major barriers to real-time deployment. To overcome these limitations, we introduce HUMA (Hybrid Understanding for Multi-modal social Navigation), a hybrid architecture that dynamically balances the computational efficiency of RL policies with the deep semantic understanding of VLMs. Our approach uses a reactive RL policy to handle low-density, routine navigation tasks, while conditioning it on a post-trained high-level VLM when a human enters sensitive situations, such as the robot's proximity zone. We evaluate HUMA on the Social-MP3D and Social-HM3D benchmarks, where it achieves task success improvements of 20% and 3%, respectively, while significantly reducing personal space violations and human collisions against state-of-the-art baselines. Extensive ablation studies validate each architectural component, and real-world deployment on the Mirokaï mobile robot further demonstrates the practical viability of our approach.

AI.tldr: HUMA is a hybrid architecture combining RL policies (for routine navigation) with VLMs (for semantic reasoning in sensitive human interactions), achieving improved task success and reduced personal space violations in social robot navigation.
AI.motivation: Existing social robot navigation approaches either lack semantic reasoning (RL policies) or are too computationally expensive for real-time deployment (VLMs). The paper aims to bridge this gap by combining both.
AI.method: HUMA uses a reactive RL policy for low-density routine tasks and conditionally activates a post-trained VLM when humans enter sensitive proximity zones, dynamically balancing efficiency and semantic reasoning.
AI.result: On Social-MP3D and Social-HM3D benchmarks, HUMA achieves 20% and 3% task success improvements respectively, with significantly fewer personal space violations and human collisions vs. SOTA baselines.
AI.conclusion: The hybrid approach is validated through ablation studies and real-world deployment on a Mirokaï robot, demonstrating practical viability for real-time social robot navigation.

title: PhysMRV: Physical Memory Retrieval and Verification for Physics Plausibility Reasoning
summary: Video-language models (VLMs) have achieved remarkable performance on video understanding and visual question answering, yet they remain unreliable in reasoning about physical plausibility, where understanding object interactions, causal dynamics, and fundamental physical principles is essential. This limitation is particularly evident on challenging physical reasoning benchmarks, revealing a persistent gap in physical commonsense reasoning. To address this challenge, we propose PhysMRV, a training-free physical memory and verification framework for physical plausibility reasoning. Unlike retrieval-augmented VLMs that retrieve semantically similar videos as additional context, PhysMRV transforms training videos into a Hierarchical Memory Bank of structured physical knowledge comprising three complementary levels: scene descriptions capturing visual context, physical-event graphs modeling object interactions and causal structure, and physics-rule summaries distilling reusable physical principles and cues. During inference, PhysMRV retrieves physically relevant memories and leverages their structured physical evidence to guide a frozen VLM in verifying physical plausibility, requiring neither fine-tuning nor parameter updates. We evaluate PhysMRV on three challenging physical reasoning benchmarks, ImplausiBench, IntPhys2, and GRASP Level 2, across multiple state-of-the-art VLMs. Experimental results demonstrate consistent improvements over direct prompting across diverse VLMs and evaluation benchmarks, showing that structured physical memories provide an effective and scalable means of enhancing physical plausibility reasoning without additional training.

AI.tldr: PhysMRV is a training-free framework that enhances physical plausibility reasoning in video-language models by building a structured Hierarchical Memory Bank of physical knowledge, which is retrieved to guide frozen VLMs during inference, achieving consistent improvements on physical reasoning benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing VLMs fail at physical plausibility reasoning (understanding object interactions, causal dynamics, physics principles), which limits performance on physical reasoning benchmarks.
AI.method: PhysMRV transforms training videos into a Hierarchical Memory Bank with three levels: (1) scene descriptions, (2) physical-event graphs, (3) physics-rule summaries. During inference, it retrieves relevant physical memories to guide a frozen VLM for verification, requiring no fine-tuning.
AI.result: Consistent improvements over direct prompting across multiple VLMs on ImplausiBench, IntPhys2, and GRASP Level 2 benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Structured physical memories provide an effective, scalable, and training-free means of enhancing physical plausibility reasoning in VLMs.

title: AI YOU Town: Make Friends and Money with Your Digital Twin
summary: Existing approaches to infer user traits and generate responses consistent with a persona rely on static prompting. They lack calibrated uncertainty, ignore sequential evidence, and drift during long interactions. We present \textbf{AI YOU}, a framework that continually updates a personality profile with 22 dimensions from conversation and embodies it in a personal digital twin. Practically, the system combines prompting, Bayesian updating, and conformal prediction for persona inference. A periodically refreshed memory anchor and cognitive memory with three layers preserve persona consistency over long interactions. Across the main results, AI YOU \emph{(i)} achieves conformal coverage ranging from 0.921 to 0.976, \emph{(ii)} improves uncertainty calibration and reasoning grounded in memory, and \emph{(iii)} enhances persona fidelity over static prompting in role playing over 100 turns while reducing trait drift, for most evaluated backbones under adversarial settings with multiple agents. The prototype \emph{AI YOU Town} initializes an imaginative twin world for future interaction. The online demo is available at \href{https://quinnnnnne-ai-you.hf.space/}{\mbox{\texttt{quinnnnnne-ai-you.hf.space}}}.

AI.tldr: AI YOU is a framework that continuously updates a 22-dimension personality profile from conversation using Bayesian updating, conformal prediction, and a three-layer cognitive memory to generate persona-consistent responses, achieving high conformal coverage (0.921-0.976) and better persona fidelity over 100-turn role-playing interactions compared to static prompting.
AI.motivation: Existing persona inference methods use static prompting, which lacks calibrated uncertainty, ignores sequential evidence, and suffers from trait drift over long interactions.
AI.method: Combines prompting, Bayesian updating, conformal prediction for inference; uses a periodically refreshed memory anchor and three-layer cognitive memory to maintain persona consistency.
AI.result: Conformal coverage 0.921-0.976; improved uncertainty calibration and reasoning; enhanced persona fidelity over static prompting in 100-turn conversations with reduced trait drift across multiple backbones under adversarial multi-agent settings.
AI.conclusion: AI YOU effectively addresses drift and calibration issues in long-term persona-consistent dialogue, demonstrated across rigorous evaluation settings, with a prototype (AI YOU Town) and online demo available.

title: Wearing A Coat: Dual-Arm Robot-Assisted Dressing with Differentiable Clothing Simulation
summary: The development of assistive robots for dressing tasks serves to augment human convenience and improve the quality of life for individuals with physical impairments. However, due to the intricate contact interactions between garments and the human limbs during dressing, most robot-assisted dressing algorithms treat clothing as an assembly of discrete segments, thereby struggling to manage the partial worn garments under contact constraints. To overcome this challenge, we propose a novel robotic dressing control algorithm that integrates realtime differentiable clothing simulation. The simulation algorithm employs explicit iterative scheme with intentionally introduced higher-order perturbations to enhance computational efficiency while maintaining stability under large time-step conditions. Through simulation, we resolve the garment state under contact constraints, which then enables a multi-phase control strategy for successful coat dressing assistance. To further improve real-time performance, we introduce a constrained local model along with its corresponding optimization solver, permitting high-frequency local compensation for the differentiable simulation based global controller. Finally, we experimentally validate our approach through both simulated and physical dressing scenarios, conclusively demonstrating its feasibility and efficacy

AI.tldr: This paper presents a robotic dressing control algorithm that integrates real-time differentiable clothing simulation with a multi-phase control strategy to handle complex garment-limb contact interactions. It introduces higher-order perturbations for simulation stability, a constrained local model for high-frequency compensation, and validates the approach through both simulated and physical experiments.
AI.motivation: Existing robot-assisted dressing algorithms treat clothing as discrete segments and struggle to manage partial worn garments under contact constraints, limiting their effectiveness in real-world dressing assistance.
AI.method: The authors propose (1) a real-time differentiable clothing simulation using explicit iterative scheme with higher-order perturbations for stability, (2) a multi-phase control strategy leveraging simulation-resolved garment states under contact constraints, and (3) a constrained local model with optimization solver for high-frequency local compensation.
AI.result: The approach was validated in both simulated and physical dressing scenarios, demonstrating feasibility and efficacy of the proposed algorithm for robotic coat dressing assistance.
AI.conclusion: Integrating differentiable clothing simulation with a multi-phase control strategy effectively addresses the challenge of managing partial worn garments under contact constraints, enabling more capable robotic dressing assistance.

title: Generative Augmentation of Raman Spectra for Glioma Classification
summary: Access to sufficiently large biomedical datasets remains a major obstacle for machine learning in Raman spectroscopy-based diagnostics. In particular, for glioma analysis, datasets are typically small and heterogeneous, affected by acquisition-specific variability. This work investigates the utility of deep generative augmentation in such a small-cohort setting. We analyze glioma biopsy spectra acquired from 58 tumor samples and consider both binary IDH-status classification and 6-class methylation subtype classification problems. To address the limited size and imbalance of the dataset, we develop a conditional variational autoencoder ($β$-CVAE) capable of generating class-conditioned synthetic Raman spectra. The generated data are evaluated in Train-on-Synthetic, Test-on-Real (TS/TR) and Train-on-Synthetic+Real, Test-on-Real (TSR/TR) settings under a strict patient-isolated cross-validation protocol. Models trained exclusively on synthetic data underperform models trained on real spectra, indicating a substantial domain gap between synthetic and real distributions. However, augmenting the real training data with synthetic spectra consistently improves classification performance across multiple models. These findings indicate that, even with a limited number of independent patient samples, generative models can capture sufficient structure to provide useful regularization for downstream classifiers. We also investigate a reconstruction-based inference strategy, termed Classification by Reconstruction (CbR), in which class prediction is based on reconstruction error under different class conditions. Overall, the results support the use of deep generative augmentation as a practical strategy for improving machine learning robustness in Raman spectroscopy applications characterized by limited biomedical datasets.

AI.tldr: This paper explores using deep generative models (β-CVAE) to augment small, heterogeneous Raman spectroscopy datasets for glioma classification (IDH-status and methylation subtypes). Synthetic data alone underperforms real data, but augmenting real training data with synthetic spectra consistently improves classification performance, supporting generative augmentation as a regularization strategy in limited-data biomedical settings.
AI.motivation: Access to sufficiently large biomedical datasets remains a major obstacle for machine learning in Raman spectroscopy-based diagnostics. Datasets are typically small, heterogeneous, and affected by acquisition-specific variability, particularly for glioma analysis.
AI.method: Developed a conditional variational autoencoder (β-CVAE) to generate class-conditioned synthetic Raman spectra. Evaluated in Train-on-Synthetic, Test-on-Real (TS/TR) and Train-on-Synthetic+Real, Test-on-Real (TSR/TR) settings under strict patient-isolated cross-validation. Also explored Classification by Reconstruction (CbR) inference strategy.
AI.result: Models trained exclusively on synthetic data underperform those trained on real data (domain gap). However, augmenting real data with synthetic spectra consistently improves classification performance across multiple models.
AI.conclusion: Generative models can capture sufficient structure from limited patient samples to provide useful regularization. Deep generative augmentation is a practical strategy for improving ML robustness in Raman spectroscopy with limited biomedical datasets.

title: Self-supervised Automatic Matting
summary: High-quality alpha mattes are notoriously expensive to annotate, creating a fundamental data bottleneck for deep image matting. While prior work attempts to reduce annotation cost using coarser labels like trimaps or masks, they remain reliant on costly per-pixel supervision, limiting scalability and generalization. In this work, we push the boundary further and ask: can we train an automatic matting model using only RGB images, with no manual annotation at all? We answer this by presenting SSMatte, a self-supervised framework that for the first time achieves performance on par with fully-supervised automatic matting. Our key insight is to decompose the problem into semantic anchoring and detail matting. SSMatte first generates a semantic matting prompt from frozen self-supervised ViT features by propagating class-token seeds via a novel, training-efficient semantic anchoring loss based on a generalized Rayleigh quotient. This prompt then anchors a detail matting network, which is optimized via a fixed-point-based loss that enforces alpha-RGB consistency. Extensive experiments show SSMatte outperforms prior weakly-supervised methods, matches the performance of fully-supervised models on portrait benchmarks, and demonstrates favorable scaling and generalization behaviors with additional data. Our work pushes automatic matting to an fresh, fully annotation-free paradigm. Code will be available.

AI.tldr: SSMatte is a self-supervised framework for image matting that achieves fully-supervised-level performance using only RGB images without any manual annotation, by decomposing the problem into semantic anchoring (using frozen self-supervised ViT features) and detail matting (via fixed-point-based alpha-RGB consistency loss).
AI.motivation: High-quality alpha mattes are expensive to annotate, creating a data bottleneck for deep image matting. Prior work reduces annotation cost but still relies on costly per-pixel supervision (trimaps/masks), limiting scalability. The authors ask whether automatic matting can be trained using only RGB images with no manual annotation at all.
AI.method: SSMatte decomposes matting into (1) semantic anchoring: generates a semantic matting prompt from frozen self-supervised ViT features by propagating class-token seeds via a generalized Rayleigh quotient-based loss; (2) detail matting: optimized via a fixed-point-based loss enforcing alpha-RGB consistency.
AI.result: Outperforms prior weakly-supervised methods, matches fully-supervised models on portrait benchmarks, and shows favorable scaling and generalization behaviors with additional data.
AI.conclusion: This work pushes automatic matting to a fully annotation-free paradigm, demonstrating for the first time that self-supervised matting can achieve performance on par with fully-supervised methods.

title: Large language model agents accelerate inverse design of metal-organic frameworks for gas separation
summary: Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) offer a highly modular platform for adsorptive gas separation, yet their vast reticular design space makes inverse design difficult under simultaneous constraints of chemical validity, separation performance, and structural diversity. Here, we present LEMO Agent, a large-language-model agent framework for closed-loop inverse design of gas-separation MOFs in MOFid space. LEMO Agent couples language-based candidate generation with MOFid standardization, explicit validity checking, Transformer-based property prediction, structured design memory, and multi-island exploration. Through iterative generate--validate--evaluate--remember cycles, the agent uses feedback from both successful and failed candidates to guide chemically constrained search across linker, metal, and topology choices. We evaluate LEMO Agent on CH$_4$/N$_2$ and CO$_2$/N$_2$ separation tasks. Compared with representative generative, optimization, and agentic baselines, LEMO Agent enriches high-performing candidates, improves predicted separation performance, and maintains broad chemical and topological diversity. Selected candidates are further reconstructed, evaluated by GCMC simulations, and passed through an experimental down-selection workflow based on chemical feasibility and ligand purchasability, leading to initial wet-lab synthesis and SEM characterization. These results demonstrate that large language model agents can serve as interpretable and scalable design engines for accelerating MOF discovery beyond conventional fixed-library screening.

AI.tldr: LEMO Agent is an LLM-based framework for closed-loop inverse design of gas-separation MOFs that iteratively generates, validates, evaluates, and remembers candidates in MOFid space, outperforming baselines on CH4/N2 and CO2/N2 separation tasks and demonstrating experimental synthesis viability.
AI.motivation: MOFs offer a modular platform for gas separation, but their vast design space makes inverse design challenging under constraints of chemical validity, performance, and diversity. Existing methods struggle to balance these simultaneously.
AI.method: LEMO Agent couples language-based candidate generation with MOFid standardization, explicit validity checking, Transformer-based property prediction, structured design memory, and multi-island exploration in an iterative generate-validate-evaluate-remember loop.
AI.result: LEMO Agent outperforms generative, optimization, and agentic baselines by enriching high-performing candidates, improving predicted separation performance, and maintaining chemical/topological diversity. Selected candidates were validated via GCMC simulations and led to wet-lab synthesis with SEM characterization.
AI.conclusion: Large language model agents can serve as interpretable and scalable design engines for accelerating MOF discovery beyond conventional fixed-library screening.

title: Affordance-Based Manipulation Planning with Text Goals and Sim-to-Real Generalisation via Real-to-Sim Image Conversion
summary: We present a manipulation planning system based on affordance recognition and action effect prediction. The system reasons through possible futures in visual form, and evaluates candidate plans by agreement of predicted outcomes with text-based goals set at run-time, using a multi-modal goal-matching module. Positions of objects named in the goal text are tracked through predictions even when occluded, making it possible to generate action plans even when objects become occluded, or when their initial descriptors cease to identify them in future states. We further expand the system with an image conversion module for translating real-world state images with objects of varied shapes and visual appearances into a consistent visual appearance, to facilitate manipulation planning in a physical robot setup. We evaluate performance of the system's modules in isolation and demonstrate the integrated system's manipulation planning capabilities on a set of challenging tasks in both simulation and on hardware.

AI.tldr: A manipulation planning system that uses affordance recognition and action effect prediction, visual reasoning about possible futures, multi-modal goal matching, and image conversion to enable robots to plan manipulations even with occluded objects, demonstrated in simulation and on real hardware.
AI.motivation: To enable robots to reason about future states visually, handle object occlusion during task planning, and bridge the gap between varied real-world object appearances and consistent manipulation planning.
AI.method: The system combines: (1) affordance recognition and action effect prediction, (2) visual reasoning of possible futures, (3) multi-modal goal-matching for evaluating candidate plans against text goals, (4) object tracking through predictions even under occlusion, and (5) an image conversion module to normalize varied real-world object appearances.
AI.result: Modules were evaluated in isolation, and the integrated system demonstrated manipulation planning capabilities on challenging tasks both in simulation and on physical hardware.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach enables effective manipulation planning with visual future reasoning and robustness to occlusion, validated on real hardware.

title: The Differential Neural Tangent Kernel and Its Positivity
summary: The Neural Tangent Kernel (NTK) is one powerful tool for analyzing the training dynamics of neural networks in the over-parameterized regime. Recently, the theoretical framework has been extended to physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) for solving linear PDEs, one highly popular class of neural PDE solvers. In the analysis, the positivity of the associated NTK plays a fundamental role. However, establishing the positivity of the NTK for PINNs is highly challenging, due to the presence of multiple differential operators. In this work, we propose a new theoretical framework, called Differential Neural Tangent Kernel (DNTK), for analyzing PINNs through the lens of the NTK, and establish the positivity of the infinite width DNTK for both shallow and deep neural networks for a wide class of activation functions, including RePU and smooth but non-polynomial activations, for all linear differential operators. These theoretical results lay the foundation for the analysis of gradient type algorithms for training PINNs.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Differential Neural Tangent Kernel (DNTK) to analyze physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) for solving linear PDEs, establishing positivity guarantees for infinite-width DNTK across various activation functions and differential operators.
AI.motivation: While NTK theory has been extended to PINNs, proving positivity of the associated NTK is highly challenging due to multiple differential operators, yet positivity is fundamental to the analysis framework.
AI.method: Proposes a new theoretical framework called Differential Neural Tangent Kernel (DNTK) and proves positivity for infinite-width DNTK in both shallow and deep networks, covering RePU and smooth non-polynomial activations, for all linear differential operators.
AI.result: Established positivity of infinite-width DNTK under broad conditions, laying theoretical groundwork for analyzing gradient-based training algorithms for PINNs.
AI.conclusion: The DNTK framework enables rigorous analysis of PINN training dynamics by proving positivity, which is essential for understanding convergence guarantees of gradient-type optimization algorithms.

title: SynthDocBench: Controlled Benchmark for Long-Context Visual Document Understanding
comment: 29 Pages, 27 Tables, 13 Figures, Accepted at COLM 2026
summary: Vision language models (VLMs) have achieved strong performance on visual document understanding benchmarks such as DocVQA, ChartQA, and MMLongBench-Doc. However, real-world documents combine multiple factors such as length, layout complexity, modality, and question difficulty, which makes it difficult to attribute model failures to specific causes. We introduce SynthDocBench, a fully synthetic benchmark for long-context visual document understanding that systematically controls factors including document length, layout structure, modality composition, and question type. The benchmark is constructed using a combinatorial design, each factor is varied independently across generated documents, enabling controlled analysis of model behavior. Documents are generated end to end using an LLM pipeline across six layout archetypes, with a 40 percent random override to prevent models from exploiting spurious correlations. Additionally, SynthDocBench spans long-context documents with substantially greater length and structural diversity than existing benchmarks. Evaluating seven frontier VLMs, we uncover three failure modes that existing benchmarks cannot surface: sharp degradation with document length, a systematic positional sensitivity in which the middle third of a document is hardest for five of six models and five of six models show a negative Early-to-Late trend (steepest decline: 8.3 percentage points), and breakdown of chart comprehension in long-document settings. These results suggest that current models may be overfitting to benchmark artifacts rather than achieving robust long-context visual document understanding.

AI.tldr: SynthDocBench is a fully synthetic, controlled benchmark for long-context visual document understanding.
