It reveals three failure modes in frontier VLMs: sharp performance degradation with document length, systematic positional sensitivity (middle third hardest), and chart comprehension breakdown in long documents, suggesting overfitting to existing benchmark artifacts.
AI.motivation: Existing VLM benchmarks (DocVQA, ChartQA, etc.) combine multiple confounding factors (length, layout, modality, difficulty) that make it impossible to attribute model failures to specific causes. A controlled, systematic benchmark is needed to isolate and diagnose these failure modes.
AI.method: SynthDocBench uses a combinatorial design with independently varied factors (length, layout, modality, question type). Documents are generated end-to-end via an LLM pipeline across six layout archetypes, with a 40% random override to prevent exploiting spurious correlations. It spans longer, more structurally diverse documents than existing benchmarks.
AI.result: Evaluating seven frontier VLMs revealed: (1) sharp degradation with document length, (2) systematic positional sensitivity where the middle third is hardest for 5/6 models, with a negative Early-to-Late trend (steepest decline 8.3 pp), and (3) chart comprehension breakdown in long-document settings.
AI.conclusion: Current VLMs may be overfitting to benchmark artifacts rather than achieving robust long-context visual document understanding, as evidenced by failure modes that existing benchmarks cannot surface.

title: CRiT-QA: Evaluating Multi-hop Reasoning with Counterfactual Chains and Distractor Traps
summary: Evaluating the multi-hop reasoning capabilities of large language models remains a significant challenge. Although current models achieve strong results on existing multi-hop question answering datasets, such performance often masks two critical vulnerabilities: (1) reliance on internal parametric knowledge rather than adherence to the provided context, and (2) exploitation of dataset shortcuts, such as single-document cues or type-matching, that diminish the need for genuine evidence aggregation across multiple documents. We introduce CRiT-QA (Counterfactual Reasoning with Traps), a dataset explicitly designed to address both limitations. To neutralize reliance on memorized knowledge and enforce strict context dependency, CRiT-QA transforms factual reasoning chains with counterfactual entities. Furthermore, it injects multi-anchor distractor chains, plausible but incorrect reasoning paths that diverge at different hops. These traps require models to follow the entire reasoning process rather than exploiting shallow heuristics. Our experiments show that LLMs exhibit substantial performance degradation on CRiT-QA compared to standard datasets, exposing their vulnerability to counterfactual conditions and distractor traps. CRiT-QA thus serves as a rigorous diagnostic tool for evaluating genuine multi-hop reasoning and provides a foundation for developing more reliable, evidence-grounded LLMs.

AI.tldr: CRiT-QA is a new multi-hop QA dataset using counterfactual entities and multi-anchor distractors to expose LLMs' reliance on memorized knowledge and shortcuts, revealing substantial performance degradation compared to standard benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing multi-hop QA benchmarks fail to detect two critical vulnerabilities in LLMs: reliance on internal parametric knowledge over context, and exploitation of dataset shortcuts (e.g., single-document cues). A more rigorous diagnostic tool is needed.
AI.method: CRiT-QA transforms factual reasoning chains using counterfactual entities to block memorization, and injects multi-anchor distractor chains—plausible but incorrect reasoning paths that diverge at different hops—to prevent shortcut exploitation.
AI.result: LLMs show substantial performance degradation on CRiT-QA compared to standard datasets, exposing their vulnerability to counterfactual conditions and distractor traps.
AI.conclusion: CRiT-QA serves as a rigorous diagnostic tool for evaluating genuine multi-hop reasoning and provides a foundation for developing more reliable, evidence-grounded LLMs.

title: Whole-Body Semantic-to-Actuation Grounding of Elephant-Inspired Soft-Trunk Motion via Lightweight Flow Matching
summary: For close-contact human-robot interaction (HRI), trunk-like continuum manipulators provide a physical channel for diverse whole-body expression, but grounding open-vocabulary responses into such robots is difficult: end-effector motion underspecifies body shape, whereas direct whole-body commands are high-dimensional and hard to keep feasible. We propose a whole-body semantic-to-actuation grounding framework for elephant-inspired soft-trunk HRI based on lightweight flow matching. The framework converts responses from a multimodal large language model into bounded, morphology-aligned intent-intensity tuples, parameterizes tendon-actuation trajectories with compact Catmull-Rom spline controls, and uses a rectified-flow generator to sample feasible whole-body trunk motions. Experiments show that the proposed framework improves held-out grounding correctness from 25.0% to 77.2% over a raw-response dense-regression baseline. Compared with a denoising-diffusion baseline, it improves correctness from 71.9% to 77.2% and reduces inference time from 7.86 ms to 4.87 ms while preserving motion diversity. A 100-participant physical HRI study further shows that adding the generated soft-trunk motion channel increases the positive overall-satisfaction rating from 46% to 82% over the audiovisual-only baseline.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a whole-body semantic-to-actuation framework for elephant-inspired soft-trunk continuum manipulators in HRI, using flow matching to convert multimodal LLM responses into feasible whole-body motions, achieving 77.2% grounding correctness (up from 25.0%) and increasing user satisfaction from 46% to 82% in a physical HRI study.
AI.motivation: Trunk-like continuum manipulators enable diverse whole-body expression for close-contact HRI, but open-vocabulary grounding is difficult because end-effector motion underspecifies body shape, while whole-body commands are high-dimensional and hard to keep feasible.
AI.method: A three-stage framework: (1) converts multimodal LLM responses into bounded, morphology-aligned intent-intensity tuples; (2) parameterizes tendon-actuation trajectories with compact Catmull-Rom spline controls; (3) uses a rectified-flow generator to sample feasible whole-body trunk motions.
AI.result: Improves held-out grounding correctness from 25.0% to 77.2% vs. raw-response dense-regression baseline; from 71.9% to 77.2% with inference time reduced from 7.86ms to 4.87ms vs. denoising-diffusion baseline; 100-participant study shows satisfaction increases from 46% to 82% over audiovisual-only baseline.
AI.conclusion: The proposed flow-matching-based framework enables effective whole-body semantic grounding for soft-trunk HRI, significantly outperforming baselines in correctness, efficiency, and user satisfaction.

title: Two Confounds in Cross-Model Value Comparison: Response Determinism and the Access Harness
summary: Cross-model comparisons read divergence in value dispositions as evidence that language models hold individuated values. Under single-draw measurement this conflates two quantities: a difference in central tendency (a genuine value difference) and a difference in response determinism (how sharply a model commits to a forced choice). We introduce a separation protocol -- no-rule value dilemmas with counterbalanced, repeated forced-choice measurement and a determinism index -- and a determinism-corrected decomposition that splits an apparent cross-model distance into a direction-flip component (genuine disagreement) and a same-side-more-extreme component we label determinism. Across nine models, determinism varies substantially (0.66-0.95 among engaging models); whether it is a per-model trait or tracks provider and scale is a question our method makes measurable but our sample leaves open. Correcting for determinism shrinks apparent individuation, while a few cross-family disagreements survive a strict test. We then isolate a second confound: the access harness serving each model. Re-collecting the same models through raw provider APIs, we find the deployment client shifts a model's value profile substantially and client-specifically: one subscription CLI moves a profile by 0.31, flips four of eighteen items, and inflates the flagship's apparent softness (0.34 via CLI vs 0.66 via raw API), whereas another provider's client is clean, confounding provider family with access client. The harness is a value-shaping layer: a base model that refuses one-in-ten forced choices is made compliant by an agent system prompt, established causally in a white-box control. An audit ranking models by single-draw value distance thus ranks a determinism-inflated quantity, confounded further by the client used. We contribute the decomposition and identify the deployment harness as a distinct value confound.

AI.tldr: Cross-model value comparisons are confounded by differences in response determinism and deployment harness (API client). The authors propose a separation protocol and determinism-corrected decomposition, showing that apparent value individuation shrinks after correction, and that the access client substantially shifts value profiles.
AI.motivation: Existing cross-model comparisons of value dispositions conflate genuine value differences (central tendency) with differences in response determinism. The authors aim to disentangle these confounds and also identify the deployment harness (API client) as an additional value-shaping factor.
AI.method: No-rule value dilemmas with counterbalanced, repeated forced-choice measurement; a determinism index; a determinism-corrected decomposition splitting cross-model distance into direction-flip (genuine disagreement) and same-side-more-extreme (determinism) components; collection via both raw provider APIs and subscription CLIs to isolate harness effects; white-box causal control with agent system prompt.
AI.result: Determinism varies substantially (0.66-0.95) across nine models. Correcting for determinism shrinks apparent individuation, though some cross-family disagreements survive. Deployment client shifts value profiles substantially and client-specifically (e.g., one CLI moves profile by 0.31, flips four of eighteen items). One provider's client inflates flagship's apparent softness (0.34 vs 0.66). Agent prompts causally increase compliance.
AI.conclusion: Single-draw value distance rankings are confounded by determinism and deployment harness. The decomposition and harness identification are contributions for more valid value auditing.

title: TVT-PAPD: Pathology-Aware Prototype Distillation for Self-Supervised Whole Slide Image Classification
summary: Self-supervised learning (SSL) has emerged as an effective paradigm for learning transferable representations from large-scale unlabeled whole slide images (WSIs). However, existing SSL methods primarily learn generic visual features and often fail to explicitly capture pathology-specific morphological patterns that are critical for disease characterization. To address this limitation, we propose Tiny Vision Transformer with Pathology-Aware Prototype Distillation (TVT-PAPD). This self-supervised pathology representation learning framework integrates a Tiny Vision Transformer (TVT) with a novel Pathology-Aware Prototype Distillation (PAPD) module. PAPD employs a learnable pathology prototype bank to discover and preserve representative tissue morphology patterns, encouraging semantically similar pathological regions to learn consistent and discriminative representations. The proposed framework enhances pathology-aware feature learning while maintaining computational efficiency with 90M parameters. Experiments on the Cancer Genome Atlas (TCGA) low-grade glioma (LGG)/glioblastoma (GBM) dataset and the Indian Pathology Brain (IPD-Brain) dataset demonstrate that TVT-PAPD achieves weighted F1-scores of 93.02% and 90.23%, respectively, for LGG-GBM classification, while exhibiting strong cross-cohort generalization across independent glioma datasets.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes TVT-PAPD, a self-supervised framework combining a Tiny Vision Transformer with Pathology-Aware Prototype Distillation to learn pathology-specific features from whole slide images, achieving strong glioma classification performance (93.02% and 90.23% F1-scores) with computational efficiency.
AI.motivation: Existing SSL methods for WSIs learn generic visual features but fail to explicitly capture pathology-specific morphological patterns critical for disease characterization, motivating the need for pathology-aware representation learning.
AI.method: TVT-PAPD integrates a Tiny Vision Transformer (TVT) with a Pathology-Aware Prototype Distillation (PAPD) module containing a learnable pathology prototype bank that discovers and preserves representative tissue morphology patterns, encouraging semantically similar regions to learn consistent representations.
AI.result: Weighted F1-scores of 93.02% on TCGA LGG/GBM dataset and 90.23% on IPD-Brain dataset for LGG-GBM classification, with strong cross-cohort generalization across independent glioma datasets.
AI.conclusion: TVT-PAPD effectively addresses the limitation of generic SSL by incorporating pathology-aware feature learning while maintaining computational efficiency (90M parameters), showing strong performance and generalization for glioma classification.

title: Laguerre Geometry for Interpreting Large Language Models
summary: Existing hypotheses represent a concept in an LLM as a single point, a linear direction, or a Gaussian cluster, yet it remains unclear how and why such structures emerge. Here, we show that concept geometry can be precisely characterized via Laguerre Geometry, in which a concept is defined as a region--a Laguerre-Voronoi cell or a union of cells--allowing us to strictly define, measure, and separate concepts. Building on this formulation, we show that finer-grained concept structures, such as inclusion and hierarchy, are naturally revealed by the Laguerre weights. We then push this geometry inside the transformer. Decomposing each layer into piecewise-linear operators, we show that a token's hidden trajectory is governed by two coupled mechanisms: a static tree of self-contained piecewise-linear flow, and a dynamic transport that hops the trajectory across trees when cross-token attention fires. This decomposition yields Geometric Lens, a training-free, hyperparameter-free method for reading out the exact concept a hidden vector encodes at any layer. We also develop Laguerre Autoencoder, a 2D visualizer that renders both the decision geometry and a model's full reasoning trajectory in one view. Finally, we move beyond explanatory geometry toward actionable interpretability, showing that Geometric Lens recovers the correct factual token when a model is prompted with in-context interference. The code is available on GitHub.

AI.tldr: This paper shows that concept geometry in LLMs can be precisely characterized using Laguerre Geometry, where concepts are regions (Laguerre-Voronoi cells). This reveals fine-grained structures like hierarchy, and leads to Geometric Lens—a training-free method to decode concepts from hidden states—along with a 2D visualizer (Laguerre Autoencoder) that aids interpretability and recovers correct tokens under in-context interference.
AI.motivation: Existing concept representations in LLMs (point, direction, Gaussian cluster) lack a precise explanation of how and why such structures emerge. The authors aim to provide a principled geometric framework that strictly defines, measures, and separates concepts.
AI.method: The authors use Laguerre Geometry to define concepts as Laguerre-Voronoi cells (regions). They decompose transformer layers into piecewise-linear operators, revealing a static tree of piecewise-linear flow and dynamic cross-token attention transport. This yields Geometric Lens (training-free, hyperparameter-free) for decoding concepts, and Laguerre Autoencoder for 2D visualization.
AI.result: Geometric Lens can read out the exact concept a hidden vector encodes at any layer. Laguerre Autoencoder visualizes decision geometry and reasoning trajectory. The method recovers correct factual tokens under in-context interference.
AI.conclusion: Laguerre Geometry provides a rigorous foundation for understanding concept representation in LLMs, enabling actionable interpretability beyond mere explanation.

title: SegDiff: Segmented Trajectory Diffusion for Consistent and Adaptive Robot Manipulation
summary: Imitation learning enables robots to acquire manipulation skills from demonstrations by mapping observations to actions. Existing approaches predict either short-horizon continuous action sequences or discrete keyposes. However, continuous prediction methods suffer from compounding errors due to short prediction horizons and struggle with multi-modal action distributions, whereas keypose-based methods necessitate an external planner, constraining real-time applicability. To address these challenges, we introduce SegDiff, a closed-loop visuomotor policy that integrates the strengths of both paradigms. SegDiff decomposes demonstrations into motion segments between keyposes and learns to predict the continuous trajectory from the current state to the next keypose, enabling long-horizon prediction with real-time refinement. Furthermore, we leverage the capability of diffusion models and DDIM inversion to propose a Dynamic Temporal Ensembling mechanism, which allows the policy to efficiently respond to dynamic environments and mitigate discontinuities caused by inconsistent multi-modal sampling. SegDiff demonstrates significant performance gains over existing approaches across various simulated and real-world scenarios, indicating its strong ability to reason over extended temporal dependencies while maintaining real-time adaptability and control stability.

AI.tldr: SegDiff is a closed-loop visuomotor policy that combines continuous trajectory prediction with keypose-based decomposition using diffusion models and DDIM inversion, achieving long-horizon prediction with real-time refinement and dynamic adaptability.
AI.motivation: Existing imitation learning methods either suffer from compounding errors (continuous prediction) or require external planners (keypose-based), limiting real-time applicability. SegDiff aims to bridge this gap.
AI.method: SegDiff decomposes demonstrations into motion segments between keyposes, predicts continuous trajectories from current state to next keypose, and uses a Dynamic Temporal Ensembling mechanism via diffusion models and DDIM inversion to handle multi-modal distributions and environmental changes.
AI.result: SegDiff achieves significant performance gains over existing approaches in both simulated and real-world scenarios, demonstrating strong long-horizon reasoning with real-time adaptability and control stability.
AI.conclusion: Integrating continuous and keypose-based paradigms via diffusion models effectively improves temporal reasoning and real-time control in imitation learning.

title: When Does Depth Survive Composition? Compute--Quality Regimes in Latent World Models
summary: Adaptive-compute world models -- early-exit or mixture-of-depths predictors that spend variable depth per step -- assume depth buys better predictions and can be routed adaptively. In autoregressive rollouts, the first assumption requires depth's per-step precision to survive composition. We test this with a pre-registered instrument, the shallow penalty $ρ=\mathrm{err}(\text{shallowest-exit rollout})/\mathrm{err}(\text{full-depth rollout})$, across nine DeepMind Control tasks under matched single-step ($K=1$) and multi-step ($K=4$) training, three seeds each. We find three regimes: on 6/9 tasks depth helps rollouts (intrinsic, $ρ$ up to $4.7\times$), on 2/9 the shallow exits beat the full stack (inversion, $ρ$ down to $0.85\times$), and one is flat. The robust inversion (cheetah) is not a property of the dynamics but is created by training: an ablation supervising early exits only at the first rollout step erases it ($ρ: 0.87\to1.18$, $n=8$, $Δ=+0.31$), while an intrinsic-tradeoff task is unaffected -- a double dissociation we call the routability catch-22, since the supervision that makes exits routable is what trains them to out-roll the full stack. The regime is partly predictable a priori: observation/action dimensionality and one-step model error correlate with $ρ$ at $|\text{Spearman}|\approx0.75$ ($n=9$). Inside a CEM planner, $ρ$'s sign predicts whether planning benefits from depth, most sharply on the inversion task, where shallow planning beats deep. Finally, three cautions: a task's regime depends on the metric space, the rollout horizon, and the encoder. All thresholds and gates were fixed before the compute campaign, including a pre-registered negative for the hypothesis that motivated the study.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates whether adaptive-compute world models benefit from variable depth per step, finding three regimes (intrinsic, inversion, flat) across nine DeepMind Control tasks. The key discovery is a "routability catch-22" where training that makes early exits routable can cause shallow exits to outperform full-depth models on some tasks. The regime is partly predictable from task features like observation/action dimensionality.
AI.motivation: To test the assumption that depth buys better predictions in adaptive-compute world models for autoregressive rollouts, as deeper models may not necessarily compose well under multi-step prediction.
AI.method: Pre-registered instrument measuring shallow penalty ρ = err(shallowest-exit rollout)/err(full-depth rollout) across 9 DeepMind Control tasks with single-step (K=1) and multi-step (K=4) training, including ablation studies and analysis of predictability.
AI.result: Found three regimes: intrinsic (6/9 tasks where depth helps, ρ up to 4.7×), inversion (2/9 where shallow beats deep, ρ down to 0.85×), and flat (1 task). Discovered routability catch-22 where supervision for routability can cause inversion. Regime partly predictable from observation/action dimensionality and one-step error (|Spearman|≈0.75).
AI.conclusion: Adaptive-compute world models face a fundamental tension: routability can create inversion effects. The regime is partially predictable, and its sign affects CEM planning outcomes. These findings come with cautions about metric space, rollout horizon, and encoder dependence.

title: GNOCHI: Generative Neural mOdel for Close Human-Human Interactions
summary: Creating realistic 3D human-human interactions in virtual environments is challenging due to the high degrees of freedom in the human body and the need for physically accurate poses that do not collide with each other. Traditional methods for human-human interaction are based on motion tracking or 3D body reconstruction, but lack generative capabilities. Recent generative methods enable the synthesis of individual or interacting motions via text or image input, but generally fall short in modeling close interactions. This paper introduces a novel generative model for close 3D human-human interactions using a conditional variational autoencoder (cVAE), which generates poses for one human conditioned on the pose of another, allowing for controlled and diverse interaction synthesis. To train our model, we address two underlying long-standing challenges in the field of human-human interaction: data scarcity, for which we propose an automated supervised data augmentation strategy that generates synthetic yet realistic interaction poses; and collision awareness in generative approaches, for which we propose a self-supervised loss based on a collision resolution technique using volumetric proxies to ensure physically correct interactions. We extensively evaluate the capabilities of our model, and demonstrate a wide variety of plausible and physically correct interactions, not possible to generate with current state-of-the-art methods.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a novel conditional variational autoencoder (cVAE) for generating realistic, collision-free 3D human-human interactions. It addresses data scarcity with a supervised data augmentation strategy and ensures physically correct poses via a self-supervised collision resolution loss using volumetric proxies.
AI.motivation: Creating realistic 3D human-human interactions in virtual environments is challenging due to high degrees of freedom and the need for physically accurate, non-colliding poses. Existing methods either lack generative capabilities (motion tracking, 3D body reconstruction) or fall short in modeling close interactions (text/image-based generative methods).
AI.method: The authors propose: (1) a conditional variational autoencoder (cVAE) that generates poses for one human conditioned on the pose of another; (2) an automated supervised data augmentation strategy to address data scarcity by generating synthetic yet realistic interaction poses; (3) a self-supervised loss based on a collision resolution technique using volumetric proxies to ensure physically correct interactions.
AI.result: The model demonstrates a wide variety of plausible and physically correct interactions that are not possible to generate with current state-of-the-art methods.
AI.conclusion: The proposed generative model effectively synthesizes close 3D human-human interactions with controlled diversity and physical correctness, outperforming existing approaches.

title: Constraint-Aware Hierarchical Search for Regulation-Driven Fine-Grained Classification
summary: Tasks such as customs tariff classification, export control categorization, and standards-based equipment coding require assigning an input instance to a fine-grained class under an explicit regulatory hierarchy. Unlike standard text classification, the correct label in these tasks is not determined by semantic similarity alone, but by rule-defined boundaries, threshold conditions, exclusion clauses, definitions, and local exceptions. As a result, two highly similar inputs may require different labels, while a retrieved passage that appears relevant may still be inapplicable under the governing rules. Existing flat classifiers, hierarchical text classification methods, and retrieval-augmented LLM systems are not designed to jointly enforce hierarchical validity, rule consistency, and fine-grained boundary reasoning. In this paper, we formulate this setting as regulation-driven fine-grained hierarchical classification, where an external instance must be assigned to a fine-grained class through a valid path in a regulatory hierarchy and supported by auditable evidence. We construct four benchmark datasets from representative regulation-intensive scenarios and validate the annotations through an expert-in-the-loop process. We further propose a constraint-aware hierarchical search framework that converts regulatory documents into a searchable tree, retrieves only valid local candidate nodes, and uses structured regulatory fields with evidence snippets to guide each next-hop decision. Experiments show that our method achieves the best mean accuracy on all four datasets and provides interpretable decision paths, with the largest gains on cases involving fine-grained neighboring categories and rule-based boundary conditions.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a regulation-driven fine-grained hierarchical classification task and proposes a constraint-aware hierarchical search framework that outperforms existing methods on four benchmark datasets by enforcing hierarchical validity, rule consistency, and interpretable decision paths.
AI.motivation: Existing flat classifiers, hierarchical text classification methods, and retrieval-augmented LLM systems fail to jointly enforce hierarchical validity, rule consistency, and fine-grained boundary reasoning required for tasks like customs tariff classification and export control categorization, where labels are determined by regulatory rules rather than semantic similarity alone.
AI.method: The authors construct four benchmark datasets from regulation-intensive scenarios with expert-in-the-loop validation, and propose a constraint-aware hierarchical search framework that converts regulatory documents into a searchable tree, retrieves only valid local candidate nodes, and uses structured regulatory fields with evidence snippets to guide each next-hop decision.
AI.result: The proposed method achieves the best mean accuracy on all four datasets, with the largest gains on cases involving fine-grained neighboring categories and rule-based boundary conditions, while also providing interpretable decision paths.
AI.conclusion: The constraint-aware hierarchical search framework effectively addresses regulation-driven fine-grained hierarchical classification, outperforming existing methods in accuracy and interpretability, particularly for challenging cases with subtle rule-based distinctions.

title: Learning to Navigate Efficiently with Only 0.58M Trainable Parameters
summary: Recent progress in visual navigation has largely been driven by scale: end-to-end policies with hundreds of millions of parameters trained on billions of frames or large-scale simulated data. We ask how much of this scale a single task family actually requires, and what structure can substitute for it. We propose a decomposed navigation model in which operations with known closed-form structure, such as projective geometry, occupancy, and coordinate transforms, are computed analytically and serve as interfaces between three small learned modules: an egress predictor that grounds the episode goal as a local subgoal in the current view, a navigation predictor that estimates a goal-conditioned posterior over where trajectories travel, and an endpoint-pinned residual diffusion generator that samples trajectory shapes from this posterior. The system trains only 0.58M out of a total of 22.7M parameters, on 44k frames in under one GPU-hour, yet approaches the performance of state-of-the-art models on navigation tasks across 6060 point-goal episodes and 60 environments, while having 233x fewer trainable parameters, the lowest collision rate among all evaluated methods, and 50 Hz inference speed. The decomposition further transfers to no-goal exploration by retraining only the 123k-parameter egress head, and its failure modes under sensor corruption are transparent and analytically correctable.

AI.tldr: A decomposed navigation model using only 0.58M trainable parameters and 44k frames (under 1 GPU-hour) approaches SOTA performance across 6060 episodes, with 233x fewer parameters, lowest collision rate, and 50Hz inference.
AI.motivation: To question how much scale (massive parameters, billions of frames) is truly necessary for navigation tasks, and to explore whether known geometric structure can substitute for data-driven scale.
AI.method: A decomposed model with analytic modules (projective geometry, occupancy, coordinate transforms) connecting three small learned components: an egress predictor for local subgoal grounding, a navigation predictor for goal-conditioned trajectory posterior, and an endpoint-pinned residual diffusion generator for trajectory sampling. Only 0.58M of 22.7M total parameters are trained.
AI.result: Approaches SOTA performance on 6060 point-goal episodes across 60 environments, with 233x fewer trainable parameters, lowest collision rate, 50 Hz inference speed. Transfers to no-goal exploration by retraining only the 123k-parameter egress head.
AI.conclusion: Explicit geometric structure can dramatically reduce the need for scale in visual navigation, yielding efficient, transparent, and transferable models with analytically correctable failure modes under sensor corruption.

title: Exploratory Analysis of Deep Learning Models for Forecasting Meteorological Parameters in the Agricultural Sector
summary: Accurate meteorological forecasting is essential for agricultural planning, irrigation management, and environmental decision support. This study conducts a comparative evaluation of recurrent and hybrid deep learning architectures for multivariate forecasting of reference evapotranspiration ($ET_0$), vapour pressure deficit (VPD), wind speed, and the sine and cosine components of wind direction. The analysis utilizes 134,376 hourly observations from Ioannina, Greece, spanning January 2011 to April 2026, sourced from ERA5 via the OpenMeteo Historical Weather API. Single and multi-layer GRU and LSTM networks are compared with hybrid 1D-CNN-GRU and 1D-CNN-LSTM models for two forecasting tasks: a 24-hour next-day forecast and a 168-hour week-ahead forecast. Performance is evaluated using normalized root mean squared error, the coefficient of determination, and a composite Weighted Quotient Score (WQS). The most effective purely recurrent models are a 64-unit LSTM for the 24-hour horizon, with a WQS of 0.816755, and a 1024-unit GRU for the 168-hour horizon, with a WQS of 0.779465. The hybrid CNN-GRU models achieved the highest overall scores of 0.827535 and 0.782863 for the 24-hour and 168-hour horizons, but with additionally more number of units respectively to LSTM models, while the CNN-LSTM models yield nearly identical results with substantially fewer parameters. Compared to the corresponding recurrent baselines, the hybrid models improve WQS by 1.22--1.63\% at 24 hours and by 0.44--0.45\% at 168 hours, indicating that convolutional feature extraction is more beneficial for short-term forecasting.

AI.tldr: This study compares recurrent (GRU, LSTM) and hybrid (1D-CNN-GRU, 1D-CNN-LSTM) deep learning models for multivariate meteorological forecasting (evapotranspiration, VPD, wind speed/direction) using 134K hourly observations from Greece. Hybrid CNN-GRU models achieve the best WQS scores (0.8275 for 24h; 0.7829 for 168h), improving over recurrent baselines by 1.22-1.63% for short-term and 0.44-0.45% for long-term forecasts, while CNN-LSTM offers comparable performance with fewer parameters.
AI.motivation: Accurate meteorological forecasting is critical for agriculture, irrigation, and environmental planning, driving the need to evaluate and improve deep learning architectures for multivariate weather prediction.
AI.method: The study compares single/multi-layer GRU, LSTM, 1D-CNN-GRU, and 1D-CNN-LSTM models on 134,376 hourly ERA5 observations from Ioannina, Greece (2011-2026), evaluating 24-hour and 168-hour forecasting horizons using NRMSE, R², and a composite Weighted Quotient Score.
AI.result: Best recurrent models: 64-unit LSTM (24h, WQS=0.8168) and 1024-unit GRU (168h, WQS=0.7795). Hybrid CNN-GRU achieved highest scores (0.8275 and 0.7829). Hybrid models improved WQS by 1.22-1.63% (24h) and 0.44-0.45% (168h). CNN-LSTM achieved near-identical results with fewer parameters.
AI.conclusion: Hybrid CNN-RNN architectures outperform pure recurrent models for meteorological forecasting, with convolutional feature extraction providing greater benefits for short-term (24h) predictions than for longer-term (168h) forecasts.

title: SPORT: Structure-Aware Prototype Disentanglement for Incomplete Multi-View Clustering
summary: Prototype-based Incomplete Multi-view Clustering has recently attracted increasing attention by exploiting prototypes as semantic anchors for missing-view imputation. However, existing approaches are still limited in three aspects. First, they typically focus on enforcing cross-view prototype consistency, while ignoring view-specific information embedded in prototypes, thus limiting multi-view expressiveness. Second, most methods rely on instance-level contrastive learning that only aligns paired samples across views, failing to preserve cluster-level relational structures. Third, missing-view imputation is usually performed using global prototypes alone, without considering local geometric neighborhood structures, leading to inaccurate recovery of missing representations. To address these limitations, we propose a novel framework termed Structure-aware PrOtotype disentanglement foR incomplete multi-view clusTering (SPORT), which explicitly disentangles shared and view-specific components of prototypes while preserving cluster-level relational structures. Specifically, we decouple prototypes into orthogonal shared and view-specific components, aligning only shared components to capture consensus semantics while de-correlating view-specific components to preserve complementary information. Meanwhile, a structure-aware contrastive learning mechanism is incorporated to explicitly model cluster-level relationships during cross-view representation learning. Furthermore, a hybrid imputation strategy integrates global prototype matching with local neighborhood matching, enabling joint exploitation of semantic prototypes and manifold structures for missing-view recovery. Extensive experiments on six benchmark datasets show that SPORT achieves superior performance over state-of-the-art methods under various missing rates.

AI.tldr: SPORT is a novel framework for incomplete multi-view clustering that disentangles prototypes into shared and view-specific components, uses structure-aware contrastive learning for cluster-level relationships, and employs a hybrid imputation strategy combining global prototypes with local neighborhoods, achieving superior results on six benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing prototype-based incomplete multi-view clustering methods suffer from three limitations: (1) ignoring view-specific information in prototypes, (2) using instance-level contrastive learning that fails to preserve cluster-level structures, and (3) relying on global prototypes alone for missing-view imputation without considering local neighborhood structures.
AI.method: SPORT explicitly disentangles prototypes into orthogonal shared and view-specific components; aligns only shared components for consensus semantics while de-correlating view-specific components for complementary info; uses structure-aware contrastive learning for cluster-level relationships; and employs a hybrid imputation strategy combining global prototype matching with local neighborhood matching.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on six benchmark datasets show SPORT achieves superior performance over state-of-the-art methods under various missing rates.
AI.conclusion: By addressing the three key limitations through prototype disentanglement, structure-aware contrastive learning, and hybrid imputation, SPORT sets a new state-of-the-art for incomplete multi-view clustering.

title: GraspGraphNet: Graph-Structured Multi-Embodiment Dexterous Grasp Generation
summary: Dexterous grasp generation across robot hands is challenging because hands differ in kinematic topology, actuation dimensions, and native command spaces. We introduce GraspGraphNet, a topology-aware grasp generation framework that represents each hand as a URDF-derived kinematic graph and directly generates executable palm poses and joint configurations. GraspGraphNet combines hierarchical object surface encoding, differentiable forward kinematics, and dynamic world-edge message passing to model evolving robot-object interactions. It applies conditional flow matching directly in executable palm-pose and joint-state space, avoiding post-processing optimization, inverse kinematics, and retargeting. Using a shared model trained on Barrett Hand, Allegro Hand, and Shadow Hand, GraspGraphNet achieves an average success rate of 83.48% with 40ms inference time per grasp on a 40-object benchmark. Without retraining, the same model achieves 72.70% success on controlled finger-removal variants, demonstrating robustness to hand-topology variations. These results suggest that graph-structured hand representations can effectively support dexterous grasp generation across robot hands with different kinematic structures. Project: https://lysees.github.io/graspgraphnet-page

AI.tldr: GraspGraphNet is a topology-aware framework that uses URDF-derived kinematic graphs to generate dexterous grasps across different robot hands (Barrett, Allegro, Shadow) with 83.48% average success rate and 40ms inference time, without requiring post-processing or retraining for hand variations.
AI.motivation: Dexterous grasp generation across different robot hands is challenging because hands differ in kinematic topology, actuation dimensions, and native command spaces, making it difficult to transfer grasps between platforms.
AI.method: GraspGraphNet represents each hand as a URDF-derived kinematic graph, combines hierarchical object surface encoding, differentiable forward kinematics, and dynamic world-edge message passing to model robot-object interactions. It applies conditional flow matching directly in executable palm-pose and joint-state space, avoiding post-processing optimization, inverse kinematics, and retargeting.
AI.result: Achieves 83.48% average success rate with 40ms inference per grasp on a 40-object benchmark across three different hands. Without retraining, achieves 72.70% success on controlled finger-removal variants, demonstrating robustness to hand-topology variations.
AI.conclusion: Graph-structured hand representations can effectively support dexterous grasp generation across robot hands with different kinematic structures, enabling generalization without retraining.

title: DSSMs: State Space Models with Explicit Memory via Delay Differential Equations
summary: State Space Models (SSMs) have emerged as a powerful paradigm for efficient long-sequence modeling, offering parallel training and fast linear-time recurrent inference. However, like other recurrent architectures, SSMs must compress an unbounded history into a fixed-size state, which limits context retention and makes precise retrieval over long-range context inherently difficult. To overcome this limitation, we propose Delay State Space Models (DSSMs), a delay differential equation (DDE)-inspired extension of diagonal SSMs that augments discrete SSM recurrences with explicit delayed-state feedback. Making explicit delayed feedback practical requires new stability parameterization, history management, and FFT-training tools. We address these challenges with a practical discretization and parameterization grounded in a simple delay-independent stability condition. To bypass direct time-domain kernel construction, we derive the DSSM transfer function and compute kernels in the frequency domain, using a kernel contour shift to suppress aliasing and recover accurate FFT training. Empirically, DSSMs substantially improve targeted delayed-retrieval tasks while outperforming S4D on most standard sequence metrics and remaining close on the others.

AI.tldr: DSSMs extend State Space Models with explicit delayed-state feedback (inspired by delay differential equations) to improve long-range context retention, using new stability parameterization, frequency-domain kernel computation, and FFT training.
AI.motivation: SSMs compress unbounded history into a fixed-size state, limiting context retention and making precise long-range retrieval inherently difficult.
AI.method: DSSMs augment diagonal SSM recurrences with explicit delayed-state feedback, grounded in a delay-independent stability condition, with kernels computed in the frequency domain using a contour shift to suppress aliasing for accurate FFT training.
AI.result: DSSMs substantially improve targeted delayed-retrieval tasks, outperform S4D on most standard sequence metrics, and remain competitive on the remaining ones.
AI.conclusion: Explicit delayed feedback in SSMs is a practical and effective way to overcome fixed-state compression limitations, improving long-range retrieval without sacrificing efficiency.

title: BOCCHI: A More Realistic and Challenging Benchmark for Local Motion Blur Detection with MSDCT-UNet
summary: Local motion blur detection requires pixel-level localization of blurred regions. Existing benchmarks let models rely on gradient shortcuts that fail to transfer. We introduce BOCCHI (Blurred Objects Captured across Cameras with Human-annotated Imagery), a real-captured benchmark whose sharp regions overlap the blur gradient distribution and defeat these shortcuts, and propose MSDCT-UNet (Multi-Scale Discrete Cosine Transform UNet), a frequency-aware encoder-decoder injecting multi-scale DCT priors through DCT Attention and FiLM. MSDCT-UNet ranks first in in-domain mIoU and boundary localization on BOCCHI, and BOCCHI-trained models outperform every other training source on cross-dataset transfer with only 633 training images.

AI.tldr: BOCCHI is a real-captured benchmark for local motion blur detection that defeats gradient shortcuts, and MSDCT-UNet, a frequency-aware encoder-decoder using multi-scale DCT priors, achieves state-of-the-art performance on it and cross-dataset transfer with only 633 training images.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks let models rely on gradient shortcuts that fail to transfer to real-world scenarios, motivating the need for a more challenging real-captured benchmark.
AI.method: MSDCT-UNet, a frequency-aware encoder-decoder that injects multi-scale DCT priors through DCT Attention and FiLM modules.
AI.result: MSDCT-UNet ranks first in in-domain mIoU and boundary localization on BOCCHI; BOCCHI-trained models outperform all other training sources on cross-dataset transfer with only 633 images.
AI.conclusion: The BOCCHI benchmark effectively defeats gradient shortcuts, and the proposed MSDCT-UNet with frequency-aware design achieves superior performance and generalization for local motion blur detection.

title: Agentic-DPO: From Imitation to Agentic Policy Optimization on Expert Trajectories
summary: Large Language Model (LLM) agents are commonly trained from expert trajectories using supervised fine-tuning (SFT), which treats multi-turn agent behavior as ordinary text imitation. This recipe is simple and low-cost, but it only learns to imitate the sequence of expert actions, rather than training the agent to choose the right action against plausible mistakes at each state. Existing methods to mitigate this problem include preference learning or reinforcement learning, but they usually need high-cost environment rollouts and reward models. We propose Agentic-DPO, a lightweight offline agent policy optimization method that turns expert trajectories into state-conditioned preference supervision. At each expert action state, Agentic-DPO samples a one-step action from the current state, treats plausible wrong actions as negatives, and contrasts them with the expert action using a DPO-style preference objective. To avoid mixing both policy and schema in preference learning, we introduce Policy-Preserving Augmentation (PPA), which renders the same latent trajectory under multiple schemas while keeping the expert policy fixed. Agentic-DPO requires no online environment rollout, reward model, or full-trajectory student exploration. We conduct experiments across StableToolBench, tau-bench retail, and Mind2Web, where Agentic-DPO consistently improves agents at different model scales beyond imitation. In particular, it raises tau-bench accuracy from 21.7% (SFT) to 41.4% for a 9B model, matching online GRPO under the same backbone with only step-level rollouts and without environment interaction during gradient steps. The results suggest that expert trajectories can support low-cost agentic policy optimization when converted from demonstrations into state-level action preferences. Code for Agentic-DPO is released at https://github.com/Schuture/Agentic-DPO.

AI.tldr: Agentic-DPO converts expert trajectories into state-conditioned preferences for lightweight, offline agent policy optimization without needing environment rollouts, reward models, or full-trajectory exploration, consistently outperforming SFT across benchmarks.
AI.motivation: LLM agents trained with SFT from expert trajectories only imitate action sequences rather than learning to choose the right action against plausible mistakes at each state. Existing solutions (preference learning, RL) require costly environment rollouts and reward models.
AI.method: Agentic-DPO samples a one-step action from the current state at each expert action state, treats plausible wrong actions as negatives, and contrasts them with the expert action using a DPO-style preference objective. It also introduces Policy-Preserving Augmentation (PPA) to avoid mixing policy and schema in preference learning.
AI.result: Consistently improves agents across StableToolBench, tau-bench retail, and Mind2Web at various model scales. Notably raises tau-bench accuracy from 21.7% (SFT) to 41.4% for a 9B model, matching online GRPO without environment interaction during gradient steps.
AI.conclusion: Expert trajectories can support low-cost agentic policy optimization when converted from demonstrations into state-level action preferences, offering an efficient alternative to costly online RL methods.

title: PAKE: Learning Whole-Body Loco-Manipulation with Partial Kinematic Embeddings
summary: Loco-manipulation has recently shown promising capabilities; however, achieving high-precision control, managing the high-dimensional action space induced by many degrees of freedom (DoFs), and fully exploiting the inherent redundancy of whole-body systems remain challenging. In this paper, we propose a novel whole-body control framework that effectively addresses these challenges by decomposing the complex loco-manipulation problem into partial reference motion generation and low-level imitation control. We introduce a new Kinematic Normalizing Flow (KNF) model, trained on a large-scale kinematic dataset, that generates diverse yet feasible partial reference motions. A high-level controller is then trained to navigate the KNF's latent space to exploit redundant solutions, while a low-level controller ensures physically feasible and accurate motion execution. We validate our approach on the quadrupedal robot equipped with a six-DoF robotic arm. In simulation, experimental results show that our approach significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods in terms of tracking accuracy and feasible workspace coverage. For hardware deployment, we evaluate the system over 24 episodes across 8 different mobile loco-manipulation tasks. The system achieves end-effector pose-tracking errors of 4.5 cm and 0.14 rad, while maintaining accurate locomotion tracking with linear and angular velocity errors of 0.1 m/s and 0.01 rad/s, respectively, outperforming competitive baselines. Our method represents a practical and powerful solution for accurate and generalized whole-body loco-manipulation in high-DoF robotic systems, with promising potential for diverse downstream robotic tasks.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a whole-body control framework for loco-manipulation that decomposes the problem into partial reference motion generation (using a novel Kinematic Normalizing Flow model) and low-level imitation control. It achieves high precision on a quadruped with a 6-DoF arm, outperforming baselines in simulation and real-world tasks.
AI.motivation: Loco-manipulation faces challenges in high-precision control, managing high-dimensional action spaces from many DoFs, and fully exploiting the inherent redundancy of whole-body systems.
AI.method: The framework decomposes loco-manipulation into partial reference motion generation and low-level imitation control. It introduces a Kinematic Normalizing Flow (KNF) model trained on large-scale kinematic data to generate diverse feasible motions, a high-level controller navigating KNF's latent space to exploit redundancy, and a low-level controller ensuring physical feasibility.
AI.result: In simulation, the approach significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods in tracking accuracy and feasible workspace coverage. On hardware over 24 episodes across 8 tasks, it achieves end-effector errors of 4.5 cm and 0.14 rad, with locomotion tracking errors of 0.1 m/s and 0.01 rad/s, outperforming competitive baselines.
AI.conclusion: The method is a practical and powerful solution for accurate and generalized whole-body loco-manipulation in high-DoF robotic systems, with promising potential for diverse downstream tasks.

title: Data-Driven Telecom Marketing Optimization: A Machine Learning-Based Churn Prediction and Customer Segmentation Framework
summary: Customer churn is a major challenge for telecommunication companies, directly eroding revenue and long term customer relationships. Traditional retention programs rely on generic, not personalized incentives and lack the precision to identify high risk customers before they leave. This paper presents a data driven marketing optimization framework integrating machine learning based churn prediction, customer segmentation combining churn risk with customer value, and tailored, segment specific marketing and Return on Investment ROI strategies. Using the IBM Telco Customer Churn dataset with 7043 customers and 21 features, three gradient boosting ensembles, XGBoost, LightGBM, and CatBoost, were trained and tuned via randomized search with stratified 5 fold cross validation, class weighting, and F1 score driven decision threshold optimization to counter a class imbalance of 73.4% versus 26.6%. CatBoost was selected as the deployment model, achieving 77.68% accuracy, an F1 score of 0.6366, a PR AUC of 0.6553, and a ROC AUC of 0.8403 on the held out test set. Customers were partitioned with K Means clustering, validated via the Elbow method and visualized with Principal Component Analysis, into High, Medium, and Low Value segments, cross tabulated against churn risk labels to define four actionable clusters. Segment specific retention, upsell, and engagement strategies were designed for each cluster, and a theoretical ROI and CLV framework quantifies the financial impact of the proposed interventions. The pipeline was operationalized in an interactive Streamlit web application allowing marketing teams to upload data, filter by segment, visualize churn drivers via SHAP, and download automated segment reports. Results confirm that combining predictive churn modeling with value aware segmentation yields more actionable and profitable marketing decisions than churn prediction alone.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a data-driven marketing optimization framework that combines gradient boosting models (CatBoost achieving best performance) with K-Means customer segmentation to predict churn and design segment-specific retention strategies, operationalized via a Streamlit app.
AI.motivation: Telecom companies face revenue erosion from customer churn, and traditional retention programs lack personalization and precision in identifying high-risk customers before they leave.
AI.method: Three gradient boosting ensembles (XGBoost, LightGBM, CatBoost) were trained on the IBM Telco dataset (7043 customers, 21 features) with randomized search, stratified 5-fold CV, class weighting, and F1-driven threshold optimization. Customers were segmented via K-Means clustering (validated by Elbow + PCA) into value tiers, then cross-tabulated with churn risk labels. A theoretical ROI/CLV framework quantifies financial impact, deployed in an interactive Streamlit app with SHAP visualization.
AI.result: CatBoost was selected as the deployment model, achieving 77.68% accuracy, F1 score of 0.6366, PR AUC of 0.6553, and ROC AUC of 0.8403 on the held-out test set. Four actionable clusters were defined by cross-tabulating value segments (High/Medium/Low) with churn risk labels.
AI.conclusion: Combining predictive churn modeling with value-aware segmentation yields more actionable and profitable marketing decisions than churn prediction alone.

title: Annotation-Free Furniture Codes: What They Encode, and How Far They Transfer
summary: Layout-based 3D scene synthesizers place each object using two human-annotated channels: a categorical class label and a canonical-pose convention. We ask whether a single self-supervised token derived from object geometry can replace both, and study such tokens directly as a representation, decoupled from any synthesizer. A Finite Scalar Quantization (FSQ) point-cloud autoencoder is chamfer-trained on placed 3D-FUTURE furniture with no labels or pose annotations. Diagnostic probes recover fine-category (62.6 +/- 0.5%), super-category (85.6 +/- 1.3%), and yaw (52.7 +/- 0.5 deg) from the codes alone. Swapping the chamfer target from the rotated to the un-rotated point cloud collapses the yaw signal while raising class recovery, showing the codes' rotation content can be set by the training objective. Scaling across asset libraries needs codes that transfer; on an unseen dataset (ShapeNet), alignment is category-dependent: box-like furniture transfers, organically-shaped furniture does not, and a target-blind augmentation partly closes the gap.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes using a single self-supervised token from object geometry (via FSQ point-cloud autoencoder) to replace both categorical class labels and canonical-pose conventions in layout-based 3D scene synthesis. The learned codes capture category and orientation information, with rotation content controllable by training objective, though transfer to unseen datasets is category-dependent.
AI.motivation: To eliminate the need for human-annotated categorical labels and canonical-pose conventions in 3D scene synthesizers by replacing them with a single self-supervised token derived from object geometry.
AI.method: A Finite Scalar Quantization (FSQ) point-cloud autoencoder is trained with chamfer loss on placed 3D-FUTURE furniture without labels or pose annotations. Diagnostic probes evaluate category and orientation recovery from the learned codes.
AI.result: The codes achieve 62.6% fine-category recovery, 85.6% super-category recovery, and 52.7-degree yaw accuracy. Swapping chamfer target from rotated to un-rotated point clouds reduces yaw signal while improving class recovery. Transfer to ShapeNet is category-dependent: box-like furniture transfers well but organically-shaped furniture does not.
AI.conclusion: A single self-supervised geometric token can encode both class and pose information, with its rotation sensitivity controllable via training objective. However, cross-dataset generalization remains challenging for non-primitive shapes, though target-blind augmentation can partially close this gap.

title: The Compliance Trap: Diagnosing How AI Agents Consume Conflicting Memory
summary: Memory is becoming a core component of long-horizon AI agents, allowing agents to reuse past experience when operating web browsers, software tools, and other interactive environments. Existing work mostly treats memory as a supply problem, asking what experience to write, how to store it, and which entry to retrieve for the next task. Yet we still lack a clear account of how models consume retrieved memory across a multi-step action trajectory. This consumption process matters because it determines not only what memories should be retrieved, but also what models and control policies are needed to use them safely. To diagnose this process, we propose Entry--Propagation--Recovery (E-P-R), a trajectory-level framework that asks where memory first changes an action, whether that change carries forward, and whether the agent can recover after leaving a correct path. We instantiate E-P-R on WebArena and on MemTrapBench, a controlled benchmark we build to isolate these phases. We find that the main failure often begins at entry: agents adopt conflicting memory at the first exposed decision point even when it is task-wrong. Repeated exposure then amplifies this early error, while recovery after divergence is weak. Together, these effects create a compliance trap: across models, conflicting memory induces similar compliance rates, but once agents comply, their success rates collapse to a low floor. Stronger agents therefore suffer larger absolute damage because each compliance event erases more baseline capability. These results suggest that memory-augmented agents should be evaluated not only by retrieval quality or final success rate, but by how they consume memory throughout the trajectory.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces E-P-R, a framework analyzing how AI agents consume retrieved memory across multi-step action trajectories. The main finding is a "compliance trap": conflicting memory leads to early entry errors that amplify over time, causing stronger agents to suffer greater performance loss when they comply with misleading memory.
AI.motivation: Existing memory research focuses on what to write, store, and retrieve (supply side), but lacks a clear account of how models consume retrieved memory across multi-step trajectories. Understanding consumption is crucial for determining what memories to retrieve and what policies are needed for safe memory use.
AI.method: Proposes E-P-R (Entry-Propagation-Recovery) trajectory-level framework analyzing where memory first changes an action (Entry), whether that change carries forward (Propagation), and whether the agent can recover after leaving the correct path (Recovery). Instantiates on WebArena and MemTrapBench (a new controlled benchmark).
AI.result: Main failure begins at entry—agents adopt conflicting memory at the first decision point. Repeated exposure amplifies early errors. Recovery after divergence is weak. This creates a compliance trap: conflicting memory induces similar compliance rates across models, but compliance collapses success rates to a low floor, causing stronger agents to suffer larger absolute damage.
AI.conclusion: Memory-augmented agents should be evaluated by how they consume memory throughout the trajectory, not just by retrieval quality or final success rate.

title: Desc++: Efficient Descriptor Enhancement for Data Association in Existing Visual SLAM Systems
summary: Reliable visual data association is fundamental to visual SLAM (V-SLAM), as it directly determines the quality of the camera pose estimation and map consistency. However, the handcrafted descriptors used by most mature real-time systems degrade under illumination and viewpoint changes, while learning-based front-ends that address this weakness typically require replacing the extraction-and-matching pipeline and introduce substantial computational overhead. Descriptor enhancement offers a compromise by refining existing descriptors within their original format, yet current methods rely on simplified attention mechanisms whose limited contextual modeling constrains the achievable matching quality. To resolve this trade-off between contextual expressiveness and efficiency, we propose Desc++, a lightweight enhancement module that jointly encodes descriptor representations and keypoint geometry and aggregates spatial context through a hybrid architecture that combines order-agnostic global attention with geometry-aware sequential modeling in linear time. The enhanced descriptors retain their original dimensionality and matching interface, enabling integration into deployed V-SLAM systems without modifying the pipeline. Experiments across descriptor matching, correspondence analysis, and system-level benchmarks with four different V-SLAM systems demonstrate that Desc++ improves matching accuracy over the state-of-the-art enhancement method, translates these gains into more accurate and stable trajectory estimation, and achieves a favorable balance between accuracy and efficiency for practical integration into existing real-time V-SLAM pipelines.

AI.tldr: Desc++ is a lightweight descriptor enhancement module that improves visual data association for V-SLAM by jointly encoding descriptor features and keypoint geometry using a hybrid attention architecture, achieving better matching accuracy and trajectory estimation without modifying existing pipelines.
AI.motivation: Existing handcrafted descriptors degrade under illumination and viewpoint changes, while learning-based front-ends are computationally heavy. Current descriptor enhancement methods use simplified attention with limited contextual modeling. The goal is to improve matching quality without sacrificing efficiency or requiring pipeline changes.
AI.method: Desc++ is a lightweight enhancement module that jointly encodes descriptor representations and keypoint geometry, and aggregates spatial context via a hybrid architecture combining order-agnostic global attention with geometry-aware sequential modeling in linear time. It retains original descriptor dimensionality and matching interface.
AI.result: Desc++ improves matching accuracy over state-of-the-art enhancement methods, leads to more accurate and stable trajectory estimation across four different V-SLAM systems, and achieves a favorable balance between accuracy and efficiency for real-time deployment.
AI.conclusion: Desc++ effectively resolves the trade-off between contextual expressiveness and efficiency, offering a practical plug-and-play solution for enhancing descriptor matching in real-time V-SLAM pipelines.

title: Sharper Analysis of Single-Loop Methods for Bilevel Optimization
summary: Bilevel optimization underpins many machine learning applications, including hyperparameter optimization, meta-learning, neural architecture search, and reinforcement learning. While hypergradient-based methods have advanced significantly, a gap persists between theoretical guarantees and practical single-loop implementations required for efficiency. We bridge this gap by establishing sharper convergence results for single-loop approximate implicit differentiation (AID) and iterative differentiation (ITD) methods, leveraging our proposed analytical framework, decoupled norm analysis (DNA). For AID, we improve the convergence rate from $\mathcal{O}(κ^6/K)$ to $\mathcal{O}(κ^5/K)$, where $κ$ is the condition number of the inner-level problem. For ITD, we prove that the asymptotic error is $\mathcal{O}(κ^2)$, exactly matching the known lower bound and improving upon the previous $\mathcal{O}(κ^3)$ guarantee. Numerical experiments on synthetic and real tasks corroborate our theoretical findings.

AI.tldr: This paper bridges the gap between theoretical guarantees and practical single-loop implementations for bilevel optimization by proposing a decoupled norm analysis (DNA) framework, achieving tighter convergence rates: O(κ⁵/K) for AID (improved from O(κ⁶/K)) and O(κ²) asymptotic error for ITD (matching lower bound, improved from O(κ³)).
AI.motivation: To close the gap between theoretical convergence guarantees and practical efficient single-loop implementations in bilevel optimization, specifically for hypergradient-based methods like AID and ITD.
AI.method: Proposes a decoupled norm analysis (DNA) framework to analyze single-loop approximate implicit differentiation (AID) and iterative differentiation (ITD) methods, yielding sharper convergence bounds.
AI.result: AID: improved convergence rate from O(κ⁶/K) to O(κ⁵/K). ITD: asymptotic error improved from O(κ³) to O(κ²), matching the known lower bound.
AI.conclusion: The proposed DNA framework successfully tightens convergence guarantees for single-loop bilevel optimization methods, validated through synthetic and real experiments.

title: Not All Color Categories Are Equally Stable: A Multilingual Free Color Naming Experiment
comment: Submitted to 2026 Joint 14th International Conference on Soft Computing and Intelligent Systems and 27th International Symposium on Advanced Intelligent Systems (SCIS&ISIS 2026)
summary: Color naming is an important part of human color perception. Its task is to allow people to describe continuous colors using discrete color categories. However, the boundaries between color categories are often unclear, and some colors may be perceived differently depending on their saturation and brightness. While certain color categories remain recognizable across a wide range of shades, others may be associated with different color names when their appearance changes. This study investigates the consistency of color naming for red, yellow, and green color categories using a free color-naming experiment. A set of 18 color samples was selected from the COLIBRI dataset to represent different shades of these colors. Participants (n = 92) were asked to freely assign color names to each sample in Kazakh, Russian, or English without being limited to predefined categories. The results show that color categories differ in their consistency. Green shades were consistently identified as green despite variations in appearance, whereas yellow shades received a wider variety of names, including gold- and brown-related descriptions. Red shades showed moderate naming consistency. Our findings suggest that some color categories occupy broader perceptual regions than others and may therefore be more robust to visual variations. The study results can be used to develop perceptually meaningful color models and color naming systems.

AI.tldr: This study examines how consistently people name red, yellow, and green colors through a free-naming experiment with 92 participants across Kazakh, Russian, and English. Results show green is the most consistently named, yellow the least (with gold/brown alternatives), and red shows moderate consistency, suggesting some color categories are more perceptually robust than others.
AI.motivation: Color categories have fuzzy boundaries, and the same hue can be perceived differently depending on saturation and brightness. Understanding naming consistency across shades is important for developing perceptually meaningful color models.
AI.method: Free color-naming experiment using 18 color samples from the COLIBRI dataset representing various shades of red, yellow, and green. 92 participants assigned color names in Kazakh, Russian, or English without predefined category restrictions.
AI.result: Green shades were consistently named green; yellow shades showed wide variation (gold, brown); red shades had moderate naming consistency.
AI.conclusion: Color categories occupy broader or narrower perceptual regions—green is more robust to visual variation, while yellow is more ambiguous. Results can inform perceptually grounded color models and naming systems.

title: Embark Now: User Demand Oriented Framework for Multi-day Urban Travel Itinerary Planning
summary: In large urban areas, planning multi-day travel itineraries is challenging due to the abundance of Points of Interest (POIs), diverse user preferences, and constraints such as opening hours. Effective solutions must dynamically accommodate diverse traveler requirements while optimizing for satisfaction and feasibility within limited computation time. This paper addresses these challenges through introducing an innovative framework that integrates Large Language Models (LLMs) to dynamically capture user requirements with precision and flexibility, and an enhanced Greedy Randomized Adaptive Search Procedure (GRASP) algorithm as a well-suited preference-aware planner to generate feasible multi-day itineraries. The effectiveness of our integrated approach is demonstrated through extensive experiments on two real-world urban datasets from Beijing and Tianjin. Our framework significantly outperforms state-of-the-art (SOTA) methods, improving the average total itinerary score by at least 4.52% and 11.09% across 5,040 user cases with diverse preferences in the two datasets. Furthermore, through end-to-end algorithmic enhancements, it achieves notable average improvements of 17.95% and 26.07% in the computed metrics, while also delivering substantial gains in time efficiency -- realizing average performance increases of 4.64% and 25.55% within shorter computation times compared to suboptimal methods that require multiple iterations. These outcomes underscore our method's superiority in delivering both enhanced itinerary quality and computational efficiency over existing methodologies.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces an LLM-enhanced GRASP framework for planning multi-day travel itineraries in large urban areas, which outperforms SOTA methods by improving total itinerary scores by up to 26% and computational efficiency by up to 25% on real-world datasets from Beijing and Tianjin.
AI.motivation: Planning multi-day travel itineraries in large urban areas is challenging due to the abundance of POIs, diverse user preferences, and constraints like opening hours, requiring dynamic solutions that balance satisfaction and feasibility within limited computation time.
AI.method: The framework integrates Large Language Models (LLMs) to dynamically capture user requirements with precision, and an enhanced Greedy Randomized Adaptive Search Procedure (GRASP) algorithm as a preference-aware planner to generate feasible multi-day itineraries.
AI.result: On Beijing and Tianjin datasets across 5,040 user cases, the framework outperforms SOTA by 4.52%-26.07% in itinerary quality scores and achieves 4.64%-25.55% improvements in time efficiency with shorter computation times.
AI.conclusion: The integrated LLM+GRASP approach delivers superior itinerary quality and computational efficiency, demonstrating effectiveness through extensive real-world experiments and outperforming existing methodologies.

title: VIA: Visual Interface Agent for Robot Control
summary: Robot manipulation is a complex task that requires visual understanding, physical reasoning, planning, and closed-loop control. General-purpose foundation models (FMs) have grown remarkably capable of some of these, especially vision and reasoning. To leverage this for generalist robot policies, current methods typically involve converting existing FMs into vision-language-action (VLA) models by fine-tuning on robot data to output low-level actions. However, VLAs are often orders of magnitude smaller than frontier FMs given the limited data and compute available for fine-tuning, which in turn limits their general capability. Inspired by the growing ability of FMs to operate software through visual interfaces, we ask whether that same competence suffices to control a robot. We present VIA (Visual Interface Agent for robot control), a framework that recasts robot control as an agentic task: an off-the-shelf FM-powered agent drives a manipulator through a browser-based 3D interface by taking screenshots, issuing intuitive commands, observing the outcome, and adjusting. The agent receives no robot-specific fine-tuning and no access to privileged state information: it perceives visual input and acts through a small set of general tools. VIA inherits the agent's general reasoning, closed-loop error recovery, and ability to plan and re-plan from what it observes. It solves a diverse suite of tabletop manipulation tasks zero-shot with both Claude Code and Codex. With the strongest model (Fable 5) it achieves 96.7% success on three LIBERO-Goal tasks and 100% on a long-horizon rainbow assembly task. Performance improves with the scale and strength of the underlying model. These results suggest that frontier agents already possess skills that transfer directly to robot control given the right interface: your coding or computer-use agent is, in a sense, secretly a robot-control agent.

AI.tldr: VIA (Visual Interface Agent) recasts robot control as an agentic task where an off-the-shelf FM-powered agent drives a manipulator through a browser-based 3D interface using screenshots and intuitive commands, achieving 96.7% success on LIBERO-Goal tasks zero-shot without robot-specific fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: Current VLA models are limited in capability because they require fine-tuning on scarce robot data, resulting in models much smaller than frontier FMs. The paper explores whether frontier FMs' ability to operate software through visual interfaces can be directly transferred to robot control.
AI.method: VIA recasts robot control as an agentic task: an off-the-shelf FM agent (Claude Code, Codex, Fable 5) controls a robot manipulator through a browser-based 3D interface by taking screenshots, issuing commands, observing outcomes, and adjusting—without any robot-specific fine-tuning or privileged state information.
AI.result: VIA solves diverse tabletop manipulation tasks zero-shot. With Fable 5 it achieves 96.7% success on three LIBERO-Goal tasks and 100% on a long-horizon rainbow assembly task. Performance scales with model capability.
AI.conclusion: Frontier agent models already possess skills that transfer directly to robot control given the right interface—coding or computer-use agents are, in a sense, secretly robot-control agents.

title: Interpreting learning dynamics of autoencoders: Transient scaling and emerging concepts of the Ising model
summary: We study how unsupervised autoencoders trained on microscopic spin configurations from the Ising model learn macroscopic, theory-relevant variables underlying the data-generating process. Without embedding domain knowledge, we mimic a typical discovery setting: We quantify learning across multiple spatial (coarse-graining) scales and reveal two distinct dynamical regimes controlled by main hyperparameters (model depth, width, and learning rate) -- a magnetization-dominated regime and an energy-dominated regime characterized by trade-offs in their representation quality. The first regime is a transitory state exhibiting dynamical scaling and fluctuations that follow an ordering-to-scale; the second gradually shifts resolution towards smaller scales relevant for the energy representation. Deep models trained at moderate and fast rates become arrested before reaching these regimes. With a novel analysis of recursive-dynamic trajectories, we demonstrate that prediction errors induce flow fields that produce a common trajectory topology across all representation spaces. A dynamical viewpoint of learning is established in which intrinsic properties expose the effects of forced changes in representation during training. We utilize the intuition that learning operates as a process driven far from equilibrium by fluctuations from the training data and optimizer to provide an interpretive basis grounded in both the physical world and the machine models that represent it.

AI.tldr: This paper studies unsupervised autoencoders trained on Ising model spin configurations, revealing two dynamical regimes (magnetization-dominated and energy-dominated) during learning, governed by hyperparameters like depth, width, and learning rate. The authors analyze recursive-dynamic trajectories and show that learning operates as a non-equilibrium process driven by data fluctuations.
AI.motivation: To understand how unsupervised neural networks learn macroscopic, theory-relevant variables from microscopic physical data (Ising model) without domain knowledge, mimicking a scientific discovery setting.
AI.method: Autoencoders trained on Ising model spin configurations; analysis across multiple coarse-graining scales; recursive-dynamic trajectory analysis; study of hyperparameter effects (depth, width, learning rate).
AI.result: Two distinct learning regimes: magnetization-dominated (transitory, ordering-to-scale dynamics) and energy-dominated (shift toward smaller scales). Deep models at moderate/fast learning rates become arrested. Prediction errors induce flow fields with common trajectory topology across representation spaces.
