AI.result: RLVP improves over pre-trained and supervised-only baselines on PDE benchmarks, shows zero-shot improvement transfer to held-out PDEs, and a smaller LLM post-trained with RLVP can outperform frontier model prompting on in-distribution PDE solver generation.
AI.conclusion: RLVP demonstrates that post-training with physics-graded rewards yields composable numerical motifs (stencils, time-stepping, boundary handling) that generalize to unseen PDE problems.

title: Why Domain Matters: Domain-Aware Benchmarking of Underwater Object Detection and Annotation Quality
summary: Underwater object detection is strongly affected by domain shift, where performance can vary significantly across different locations, habitats, and deployment conditions. However, detector performance is typically evaluated using aggregate metrics that hide failures in specific environments, while existing domain generalization benchmarks often rely on synthetic variations that do not reflect real-world conditions. We introduce a framework that characterizes underwater images by appearance, scene composition, and acquisition geometry to assign domain labels. Using this framework, we perform the first systematic study of how domain factors influence both human annotation quality in underwater object detection datasets and deep learning-based detector performance, revealing substantial domain-dependent discrepancies. By incorporating physically meaningful domain labels, domain shift becomes something we can characterize, measure, benchmark, and act on. We highlight how this can be used to guide data collection and annotation, design more informative benchmarks, and assess detector robustness across diverse underwater environments.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a framework to characterize underwater images by domain factors (appearance, scene composition, acquisition geometry), revealing that domain shift causes substantial discrepancies in both human annotation quality and deep learning detector performance, which are masked by aggregate metrics.
AI.motivation: Underwater object detection suffers from domain shift across different environments, but existing evaluation uses aggregate metrics that hide failures, and current benchmarks rely on synthetic variations that don't reflect real-world conditions.
AI.method: The authors propose a framework that assigns domain labels to underwater images based on three factors: appearance, scene composition, and acquisition geometry. They systematically study how these domain factors affect both human annotation quality and deep learning detector performance.
AI.result: Significant domain-dependent discrepancies were found in both human annotations and detector performance, demonstrating that aggregate metrics mask environment-specific failures.
AI.conclusion: By incorporating physically meaningful domain labels, domain shift becomes a measurable and actionable problem, enabling better data collection, annotation guidance, informative benchmarks, and robustness assessment across diverse underwater environments.

title: Toward Contemplative LLM: A Modular Framework for Evaluating and Enhancing LLM Alignment in Mental Health
comment: Accepted as an oral presentation at HARMONY 2026 (Human-centered AI Research for Mental Health, an Open Networking Symposium), co-located with IEEE/ACM Conference on Connected Health: Applications, Systems, and Engineering Technologies (CHASE 2026) held in Pittsburgh, August 6, 2026
summary: Contemplative traditions have long guided ethical behavior and prosocial interaction, and recent work suggests that contemplative principles (e.g., mindfulness, compassion, non-dual reasoning) may offer a promising paradigm for aligning large language models (LLMs), improving cooperation and reducing ethical violations in LLM outputs. However, as new models, evaluation metrics, and benchmarks emerge rapidly, it remains challenging to systematically assess whether and how contemplative principles enhance LLM alignment across diverse and evolving scenarios, and existing approaches are often ad hoc and fail to generalize. We present a modular, extensible evaluation framework, initially targeted at the mental health domain, that enables seamless integration of new models, metrics, and benchmarks through a reusable pipeline. The framework currently reproduces existing state-of-the-art results and supports systematic cross-evaluation by flexibly mixing and matching models, metrics, and benchmarks, enabling fair comparison and deeper insight. Its plug-and-play prompting module offers a principled pathway for incorporating ethical perspectives such as contemplative principles, allowing domain experts to define alignment criteria without requiring technical expertise. Although initially focused on mental health, the framework is domain-agnostic and extends naturally to areas such as decision-making, moral reasoning, and human-AI collaboration. By bridging computational evaluation with human-centered ethical reasoning, this work lays the groundwork for interdisciplinary research spanning cognitive science, behavioral economics, philosophy, and system design, toward robust, trustworthy, and socially beneficial human-AI ecosystems.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a modular, extensible evaluation framework for assessing whether contemplative principles (mindfulness, compassion, non-dual reasoning) can improve LLM alignment, initially targeting mental health but designed to be domain-agnostic. It reproduces state-of-the-art results and enables plug-and-play incorporation of ethical perspectives without requiring technical expertise.
AI.motivation: Contemplative traditions offer promising principles for aligning LLMs (improving cooperation, reducing ethical violations), but existing approaches are ad hoc and fail to generalize across diverse and evolving models, metrics, and benchmarks. A systematic evaluation framework is needed.
AI.method: A modular, extensible evaluation framework with a reusable pipeline that supports plug-and-play integration of new models, metrics, benchmarks, and ethical prompting modules (e.g., contemplative principles). Domain experts can define alignment criteria without technical expertise.
AI.result: The framework currently reproduces existing state-of-the-art results and supports systematic cross-evaluation by flexibly mixing and matching models, metrics, and benchmarks, enabling fair comparison.
AI.conclusion: This work lays groundwork for interdisciplinary research spanning cognitive science, behavioral economics, philosophy, and system design, toward robust, trustworthy, and socially beneficial human-AI ecosystems.

title: From Sketch Prior to Trajectories: A Mission-Oriented Coordinated Navigation Framework for Indoor UAV Swarm
summary: UAV swarm for applications, such as indoor inspection, security patrol, and logistics delivery, are often mission-oriented rather than exploration-oriented. In these tasks, UAVs are required to visit task-relevant regions in a prescribed sequence, and such region-level mission information can often be obtained from pre-deployment sketch-map priors, such as floor plans, CAD layouts, or evacuation diagrams. Although these tasks are executed in three-dimensional space, UAVs usually fly within a specific altitude layer or a nearly fixed altitude range on each floor, making mission-level region transitions mainly governed by planar connectivity. Based on these observations, this paper proposes a mission-oriented coordinated navigation framework that exploits sketch-map priors for multi-UAV indoor operations. Onboard observations are used to perform topological alignment, and the aligned prior is fused with online observations to construct a mission-oriented traversability representation. A layered 2D--3D coordinated navigation framework is further developed, where 2D guided path planning generates mission-oriented guide paths and guide-driven 3D trajectory optimization produces dynamically feasible and collision-free trajectories. Simulation and real-world experiments validate the effectiveness of the proposed framework in structured multi-room indoor environments and further demonstrate its coordinated navigation capability under both communication-available and communication-loss conditions. Multi-floor simulation results show the scalability of the system to layered indoor structures.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a mission-oriented coordinated navigation framework for multi-UAV swarms in indoor environments, using pre-deployment sketch-map priors (floor plans, CAD layouts) combined with onboard observations for topological alignment and 2D-3D coordinated navigation, validated in simulations and real-world experiments.
AI.motivation: UAV swarm missions (indoor inspection, patrol, delivery) are mission-oriented rather than exploration-oriented, requiring UAVs to visit task-relevant regions in prescribed sequences. Existing approaches often neglect the availability of sketch-map priors and the planar nature of indoor region transitions.
AI.method: A layered 2D-3D coordinated navigation framework: (1) topological alignment of sketch-map priors with onboard observations; (2) fused traversability representation from aligned prior and online observations; (3) 2D guided path planning for mission-oriented guide paths; (4) guide-driven 3D trajectory optimization for dynamically feasible, collision-free trajectories.
AI.result: Simulation and real-world experiments validate effectiveness in structured multi-room indoor environments; demonstrates coordinated navigation under both communication-available and communication-loss conditions; multi-floor simulation results show scalability to layered indoor structures.
AI.conclusion: The sketch-map-prior-based framework effectively enables mission-oriented coordinated navigation for multi-UAV swarms in indoor environments, with robustness to communication constraints and scalability to multi-floor scenarios.

title: ARMOR: Stabilizing On-Policy LLM RL with Off-Policy Anchor Samples
summary: Reinforcement learning (RL) has significantly enhanced the reasoning capabilities of large language models (LLMs), yet the training process remains notoriously fragile. In this work, we investigate a critical source of this instability: over-optimization, where models exploit training heuristics at the expense of generalizable reasoning. While reverse KL regularization is the standard defense against such degradation, our analysis reveals that it is often insufficient in this regime, as it fails to ensure comprehensive coverage of the reference distribution. To address this, we propose ARMOR (Anchor Rollout and Mixed Optimization for RL), a framework that shifts the paradigm from passive penalty to active sample stabilization. ARMOR comprises two key components: (1) Anchor Rollout, which leverages off-policy data from the reference policy to preserve established solution patterns; and (2) Mixed Optimization, which reformulates the policy objective to enable controlled exploration without relying on auxiliary losses. Extensive experiments on reasoning benchmarks validate that ARMOR effectively mitigates validation collapse, enabling sustained performance improvements over extended training horizons.

AI.tldr: ARMOR is a reinforcement learning framework that improves LLM reasoning by using anchor rollouts (off-policy reference data) and mixed optimization to prevent over-optimization and validation collapse during training.
AI.motivation: RL-based training for LLMs suffers from instability due to over-optimization, where models exploit training heuristics and lose generalizable reasoning ability. Standard reverse KL regularization fails to adequately prevent this degradation.
AI.method: ARMOR framework with two components: (1) Anchor Rollout uses off-policy data from the reference policy to preserve established solution patterns; (2) Mixed Optimization reformulates the policy objective to enable controlled exploration without auxiliary losses.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on reasoning benchmarks show ARMOR effectively mitigates validation collapse and enables sustained performance improvements over extended training horizons.
AI.conclusion: Shifting from passive penalty-based regularization to active sample stabilization (via anchor rollouts and mixed optimization) provides a more effective defense against over-optimization in RL-based LLM reasoning training.

title: DiffUE: Enhancing Utility-Unlearnability Trade-off of Unlearnable Examples via Diffusion Autoencoders
comment: To appear at the European Conference on Computer Vision (ECCV), 2026
summary: AI models are increasingly trained on personal images scraped from social media and public platforms, often without consent, leading to serious privacy violations, such as unauthorized facial recognition and targeted advertising. To counter this, researchers have developed unlearnable examples (UEs), images modified with imperceptible noise to prevent AI models from extracting meaningful information. However, existing UE methods primarily rely on pixel-space noise, which can be bypassed by relearning strategies such as adversarial training, image transformation, and compression. While some techniques improve robustness, they often come at the expense of significant degradation in image utility and perceptual quality. In this paper, we introduce DiffUE to overcome these limitations by injecting noise into the semantic space of images instead of the pixel space. Instead of corrupting pixel values, DiffUE modifies high-level semantic features of images, ensuring robust unlearnability while preserving visual quality and utility. By leveraging a diffusion-based autoencoder framework to manipulate semantic features, DiffUE generates purposeful, natural-looking modifications that effectively resist advanced relearning strategies. Extensive experiments on four datasets, CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, CelebA-HQ, and ImageNet, as well as a subjective user study, demonstrate that DiffUE significantly enhances the trade-off between image quality and unlearnability, offering a more robust and effective solution for safeguarding personal data in an increasingly exploitative AI landscape.

AI.tldr: DiffUE introduces a diffusion-based autoencoder framework that injects noise into the semantic space of images (rather than pixel space) to create unlearnable examples that robustly prevent AI models from learning from personal data while preserving high visual quality and utility.
AI.motivation: Existing unlearnable example methods rely on pixel-space noise, which can be bypassed by relearning strategies (adversarial training, image transformations, compression), and robust alternatives often degrade image utility and perceptual quality.
AI.method: DiffUE leverages a diffusion-based autoencoder framework to inject noise into the semantic feature space of images, modifying high-level semantic features rather than pixel values, to generate natural-looking modifications that resist advanced relearning strategies.
AI.result: Experiments on CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, CelebA-HQ, and ImageNet datasets, plus a subjective user study, show that DiffUE significantly enhances the trade-off between image quality and unlearnability compared to existing methods.
AI.conclusion: DiffUE offers a more robust and effective solution for safeguarding personal data by preserving visual quality while ensuring strong unlearnability against sophisticated adversarial attacks.

title: LOGOS: A Living Logic for AI Agent Teams That Evolve With Humans
summary: AI agents are evolving from answer engines into persistent teams that use tools, delegate work, learn from experience, and modify the artifacts that shape their future behavior. The defining question for deployment is no longer merely what agents can do, but who controls what they are allowed to become. We introduce logos, a pluggable layer for self-evolution and governance that strengthens existing multiagent frameworks rather than replacing them. logos compiles heterogeneous multimodal inputs, including documents, images, audio, tables, databases, APIs, and human instructions into versioned agent packs containing agents, tools, knowledge, tests, permissions, and policies. During operation, it transforms agent activity into portable, auditable event traces and applies fail-closed verification across frameworks and backends. Every learned prompt, memory, skill, tool, role, or workflow remains an untrusted release candidate until held-out execution evidence, human-controlled policy, and explicit authorization permit its promotion. This architecture enables "verifiable human-agent loop engineering": agents can act, ask, learn, and propose improvements, while humans can steer objectives, permissions, approvals, and irreversible actions without interrupting continuous operation. logos provides a living logic for accountable automation. Agents may evolve at machine speed, but only evidence and human authority can close the loop.

AI.tldr: Logos is a pluggable governance layer for multi-agent systems that controls agent evolution through versioned packs, auditable traces, fail-closed verification, and human-in-the-loop authorization, enabling accountable automation where agents can propose changes but only humans can approve them.
AI.motivation: The key question shifts from "what agents can do" to "who controls what they become." Existing frameworks lack mechanisms to govern agent self-evolution (learning, modifying tools, changing behavior), creating risks of uncontrolled autonomous change.
AI.method: Logos compiles multimodal inputs into versioned agent packs (agents, tools, knowledge, tests, permissions, policies); transforms activity into auditable event traces; applies fail-closed verification across frameworks; treats all learned artifacts as untrusted release candidates requiring execution evidence, policy, and human authorization for promotion.
AI.result: Enables "verifiable human-agent loop engineering" where agents act, learn, and propose improvements while humans steer objectives/approvals without interrupting operations.
AI.conclusion: Agents may evolve at machine speed, but only evidence and human authority can close the loop, providing accountable automation.

title: WALA Learning Executable Latent Actions from Action-Labeled Demonstrations and Action-Free Videos
summary: Generalizable robot policies typically rely on action-labeled robot demonstrations, which are expensive to collect and difficult to scale. In contrast, large-scale human and robot videos contain rich physical interactions but often lack executable robot action labels. We present WALA, a framework for learning executable latent actions from both action-labeled demonstrations and action-free videos. WALA first pretrains a semantic-geometric latent action model from videos by modeling the evolution between current observations and sparsely sampled future observations. Instead of reconstructing raw pixels, WALA predicts future deltas in the DINOv3 feature space and dense depth space, preserving task-relevant semantic and geometric structure while reducing sensitivity to appearance details. During policy training, the pretrained encoder provides stable latent action targets, and the decoder serves as a trainable latent world model. The latent actions generated by the vision-language backbone are jointly supervised by robot action prediction, latent action target matching, and future dynamics prediction. This enables action-labeled demonstrations to provide executable control supervision, while action-free videos contribute dynamics supervision without requiring robot action annotations. Experiments show that WALA achieves strong performance on RoboTwin, sets a new state-of-the-art result on RoboCasa with 75.2% average success, and improves both policy performance and generalization in real-world manipulation tasks.

AI.tldr: WALA learns executable latent actions from both action-labeled robot demos and action-free videos by modeling semantic-geometric latent actions via DINOv3 and depth features, achieving SOTA results on RoboCasa (75.2%) and strong real-world manipulation performance.
AI.motivation: Generalizable robot policies require action-labeled demonstrations which are expensive and hard to scale, while large-scale human/robot videos lack executable action labels.
AI.method: WALA pretrains a semantic-geometric latent action model from videos by predicting future deltas in DINOv3 feature space and dense depth space, then jointly supervises the policy with robot action prediction, latent action target matching, and future dynamics prediction.
AI.result: State-of-the-art 75.2% average success on RoboCasa, strong performance on RoboTwin, and improved policy performance and generalization in real-world tasks.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating action-free video data via latent action learning effectively bridges the scalability gap between expensive robot demos and abundant video data, improving both performance and generalization.

title: EvidentialRAG: Quantifying and Mitigating Information Conflict in Multi-Source Retrieval-Augmented Generation via Evidential Deep Learning
summary: Retrieval-augmented generation grounds large language models in external evidence, but most pipelines still treat retrieved passages as deterministic and mutually consistent context. In open information environments, retrieved sources may disagree because of temporal drift, source error, ambiguity, or genuine uncertainty. This paper introduces ERAG, an uncertainty-aware RAG framework that converts retrieved chunks into probabilistic evidence before generation. A lightweight evaluator extracts candidate claims and maps chunk-level support to Dirichlet evidence. A conflict-preserving Dempster-Shafer fusion rule then transfers unresolved disagreement into epistemic uncertainty rather than normalizing it away. The generator is routed to direct answering, conflict-aware answering, or abstention according to the fused uncertainty score. Experiments on CRAG, ConflictQA, and MuSiQue show that ERAG remains competitive with the strongest matched baseline on standard question answering while improving behavior under conflict. On the CRAG ambiguous subset, hallucination decreases from 45.3% for Corrective RAG to a human-calibrated estimate of 34.8%, conflict resolution increases from 35.2% to 51.2%, and expected calibration error improves to 0.122. These results suggest that evidential modeling is a practical mechanism for trustworthy information processing in foundation-model-based retrieval systems.

AI.tldr: ERAG introduces an uncertainty-aware RAG framework that models retrieved passages as probabilistic evidence using Dirichlet distributions and Dempster-Shafer fusion, enabling the model to detect and handle conflicts through direct answering, conflict-aware answering, or abstention.
AI.motivation: Retrieved passages in RAG systems often disagree due to temporal drift, source error, ambiguity, or genuine uncertainty, but existing pipelines treat them as deterministic and mutually consistent, which leads to hallucinations and poor handling of conflicting information.
AI.method: ERAG converts retrieved chunks into probabilistic evidence: (1) a lightweight evaluator extracts candidate claims and maps chunk support to Dirichlet evidence, (2) a conflict-preserving Dempster-Shafer fusion rule transfers unresolved disagreement into epistemic uncertainty, and (3) the generator routes to direct answering, conflict-aware answering, or abstention based on the fused uncertainty score.
AI.result: On CRAG, ConflictQA, and MuSiQue, ERAG remains competitive with the strongest baseline on standard QA. On the CRAG ambiguous subset, hallucination drops from 45.3% to 34.8%, conflict resolution improves from 35.2% to 51.2%, and expected calibration error reaches 0.122.
AI.conclusion: Evidential modeling is a practical mechanism for trustworthy information processing in foundation-model-based retrieval systems.

title: Benchmarking UAV-based Vehicle Re-Identification under Simulated Weather Conditions
comment: Accepted at the 2026 International Conference on Multimedia Analysis and Pattern Recognition (MAPR 2026)
summary: UAV-based vehicle re-identification (ReID) has emerged as a promising technique for traffic surveillance, urban monitoring, and public-safety applications thanks to the flexible viewpoints and wide-area coverage provided by unmanned aerial vehicles. However, despite recent progress on UAV-based vehicle ReID benchmarks, the robustness of existing methods under adverse weather remains insufficiently studied. This is important because weather degradation can significantly affect the fine-grained appearance cues required for reliable vehicle matching in aerial imagery, especially under small object scale, viewpoint variation, and complex backgrounds. In this paper, we present a controlled comparative study of three representative recent vehicle ReID methods, namely CLIP-ReID, MSINet, and AdaSP, on two UAV-based benchmarks, VRU and UAV-VeID. To ensure consistent robustness evaluation, we generate synthetic foggy and rainy variants of both datasets using an analytical weather-effect pipeline while preserving the original identities and data splits. All methods are then trained and evaluated under matched clean, foggy, and rainy conditions. Experimental results show that adverse weather consistently degrades retrieval performance across both datasets, with rain causing larger drops than fog in nearly all settings. Among the evaluated methods, AdaSP demonstrates the strongest robustness, achieving 93.0% and 88.5% mAP on VRU-Large, and 88.7% and 76.2% mAP on UAV-VeID-Test under foggy and rainy conditions, respectively. Overall, our findings show that simulated adverse weather substantially increases the difficulty of UAV-based vehicle ReID, reveals clear robustness differences among recent methods, and highlights the need for weather-aware model design and evaluation protocols in future aerial ReID research. The code is released at https://github.com/tranminhvu945/Benchmarking-ReID.

AI.tldr: This paper evaluates three recent vehicle ReID methods (CLIP-ReID, MSINet, AdaSP) under synthetic foggy and rainy conditions on UAV benchmarks VRU and UAV-VeID, finding that adverse weather degrades performance (rain more than fog), with AdaSP being the most robust.
AI.motivation: Despite progress in UAV-based vehicle ReID, robustness under adverse weather remains understudied, which is critical because weather degradation affects fine-grained appearance cues needed for reliable matching in aerial imagery.
AI.method: The authors conduct a controlled comparative study of three recent methods (CLIP-ReID, MSINet, AdaSP) on two UAV benchmarks (VRU, UAV-VeID), generating synthetic foggy/rainy variants via an analytical weather-effect pipeline while preserving identities/splits, then evaluate under matched clean, foggy, and rainy conditions.
AI.result: Adverse weather consistently degrades retrieval performance across both datasets; rain causes larger drops than fog. AdaSP shows strongest robustness (93.0%/88.5% mAP on VRU-Large; 88.7%/76.2% mAP on UAV-VeID-Test under fog/rain respectively).
AI.conclusion: Simulated adverse weather substantially increases UAV-based vehicle ReID difficulty, reveals clear robustness differences among methods, and highlights the need for weather-aware model design and evaluation protocols in future aerial ReID research.

title: First-Order Modal Logic in HOL: Deep and Shallow Embeddings with Automated Faithfulness (Extended Preprint)
comment: 21 pages. Extended version, with a source-code appendix, of a paper accepted at ARQNL 2026 (International Workshop on Automated Reasoning in Quantified Non-Classical Logics). The full Isabelle/HOL development is included as ancillary files
summary:
We extend, in Isabelle/HOL, the deep-and-shallow embedding methodology of our prior work from propositional to first-order modal logic (FML) with constant-domain Kripke semantics. Three embeddings of FML into classical higher-order logic (HOL) are provided side by side: a deep embedding, a heavyweight maximal-shallow embedding, and a lightweight minimal-shallow embedding. The minimal-shallow embedding is presented as an Isabelle/HOL locale, parametrised by an accessibility relation, a world-indexed interpretation, a universe of worlds, and a variable assignment; the locale form admits a global faithfulness theorem, stating that quantifying over all minimal-shallow interpretations recovers exactly deep validity.
  A central technical contribution is a mechanisation, for FML under constant-domain Kripke semantics, of the (countable) downward Löwenheim-Skolem theorem, which underpins the automation of our faithfulness proof between the deep and minimal-shallow embeddings. Deploying it inside an extension of the minimal-shallow locale resolves the surjectivity problem that arises against an uncountable domain of individuals -- where the locale's variable assignment, having countable domain V = nat, cannot be surjective onto the domain -- and thereby yields faithfulness over the full domain.
  Since prior work treats only the propositional fragment, we develop here the substitution machinery (free/bound-variable predicates, the fresh-variable function, capture-avoiding substitution, alphabetic renaming, the substitutability predicate, the substitution lemma, and size-based induction principles) needed for the first-order quantifiers.

AI.tldr: The paper extends deep-and-shallow embedding methodology from propositional to first-order modal logic (FML) in Isabelle/HOL, provides three embeddings (deep, heavyweight maximal-shallow, lightweight minimal-shallow), and mechanizes the Löwenheim-Skolem theorem to prove faithfulness between deep and minimal-shallow embeddings. It also develops substitution machinery for first-order quantifiers.
AI.motivation: To extend prior deep-and-shallow embedding methodology from propositional to first-order modal logic, enabling mechanized reasoning about modal logic with quantifiers in Isabelle/HOL.
AI.method: Three embeddings of FML into classical HOL are provided: deep embedding, heavyweight maximal-shallow embedding, and lightweight minimal-shallow embedding (as an Isabelle/HOL locale). The countable downward Löwenheim-Skolem theorem is mechanized to resolve surjectivity issues with uncountable domains, and substitution machinery for first-order quantifiers is developed.
AI.result: Successful mechanization of faithfulness between deep and minimal-shallow embeddings via the Löwenheim-Skolem theorem, resolving the surjectivity problem for uncountable domains. Substitution machinery for first-order quantifiers is fully developed.
AI.conclusion: The deep-and-shallow embedding methodology is successfully extended to first-order modal logic, with mechanized faithfulness proofs enabled by the Löwenheim-Skolem theorem and comprehensive substitution machinery.

title: EDAR: Learning Environment-Dependent Action Representations for Robotic Manipulation
summary: Learning effective action representations is critical for robotic manipulation, where raw control trajectories are often noisy, redundant, and difficult to model directly. Existing methods mainly encode the structure of the action stream itself, treating the role of actions in the environment as implicit. Yet manipulation is about changing the world: the same action segment can induce different outcomes under different scene contexts, making action semantics inherently environment-dependent. We propose EDAR, an Environment-Dependent Action Representation that grounds action tokens in both executable control structure and expected visual consequences. By coupling motor commands with their environment-conditioned effects, EDAR encourages the learned action space to capture interaction semantics rather than merely command-level patterns. Experiments on simulated and real-robot manipulation benchmarks demonstrate that EDAR improves downstream policy learning, especially in long-horizon manipulation. These results highlight the importance of grounding action representations in executable control structure and environment-conditioned visual change.

AI.tldr: EDAR learns action representations that combine motor commands with their predicted visual effects, improving policy learning for robotic manipulation, especially in long-horizon tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing action representations encode only the structure of action streams, ignoring that actions are about changing the world; the same action can have different effects depending on scene context.
AI.method: EDAR grounds action tokens in both executable control structure and expected visual consequences, coupling motor commands with environment-conditioned effects to capture interaction semantics.
AI.result: EDAR improves downstream policy learning on simulated and real-robot manipulation benchmarks, particularly for long-horizon tasks.
AI.conclusion: Grounding action representations in both executable control structure and environment-conditioned visual change is important for effective robotic manipulation.

title: Learning from Noise: Effective-Rank Collapse and Out-of-Distribution Rejection in Restricted Boltzmann Machines
summary: Restricted Boltzmann machines (RBMs) represent data by shaping an energy landscape over visible and hidden configurations, but their discriminative use is fragile under out-of-distribution (OOD) inputs: samples outside the training distribution can be absorbed into one of the learned class basins rather than rejected. Here, we analyze this failure mode through the spectrum of the induced visible--visible interaction $J=WW^{T}$, where \(W\) is the visible--hidden weight matrix. Relative to a Marchenko--Pastur random-matrix reference, conventional training spreads spectral weight into many weak, bulk-compatible directions, increasing the effective rank of $J$. When auxiliary random binary images are assigned to a rejection label during training, the learned interaction undergoes effective-rank collapse: weak bulk-like modes are depleted, spectral weight concentrates into fewer dominant eigendirections, and the effective rank of $J$ approaches that of the empirical data covariance matrix. The resulting RBM rejects structured OOD image datasets while preserving MNIST classification accuracy, showing that random auxiliary exposure can reshape both the interaction spectrum and the free-energy landscape of an energy-based classifier.

AI.tldr: RBMs fail on OOD data because their energy landscape absorbs outliers; training with random auxiliary images as a rejection label collapses the effective rank of the visible-visible interaction matrix, making the model reject OOD inputs while preserving in-distribution accuracy.
AI.motivation: To understand why discriminative RBMs fail under out-of-distribution (OOD) inputs and to find a principled way to fix this fragility by analyzing the spectral properties of the visible-visible interaction matrix.
AI.method: Analyze the spectrum of J = WW^T relative to the Marchenko-Pastur random-matrix reference; train RBMs with auxiliary random binary images assigned to a rejection label, inducing effective-rank collapse in J.
AI.result: Conventional training spreads spectral weight into many weak bulk-compatible directions. Adding random auxiliary rejection training depletes weak modes, concentrates spectral weight into dominant eigendirections, and makes the effective rank of J approach that of the empirical data covariance. The resulting RBM rejects structured OOD datasets while keeping MNIST classification accuracy.
AI.conclusion: Random auxiliary exposure during training reshapes both the interaction spectrum and the free-energy landscape of an energy-based classifier, enabling OOD rejection without sacrificing in-distribution performance.

title: Anomalous Frame Detection by Grouping Frame Similarities between Two Videos Computed by Vision-Language Model to Extract Expert Workers' Unique Actions
summary: Maintenance of critical infrastructures, such as railways and power plants, is essential for operational safety and reliability. However, the declining number of skilled maintenance workers poses a serious challenge to sustaining these operations, highlighting the need to effectively transfer expert know-how to less experienced workers. Although traditional interview-based approaches have been used to elicit maintenance skills, they struggle to capture know-how that experts themselves may not consciously recognize. To address this gap, we proposed a method that detects anomalous frames of candidate actions including know-how by comparing a video of manual-based work with that of expert maintenance workers. In a simulated maintenance experiment involving a distribution board, our method targeted 11 types of actions not described in the manual and achieved a 66.9% extraction rate, marking a 50-percentage-point improvement over conventional techniques. These findings underscore the effectiveness of our approach in revealing hidden maintenance knowledge, thereby contributing to enhanced skill transfer and workforce development in critical infrastructure maintenance.

AI.tldr: A method that detects anomalous frames in maintenance videos by comparing manual-based work with expert work, achieving 66.9% extraction rate for undocumented actions (50 percentage points improvement), to transfer hidden know-how from experts to less experienced workers.
AI.motivation: The declining number of skilled maintenance workers threatens critical infrastructure operations, and traditional interview-based methods fail to capture tacit know-how that experts cannot consciously articulate.
AI.method: Detects anomalous frames of candidate actions by comparing video recordings of manual-based work with those of expert maintenance workers, targeting actions not described in manuals.
AI.result: Achieved 66.9% extraction rate for 11 types of undocumented actions in a simulated distribution board maintenance experiment, improving by 50 percentage points over conventional techniques.
AI.conclusion: The approach effectively reveals hidden maintenance knowledge, enhancing skill transfer and workforce development in critical infrastructure maintenance.

title: SETA: Scaling Environments for Terminal Agents
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are rapidly shifting toward agents that solve tasks through diverse interfaces, including web and graphical user interfaces (GUIs). Among these, the terminal command line provides a text-based, general-purpose interface, covering tasks from system operations to data science and machine learning. However, scaling terminal-agent training remains challenging, as it requires diverse and coherent task instructions, executable environments, and reliable verification, while lacking naturally grounded supervision data. In this work, we propose SETA, a scalable framework for generating verifiable terminal environments for reinforcement learning (RL). The framework consists of two pipelines sharing a unified verification mechanism: SETA-Synth converts diverse sources into standardized RL environments, and SETA-Evol further expands from existing environments with adaptive control of difficulty and diversity. Together, we construct and release SETA-Env, the largest open-source verifiable terminal RL dataset to date, containing over 4,500 environments. We evaluate our dataset by training Qwen3-8B with GRPO on SETA-Env, achieving 12% pass rate on Terminal-Bench 2.0, the best reported result for an RL-trained model at the 8B scale. We further observe gains on DeepSeek-V4-Flash under the same terminal agent harness, with pass@1 on Terminal-Bench 2.0 improving from 40% to 43% and pass@5 improving from 54% to 58%. These results demonstrate that SETA- Env provides high-quality training environments for terminal agents and serves as a valuable resource for advancing research on terminal-based agent learning.

AI.tldr: SETA is a scalable framework for generating verifiable terminal environments to train reinforcement learning agents, resulting in the largest open-source terminal RL dataset (SETA-Env, 4,500+ environments) that improves terminal agent performance by up to 12% pass rate on benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Terminal agent training is challenging due to the lack of diverse, coherent task instructions, executable environments, reliable verification, and naturally grounded supervision data.
AI.method: SETA consists of two pipelines with unified verification: SETA-Synth converts diverse sources into standardized RL environments, and SETA-Evol expands existing environments with adaptive difficulty and diversity control.
AI.result: Training Qwen3-8B with GRPO on SETA-Env achieves 12% pass rate on Terminal-Bench 2.0 (best reported for 8B RL-trained model). DeepSeek-V4-Flash improves from 40% to 43% (pass@1) and 54% to 58% (pass@5) on Terminal-Bench 2.0.
AI.conclusion: SETA-Env provides high-quality training environments for terminal agents and serves as a valuable resource for advancing terminal-based agent learning research.

title: Towards Human-level Dexterous Teleoperation
summary: Humans routinely wield tools, swap grasps, and reposition objects within a single hand, seamlessly orchestrating contact transitions that span translation, reorientation, and finger gaiting. Endowing robot dexterous hands with this level of in-hand dexterity through teleoperation requires precise control of object motion via dynamic hand-object contact, yet current teleoperation systems remain far from this capability. To bridge this gap, we take a major step towards human-level dexterous teleoperation by introducing TeleDexter, a hand-object co-tracking controller that maps operator intent into learned, low-level contact execution. The controller is trained on consecutive co-tracking subgoals derived from human reference motions, utilizing a hybrid reward that couples sparse subgoal objectives with dense tracking rewards to enable learning across diverse interaction modalities rather than frame-wise trajectory imitation. The entire pipeline requires only single-stage RL and, with random action masking and domain randomization, transfers zero-shot to the real robot. We evaluate TeleDexter on seven challenging dexterous teleoperation tasks spanning object reorientation and long-horizon tool use across two dexterous hands, achieving a 75% average success rate where all baselines consistently fail. Furthermore, the collected demonstrations successfully train autonomous policies via behavioral cloning, marking a concrete step towards human-level dexterous teleoperation.

AI.tldr: TeleDexter introduces a hand-object co-tracking controller that enables human-level dexterous teleoperation for robot hands by mapping operator intent into learned contact execution, achieving 75% success on challenging tasks where baselines fail.
AI.motivation: Current teleoperation systems cannot achieve human-level in-hand dexterity (e.g., swapping grasps, repositioning objects, using tools) due to the difficulty of precisely controlling object motion through dynamic hand-object contact.
AI.method: A hand-object co-tracking controller trained with single-stage RL on consecutive co-tracking subgoals derived from human reference motions, using a hybrid reward (sparse subgoal + dense tracking), random action masking, and domain randomization for zero-shot sim-to-real transfer.
AI.result: 75% average success rate on seven challenging tasks (object reorientation, long-horizon tool use) across two dexterous hands, where baselines consistently fail; collected demonstrations successfully train autonomous policies via behavioral cloning.
AI.conclusion: This work marks a concrete step towards human-level dexterous teleoperation by enabling learned, low-level contact execution from operator intent across diverse interaction modalities.

title: Conditional Optimal Bridge for Riemannian Activation Steering
summary: Activation steering offers a lightweight alternative to fine-tuning for controlling large language models at inference time. While many existing methods implicitly optimize a log-density-ratio objective between desired and undesired activation distributions, they do so heuristically rather than deriving it from a principled optimization problem. Moreover, these methods produce query-independent steering directions that can degrade performance on both in-distribution and out-of-distribution (OOD) inputs. We introduce \textsc{Cobras} (Conditional Optimal Bridge for Riemannian Activation Steering), which addresses both limitations by casting activation steering as a Schrödinger Bridge on the residual-stream hypersphere. This formulation yields, to our knowledge, the first principled derivation of the log-density-ratio steering objective from a well-posed optimization problem. Solving the bridge via entropic optimal transport and extracting the probability flow ODE recovers the widely used density-ratio gradient as a special case when the Sinkhorn potentials are uniform. Crucially, the Schrödinger potentials are evaluated at the current activation, making the resulting steering direction inherently query-adaptive. Empirically, across four models and three alignment axes (helpfulness, truthfulness, and detoxification), \textsc{Cobras} consistently outperforms prior activation steering baselines while avoiding the OOD degradation commonly observed in existing methods. The code can be found at https://github.com/arshandalili/cobras.

AI.tldr: Cobras introduces a Schrödinger Bridge formulation for activation steering in LLMs, providing a principled derivation of the log-density-ratio objective and producing query-adaptive steering directions that outperform prior methods while avoiding OOD degradation.
AI.motivation: Existing activation steering methods optimize log-density-ratio objectives heuristically without a principled optimization foundation, and produce query-independent steering directions that degrade performance on both in-distribution and out-of-distribution inputs.
AI.method: Cobras casts activation steering as a Schrödinger Bridge on the residual-stream hypersphere, solved via entropic optimal transport. The probability flow ODE recovers the density-ratio gradient as a special case, and the Schrödinger potentials are evaluated at the current activation, yielding query-adaptive steering directions.
AI.result: Across four models and three alignment axes (helpfulness, truthfulness, detoxification), Cobras consistently outperforms prior activation steering baselines while avoiding OOD degradation.
AI.conclusion: Cobras provides a principled, theoretically grounded approach to activation steering that is both more effective and more robust than existing heuristic methods.

title: End-to-End Real-Time Drone-Based Person Detection Framework Using Deep Learning
summary: In recent years, Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs) or drones have gained rapid response in terms of security, search and rescue (SAR), border surveillance, etc. Existing monitoring frameworks often struggle to maintain detection consistency when targets undergo significant scale variations due to altitude changes, leading to critical information gaps. To address this issue, this work proposes an integrated real-time detection pipeline for detecting targets through the wireless live drone video feed. Build upon YOLOv8-nano architecture, extensive flight experiments were conducted to determine the detection performance across multiple flight altitudes. Trained on VisDrone2019 dataset, the results of YOLOv8-nano model achieves 57.4%, 41%, 44.8% and 20.3% in precision, recall, mAP and mAP50:95 respectively. While demonstrating on real environment, this analysis revealed that the algorithm achieves near-total detection reliability at altitudes between 16 and 25 meters with the detection frame rate consistently maintained above 41 FPS and reaching a peak of 50 FPS. However, the goal of this work is to enable real-time person detection from an aerial platform via wireless transmission. This approach effectively addresses the dual challenges of identifying targets at varying scales and ensuring near-to-accurate localization during aerial observation.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a real-time drone-based detection pipeline using YOLOv8-nano, achieving near-total detection reliability at 16-25m altitude with 41-50 FPS, while addressing scale variation challenges in aerial surveillance.
AI.motivation: Existing monitoring frameworks struggle with detection consistency when targets undergo significant scale variations due to altitude changes, leading to critical information gaps in UAV-based surveillance.
AI.method: An integrated real-time detection pipeline built on YOLOv8-nano architecture, trained on VisDrone2019 dataset, with extensive flight experiments across multiple altitudes to evaluate detection performance.
AI.result: YOLOv8-nano achieved 57.4% precision, 41% recall, 44.8% mAP, 20.3% mAP50:95; near-total detection reliability at 16-25m altitude; frame rate consistently above 41 FPS, peaking at 50 FPS.
AI.conclusion: The approach effectively addresses dual challenges of identifying targets at varying scales and ensuring near-accurate localization during aerial observation for real-time person detection from drones.

title: Incremental Transformer for Surrogate-Based Inverse Design of Geopolymer Mixtures
summary: Small-data inverse design is challenging in engineering informatics when observations are heterogeneous, mixed-type, and constrained by physical relations among design variables. This work proposes a topology-aware surrogate framework guided by an Incremental Transformer (INCRT) for physics-constrained inverse design, applied to geopolymer mixture design. The method integrates intrinsic-dimensionality analysis, mixed-variable design-space representation, tabular surrogate prediction, INCRT-based manifold rationalisation, and constrained inverse optimisation. Using a public benchmark of fly-ash and slag-based geopolymer concrete mixtures with compressive-strength and carbon-emission targets, the high-dimensional design space proves strongly redundant, organising around fewer effective mixture regimes. Compressive strength requires nonlinear tabular surrogates, while carbon emission is largely determined by composition and well recovered by regularised linear models. INCRT thus acts not as a replacement for tabular predictors but as a rationalisation layer providing prototype regimes and a manifold-support score for inverse design. Three strategies are compared: unconstrained surrogate optimisation, physics-constrained optimisation, and topology-aware physics-constrained optimisation. Unconstrained optimisation can match target strength but may yield physically invalid or off-manifold candidates; physics-only constraints do not always ensure data support. The topology-aware strategy yields candidates balancing target compliance, carbon reduction, physical admissibility, and proximity to the learned feasible manifold. The framework aims not to replace experimental validation but to support screening of credible candidate mixtures from small, mixed, physically constrained engineering datasets.

AI.tldr: This paper presents INCRT, a topology-aware surrogate framework that integrates incremental transformers with manifold rationalisation for physics-constrained inverse design on small, heterogeneous, mixed-type engineering datasets, demonstrated on geopolymer mixture design. The topology-aware strategy outperforms unconstrained and physics-only approaches by balancing target compliance, carbon reduction, physical admissibility, and proximity to the learned feasible manifold.
AI.motivation: Small-data inverse design in engineering is challenging due to heterogeneous, mixed-type observations with physical constraints. Existing methods struggle with high-dimensional redundancy, physical validity, and data support when datasets are small.
AI.method: The framework combines intrinsic-dimensionality analysis, mixed-variable design-space representation, tabular surrogate prediction (nonlinear for strength, regularized linear for carbon emission), INCRT-based manifold rationalization providing prototype regimes and a manifold-support score, and constrained inverse optimization. Three strategies compared: unconstrained, physics-constrained, and topology-aware physics-constrained optimization.
AI.result: The high-dimensional design space is strongly redundant. Compressive strength requires nonlinear surrogates; carbon emission is well recovered by regularized linear models. Unconstrained optimization may yield invalid candidates; physics-only constraints don't ensure data support. Topology-aware strategy best balances target compliance, carbon reduction, physical admissibility, and manifold proximity.
AI.conclusion: The topology-aware INCRT framework effectively screens credible candidate mixtures from small, mixed, physically constrained datasets, serving as a support tool for experimental validation rather than replacing it.

title: See like a Robot: Robot-Centric Pointmaps for Vision-Language-Action Models
summary: Vision-language-action (VLA) models predict robot actions from visual observations and language instructions. These actions are defined in the robot's own 3D coordinate frame, yet most VLAs observe the scene in the camera frame, creating a frame mismatch between where the scene is observed and where actions are defined. The mismatch is benign under a fixed viewpoint, where the policy can memorize a single observation-to-action mapping, but grows harder as large-scale datasets aggregate demonstrations across diverse camera setups and the policy must generalize this mapping across viewpoints. We address this mismatch with robot-centric pointmaps, images whose pixels store the 3D coordinates of scene points in the robot frame. Pointmaps provide robot-frame 3D geometry while preserving the dense H x W grid expected by pretrained 2D VLAs, so they integrate into existing VLAs with minimal architectural change. On RoboCasa, pointmaps improve both pi0.5 and SmolVLA and outperform representative camera-viewpoint and 3D-aware baselines. In real-robot experiments, their advantage over an RGB-only policy widens when the camera is moved to a placement unseen during training.

AI.tldr: The paper addresses the frame mismatch between camera-based observations and robot-frame actions in VLA models by introducing robot-centric pointmaps—images storing 3D coordinates in the robot frame—which improve generalization across camera viewpoints with minimal architecture changes.
AI.motivation: VLAs have a frame mismatch: actions are defined in robot 3D coordinates, but observations are in camera frame. This is problematic when generalizing across diverse camera setups in large datasets.
AI.method: Proposes robot-centric pointmaps: images whose pixels store 3D coordinates of scene points in the robot frame, providing robot-frame 3D geometry while preserving the H×W grid format expected by pretrained 2D VLAs.
AI.result: On RoboCasa, pointmaps improve both pi0.5 and SmolVLA, outperforming camera-viewpoint and 3D-aware baselines. In real-robot experiments, they show greater advantage over RGB-only policy when camera is moved to unseen placements.
AI.conclusion: Pointmaps effectively resolve the frame mismatch in VLAs, enabling better generalization across viewpoints with minimal architectural changes.

title: LLM-PDESR: Robust PDE Discovery via Subdomain Weighted Residuals and LLM-Guided Symbolic Hypothesis Generation
summary: Discovering governing partial differential equations (PDEs) from noisy observational data is a fundamental challenge in scientific machine learning. Traditional symbolic regression (SR) methods often struggle to identify accurate equations within vast combinatorial search spaces, largely due to their inability to incorporate essential domain-specific prior knowledge. Furthermore, reliance on pointwise evaluations and discrete finite differences inherently amplifies high-frequency noise, creating deceptive fitness landscapes that derail the optimization process. To resolve these bottlenecks, we propose LLM-PDESR, a framework that integrates the structural hypothesis generation of Large Language Models (LLMs) with a mathematically rigorous evaluation environment. By employing C^4-continuous quintic splines for robust differentiation and subdomain weighted residuals as natural low-pass filters, our approach effectively mitigates the fitness landscape distortion that plagues existing methods. A Pareto-driven feedback loop then enables the LLM to iteratively refine candidate equations, balancing predictive accuracy with structural parsimony. We evaluate LLM-PDESR on 23 canonical PDEs and five structurally novel equations (including a multivariate system) specifically designed to preclude dataset memorization and test true discovery capabilities. Demonstrating real-world applicability, the framework successfully extracts a consistent structural skeleton for an interpretable 1D dynamical surrogate (1D-CACE) directly from noisy ERA5 reanalysis data. Extensive experiments and out-of-distribution testing confirm that LLM-PDESR significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methodologies in structural recovery, noise resilience, and the avoidance of spurious complexity and equation bloat.

AI.tldr: LLM-PDESR is a framework that combines Large Language Models with robust numerical methods (quintic splines, subdomain weighted residuals) to discover governing PDEs from noisy data, significantly outperforming existing methods on 23 canonical and 5 novel PDEs plus real-world ERA5 data.
AI.motivation: Symbolic regression methods struggle with PDE discovery due to vast combinatorial search spaces, inability to incorporate domain-specific priors, and noise amplification from pointwise evaluations and finite differences, which create deceptive fitness landscapes.
AI.method: Integrates LLMs for structural hypothesis generation with C^4-continuous quintic splines for robust differentiation and subdomain weighted residuals as low-pass filters, with a Pareto-driven feedback loop for iterative refinement balancing accuracy and parsimony.
AI.result: Outperforms state-of-the-art methods on 23 canonical PDEs and 5 structurally novel equations, successfully extracts a consistent 1D-CACE surrogate from noisy ERA5 reanalysis data, with superior structural recovery, noise resilience, and avoidance of spurious complexity.
AI.conclusion: LLM-PDESR effectively addresses key bottlenecks in data-driven PDE discovery by combining LLM-driven hypothesis generation with mathematically rigorous evaluation, demonstrating strong generalization and practical applicability.

title: WasteAssistant: Regulation-Guided Visual Question Answering Framework for Intelligent Waste Segregation and Sustainable Managemen
summary: Efficient waste segregation is critical for sustainable urban management and environmental governance. Existing automated systems are limited by single-modality visual processing, insufficient contextual understanding, and weak regulatory alignment. To address these issues, we propose a language-guided vision-AI framework that integrates vision-language models and multimodal large language models for joint visual-linguistic reasoning. This framework implements a visual question answering paradigm aligned with India's Solid Waste Management Rules 2016. We construct a new WasteVQA dataset with 13,500 question-answer pairs across 21 waste categories. Experiments show that the BLIP-based model achieves a BLEU score of 0.8291 and a BERTScore of 0.9273, outperforming traditional CNN-based methods. This work improves source-level segregation accuracy, ensures regulatory compliance, and supports scalable deployment for municipal and citizen-facing waste management, promoting multimodal AI in sustainable urban infrastructure. The source code and dataset are available at: https://github.com/Khushkataruka/WasteAssistant

AI.tldr: A language-guided vision-AI framework using vision-language models for waste segregation, aligned with India's Solid Waste Management Rules 2016. Introduces WasteVQA dataset (13,500 QA pairs, 21 categories). BLIP-based model achieves BLEU 0.8291 and BERTScore 0.9273.
AI.motivation: Existing automated waste segregation systems are limited by single-modality visual processing, insufficient contextual understanding, and weak regulatory alignment, motivating the need for a multimodal AI framework.
AI.method: Proposes a language-guided vision-AI framework integrating vision-language models and multimodal large language models. Uses a visual question answering paradigm aligned with India's SWM Rules 2016. Constructs the WasteVQA dataset with 13,500 QA pairs across 21 categories.
AI.result: BLIP-based model achieves BLEU score of 0.8291 and BERTScore of 0.9273, outperforming traditional CNN-based methods.
AI.conclusion: The framework improves source-level segregation accuracy, ensures regulatory compliance, supports scalable deployment for municipal and citizen-facing waste management, and promotes multimodal AI in sustainable urban infrastructure.

title: Learning Linear Temporal Specifications from Demonstrations with Uncertainty
comment: This paper has been accepted by the ACC2026 (American Control Conference)
summary: Learning temporal logic specifications from system demonstrations is essential for tasks such as formal verification and controller synthesis, especially in safety-critical domains. Existing approaches typically assume demonstrations are correct or only affected by misclassification errors. In practice, however, system traces are often uncertain or incomplete due to sensor faults, measurement errors, or data loss. We present a framework for learning minimal Linear Temporal Logic (LTL) formulas from demonstrations with uncertainty. Our approach models uncertainty via Hamming distance to generate possible estimates around each observed trace, which are grouped with constraints requiring that at least one trace per group is consistent with the learned formula. Our problem is then reduced to an equivalent Pseudo-Boolean Optimization. We evaluate our method against state-of-the-art LTL learning approaches and show that it recovers specifications that more closely align with ground-truth formulas under uncertainty.

AI.tldr: A framework for learning minimal LTL formulas from uncertain/incomplete system demonstrations using Hamming distance modeling and Pseudo-Boolean Optimization.
AI.motivation: Existing LTL learning methods assume demonstrations are correct or only have misclassification errors, but real-world system traces often suffer from sensor faults, measurement errors, or data loss, leading to uncertain/incomplete data.
AI.method: Models uncertainty via Hamming distance to generate possible trace estimates, groups them with constraints requiring at least one trace per group consistent with the learned formula, then reduces the problem to Pseudo-Boolean Optimization.
AI.result: The proposed method recovers specifications more closely aligned with ground-truth formulas under uncertainty compared to state-of-the-art LTL learning approaches.
AI.conclusion: This framework effectively handles uncertain demonstrations in LTL learning, producing more accurate specifications for safety-critical applications.

title: ERR@HRI 3.0 Challenge: Multimodal Detection of Errors and Anticipation in Human-Robot Interactions
summary: As robots become increasingly integrated into human environments, their ability to detect and respond to errors remains critical for maintaining user trust and interaction quality. While recent advances in machine learning have improved error detection capabilities, most approaches are limited to specific contexts, controlled settings, or pre-extracted features, limiting their generalizability and applicability to real-world conditions. To address this challenge, the third edition of the ERR@HRI Challenge (ERR@HRI 3.0) provided researchers with two complementary datasets that enable end-to-end innovation in methods for both detecting and preventing errors in human-robot interaction. The challenge offered raw, non-anonymized video data from naturalistic settings: (1) the Bystander Affect Detection (BAD) dataset, containing webcam recordings of 45 participants' spontaneous reactions to robot and human failure scenarios; and (2) the Bad Idea dataset, featuring 29 participants' anticipatory facial responses while predicting action outcomes before failures occur. Both datasets were collected via crowdsourcing, capturing the inherent variability of real-world conditions. This naturalistic variability, while challenging, provides an authentic testbed for developing robust error detection systems. Participants developed multimodal machine learning models for bystander reaction detection (Track 1) and anticipatory outcome prediction (Track 2), with an optional cross-dataset generalization track (Track 3). Three teams submitted valid models, all of which surpassed our convolutional neural network baselines. This paper describes the datasets, tasks, baselines, and results of ERR@HRI 3.0, and discusses implications for building generalizable, context-aware, and anticipatory error detection systems for human-robot interaction.

AI.tldr: This paper describes ERR@HRI 3.0 challenge, offering two crowdsourced datasets (BAD and Bad Idea) for detecting and preventing errors in human-robot interaction through multimodal machine learning models, with three teams outperforming CNN baselines.
AI.motivation: Existing error detection approaches are limited to specific contexts, controlled settings, or pre-extracted features, lacking generalizability and real-world applicability for HRI.
AI.method: The challenge provided two naturalistic crowdsourced datasets (BAD and Bad Idea), with three tracks: bystander reaction detection, anticipatory outcome prediction, and cross-dataset generalization. Participants developed multimodal ML models.
AI.result: Three teams submitted valid models, all surpassing the CNN baselines on the provided datasets.
AI.conclusion: The ERR@HRI 3.0 challenge demonstrates progress toward building generalizable, context-aware, and anticipatory error detection systems for human-robot interaction.

title: Learning from Local Walks on Dynamic Graphs with Bandit Feedback
summary: We study stochastic multi-armed bandits on dynamic graphs, where arms correspond to the vertices of a network with time-varying edges. In this setting, the learner is restricted to local movement, selecting only its current node or an immediate neighbor at each round. This constraint decouples best-arm identification from exploitation: even after the optimal arm is identified, the learner may remain unable to reach it through the evolving topology. We identify a process-agnostic structural condition, based on sliding-window mixing, that ensures the graph's intrinsic walk remains stable for both exploration and navigation. Under this regime, we analyze a family of local explore-then-commit algorithms and establish sublinear expected regret. Our framework includes a reward-aware strategy, for which we prove a worst-case safety theorem and a separate performance gain theorem.

AI.tldr: The paper studies stochastic multi-armed bandits on dynamic graphs where the learner is restricted to local movement. It identifies a sliding-window mixing condition that ensures stable graph walks, enabling sublinear regret for local explore-then-commit algorithms. A reward-aware strategy with safety and performance guarantees is proposed.
AI.motivation: To address the challenge of best-arm identification in dynamic graph bandits where local movement constraints decouple identification from exploitation—even after finding the optimal arm, the learner may not be able to reach it due to topology changes.
AI.method: Identifies a process-agnostic structural condition (sliding-window mixing) for stable graph walks. Analyzes a family of local explore-then-commit algorithms. Proves sublinear expected regret bounds under this regime, including a reward-aware strategy with safety and performance theorems.
AI.result: Sublinear expected regret is established for the proposed local explore-then-commit algorithms. Reward-aware strategy has a worst-case safety theorem and a performance gain theorem.
AI.conclusion: The sliding-window mixing condition enables both exploration and navigation in dynamic graph bandits, allowing effective algorithms despite the decoupling of identification and exploitation.

title: Spectral Consistent Flow for One-step 3D Medical Image Translation
summary: We present Spectral Consistent Flow (SC-Flow), a 3D medical image translation framework with a single function evaluation (1-NFE) in the latent space. This approach reformulates medical image translation as a stochastic Brownian bridge process that directly constructs a mapping between source and target modalities by predicting the support regularized mean velocity field. To mitigate modality entanglement, over-smoothing, and artifacts induced by the implicit low-pass modulation of the latent average velocity, we introduce a Spectral Consistency Corrector that dynamically regularizes the evolution of the power spectral density via learnable frequency-domain gain modulation. This mechanism establishes an explicit bridge between spatial textures and spectral energy flow, enabling the model to recover fine-grained anatomical fidelity while maintaining global structural coherence. Extensive experiments on four datasets demonstrate that SC-Flow delivers significantly more accurate, consistent, and robust performance across various translation scenarios.

AI.tldr: SC-Flow is a 3D medical image translation framework using a single function evaluation in latent space, reformulating translation as a stochastic Brownian bridge process with a Spectral Consistency Corrector to enhance fine-grained anatomical fidelity.
AI.motivation: To address modality entanglement, over-smoothing, and artifacts caused by implicit low-pass modulation in latent average velocity during medical image translation.
AI.method: Reformulates medical image translation as a stochastic Brownian bridge process predicting a regularized mean velocity field, enhanced by a Spectral Consistency Corrector that dynamically regularizes power spectral density evolution through learnable frequency-domain gain modulation.
AI.result: SC-Flow delivers significantly more accurate, consistent, and robust performance across four diverse datasets in various translation scenarios.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach effectively bridges spatial textures and spectral energy flow, recovering fine-grained anatomical details while preserving global structural coherence.

title: SVR-R1: Bootstrapping Multi-modal Reasoning with Self-verification in Reinforcement Learning
summary: We introduce Self-Verified Reasoner (SVR-R1), a multi-turn RL framework that turns a model's own verification into a learning signal for multimodal reasoning. For each query, the model proposes an answer using the same weights, and issues a binary self-verdict (Yes/No). A 'No' triggers a second-chance rethink; a 'Yes,' or a turn cap, finalizes the output for computing the outcome-based reward. SVR-R1 is implemented with GRPO and an asynchronous multi-turn rollout framework and needs no external supervision or auxiliary critics. We evaluate SVR-R1 on vision-language reasoning benchmarks and show that it improves accuracy by a large margin over strong standard GRPO baselines. Training dynamics show decreasing reliance on verification-fewer verification turns, yet higher test accuracy-indicating that the gap between verification and generation narrows as the policy internalizes self-correction and chooses the most confident answer via our framework. SVR-R1 bridges the less explored intersection of inference-time self-refinement and RL training for VLMs, offering a simple yet effective recipe for bootstrapping multimodal reasoning. We will open-source \textbf{SVR-R1} to facilitate future research in VLMs.

AI.tldr: SVR-R1 is a multi-turn RL framework that uses a model's own binary self-verification (Yes/No) as a learning signal to improve multimodal reasoning, showing significant accuracy gains over standard GRPO baselines on vision-language benchmarks.
AI.motivation: To bridge the gap between inference-time self-refinement and RL training for Vision-Language Models (VLMs) without requiring external supervision or auxiliary critics.
AI.method: SVR-R1 uses a multi-turn RL framework (GRPO + asynchronous multi-turn rollout) where the model proposes an answer, issues a binary self-verdict (Yes/No), triggers a rethink on 'No', and finalizes output on 'Yes' or when a turn cap is reached. Reward is outcome-based without external critics.
AI.result: Improves accuracy by a large margin over strong standard GRPO baselines on vision-language reasoning benchmarks. Training dynamics show fewer verification turns yet higher test accuracy, indicating internalized self-correction.
AI.conclusion: SVR-R1 offers a simple yet effective recipe for bootstrapping multimodal reasoning by turning a model's own verification into a learning signal, with plans to open-source for future VLM research.

title: WarpMPC: Large-Batch MPC on GPU via ADMM with Unrolled $LDL^\top$ Factorization
summary: This paper introduces numerical optimizations for maximizing throughput on GPU when solving large batches (10,000 to over 100,000) of sequential quadratic programming (SQP) iterations, where all problems have the same structure. The optimizations are implemented in a toolbox WarpMPC for model-predictive control (MPC) in JAX and Warp. Based on the insight that all MPC problem instances in a batch share the same sparsity in time, cost, and constraints, we propose unrolling sparse linear factorizations and solves, which dominate alternating direction method of multipliers (ADMM) solver runtime. We avoid memory access bottlenecks and wasting computations via optimized memory layout, padding-reducing segmentation of the unrolled factorization, and dependency level scheduled backsolves, additionally accelerating sensitivity computation. We achieve throughputs of 8,000 to 250,000 SQP iterations per second on nonlinear cartpole, quadrotor, and humanoid robot benchmarks, outperforming baselines by 3$\times$ to 25$\times$. We illustrate practical usefulness by synthesizing a dataset and training a neural network approximation of an MPC in under 4 minutes that stabilizes a nano quadrotor in hardware experiments.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces numerical optimizations for GPU-accelerated batch SQP solvers in MPC, achieving 3-25× speedup over baselines by unrolling sparse linear factorizations with optimized memory layouts and dependency scheduling.
AI.motivation: To maximize throughput when solving large batches (10K-100K+) of SQP iterations on GPU for MPC, addressing memory bottlenecks and wasted computations in existing solvers.
AI.method: Proposes unrolling sparse linear factorizations/solves (dominant in ADMM runtime), optimized memory layout, padding-reducing segmentation, and dependency-level scheduled backsolves, implemented in the WarpMPC toolbox using JAX and Warp.
AI.result: Achieves 8K-250K SQP iterations/second on nonlinear cartpole, quadrotor, and humanoid benchmarks; outperforms baselines by 3× to 25×; enables synthesizing a dataset and training a neural network MPC approximation in under 4 minutes for nano quadrotor hardware stabilization.
AI.conclusion: Exploiting shared sparsity patterns across batch MPC problems enables significant GPU throughput gains, making MPC-based data generation and neural network training practical within minutes for real-time hardware deployment.

title: MemDecay: Region-Aware KV Cache Eviction for Efficient LLM Agent Inference
summary:
Large language model (LLM) agents accumulate heterogeneous context, including system instructions, plans, user turns, retrieved documents, tool outputs, and intermediate reasoning, whose key-value (KV) cache can become a major memory bottleneck. Existing eviction policies generally apply the same attention- or recency-based rule to every token, ignoring semantic structure already available to the agent orchestrator.
  We introduce MemDecay, a training-free, region-aware KV-cache eviction policy. MemDecay assigns tokens region-specific base priorities and decay rates, refreshes retention scores when tokens receive attention, and evicts the lowest-scoring pages under a fixed cache budget while allowing critical regions to be pinned. We also provide a procedure for calibrating decay rates from measured attention lifetimes.
  We evaluate MemDecay at approximately 450 and 1,700 token contexts using Qwen2.5-1.5B and 3B. Across all settings, attention lifetimes differ by an order of magnitude across regions: system-token half-lives range from 148 to 189 decoding steps, compared with 14 to 16 for scratchpad tokens. Pinning preserves system-region facts at full-cache accuracy in every setting, while no baseline preserves more than 13 of 24. Region-aware retention remains effective as context grows, whereas recency-based retention collapses. Accumulated-attention retention performs better on unpinned content, however, and ablations identify attention-score normalization as the main limitation of the current formulation. These results establish semantic prompt structure as a robust signal for KV-cache management while clarifying how it should be combined with attention-based importance.

AI.tldr: MemDecay is a training-free KV-cache eviction policy for LLM agents that uses region-specific priority and decay rates based on semantic structure, outperforming recency-based baselines and preserving system-level context at full accuracy.
AI.motivation: LLM agents accumulate diverse context types (system instructions, user turns, tool outputs, etc.), causing KV-cache memory bottlenecks. Existing eviction policies ignore the semantic structure already available to the agent orchestrator.
AI.method: MemDecay assigns region-specific base priorities and decay rates to tokens, refreshes retention scores when tokens receive attention, evicts lowest-scoring pages under a fixed budget, and allows pinning critical regions. Decay rates are calibrated from measured attention lifetimes.
AI.result: Attention lifetimes differ by an order of magnitude across regions (e.g., system tokens: 148-189 half-life vs. scratchpad: 14-16). Pinning preserves system facts at full-cache accuracy; no baseline preserves >13/24. Region-aware retention remains effective as context grows, while recency-based retention collapses.
AI.conclusion: Semantic prompt structure is a robust signal for KV-cache management, but attention-score normalization remains a limitation, and accumulated-attention retention performs better on unpinned content, suggesting a need to combine region awareness with attention-based importance.

title: Spectral Heat Flow for Conservative Token Condensation in Vision-Language Models
summary: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) are costly at inference time because they must process long sequences of visual tokens. Existing token pruning methods often degrade under high compression by blindly discarding information, breaking spatial structure or collapsing diversity.
