Experiments on two public datasets, LIDC and MCT-LTDiag, and one private dataset demonstrate its effectiveness and clinical consistency. Code will be made public upon acceptance.

AI.tldr: PD-CTAgent is a policy-driven AI framework that dynamically selects CT imaging phases (non-contrast, arterial, venous) based on clinical guidelines, using a Clinical Structure Abstraction Module and Knowledge-Guided Diagnostic Control Model, achieving effective phase selection and diagnostic reasoning across multiple datasets.
AI.motivation: Existing CT-based AI methods assume all imaging phases are available and focus on static tasks, failing to model whether additional phases are needed—a critical limitation given that phase-selection protocols vary across hospitals, regions, and guidelines, and are essential for reducing unnecessary radiation exposure.
AI.method: PD-CTAgent consists of two key components: (1) Clinical Structure Abstraction Module (CSAM) that harmonizes heterogeneous CT phases into a unified, phase-aware evidence representation; (2) Knowledge-Guided Diagnostic Control Model (KDCM) that evaluates phase sufficiency and iteratively requests additional phases when needed. The policy-driven agent design allows flexible adherence to different institutional or guideline-specific protocols.
AI.result: Experiments on two public datasets (LIDC, MCT-LTDiag) and one private dataset demonstrate its effectiveness and clinical consistency in CT phase selection and diagnostic reasoning.
AI.conclusion: PD-CTAgent bridges the gap between static CT analysis and real-world clinical workflows by dynamically selecting phases, reducing unnecessary radiation exposure, and adapting to varying diagnostic protocols.

title: Mixture of Cognitive Experts in Large Vision-Language Models
summary: Large Vision Language Models (LVLMs) require strong reasoning over both visual and textual input. Recent work suggests that cognitive elements, especially diverse representations and metacognition, correlate with better performance. Many of the needed perceptual functions are already provided by specialized domain-specific computer vision models, which act as the perceptual subsystem for detecting objects, localizing them, inferring states, recovering spatial layout, and reading text. The key challenge is to integrate these multi-encoder experts into a trustworthy, interpretable, and coherent representation that improves verifiability and reduces hallucinations. This is difficult because vision-language questions span different cognitive levels, yet most LVLM pipelines apply the same perception-reasoning routing regardless of the demand of each query. We propose an evidence-driven multimodal reasoning framework that utilizes a Bloom-inspired taxonomy as a hierarchical reasoning protocol. The two-stage cognitive verbalization first produces a Literal Evidence Summary by decomposing expert outputs into short, atomic evidence statements. It then performs Bloom Verbalization to turn these evidence items into a staged reasoning trace, and a lightweight Reasoning Trace Module quantitatively analyzes the trace to make evidence usage and reasoning progression explicit. Through this integration, we observed several improvements in perception and reasoning abilities. Moreover, the trace module provides quantitative evidence that different queries induce different cognitive entry levels and evidence-use trajectories that enable fine-grained analysis.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes an evidence-driven multimodal reasoning framework for LVLMs that uses a Bloom-inspired taxonomy to hierarchically reason over diverse expert outputs. It employs two-stage cognitive verbalization (Literal Evidence Summary + Bloom Verbalization) and a Reasoning Trace Module to make evidence usage explicit, improving perception, reasoning, and verifiability while reducing hallucinations.
AI.motivation: LVLMs need strong visual-textual reasoning but often lack interpretability and suffer from hallucinations. Existing pipelines apply uniform perception-reasoning routing regardless of query complexity, failing to leverage diverse domain-specific expert models or adapt to different cognitive levels of questions.
AI.method: A Bloom-inspired taxonomy guides hierarchical reasoning. Two-stage cognitive verbalization: (1) Literal Evidence Summary extracts atomic evidence from multiple expert outputs; (2) Bloom Verbalization converts evidence into staged reasoning traces. A lightweight Reasoning Trace Module quantifies evidence usage and reasoning progression.
AI.result: Improvements in perception and reasoning abilities. The trace module shows different queries induce different cognitive entry levels and evidence-use trajectories, enabling fine-grained analysis of reasoning behavior.
AI.conclusion: Integrating multi-encoder experts with a structured, traceable reasoning protocol improves LVLM interpretability, verifiability, and performance while reducing hallucinations.

title: Heterogeneous Agent Cohorts for Safe Open-Ended Exploration with Runtime Constraint Memory
summary: LLM agents today are caught in an awkward bind. Lock them down with static safety instructions and they rarely venture beyond the obvious; give them free reign with tools and multi-agent debate, and safety violations quickly follow. Rather than forcing a single model to juggle both creativity and caution, we separate the concerns across specialized roles. A Disrupter generates unconventional proposals, a Validator enforces hard runtime checks at the tool gateway, and a Broker pulls in distant but relevant analogies. Failures are not discarded -- they are compiled, via MCTS, into compact, signed constraint patches we call Scars. These patches are cached locally and inherited by future cohorts, turning repeated failures into reusable, low-cost runtime constraints. In a spatial-semantic sandbox (N=20 runs, p<0.01), our cohort reaches remote targets where debate fails, the Validator prevents all executed breaches, and Scars reduce token consumption by 15.1% by avoiding redundant validator checks. Furthermore, credit-based Communication Allocation Scores (CAS) restrict outbound bandwidth, reducing overall token costs by 55.9% under resource constraints.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a multi-agent architecture separating creativity (Disrupter), safety (Validator), and analogy (Broker) roles, with MCTS-generated constraint patches (Scars) and credit-based communication allocation to balance creativity and safety in LLM agents.
AI.motivation: Current LLM agents face a dilemma: static safety instructions limit creativity, while free reign with tools risks safety violations. The paper aims to decouple creativity from caution across specialized roles.
AI.method: Three specialized roles: Disrupter (unconventional proposals), Validator (runtime safety checks at tool gateway), Broker (distant analogies). Failures are compiled via MCTS into signed constraint patches (Scars) cached locally. Credit-based Communication Allocation Scores (CAS) regulate outbound bandwidth.
AI.result: In a spatial-semantic sandbox (N=20, p<0.01): cohort reaches remote targets where debate fails; Validator prevents all breaches; Scars reduce tokens by 15.1%; CAS reduces overall token costs by 55.9%.
AI.conclusion: Separating concerns across specialized roles with memory of past failures (Scars) and communication budgeting (CAS) effectively resolves the creativity-safety tension in LLM agents.

title: A Compact Top-Loading Robot for Endovascular Interventions: Design, Control and Evaluation
summary: Robot-assisted endovascular intervention can potentially reduce radiation exposure, improve surgeon ergonomics, enable telesurgery, support active assistance and autonomy, and enhance procedural precision. However, existing systems often suffer from limited procedural coverage because constrained patient-side setups, restricted flexibility, and complex instrument exchange hinder clinical workflow integration. This work presents a compact robotic system for endovascular interventions that enables continuous translational and rotational manipulation of standard endovascular instruments. The system consists of two alternating carts with pneumatically actuated membrane grippers integrated into rotating gripper gears. Its top-loading design allows rapid exchange of instruments such as guidewires and catheters without changing the robotic setup. A leader-follower control strategy enables continuous motion despite the finite stroke of each cart. The system was evaluated in motion-tracking experiments with guidewires and catheters and in an in vitro vascular phantom. The motion-tracking experiments showed generally smooth translational and rotational motion profiles. Across all tested guidewire and catheter experiments, the mean relative tracking errors were 3.6% for translational motion and 4.1% for rotational motion. In the vascular phantom, robot-assisted navigation reached the target in most trials, demonstrating the feasibility of the proposed manipulation concept under in vitro conditions. The presented robotic system demonstrates technical feasibility for continuous manipulation of standard endovascular instruments in bench-top and in vitro experiments. The compact top-loading design may ease instrument exchange and clinical workflow integration. Future work will focus on improving gripping performance, actuation speed, force feedback, and evaluation in more clinically realistic settings.

AI.tldr: A compact robotic system for endovascular interventions with pneumatically actuated membrane grippers, top-loading design, and leader-follower control enables continuous manipulation of standard instruments, achieving 3.6% translational and 4.1% rotational tracking errors in experiments.
AI.motivation: Existing robotic systems for endovascular intervention suffer from limited procedural coverage due to constrained patient-side setups, restricted flexibility, and complex instrument exchange, hindering clinical workflow integration.
AI.method: Two alternating carts with pneumatically actuated membrane grippers integrated into rotating gripper gears, top-loading design for rapid instrument exchange, leader-follower control strategy for continuous motion despite finite stroke.
AI.result: Mean relative tracking errors: 3.6% (translational) and 4.1% (rotational). Successful navigation in vascular phantom trials demonstrating feasibility.
AI.conclusion: The system demonstrates technical feasibility for continuous manipulation of standard endovascular instruments. Compact top-loading design may ease instrument exchange and clinical workflow integration. Future work will improve gripping, actuation speed, force feedback, and clinical evaluation.

title: The VC dimension of partial concept classes via Radon's theorem
summary:
Following Alon, Hanneke, Holzman, and Moran (FOCS 2021), we define a partial concept class (PCC) as a family of partial functions \(f: V\to\{0,1,\ast\}\); equivalently, its concepts partition the ground set into black ($f^{-1}(1)$), grey ($f^{-1}(\ast)$), and white parts ($f^{-1}(0)$). Its VC dimension is defined by shattering sets on which the value $\ast$ is not taken. We study two geometric PCCs in real Banach spaces, both with a margin \(δ>0\): expanded half-spaces, where the grey part is a strip of width at least \(δ\) adjacent to a half-space, and expanded balls, where the grey part is an annulus of width \(δ\) around a unit radius ball.
  Our main results are dimension-free upper bounds on the VC dimension of the PCC of expanded balls in \(L_p\parenthμ\), \(1\le p<\infty\), including the non-Euclidean and algorithmically particularly relevant case \(\ell^d_1\). These bounds depend on the margin and on the radii, but not on the ambient dimension or the underlying measure space. These are extensions of the work of Bourneuf, Charbit, and Thomassé (FOCS 2025) who studied the PCC of expanded balls in Euclidean space, that is, $\ell_2^d$. We also prove lower bounds on the VC dimension that match the upper bounds in terms of the margin parameter $δ$. Finally, we derive a Dense Neighborhood Lemma in \(L_p\)-spaces, again extending the known Euclidean results.
  Our method relies on the linearization of the distance through a map into a space of non-trivial Rademacher type, and then the use of a balanced signed-sum estimate, or a no-dimensional Radon theorem. The arguments rely on ideas from functional analysis that are clearly explained for the non-expert in that field.

AI.tldr: This paper studies partial concept classes (PCCs) of expanded half-spaces and expanded balls in Lp spaces, proving dimension-free VC dimension bounds that depend on margin but not on dimension, extending Euclidean results to general Banach spaces.
AI.motivation: To extend the geometric partial concept class theory beyond Euclidean spaces to general Lp Banach spaces, particularly the algorithmically relevant ℓ1 case, and obtain dimension-free VC bounds.
AI.method: Linearizes distance via a map into a space of non-trivial Rademacher type, then applies balanced signed-sum estimates or a no-dimensional Radon theorem. Uses functional analysis tools explained for non-experts.
AI.result: Dimension-free upper bounds on VC dimension of expanded balls in Lp(μ) for 1≤p<∞, with matching lower bounds in margin parameter δ. Also a Dense Neighborhood Lemma in Lp spaces.
AI.conclusion: The VC dimension bounds depend on margin and radii but not ambient dimension, extending prior Euclidean results; the approach demonstrates functional analysis techniques are applicable to geometric PCC problems.

title: Compositional Context Fine-Tuning Vision-Language Model for Complex Assembly Action Understanding from Videos
comment: Accepted by ICRA 2026. Video Understanding; Vision Language Model; Multi-modal LLM; Action Recognition; Assembly
summary: Assembly action understanding is a key enabler for effective human-robot collaborative assembly, yet it remains challenging due to subtle motions and fine-grained hand-object interactions. We adapt vision-language models (VLMs) to this challenging domain with Compositional Context Fine-Tuning (CCFT), a method that decomposes assembly actions into semantic elements (Verb, Object, Tool) and fine-tunes VLMs to recognize each action element using templated question-answering pairs. This approach ensures near-deterministic outputs. To enable efficient and effective multi-task learning under limited data, a Layer-Partitioned Alternating Training (LP-AT) method is presented, which assigns distinct model layers to recognize specific action elements through element-specific low-rank adapters. LP-AT alternates weight updates across element-specific adapters, reducing cross-task interference while enabling per-adapter hyperparameter optimization. Furthermore, we create HA-ViD-VQA and IKEA-ASM-VQA datasets from existing assembly video datasets. Extensive experiments on these datasets demonstrate that our method consistently outperforms strong action recognition baselines while providing interpretable element-level predictions that can support diverse downstream applications.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces Compositional Context Fine-Tuning (CCFT) and Layer-Partitioned Alternating Training (LP-AT) to adapt vision-language models for fine-grained assembly action understanding by decomposing actions into Verb, Object, and Tool elements, achieving superior performance on newly created VQA datasets.
AI.motivation: Assembly action understanding is critical for human-robot collaboration but is challenging due to subtle motions and fine-grained hand-object interactions. Existing methods struggle with these nuances.
AI.method: Proposes CCFT which decomposes assembly actions into semantic elements (Verb, Object, Tool) and fine-tunes VLMs via templated QA pairs. LP-AT assigns distinct model layers to recognize specific elements through element-specific low-rank adapters, alternating weight updates to reduce cross-task interference.
AI.result: Outperforms strong action recognition baselines on HA-ViD-VQA and IKEA-ASM-VQA datasets while providing interpretable element-level predictions.
AI.conclusion: The proposed method effectively addresses the challenges of fine-grained assembly action understanding, enabling interpretable and accurate predictions for downstream human-robot collaborative applications.

title: Bringing Back Rule Induction to Fluid Intelligence Research? An Initial Validation of the ARC-AGI Benchmark in Humans
summary: Two competing perspectives on fluid intelligence (gf) measures propose that performance is primarily constrained either by working memory capacity or by the ability to induce novel relations. The first perspective is currently dominant in measurement, as evident from the use of a limited set of recurring rules, whereas the second perspective is reflected in many definitions but rarely present in measurement. The ARC-AGI benchmark predominantly requires rule induction and was proposed as a measure of gf for both humans and artificial systems. However, its psychometric properties have not yet been examined in human samples. We therefore investigated the psychometric characteristics and nomological network of ARC-AGI in a first study with 100 participants. A compilation of ARC-AGI items showed good psychometric properties and correlated substantially with figural fluid intelligence as measured by a figural reasoning test (\r{ho} = .63). Associations with figural originality were weak. These findings provide initial support for the validity of ARC-AGI as a measure of human fluid intelligence. Future research should include more rule induction tasks as well as additional multivariate covariates. This study is unusual by studying a task in humans that was initially designed for machines. We suggest systematically embedding AI benchmarks into the nomological network of human cognitive abilities to enable more systematic evaluation and interdisciplinary cooperation.

AI.tldr: This study examines ARC-AGI, an AI benchmark requiring rule induction, as a measure of human fluid intelligence. With 100 participants, it shows good psychometric properties and strong correlation with figural fluid intelligence (r=.63), supporting its validity for measuring human gf, though weak association with figural originality was found.
AI.motivation: Two competing perspectives on fluid intelligence exist—one emphasizing working memory capacity and the other emphasizing rule induction ability. The rule induction perspective is often defined but rarely measured. ARC-AGI benchmark requires rule induction but its psychometric properties for humans were unknown.
AI.method: A study with 100 human participants examined the psychometric characteristics and nomological network of ARC-AGI items, comparing them with figural fluid intelligence measures and figural originality.
AI.result: ARC-AGI showed good psychometric properties, correlated substantially with figural fluid intelligence (r=.63), and had weak associations with figural originality.
AI.conclusion: Initial support for ARC-AGI's validity as a measure of human fluid intelligence is provided. Future research should include more rule induction tasks and multivariate covariates. The study suggests systematically embedding AI benchmarks into human cognitive ability nomological networks for better interdisciplinary cooperation.

title: MIRA: A Modular Open-Source Micro-UAV for Indoor Research
summary: Indoor robotics research increasingly relies on micro-UAVs whose airframe, electronics, and control software are fully open to modification. Off-the-shelf platforms rarely expose the low-level access required for such modifications, while building a custom alternative typically requires substantial engineering effort before flight testing can begin, leaving many laboratories to work within constraints that limit the scope of their research. We present MIRA (Modular Indoor Research Architecture), a low-cost, open-source micro-UAV for indoor research built around a replicable 3D-printed PLA airframe and a containerized low-level software package managing the companion-to-autopilot communication bridge via Micro XRCE-DDS. Designed as a white-box architecture, core subsystems are individually replaceable without firmware refactoring, supporting local fabrication and component substitution from existing lab inventory. We characterize MIRA through manual flight in position-control mode within an optical motion-capture volume, where the communication pipeline sustains a median companion-to-autopilot latency of 0.02 ms and power spectral density analysis confirms the structural vibration energy stays concentrated in a narrow 90 to 110 Hz band, isolated from the sub-20 Hz control bandwidth and within the autopilot safety thresholds.

AI.tldr: MIRA is a low-cost, open-source micro-UAV for indoor research with a 3D-printed PLA airframe and containerized software, achieving 0.02 ms median communication latency and structural vibrations isolated from control bandwidth.
AI.motivation: Off-the-shelf micro-UAVs lack low-level access for modification, while custom builds require substantial engineering effort before flight testing, limiting research scope.
AI.method: The authors designed MIRA around a 3D-printed PLA airframe and a containerized low-level software package using Micro XRCE-DDS for companion-to-autopilot communication, with a white-box architecture allowing individual subsystem replacement.
AI.result: MIRA achieved 0.02 ms median companion-to-autopilot latency and structural vibration energy concentrated in 90-110 Hz band, isolated from sub-20 Hz control bandwidth.
AI.conclusion: MIRA provides an accessible, modifiable platform for indoor micro-UAV research, reducing engineering barriers while maintaining performance suitable for research use.

title: LSTrans: Efficient Knowledge Transfer for Lightweight and Automated ECG Classification
summary: Deploying deep learning models for automated electrocardiogram classification on resource-constrained wearable devices remains challenging due to high computational costs. To address this, we propose LSTrans, a lightweight hybrid model designed for efficient and sensitive ECG analysis. LSTrans introduces a specialized 1D convolutional backbone with an interleaved layer architecture to capture both macroscopic rhythmic trends and microscopic morphological variations. This backbone is cascaded with a Transformer encoder to model long-range temporal dependencies, incorporating Low-Rank Adaptation across critical layers to compress the model and reduce the trainable parameter space. We further employ homogeneous and heterogeneous knowledge distillation to transfer diagnostic expertise from high-capacity teacher models to the student. Experimental results on multiple benchmark datasets demonstrate that LSTrans achieves a competitive balance between diagnostic sensitivity and resource efficiency, substantially reducing peak memory footprints and training latency during downstream adaptation. The source code is available for review at https://github.com/zyee00128/LSTrans4BIBM.

AI.tldr: LSTrans is a lightweight hybrid model (1D CNN + Transformer with LoRA) for efficient ECG classification on wearable devices, using knowledge distillation to balance diagnostic accuracy and resource efficiency.
AI.motivation: Deploying deep learning models for automated ECG classification on resource-constrained wearable devices is challenging due to high computational costs.
AI.method: LSTrans combines a 1D convolutional backbone with interleaved layers for multi-scale ECG feature extraction, cascaded with a Transformer encoder using Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) to compress parameters, plus homogeneous and heterogeneous knowledge distillation from teacher models.
AI.result: LSTrans achieves a competitive balance between diagnostic sensitivity and resource efficiency, significantly reducing peak memory footprints and training latency on multiple benchmark datasets.
AI.conclusion: The proposed lightweight hybrid model enables efficient and sensitive ECG analysis suitable for wearable devices, with open-source code available.

title: h-Flow: Flexible Flow-based Image Editing via Doob's h-Transform
summary: Editing images with pre-trained text-to-image flow models typically requires carefully balancing target alignment with the desired prompt and source consistency with the original image. Existing approaches either rely on inversion-based pipelines or heuristic source-to-target trajectory constructions, which often depend on architecture-specific designs or are sensitive to hyperparameters. In this paper, we propose h-Flow, a training-free and theoretically grounded flow-based editing framework. Inspired by Doob's $h$-Transform, we reformulate image editing as conditional generation under multiple terminal events corresponding to source consistency and target alignment. We first extend the classical $h$-Transform from SDE-based models to the deterministic RF framework by constructing an equivalent SDE with identical marginals. Within this formulation, we design dedicated $h$-functions for source consistency and target alignment, yielding closed-form reconstruction guidance and velocity-based semantic editing signals. We further introduce a velocity orthogonal decomposition to decouple reconstruction and editing directions, enabling a controllable trade-off between the two objectives. Extensive experiments demonstrate that h-Flow achieves effective, robust, and flexible editing across diverse scenarios. The code will be released soon.

AI.tldr: h-Flow is a training-free, theoretically grounded flow-based image editing framework using Doob's h-Transform to balance source consistency and target alignment, with velocity orthogonal decomposition for controllable trade-offs.
AI.motivation: Existing image editing methods using text-to-flow models require careful balancing between target alignment and source consistency, and often rely on inversion-based pipelines or heuristic trajectory constructions that are architecture-specific or hyperparameter-sensitive.
AI.method: Extends Doob's h-Transform from SDE-based models to deterministic RF (Rectified Flow) frameworks by constructing an equivalent SDE with identical marginals, designs dedicated h-functions for source consistency and target alignment, and introduces velocity orthogonal decomposition to decouple reconstruction and editing directions.
AI.result: Achieves effective, robust, and flexible editing across diverse scenarios with controllable trade-off between source consistency and target alignment.
AI.conclusion: h-Flow provides a principled, training-free, and theoretically grounded solution for flow-based image editing, outperforming heuristic approaches in robustness and flexibility.

title: Valid $\ne$ Necessary: Diagnosing Latent Inefficiency in Chain-of-Thought
summary: Chain-of-Thought (CoT) prompting has significantly advanced the reasoning capabilities of Large Language Models (LLMs), yet it often incurs substantial computational costs due to over-reasoning: the generation of redundant, verbose, or irrelevant steps. While existing reasoning step evaluators effectively detect logical fallacies and factual errors, our analysis reveals a critical blind spot: they fail to penalize valid but inefficient reasoning steps that inflate token usage without contributing to the solution. To systematically diagnose this limitation, we introduce RIV-GSM8K, a diagnostic benchmark injected with five distinct types of inefficiencies, including circular reasoning and excessive decomposition. Diagnostic experiments reveal that state-of-the-art evaluators struggle to distinguish these inefficiencies from necessary reasoning. To address this gap, we propose CAID (Context-Aware Information Density), a training-free metric grounded in information theory that identifies low-utility steps. To validate the metric's practical utility, we apply it within PACE, a post-hoc compression strategy. Additional control experiments show that the gains of PACE are not explained by trivial pruning: compared with random step removal and PRM-based compression baselines, it preserves accuracy at substantially higher compression rates. Empirical results on GSM8K, StrategyQA, and ARC-Challenge demonstrate that PACE reduces token consumption by 31-53% while maintaining accuracy, confirming that CAID successfully distills informational froth from reasoning chains without compromising deductive validity.

AI.tldr: The paper identifies that current CoT reasoning evaluators fail to penalize inefficient but logically valid reasoning steps. The authors introduce RIV-GSM8K (a diagnostic benchmark with 5 inefficiency types), propose CAID (a training-free metric for detecting low-utility steps), and implement PACE (a compression strategy) that reduces token usage by 31-53% while preserving accuracy.
AI.motivation: Existing reasoning step evaluators detect logical fallacies and factual errors but overlook "over-reasoning" — valid but inefficient steps that waste tokens without contributing to the solution.
AI.method: The authors (1) introduce RIV-GSM8K, a benchmark with 5 types of inefficiencies (e.g., circular reasoning, excessive decomposition), (2) propose CAID, a training-free information-theoretic metric, and (3) apply CAID within PACE, a post-hoc compression strategy for pruning low-utility steps.
AI.result: SOTA evaluators fail to distinguish inefficient from necessary reasoning. PACE reduces token consumption by 31-53% while maintaining accuracy across GSM8K, StrategyQA, and ARC-Challenge.
AI.conclusion: CAID successfully distills "informational froth" from reasoning chains without compromising deductive validity, confirming that information density is a useful signal for pruning.

title: Casting Everything to Online API Services? A Survey of Integrating Localized Speech Recognition Models in Robotic Systems
comment: accepted in 18th International Conference on Social Robotics (ICSR + ART 2026)
summary: Automatic speech recognition (ASR) has become a critical component of modern robotic systems because it is one of the most natural and intuitive ways for humans to interact with robots. A commonly used method is to directly use API services online. But is that all we can do? This article provides an overview of how ASR technologies are integrated into various intelligent robots and machines. We discuss the evolution of speech recognition from established approaches to state-of-the-art deep learning models, such as OpenAI's Whisper. We also list large-scale datasets and open source toolkits that have been widely used in both industry and academia. We structure the survey around ASR model families, deployment strategies in robotics (especially ROS-based, cloud-based, and hybrid solutions), and several real-world robotic platforms. Finally, we outline the challenges of deploying robust speech recognition in robots and discuss future directions, including multimodal interaction in diverse and dynamic environments. This paper can help social robotics researchers better navigate the emerging domain of language-based natural human-robot interaction.

AI.tldr: This paper surveys how ASR technologies are integrated into robotic systems, covering evolution from traditional to deep learning models (e.g., Whisper), deployment strategies (ROS-based, cloud, hybrid), real-world platforms, and future challenges in multimodal interaction.
AI.motivation: ASR is a critical component for natural human-robot interaction, yet there is a need for a comprehensive overview bridging ASR technologies and robotic deployment strategies to help social robotics researchers navigate this emerging domain.
AI.method: The paper structures its survey around three main axes: ASR model families (from established to deep learning models like Whisper), deployment strategies in robotics (ROS-based, cloud-based, hybrid solutions), and real-world robotic platforms, while also listing large-scale datasets and open-source toolkits.
AI.result: The survey provides a structured overview of how ASR is integrated into intelligent robots, including the evolution of speech recognition models, deployment architectures, and practical robotic platforms.
AI.conclusion: The paper outlines key challenges in deploying robust speech recognition in robots and discusses future directions, particularly multimodal interaction in diverse and dynamic environments.

title: Hierarchical Bayesian Quadrature
comment: 17 pages (9 main text), 10 figures. Accepted at the 42nd Conference on Uncertainty in Artificial Intelligence (UAI 2026)
summary: Numerical integration is a cornerstone of various scientific computing applications, such as engineering simulations and model evidence computations in probabilistic machine learning. Bayesian Quadrature uses Gaussian process surrogates that explicitly encode structural assumptions about the integrand to obtain integral estimates with quantified uncertainty. These surrogates are predominantly based on stationary covariance functions, which results in model misspecification for integrands exhibiting nonstationary behavior. We tackle this issue through an adaptively growing, tree-based partition of the integration domain into local stationary models. Our method recombines the local integral estimates through a hierarchy of GP conditioning that reintroduces cross-subdomain correlations, while model selection criteria control the tree growth to avoid unnecessary partitioning. The resulting algorithm is simple, requires no MCMC, and adapts its evaluation budget to local integrand complexity. On benchmark integration problems and a model evidence computation for an epidemiological model, Hierarchical Bayesian Quadrature achieves substantial gains over standard Bayesian Quadrature on nonstationary integrands while matching its performance on stationary ones.

AI.tldr: Hierarchical Bayesian Quadrature (HBQ) improves numerical integration by using an adaptively grown tree-based partition of the domain into local stationary GP models, recombining them via hierarchical GP conditioning, achieving better performance on nonstationary integrands without MCMC.
AI.motivation: Standard Bayesian Quadrature relies on stationary covariance functions, which leads to model misspecification for nonstationary integrands. The authors aim to address this limitation.
AI.method: The method uses an adaptively growing tree-based partition of the integration domain into local stationary Gaussian process models, recombines local integral estimates through hierarchical GP conditioning to restore cross-subdomain correlations, and uses model selection criteria to control tree growth.
AI.result: On benchmark integration problems and a model evidence computation for an epidemiological model, HBQ achieves substantial gains over standard Bayesian Quadrature on nonstationary integrands while matching its performance on stationary ones.
AI.conclusion: Hierarchical Bayesian Quadrature is a simple, MCMC-free algorithm that adapts its evaluation budget to local integrand complexity and effectively handles both stationary and nonstationary integrands.

title: 3D-DefectBench: A Controlled Factorial Study of Vision-Language Model Evaluation Pipelines for Fine-Grained 3D Generation Defects
summary: Automated evaluation is essential for scaling generative 3D systems, where exhaustive human review is costly and slow. However, the reliability of an automated judge depends on the entire evaluation pipeline, not only the underlying vision-language model (VLM), but also how assets are rendered, what visual evidence is provided, how the task is specified, and how human reference labels are constructed. We introduce 3D-DefectBench, a benchmark and framework for systematic analysis of VLM-based 3D defect detection pipelines. It complements holistic ratings and pairwise preferences with nine fine-grained binary defects spanning geometry, texture, and prompt adherence, providing actionable diagnostics for generator development and judge evaluation. Using a balanced factorial design, we vary four pipeline factors, VLM, camera protocol, visual input, and prompt schema, across 84 inference designs and approximately 3.2 million scored defect decisions, followed by staged validation on a broader set of frontier models. Model choice is the largest determinant of agreement with human labels, but the remaining factors also affect performance, interact with model selection, and can change the best configuration. Within the evaluated design space, a compact six-view RGB protocol performs comparably to denser multi-view settings and inputs augmented with depth or surface normals, making it a strong cost-effective default. Under this standardized pipeline, the best of 12 VLM judges still lag behind trained human labelers, while texture agreement drops sharply when expert-consensus labels are replaced by noisier silver labels. These findings show that automated judges should be evaluated as complete pipelines and calibrated across human reference regimes, rather than benchmarked only as standalone models. We release labels, prompts, predictions, and Croissant metadata on Hugging Face.

AI.tldr: 3D-DefectBench is a benchmark for analyzing VLM-based 3D defect detection pipelines, finding that model choice is the biggest factor but all pipeline components matter, and that a compact six-view RGB protocol is cost-effective yet still lags behind human labelers.
AI.motivation: Automated evaluation is needed for scaling generative 3D systems because human review is costly and slow, but the reliability of automated judges depends on the entire evaluation pipeline—not just the VLM—including rendering, visual evidence, task specification, and label construction.
AI.method: The authors introduce 3D-DefectBench with nine fine-grained binary defects across geometry, texture, and prompt adherence. Using a balanced factorial design, they vary four pipeline factors (VLM, camera protocol, visual input, prompt schema) across 84 inference designs and ~3.2 million scored defect decisions, with staged validation on frontier models.
AI.result: Model choice is the largest determinant of agreement with human labels, but other factors also affect performance and interact with model selection. A compact six-view RGB protocol performs comparably to denser multi-view settings. The best VLM judges still lag behind trained human labelers, and texture agreement drops sharply when expert-consensus labels are replaced by noisy silver labels.
AI.conclusion: Automated judges should be evaluated as complete pipelines and calibrated across human reference regimes, not benchmarked only as standalone models.

title: Efficient Test-Time Optimization for Multi-Agent Proof Autoformalization
summary: Full-proof autoformalization bridges extensive mathematical proofs in natural language with formally validated reasoning, offering a pathway to elevate the ceiling of verifiable mathematical reasoning. Unlike statement-level formalization, proof autoformalization is a long-horizon challenge requiring coordination of claims, contexts, and dependencies across many proof steps, yet has only recently come under focused study. Current approaches either rely on costly model training or apply excessive, unguided repair at inference time. To this end, we introduce ToMap, a multi-agent framework that structures proof autoformalization as a Decomposer-Formalizer-Prover pipeline with efficient test-time optimization guided by formal verification and semantic rubrics for proof quality. Rather than distributing test-time compute across all agents, we perform bottleneck analysis and identify the Decomposer as the critical bottleneck: the quality of its atomic, self-contained proof units directly determines whether downstream agents can successfully formalize and prove each step. ToMap therefore treats the Formalizer and Prover as downstream executors and efficiently focuses test-time compute on Decomposer refinement. This refinement follows a loop inspired by GEPA, evolving prompts over candidate decompositions and using formal verification progress together with semantic proof rubrics to define a Pareto frontier that guides the next decomposition update. Experiments on ProofFlowBench show that ToMap improves over the best previous method by 19.0% when evaluated by both syntactic correctness and semantic faithfulness, while requiring lower test-time cost. Scaling analysis shows that most gains emerge within a few iterations of decomposition evolution, guiding test-time budget selection.

AI.tldr: ToMap is a multi-agent framework for proof autoformalization that uses a Decomposer-Formalizer-Prover pipeline with test-time optimization, identifying and focusing compute on the Decomposer bottleneck, achieving 19% improvement over prior methods on ProofFlowBench.
AI.motivation: Proof autoformalization is a long-horizon challenge requiring coordination across many steps, yet current approaches rely on costly training or excessive unguided repair at inference time.
AI.method: ToMap is a multi-agent framework with a Decomposer-Formalizer-Prover pipeline. It identifies the Decomposer as the bottleneck and focuses test-time compute on refining it using a GEPA-inspired loop with Pareto frontier guidance, while Formalizer and Prover act as downstream executors.
AI.result: ToMap improves over the best previous method by 19.0% on ProofFlowBench (evaluated on both syntactic correctness and semantic faithfulness) with lower test-time cost. Most gains emerge within a few decomposition evolution iterations.
AI.conclusion: ToMap demonstrates that focusing test-time compute on decomposition refinement (the bottleneck) rather than distributing it across all agents is an effective and efficient strategy for proof autoformalization.

title: Robust bipedal locomotion on flowable slopes via foot-driven terrain manipulation
summary: Bipedal robots are challenging to control because they operate close to instability, where small variations in foot-terrain contact can rapidly destabilize locomotion. On rigid terrain, bipedal robots mitigate this fragility by using well-established contact mechanics and control strategies. On flowable surfaces such as granular slopes, foot contact can induce large surface deformations and solid-fluid-like transitions, coupling terrain effects with robot dynamics, leading to underperformance or failure. This is partly due to the lack of reliable methods to represent the dynamics of flowable terrain, making it difficult to account for terrain effects in locomotion design. Here, we investigate how controlling terrain response can improve bipedal locomotion on granular slopes by studying the terradynamics of cleated feet, thin plates emanating from the foot soles. Systematic studies of a small-scale (1.4 kg) robophysical biped reveal that cleats with sparse and dense spacing lead to excessive terrain yielding and resistance, respectively, degrading performance and leading to failure. An intermediate cleat spacing distributes interaction forces to maintain substrate stresses near (or below) the yield threshold, enabling walking on granular slopes up to 30 degrees. Guided by these principles, we design a foot that actively adjusts cleat depth and accommodates both rigid and granular terrain. We also demonstrate that the principles of effective foot-terrain interaction translate to a larger (15 kg) autonomous biped. Our study presents an alternative to conventional body-centric robot control approaches, which regulate terrain-induced effects through body motion, by instead regulating terrain interactions through limb-centric approach.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates how controlling terrain response via cleated feet can improve bipedal locomotion on granular slopes. Through systematic studies of a robophysical biped, the authors find that intermediate cleat spacing distributes forces optimally to prevent terrain yielding, enabling walking on slopes up to 30°. They validate principles on both small (1.4 kg) and larger (15 kg) autonomous bipeds, proposing a limb-centric approach to terrain interaction as an alternative to conventional body-centric control.
AI.motivation: Bipedal robots struggle on flowable surfaces like granular slopes because foot contact induces large deformations and solid-fluid transitions that couple with robot dynamics, leading to failure. Current methods lack reliable ways to represent flowable terrain dynamics, making it difficult to account for terrain effects in locomotion design.
AI.method: The authors systematically study cleated feet (thin plates from foot soles) on a small-scale (1.4 kg) robophysical biped walking on granular slopes. They vary cleat spacing and measure performance. They then design an active foot that adjusts cleat depth and validate principles on a larger (15 kg) autonomous biped.
AI.result: Cleats with sparse spacing cause excessive terrain yielding, while dense spacing creates excessive resistance—both degrade performance. Intermediate cleat spacing distributes forces to maintain substrate stresses near/below yield threshold, enabling walking on granular slopes up to 30°. Principles translate to a larger 15 kg biped.
AI.conclusion: The study presents an alternative limb-centric approach that regulates terrain interactions through foot design rather than conventional body-centric control that compensates for terrain effects through body motion.

title: Weight-Adjusted Gradients Reveal Parameter Importance and Failure Modes in LLMs
summary: Understanding which parameters are influential in Large Language Models (LLMs) is central to improving their efficiency, reliability, and interpretability. We introduce Weight-Adjusted Gradients (WAG), a simple yet effective approach for estimating parameter importance that explicitly captures the interaction between model weights and first-order gradient information and identifies parameters that disproportionately influence model behavior, such as those responsible for collapse phenomena in LLMs. Across a range of models and settings, we show that WAG surfaces a tiny but critical subset of parameters whose modification leads to dramatic degradation in performance, a failure mode that existing importance metrics overlook. These findings reveal a previously underexplored interplay between weights and gradients, suggesting that parameter importance cannot be fully understood through either signal alone. The surprising effectiveness of WAG points to fundamental structural properties of trained networks and motivates new open questions about the role of zeroth-order and first-order information in deep learning. We demonstrate the practical utility of WAG across multiple applications, including expert allocation in mixture-of-expert architectures, parameter-specific unlearning, mixed-precision quantization, and layer selection for knowledge editing. Our results position WAG as a unified approach for analyzing, debugging, and controlling LLMs, and opens new directions for principled model-level interpretation.

AI.tldr: WAG is a method combining weights and gradients to identify critical parameters in LLMs that other importance metrics miss, with applications in MoE, unlearning, quantization, and knowledge editing.
AI.motivation: Understanding which parameters are influential in LLMs is central to improving their efficiency, reliability, and interpretability. Existing importance metrics overlook certain failure modes like collapse phenomena.
AI.method: Weight-Adjusted Gradients (WAG) — a method that explicitly captures the interaction between model weights and first-order gradient information to estimate parameter importance.
AI.result: WAG surfaces a tiny but critical subset of parameters whose modification leads to dramatic degradation (collapse), which existing importance metrics miss. Demonstrates utility across MoE expert allocation, unlearning, quantization, and knowledge editing.
AI.conclusion: Parameter importance cannot be fully understood through weights or gradients alone. WAG reveals fundamental structural properties of trained networks and serves as a unified approach for analyzing, debugging, and controlling LLMs.

title: OmniX: Any-view and Any-time 4D Reconstruction via Feed-forward Trajectory Fields
comment: Accepted by ECCV 2026, project page: https://omnix4d.github.io/
summary: Previous feed-forward 4D reconstruction methods either predict per-frame static point clouds, ignoring foreground motion, or estimate point cloud trajectories while being limited to small camera motions. This restricts their ability to aggregate observations over time and reconstruct complete dynamic scenes under large viewpoint changes. To address this limitation, we propose OmniX, a feed-forward 4D reconstruction framework that predicts dense 3D point trajectories for every pixel from videos with large camera motion. OmniX decouples dynamic motion modeling from static geometry prediction and represents motion using a compact set of dynamic tokens. By leveraging the sparse and low-rank structure of 3D motion, these tokens generate trajectory fields for all pixels across all images while efficiently preserving global interactions. To facilitate training, we further build an automatic UE5-based 4D data engine and introduce a large-scale dataset containing 80K scenes and 1.28M multi-view videos with full geometric annotations. OmniX achieves state-of-the-art performance on dense 3D point trajectory prediction and 3D point tracking, while also demonstrating competitive results on video depth estimation and camera pose estimation.

AI.tldr: OmniX is a feed-forward 4D reconstruction framework that predicts dense 3D point trajectories from videos with large camera motion, using dynamic tokens to decouple motion from static geometry, trained on a large UE5-based synthetic dataset, achieving SOTA on trajectory prediction and 3D tracking.
AI.motivation: Previous 4D reconstruction methods either ignore foreground motion or are limited to small camera motions, hindering their ability to aggregate observations over time and reconstruct complete dynamic scenes under large viewpoint changes.
AI.method: OmniX decouples dynamic motion modeling from static geometry prediction, represents motion via a compact set of dynamic tokens (leveraging sparse/low-rank structure of 3D motion), and generates trajectory fields for all pixels across images while preserving global interactions. It also uses an automatic UE5-based 4D data engine with 80K scenes and 1.28M multi-view videos.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on dense 3D point trajectory prediction and 3D point tracking, and competitive results on video depth estimation and camera pose estimation.
AI.conclusion: OmniX effectively addresses the limitation of prior methods by enabling 4D reconstruction from videos with large camera motion, demonstrating strong performance across multiple tasks.

title: A Minimalist Retargeting-Guided Reinforcement Learning Recipe for Dexterous Manipulation
summary: Recent work in humanoid whole-body control has found success with a simple recipe: retarget human motion to robot kinematic references, then train policies via reinforcement learning (RL) to track them. But how does this recipe transfer to dexterous manipulation? The answer is not obvious, as manipulation involves complex, contact-rich dynamics and requires delicate regulation of contact modes and forces. We present REGRIND, a minimalist retargeting-guided RL pipeline that learns dexterous manipulation policies from a single human demonstration. REGRIND retargets human hand-object motion to a robot reference that preserves hand-object spatial and contact relationships, trains a residual RL policy in simulation to track object-centric keypoints along that reference, and transfers the resulting policy zero-shot to hardware with careful system identification. The resulting policies produce fluid, human-like behavior on two different multi-fingered hands across contact-rich tool-use tasks, including operating a pair of scissors and turning a screwdriver. Through systematic hardware experiments, we identify and analyze the key factors that govern sim-to-real transfer in dexterous manipulation, offering practical guidance for retargeting-based learning in contact-rich settings. Videos and code are available at https://yunhaifeng.com/REGRIND.

AI.tldr: REGRIND is a minimalist pipeline that retargets a single human demonstration to robot references, then uses residual RL in simulation to learn dexterous manipulation policies that transfer zero-shot to real hardware, achieving fluid tool-use like scissors and screwdriver operation.
AI.motivation: Existing whole-body control recipes (retarget human motion → RL tracking) don't obviously transfer to dexterous manipulation due to complex contact-rich dynamics, and the key factors governing sim-to-real transfer in this setting are poorly understood.
AI.method: (1) Retarget human hand-object motion to robot references preserving spatial/contact relationships; (2) Train a residual RL policy in simulation to track object-centric keypoints; (3) Zero-shot transfer to real hardware with careful system identification.
AI.result: Produces fluid, human-like behavior on two different multi-fingered hands for tool-use tasks (scissors, screwdriver), with systematic hardware experiments identifying key factors for sim-to-real transfer.
AI.conclusion: Retargeting-guided RL is effective for dexterous manipulation from a single human demo, and the paper provides practical guidance for contact-rich sim-to-real transfer.

title: When does distribution shift break graph neural networks calibration?
summary: Graph neural networks (GNNs) are increasingly deployed in real-world applications where distribution shift is un-avoidable. However, how such shifts affect model calibration, defined as the agreement between predictive confidence and actual accuracy, remains poorly understood, and existing graph calibration methods typically rely on labeled validation data from the deployment distribution. In this work, I present the first closed-form theoretical characterization of GNN calibration under distribution shift. I show that calibration is governed by a single scalar quantity that explicitly depends on structural changes between the source and target graphs, as well as feature quality. This characterization precisely identifies when a model becomes over-confident, under-confident, or remains calibrated, and directly yields the optimal temperature scaling strategy. I further extend the analysis to graph convolutional networks with symmetric normalization, multi-class classification, and covariate shift, and derive a theoretical upper bound on the expected calibration error. My analysis also reveals that, under homogeneous distribution shift, a single global temperature is theoretically optimal, providing a principled explanation for why more complex node-wise recalibration methods offer no additional benefit. Building on these theoretical insights, I propose STAC, a source-free, label-free calibration method. Experiments on synthetic benchmarks demonstrate substantial calibration improvements, while evaluations on five real-world graph datasets show that reliable calibration without target labels remains challenging despite the strong predictive power of the theory.

AI.tldr: This paper provides the first closed-form theoretical characterization of GNN calibration under distribution shift, showing calibration depends on structural changes and feature quality, and proposes a source-free, label-free calibration method (STAC).
AI.motivation: GNNs are deployed in real-world scenarios with unavoidable distribution shifts, but how such shifts affect model calibration (agreement between confidence and accuracy) is poorly understood. Existing calibration methods require labeled validation data from the target distribution, which is often unavailable.
AI.method: The author derives a closed-form theoretical characterization of GNN calibration under distribution shift, identifying a single scalar quantity governing calibration that depends on structural graph changes and feature quality. Extends analysis to GCNs, multi-class classification, and covariate shift. Proposes STAC, a source-free, label-free calibration method based on theoretical insights.
AI.result: The theory precisely identifies when models become over-confident, under-confident, or remain calibrated. Under homogeneous shift, a single global temperature is theoretically optimal. Synthetic benchmarks show substantial calibration improvements; real-world evaluations show reliable calibration without target labels remains challenging.
AI.conclusion: The theoretical framework provides principled understanding of GNN calibration under shift, but practical source-free calibration on real graphs remains difficult despite strong theoretical predictions.

title: Align and Segment: Unsupervised Learning for Building Segmentation From Misaligned Labels
comment: main draft with references is 17 pages
summary: Supervised learning for image segmentation typically requires spatially aligned image and label sets. When images and labels originate from different sources, the pairing may be misaligned, which can significantly deteriorate the performance of the learned models. This is especially common in remote sensing, when aerial or satellite images are co-registered with labels from another source (e.g., OpenStreetMap). In this work, we propose a novel approach for training on misaligned labels, where we simultaneously learn the label alignment. Our align and segment (AnS) approach builds on the spatial transformer module to transform the misaligned labels using an affine transformation to provide a better learning target for a canonical semantic segmentation network. We prevent shortcut learning of misaligned labels in these semantic segmentation networks through a self-supervised regularization loss and show that it is complementary to data augmentation, especially for systematically misaligned training data. A decisive characteristic of our AnS approach is that it learns without requiring any golden labels. We experimentally show on both synthetic and real-world data from different cities that our approach enables high-quality building segmentation and precise label-image alignment at the same time. Code and derived datasets are available at https://github.com/venkanna37/align-and-segment

AI.tldr: Proposes AnS (Align and Segment) approach that simultaneously learns label alignment and segmentation from misaligned training data using spatial transformers and self-supervised regularization, without requiring any ground truth labels.
AI.motivation: Misaligned labels between images and label sets from different sources (e.g., remote sensing images vs. OpenStreetMap labels) significantly degrade segmentation model performance, yet existing methods typically require perfectly aligned training data.
AI.method: Integrates a spatial transformer module to learn affine transformations that align misaligned labels, combined with a self-supervised regularization loss to prevent shortcut learning, all trained jointly with a canonical semantic segmentation network without golden labels.
AI.result: Demonstrates on synthetic and real-world data from different cities that the approach achieves high-quality building segmentation and precise label-image alignment simultaneously, and that the self-supervised regularization is complementary to data augmentation.
AI.conclusion: The proposed AnS method effectively handles misaligned training labels in remote sensing image segmentation by jointly learning alignment and segmentation, offering a practical solution for real-world scenarios where perfectly paired data is unavailable.

title: Verifier-Guided Twelve-Tone Composition: A Generate-Verify-Repair Harness for Symbolic Music Generation
summary: Large language models can produce superficially legal twelve-tone scores that collapse into degenerate textures. We introduce a neuro-symbolic harness that wraps a language-model proposer in a generate-verify-repair-trace loop with symbolic verification. The complete pipeline improves event-local consistency without claiming whole-piece legality. Across 40 controlled tasks and four paired models, audited delivery yield rises from 13.3% under raw generation to 48.1% with the harness, which explicitly abstains otherwise. The pass rate of a narrower collision and serialisation-consistency check rises from 33.5% to 58.3%, while degeneracy remains near 0.05, including under exploratory adversarial prompting. A blinded evaluation by five experts also shows a descriptive aggregate preference for harness candidates over raw generation in adherence, perceived legality, coherence, and overall quality.

AI.tldr: A neuro-symbolic harness wrapping language models with a generate-verify-repair-trace loop significantly improves twelve-tone score legality (48.1% vs 13.3% raw) while reducing degeneracy, validated through controlled tasks and expert evaluation.
AI.motivation: Large language models generate twelve-tone scores that look plausible but collapse into degenerate textures, failing to maintain event-local consistency and legal structure.
AI.method: A neuro-symbolic harness that wraps an LM proposer in a generate-verify-repair-trace loop with symbolic verification, checking for collisions and serialization consistency.
AI.result: Audited delivery yield rose from 13.3% to 48.1%; pass rate of consistency checks rose from 33.5% to 58.3%; degeneracy stayed near 0.05 under adversarial prompting; experts preferred harness outputs across adherence, legality, coherence, and quality.
AI.conclusion: The neuro-symbolic harness substantially improves event-local consistency and overall perceived quality of twelve-tone compositions without claiming whole-piece legality, and remains robust under adversarial conditions.

title: Mixture of Frames Policy: Multi-Frame Action Denoising for Bimanual Mobile Manipulation
summary: Robotic manipulation is inherently multi-frame: local actions may be simple in an end-effector frame, while transport, upright-object handling, and whole-body coordination are better represented in a base-aligned frame. However, modern diffusion-based visuomotor policies typically commit to a single predefined action frame, forcing one denoiser to model action distributions that are often unnecessarily complex in that frame. We propose Mixture of Frames Policy (MoF), a diffusion policy that performs synchronized action denoising across multiple coordinate frames. MoF maintains a single canonical diffusion state, re-expresses it in several task-relevant frames, applies frame-specialized denoisers, and fuses their noise predictions back in the canonical frame. To make this possible for intermediate noisy diffusion states, we introduce a column-based 6D rotation representation within an SE(3) action parameterization that supports exact, differentiable frame transformations without requiring noisy rotations to lie on the SO(3) manifold. Across nine simulated bimanual manipulation tasks, we show that the best action frame is task-dependent and that MoF improves over oracle frame selection and standard Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) baselines. We further evaluate MoF on two real-world bimanual mobile manipulation tasks, demonstrating that it outperforms all constituent single-frame baselines. Project homepage: https://mofpo.github.io

AI.tldr: MoF proposes a diffusion policy that denoises actions across multiple coordinate frames simultaneously, outperforming single-frame baselines in bimanual manipulation tasks.
AI.motivation: Robotic manipulation actions are naturally multi-frame (end-effector vs base-aligned), but existing diffusion policies commit to a single predefined action frame, forcing unnecessarily complex action distribution modeling.
AI.method: MoF maintains a canonical diffusion state, re-expresses it in multiple task-relevant frames, applies frame-specialized denoisers, and fuses predictions back to the canonical frame. It introduces a column-based 6D rotation representation supporting exact, differentiable SE(3) frame transformations without requiring SO(3) manifold constraints for noisy rotations.
AI.result: Across 9 simulated bimanual tasks, MoF improves over oracle frame selection and standard MoE baselines. On 2 real-world bimanual mobile manipulation tasks, MoF outperforms all constituent single-frame baselines.
AI.conclusion: The best action frame is task-dependent, and MoF's multi-frame synchronized denoising approach is an effective strategy for handling diverse manipulation tasks without committing to a single frame.

title: Lower Bound on the Cumulative Constrained Violation for the OGD+Projection algorithm for Constrained Online Convex Optimization (COCO)
summary: The problem of constrained online convex optimization is considered, where at each round, once a learner commits to an action $x_t \in \mathcal{X} \subset \mathbb{R}^d$, a convex loss function $f_t$ and a convex constraint function $g_t$ that drives the constraint $g_t(x)\le 0$ are revealed. The objective is to simultaneously minimize the static regret and cumulative constraint violation (CCV) compared to the benchmark that knows the loss functions and constraint functions $f_t$ and $g_t$ for all $t$ ahead of time, and chooses a static optimal action that is feasible with respect to all $g_t(x)\le 0$. Currently, the best known algorithm is OGD+Projection algorithm of [Vaze and Sinha, 2025] that has simultaneous regret of $O(\sqrt{T})$ and CCV of $O(T^{1/3})$ for $d=2$ [Balasundaram et al., 2026], and simultaneous regret of $O(\sqrt{T})$ and CCV of $O(\sqrt{T})$ for any $d$ [Sarkar and Sinha, 2026]. In this paper, we show that the CCV of the OGD+Projection algorithm is $Ω(T^{\frac{d-1}{2d}})$. This is the first such lower bound result.

AI.tldr: This paper proves a lower bound on cumulative constraint violation for the OGD+Projection algorithm in constrained online convex optimization, showing CCV is Ω(T^{(d-1)/(2d)}), which is the first such lower bound.
AI.motivation: To establish a lower bound on the cumulative constraint violation (CCV) of the OGD+Projection algorithm, which was previously unknown, thereby providing a theoretical limit on its performance.
AI.method: The paper analyzes the OGD+Projection algorithm and derives a lower bound of Ω(T^{(d-1)/(2d)}) for cumulative constraint violation in d-dimensional space, where T is the time horizon.
AI.result: The CCV lower bound for OGD+Projection is Ω(T^{(d-1)/(2d)}), which is dimension-dependent and the first such lower bound result.
AI.conclusion: This lower bound reveals the inherent limitation of OGD+Projection, particularly highlighting performance degradation in higher dimensions for constraint violation.

title: Learning To Focus: Anatomy-Guided Attention Regularization for Medical Image Classification
summary: Medical image classification models are ideally expected to identify diagnostically relevant regions while making predictions, yet standard classification losses rarely provide spatial supervision. Explicit supervision via anatomical shape information, such as segmentation masks of task-relevant anatomy, has been shown to guide the network toward regions relevant to the target prediction. However, obtaining such masks incurs substantial manual annotation effort and computational overhead. With the advent of segmentation foundation models that exhibit strong localization of anatomical structures across diverse imaging modalities, we leverage this capability to extract anatomical shape priors without the burden of training a dedicated segmentation model. In this paper, we propose a new framework, Locus, an anatomical attention regularization framework that leverages pretrained segmentation foundation models to guide a classifier's attention toward diagnostically meaningful anatomical structures across diverse imaging modalities. Instead of enforcing pixel-wise alignment with the foundation-model-derived mask, we introduce a regularization term that adaptively balances attention between anatomical (foreground) and background regions, penalizing the classifier when background attention dominates. We validate Locus on eight diverse medical imaging datasets spanning dermoscopy, X-ray, histopathology, and cardiac MRI, showing consistent gains in classification performance alongside improved anatomically grounded attention.

AI.tldr: Locus is an anatomical attention regularization framework that leverages pretrained segmentation foundation models to guide classifier attention toward diagnostically relevant anatomical structures without requiring dedicated segmentation training, achieving consistent gains across eight diverse medical imaging datasets.
AI.motivation: Medical image classifiers often lack spatial supervision to focus on diagnostically relevant regions. Anatomical shape supervision via segmentation masks improves attention but requires costly manual annotation and computational overhead for training dedicated segmentation models.
AI.method: Locus uses pretrained segmentation foundation models to extract anatomical shape priors, then introduces a regularization term that adaptively balances attention between foreground (anatomical) and background regions, penalizing the classifier when background attention dominates, without enforcing pixel-wise alignment.
AI.result: Validated on eight diverse medical imaging datasets (dermoscopy, X-ray, histopathology, cardiac MRI), showing consistent classification performance gains alongside improved anatomically grounded attention.
AI.conclusion: Leveraging pretrained segmentation foundation models for spatial regularization is an effective and efficient way to improve both classification accuracy and anatomical attention without needing dedicated segmentation model training.

title: AutoVSR: Automatic Visual-to-Symbolic Reasoning for Symbolic Expression Generation from Circuit Schematic
summary: Symbolic expressions can effectively characterize and predict circuit behavior, but deriving them directly from circuit schematics is challenging. This process requires accurate visual-to-symbolic construction of circuit structure from images and correct multi-step symbolic derivation, both of which impose strict correctness requirements. This work proposes AutoVSR, an automated framework for visual-to-symbolic generation of circuit expressions using Vision Language Models (VLMs). By reconstructing circuit diagrams into an executable intermediate representation (Executable IR) and leveraging a symbolic solver for reasoning, AutoVSR significantly improves the accuracy of symbolic expression generation. AutoVSR introduces two key innovations: an IR construction method guided by component rule retrieval and verification-based feedback, and a symbolic solver implemented as a planning agent equipped with a symbolic tool library for reliable multi-step derivation. Compared with end-to-end VLM approaches and specialized methods on the main symbolic expression generation task, AutoVSR achieves accuracy improvements of 30.01--59.45% and 41.96--51.84%, respectively. Moreover, AutoVSR surpasses closed-source state-of-the-art VLMs in inference cost and computational efficiency. Code is available at https://github.com/LongfeiLi1/AutoVSR.

AI.tldr: AutoVSR is an automated framework that uses Vision Language Models to convert circuit diagrams into symbolic expressions by reconstructing them into an executable intermediate representation and using a symbolic solver, achieving 30-59% accuracy improvements over existing methods.
AI.motivation: Deriving symbolic expressions from circuit schematics is challenging due to the need for accurate visual-to-symbolic construction and correct multi-step symbolic derivation, both of which require high correctness.
AI.method: AutoVSR reconstructs circuit diagrams into an Executable IR using component rule retrieval and verification-based feedback, then leverages a symbolic solver (planning agent with symbolic tool library) for reliable multi-step derivation.
AI.result: Accuracy improvements of 30.01-59.45% over end-to-end VLMs and 41.96-51.84% over specialized methods; surpasses closed-source SOTA VLMs in inference cost and efficiency.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework effectively combines VLMs with symbolic reasoning for reliable circuit expression generation, demonstrating both accuracy and computational efficiency advantages.

title: Diachronic Sample Integration: Robust Tail-Risk Estimation with Generative Models
summary: Deep generative models are increasingly used as simulators for downstream decision-making under data scarcity, but in risk-sensitive applications their usefulness depends on rare adverse scenarios rather than typical samples. Standard generative objectives prioritize bulk distributional fidelity, leaving low-probability tails vulnerable to localized optimization noise and making tail-dependent functionals unstable under finite simulation budgets. We introduce Diachronic Sample Integration (DSI), a test-time inference framework that ensembles generated samples across checkpoints from a stochastic training trajectory. DSI targets a checkpoint-mixture distribution that averages checkpoint-specific tail fluctuations rather than relying on a single brittle endpoint. We formalize this mechanism through a finite-budget bias-variance theory. Empirically, across multivariate synthetic processes and high-frequency trading data, DSI substantially reduces tail-estimation error compared to single-checkpoint baselines under fixed simulation budgets, outperforming standard diffusion and state-of-the-art tail-aware baselines without modifying the generative objective.

AI.tldr: DSI is a test-time framework that ensembles generated samples across training checkpoints to improve tail estimation in deep generative models, reducing bias and variance for rare adverse scenarios without modifying training objectives.
AI.motivation: Deep generative models used in risk-sensitive downstream tasks often fail at capturing rare adverse scenarios because standard training objectives prioritize bulk distributional fidelity, making tail-dependent functionals unstable under finite simulation budgets.
AI.method: Diachronic Sample Integration (DSI) ensembles generated samples across multiple checkpoints from a stochastic training trajectory at test time, targeting a checkpoint-mixture distribution that averages tail fluctuations rather than relying on a single endpoint model.
AI.result: DSI substantially reduces tail-estimation error compared to single-checkpoint baselines under fixed simulation budgets, and outperforms standard diffusion and state-of-the-art tail-aware baselines without modifying the generative objective, on multivariate synthetic processes and high-frequency trading data.
AI.conclusion: DSI provides a simple, effective test-time framework for improving tail reliability in generative models, formalized through finite-budget bias-variance theory, without requiring changes to training objectives.

title: Diversify Diffusion with Temperature Sampling and Variance-Corrective Time Shifting
summary: Diffusion models faithfully reproduce their training distribution, but also inherit its imbalances and leave rare or under-represented modes hard to reach. A natural inference-time remedy is to sample from the high-temperature target $p^{(γ)}_0(x) \propto p_0(x)^γ$ for $0 < γ< 1$, which flattens dominant modes and lifts rare ones. However, naive score scaling while correctly reweighting modes also inflates the per-mode variance, breaking the reverse diffusion process and degrading sample quality. We introduce variance-corrective time shifting, a training-free fix that queries the network at a shifted timestep and scales the resulting score by $γ$, canceling the variance inflation while preserving the mode reweighting. The correction turns simple temperature sampling into a practical diversity knob for pretrained diffusion and flow-matching backbones with no retraining, and we demonstrate consistent gains at minimal cost to sample quality and condition fidelity across DiT, Stable Diffusion and Motion Diffusion models. We further show that the timing of the temperature intervention enables coarse-to-fine control: high-noise stages drive compositional diversity across modes, while low-noise stages drive local appearance variation under a fixed composition.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces variance-corrective time shifting, a training-free method that enables temperature-based diversity control in diffusion models by canceling variance inflation while preserving mode reweighting, demonstrated across DiT, Stable Diffusion, and Motion Diffusion models.
AI.motivation: Diffusion models inherit distribution imbalances from their training data, making rare or under-represented modes hard to sample. While high-temperature sampling ($p^{(γ)}_0(x) \propto p_0(x)^γ$) can flatten dominant modes and lift rare ones, naive score scaling inflates per-mode variance and degrades sample quality.
AI.method: Variance-corrective time shifting: a training-free fix that queries the network at a shifted timestep and scales the resulting score by $γ$, canceling variance inflation while preserving mode reweighting.
AI.result: Consistent diversity gains at minimal cost to sample quality and condition fidelity across DiT, Stable Diffusion, and Motion Diffusion models. Temperature intervention timing enables coarse-to-fine control: high-noise stages drive compositional diversity, low-noise stages drive local appearance variation.
AI.conclusion: The correction turns temperature sampling into a practical diversity knob for pretrained diffusion and flow-matching backbones with no retraining needed.

title: Compile, Then Page: Executable SOP Programs and a Capability-Gated Runtime for Procedural LLM Agents
summary: Enterprise agents must follow long-horizon, conditional, safety-critical standard operating procedures (SOPs). We compile machine-readable SOP constraints into executable pseudo-code and run them with a program-guided (PG) stack machine that pages the active frame while an LLM performs semantic execution. A three-arm SOPBench study across six models separates representation from runtime: compiled text never significantly hurts and gains up to 16.0 points where official prose underperforms. Runtime guidance is capability-gated. Two strong models independently show positive seven-domain PG contrasts (58:19 and 75:31 discordant pairs), whereas weak models are harmed. A full-program cursor ablation (active frame first, complete program retained) recovers much of the strong-model refusal gain; selective visibility adds a smaller improvement. Paired probe and audit measurements track this divide to spontaneous state discipline rather than reconstruction ability. On Bank the three primary arms rise from 70.4 to 86.4 to 92.8, with 100% refusal correctness. Practical guidance: compile first; enable active-frame paging only after a model-level discipline check.

AI.tldr: Enterprise agents can better follow long-horizon safety procedures by compiling SOPs into executable pseudo-code and running them on a stack machine with LLM semantic execution. This approach yields up to 16-point gains, with active-frame paging benefiting strong models but harming weak ones.
