AI.motivation: To enable enterprise agents to reliably follow complex, safety-critical standard operating procedures (SOPs) by addressing the gap between natural language instructions and executable constraints.
AI.method: Compiles machine-readable SOP constraints into executable pseudo-code, uses a program-guided stack machine with active-frame paging, and performs semantic execution via an LLM. Includes a three-arm SOPBench study separating representation from runtime effects.
AI.result: Compiled text never significantly hurts and gains up to 16.0 points. Strong models show positive PG contrasts (58:19 and 75:31), while weak models are harmed. Full-program cursor ablation recovers much of the gain. Performance rises from 70.4 to 86.4 to 92.8 on Bank task.
AI.conclusion: Practical guidance: compile first; enable active-frame paging only after a model-level discipline check.

title: Graph Neural Networks for RFID-Based Spatial Geometry Inference in Spatial AI Systems
comment: 38 pages, 10 figures. Introduces a Graph Neural Network framework for RFID-based spatial geometry inference using indoor floorplan semantics and digital twin representations
summary: Indoor spatial understanding remains a fundamental challenge for intelligent systems operating in physical environments. Traditional RFID localization techniques typically estimate positions of tags using signal strength measurements but fail to capture higher-order spatial relationships between objects and infrastructure. Recent work on RFID and wireless indoor localization has increasingly emphasized robust learning under noisy propagation, while recent graph-based localization methods demonstrate the value of relational modeling over isolated samples. This paper introduces a graph-based learning framework that leverages Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) to infer spatial geometry from RFID observations. Rather than predicting isolated coordinates, the proposed system models relationships between RFID readings, antennas, and physical structures within an indoor floorplan. This framing is aligned with recent graph-based indoor positioning and graph construction literature, where topology is a first-class source of information for downstream inference. The approach integrates signal strength data, floorplan semantics, and spatial constraints into a graph representation where nodes correspond to RFID observations and edges encode proximity and contextual relationships. A GNN is then trained to predict geometric patterns such as linear trajectories, rectangular bounding regions, and movement paths of objects in space.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a graph-based learning framework using Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) to infer spatial geometry from RFID observations, modeling relationships between RFID readings, antennas, and physical structures to predict geometric patterns like trajectories and bounding regions.
AI.motivation: Traditional RFID localization fails to capture higher-order spatial relationships between objects and infrastructure, relying only on signal strength for isolated coordinate estimation.
AI.method: The system constructs a graph representation where nodes correspond to RFID observations and edges encode proximity and contextual relationships, integrating signal strength, floorplan semantics, and spatial constraints. A GNN is trained to predict geometric patterns.
AI.result: The GNN predicts geometric patterns such as linear trajectories, rectangular bounding regions, and movement paths of objects in space.
AI.conclusion: Graph-based relational modeling with GNNs improves indoor spatial understanding over traditional isolated coordinate prediction approaches.

title: AU-Guided Synthetic Video Generation for Micro-Expression Recognition
summary: Micro-expression recognition is limited by the small scale, narrow demographic coverage, and restricted emotion labels of existing datasets. We introduce EquiME, a synthetic micro-expression dataset built from AU-guided image-to-video generation. EquiME contains 75K videos generated from 15K source face images across five target emotions, together with automatically inferred demographic metadata and video-quality measurements. We evaluate EquiME using frame-pair similarity, spatial variation, and no-reference perceptual-quality metrics, together with cross-dataset MER experiments on SAMM and CASME II. Models trained on EquiME achieve competitive cross-dataset performance on SAMM and CASME II and show comparatively low variation across the four evaluated architectures. This paper focuses on the dataset design, the structured AU-conditioning pipeline used for video generation, and the empirical evidence needed to assess EquiME as a synthetic MER resource. Project page: https://kirito-blade.github.io/me-vlm/

AI.tldr: EquiME is a large-scale synthetic micro-expression dataset (75K videos) generated via AU-guided image-to-video, enabling competitive cross-dataset performance on SAMM and CASME II.
AI.motivation: Existing micro-expression datasets suffer from small scale, narrow demographic coverage, and limited emotion labels, hindering model generalization.
AI.method: EquiME was built using AU-guided image-to-video generation, producing 75K videos from 15K source face images across five emotions, with automatic demographic metadata and quality measurements.
AI.result: Models trained on EquiME achieve competitive cross-dataset performance on SAMM and CASME II, with low variation across four evaluated architectures.
AI.conclusion: EquiME serves as a viable synthetic resource for micro-expression recognition, validated through quality metrics and cross-dataset experiments.

title: From Neural Network Decisions to Training Cases: An Exact Account via Case-Based Decision Theory
comment: Preprint. 15 pages, 9 figures, 4 tables. Includes appendix
summary: Neural networks increasingly guide decisions in high-stakes domains such as medical diagnosis, credit approval, and energy bidding. Audit in these settings requires case-level evidence: which training cases support an action and what outcomes they carried. Case-based decision theory (CBDT) formalizes this reasoning by aggregating outcome support from remembered cases. We show that an OLS action readout fitted on a fixed neural representation admits an exact case-based decomposition. Each action score is a weighted sum of training-case returns, with coefficients determined by empirical Gram geometry. We identify a sufficient regime for CBDT similarity semantics; outside it, the coefficients should generally be treated as signed Gram-geometric influence. The decomposition yields audit signals that trace scores to training cases, measure action coherence, and identify weak support. Across synthetic CBDT, PJM, Adult Income, and Default Credit tasks, the method recovers case-level preference structure and achieves the highest mean Top-30 consistency among compared attribution baselines, while remaining competitive on support reconstruction. The audit requires only fitting an OLS top-layer probe, without retraining the representation or accessing the original optimization trajectory; probe fidelity is measured by score reconstruction.

AI.tldr: The authors show that an OLS probe on a fixed neural representation yields an exact case-based decomposition, enabling case-level audit by tracing action scores to training cases via Gram-geometric influence, with strong empirical performance.
AI.motivation: High-stakes neural network decisions (medical, credit, energy) require case-level evidence tracing which training cases support an action and their outcomes, but existing methods lack such fine-grained auditability.
AI.method: An OLS action readout fitted on fixed neural representations produces an exact case-based decomposition where action scores are weighted sums of training-case returns, with weights determined by empirical Gram geometry. The method identifies regimes for CBDT similarity semantics and provides audit signals.
AI.result: Across synthetic CBDT, PJM, Adult Income, and Default Credit tasks, the method recovers case-level preference structure, achieves highest mean Top-30 consistency among baselines, and remains competitive on support reconstruction.
AI.conclusion: Fitting an OLS top-layer probe enables case-level audit without retraining or accessing the optimization trajectory, with probe fidelity measured by score reconstruction.

title: Predictive Divergence Masks for LLM RL
summary: Reinforcement learning for large language models (LLMs) typically relies on trust-region masks to stabilize off-policy updates. The dominant PPO-style approach uses the sampled-token importance ratio for two criteria: a proximity criterion, which asks whether the policy has moved too far from the behavior policy, and a direction criterion, which asks whether the update pushes it farther away. Recent work DPPO improves the proximity criterion by replacing PPO's ratio-based test with a probability divergence between the behavior and training policies. However, its direction criterion is still inherited from PPO. A token can be masked only when the sampled-token importance ratio moves away from one. We observe that this ratio-based direction criterion is a single-sample proxy that can disagree in sign with the change of the divergence that defines the proximity criterion. We therefore propose the predictive divergence mask, which asks whether the next policy-gradient step will increase or decrease the same divergence used by the trust region. For the discrete softmax policies used in LLM RL, we derive this prediction in closed form. Because production rollout engines expose only a truncated (top-K) view of the vocabulary, we develop two lightweight top-$K$ estimators for this prediction. Detailed analysis shows the divergence-based direction is better aligned with the realized change of the divergence than the sampled ratio, and the resulting masks improve RL training across model scales and precision settings.

AI.tldr: The paper identifies a misalignment between PPO's ratio-based direction criterion and DPPO's divergence-based proximity criterion in RL for LLMs. It proposes a predictive divergence mask that uses divergence change prediction (instead of importance ratio) for the direction criterion, with closed-form derivation for softmax policies and lightweight top-K estimators for production settings.
AI.motivation: PPO-style trust-region masks use two criteria—proximity (has the policy moved too far?) and direction (is the update pushing farther away?). DPPO improved proximity with divergence but kept PPO's ratio-based direction criterion. The paper observes that this ratio-based criterion is a single-sample proxy that can disagree in sign with the actual divergence change, leading to misaligned masking decisions.
AI.method: The authors propose the predictive divergence mask: instead of using the sampled-token importance ratio to judge direction, they predict whether the next policy-gradient step will increase or decrease the same divergence used by the trust-region proximity criterion. They derive this prediction in closed form for discrete softmax policies. To handle production rollout engines that expose only top-K vocabulary, they develop two lightweight top-K estimators for the prediction.
AI.result: The divergence-based direction criterion is better aligned with the realized divergence change than the sampled ratio-based approach. The resulting masks improve RL training across different model scales and precision settings.
AI.conclusion: Replacing the ratio-based direction criterion with a divergence-based prediction (aligned with the proximity criterion) yields better masking decisions and improved RL training for LLMs, with practical top-K estimators enabling deployment in production.

title: OpsMem: Dual-Memory Reasoning with Cross-Memory Resonance for Failure Diagnosis
summary: Failure diagnosis in modern software systems requires iterative evidence acquisition and hypothesis reasoning guided by operational experience. Existing LLM-based methods improve diagnosis through agentic reasoning or knowledge augmentation, but they often lack a mechanism to coordinate the evolving diagnostic state with operational experience during iterative diagnosis. We propose OpsMem, a dual-memory framework that maintains a short-term memory for the current diagnostic state and a long-term memory for reusable operational experience. OpsMem uses cross-memory resonance to activate state-relevant long-term memory, conditions multi-agent diagnosis on the short-term and activated long-term memories, and consolidates reusable experience from solved incidents back into long-term memory. Experiments on a real-world Huawei microservice failure diagnosis dataset show that OpsMem outperforms representative agentic-reasoning and knowledge-augmented baselines, improving Match and Relevant by up to 46.88% and 18.39% over the strongest baseline, respectively.

AI.tldr: OpsMem is a dual-memory framework (short-term + long-term) for LLM-based failure diagnosis that uses cross-memory resonance to coordinate evolving diagnostic state with operational experience, achieving ~47% and ~18% improvements in Match and Relevant metrics over strong baselines on Huawei microservice data.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM-based diagnosis methods lack a mechanism to coordinate the evolving diagnostic state with operational experience during iterative diagnosis, limiting their effectiveness.
AI.method: OpsMem uses a dual-memory framework: short-term memory for current diagnostic state, long-term memory for reusable operational experience, connected via cross-memory resonance. It conditions multi-agent diagnosis on both memories and consolidates solved incidents back into long-term memory.
AI.result: Outperforms representative agentic-reasoning and knowledge-augmented baselines on Huawei microservice failure diagnosis dataset, improving Match by up to 46.88% and Relevant by up to 18.39% over the strongest baseline.
AI.conclusion: The dual-memory framework with cross-memory resonance effectively bridges the gap between dynamic diagnostic state and static operational knowledge, significantly improving failure diagnosis performance.

title: Reliability Scaling Laws for Quantized Large Language Models
summary: Quantization is a powerful strategy to build capable and resource-efficient large language models (LLMs) by reducing the bitwidth of the parameters. While quantized LLMs achieve state-of-the-art performance on unperturbed inputs using standard predictive metrics, their performance on perturbed inputs, measured using reliability metrics, remains underexplored, despite its importance for reliable deployment. To address this gap, we first conduct a comprehensive reliability evaluation of quantized LLMs consisting of three key components: (1) Uncertainty: We assess the trustworthiness of LLMs quantized to 2, 3, 4, and 8 bits using six different quantization methods, employing established uncertainty metrics. (2) Calibration: We assess how well-calibrated the uncertainty estimates of quantized models are across model scales and bit precisions. (3) Robustness: We design character-level and word-level input perturbations to evaluate the reliability of quantized models under semantically-preserving variations in the inputs that arise in real-world applications. Second, we characterize how reliability scales with the total number of model bits. Our study reveals that while the performance scales monotonically with the total number of bits, the reliability scalings are nonlinear. A reliability peak occurs for 4-bit quantized models, indicating that quantizing moderately sized models offers the best reliability-efficiency trade-off. Additionally, our empirical findings reveal that quantization enhances the robustness of LLMs to natural input perturbations.

AI.tldr: This paper evaluates the reliability (uncertainty, calibration, robustness) of quantized LLMs across bit precisions and finds that 4-bit quantization offers the best reliability-efficiency trade-off, with nonlinear reliability scaling and improved robustness to natural perturbations.
AI.motivation: While quantized LLMs achieve strong performance on unperturbed inputs, their reliability (uncertainty, calibration, robustness) on perturbed inputs is underexplored, yet crucial for trustworthy real-world deployment.
AI.method: The authors conduct a comprehensive evaluation of quantized LLMs (2, 3, 4, 8 bits, six quantization methods) using uncertainty metrics, calibration assessment across model scales, and character-/word-level input perturbations to measure robustness.
AI.result: Performance scales monotonically with total bits, but reliability scales nonlinearly — a reliability peak occurs at 4-bit quantization. Quantization also enhances robustness to natural input perturbations.
AI.conclusion: Moderately quantized (4-bit) models provide the best reliability-efficiency trade-off, and quantization can improve robustness to real-world input variations.

title: Design Choices in Splitting-Based Self-Supervised Sparse-View CT Reconstruction
comment: 12 pages (main manuscript), 9 pages (supplementary material and figures)
summary: Self-supervised data splitting has emerged as a promising paradigm for sparse-view CT reconstruction, enabling training from incomplete measurements without fully sampled ground truth. However, the influence of key design choices, including partitioning strategy, preprocessing, and inference, remains insufficiently understood. In this work, we introduce a unified framework that decomposes splitting-based reconstruction into these three components, enabling controlled comparison of existing methods and two incremental extensions: multi-partition splitting and an alternative inference strategy. Experiments on simulated LoDoPaB-CT data under independent and correlated noise, together with validation on the real-world 2DeteCT dataset, show that the optimal partitioning strategy strongly depends on the measurement noise structure. Lattice-based splitting performs favorably under independent noise, whereas angular masking is more robust under correlated noise and real measured data. Multi-partition splitting consistently improves over pure projection-wise splitting in several settings. Complementary perceptual and structural metrics, including LPIPS and HaarPSI, reveal differences between masking strategies that are less apparent from PSNR and SSIM alone. These results provide practical guidelines for designing self-supervised sparse-view CT reconstruction methods and highlight the limitations of common independence assumptions in realistic imaging environments.

AI.tldr: This paper systematically analyzes design choices (partitioning, preprocessing, inference) in self-supervised data splitting for sparse-view CT reconstruction, showing that optimal partitioning depends on noise structure—lattice splitting works best for independent noise, angular masking for correlated noise—and that multi-partition splitting and broader metrics (LPIPS, HaarPSI) reveal differences hidden by traditional metrics.
AI.motivation: The influence of key design choices (partitioning strategy, preprocessing, inference) in self-supervised data splitting for sparse-view CT reconstruction remains insufficiently understood, hindering optimal method selection.
AI.method: Introduces a unified framework decomposing splitting-based reconstruction into three components (partitioning, preprocessing, inference), enabling controlled comparison. Proposes two extensions: multi-partition splitting and an alternative inference strategy. Evaluated on simulated LoDoPaB-CT data (independent & correlated noise) and real-world 2DeteCT dataset.
AI.result: Optimal partitioning depends on noise structure: lattice-based splitting excels under independent noise, angular masking under correlated/real data. Multi-partition splitting consistently improves over pure projection-wise splitting. LPIPS and HaarPSI reveal differences between masking strategies not apparent from PSNR/SSIM alone.
AI.conclusion: Provides practical guidelines for designing self-supervised sparse-view CT methods and highlights limitations of common independence assumptions in realistic imaging environments.

title: StructAgent: Harness Long-horizon Digital Agents with Unified Causal Structure
summary: Recent advances in large language models (LLMs) and vision-language models (VLMs) have enabled increasingly capable digital agents for computer use. However, real-world tasks are often long-horizon and involve evolving contexts containing accumulated observations, intermediate edits, failed attempts, and partially completed executions. Existing agents typically operate over raw interaction history, making task progress difficult to interpret, verify, and recover, which ultimately limits reliable long-horizon execution. In this paper, we argue that addressing this challenge requires explicitly structuring both the agent's state and workflow around a unified causal representation of task progress. We present \textbf{StructAgent}, a state-centered framework that introduces a unified state for maintaining compact, verifiable task progress and a structured workflow that regulates progress through verifier-backed state transitions. Building on this design, StructAgent further enables explicit progress checkpointing, evidence-driven task completion, targeted failure recovery, and tool-supported execution, while ensuring that all progress updates remain grounded in verification. Extensive experiments demonstrate that StructAgent consistently improves a wide range of LLM and VLM backbones on long-horizon computer-use tasks. On OSWorld-Verified, it improves Qwen3.5-9B from 27.0\% to 46.9\% success rate and Qwen3.5-27B from 31.6\% to 62.2\%, while achieving a new open-source state of the art of 78.9\% with MiniMax-M3. Moreover, the same framework generalizes beyond desktop environments to Minecraft, demonstrating the generality of our design.

AI.tldr: StructAgent introduces a state-centered framework that structures agent state and workflow around a unified causal representation of task progress, achieving significant improvements on long-horizon computer-use tasks (e.g., boosting Qwen3.5-9B from 27.0% to 46.9% on OSWorld-Verified) and generalizing to Minecraft environments.
AI.motivation: Real-world tasks are long-horizon with evolving contexts (accumulated observations, intermediate edits, failed attempts), and existing agents operating over raw interaction history find it difficult to interpret, verify, and recover task progress, limiting reliable long-horizon execution.
AI.method: StructAgent is a state-centered framework with (1) a unified state for maintaining compact, verifiable task progress, and (2) a structured workflow that regulates progress through verifier-backed state transitions, enabling explicit progress checkpointing, evidence-driven task completion, targeted failure recovery, and tool-supported execution with verification-grounded updates.
AI.result: On OSWorld-Verified, StructAgent improves Qwen3.5-9B from 27.0% to 46.9%, Qwen3.5-27B from 31.6% to 62.2%, and achieves a new open-source SOTA of 78.9% with MiniMax-M3. The framework also generalizes to Minecraft environments.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly structuring agent state and workflow around a unified causal representation of task progress consistently improves LLM/VLM backbones on long-horizon computer-use tasks, and the design generalizes beyond desktop environments.

title: Singular perturbations and hierarchical learning in two-layer neural networks
summary: We study the population gradient flow of an infinitely wide two-layer neural network learning a misspecified single-index model in high dimension. The two layers are optimized jointly, with a perturbative parameter tuning the relative training speed between the first and second layer. This setting was considered by Berthier, Montanari and Zhou in \cite{berthier2024learning}, who conjectured a hierarchical learning scenario with explicit timescales as the second layer is trained faster than the first. In this paper, we prove that the constant and linear components of the hidden link function are indeed recovered within the predicted timescales, at sharp explicit thresholds. We then analyze the onset of learning of the quadratic component and show that the components learned at earlier stages continue to influence the dynamics in an essential way. Our proof is based on quantitative approximation results for singularly perturbed flows evolving near a manifold defined by integral constraints. At a phenomenological level, we also show that the empirical measure of the weights displays singular behaviour when reaching the quadratic component of the hidden link, with a small fraction of neurons growing significantly while the remaining ones rearrange to preserve the components already learned.

AI.tldr: This paper provides rigorous proof for the hierarchical learning timescales conjectured by Berthier, Montanari, and Zhou in two-layer neural networks with joint training. It shows that constant and linear components of a misspecified single-index model are recovered at sharp thresholds, then studies the onset of quadratic component learning, using singular perturbation theory and revealing singular behavior in the weight empirical measure.
AI.motivation: To rigorously prove the conjectured hierarchical learning scenario and explicit timescales in two-layer neural networks learning a misspecified single-index model in high dimension, where the relative training speed between layers controls the learning dynamics.
AI.method: Uses quantitative approximation results for singularly perturbed flows evolving near a manifold defined by integral constraints. The analysis focuses on the population gradient flow of an infinitely wide two-layer neural network with a perturbative parameter controlling the relative training speed between layers.
AI.result: Proves that constant and linear components of the hidden link function are recovered within predicted timescales at sharp explicit thresholds. Shows that components learned at earlier stages continue to influence later dynamics. Demonstrates singular behavior in the empirical measure of weights when reaching the quadratic component.
AI.conclusion: The paper confirms and proves the hierarchical learning scenario with explicit timescales, providing rigorous mathematical foundations for understanding how two-layer neural networks learn different components of the target function in a structured, sequential manner depending on relative training speeds.

title: DP-Splat: Bayesian Nonparametric Complexity Control for Gaussian Splatting
comment: 18 pages, 8 figures. Code and experiment records: https://github.com/archiedong/dp-splat
summary: 3D Gaussian Splatting represents scenes as finite mixtures of anisotropic Gaussians whose number of components $K$ is set by heuristic density control or user caps. Variational Bayes Gaussian Splatting (VBGS) recast splat fitting as conjugate variational inference, but $K$ remains fixed. We replace the finite symmetric Dirichlet over mixture weights with a truncated stick-breaking Dirichlet-process prior -- and, as a theory-backed alternative, a sparse overfitted finite Dirichlet -- so that the number of occupied components adapts to the data while every update remains a closed-form coordinate-ascent step; a natural-gradient stochastic variant makes the per-step cost independent of the number of points. We give an exact monotonicity guarantee, a rigorous truncation-error bound correcting an anti-conservative large-$α$ approximation in common use, and an honest account of what the fitted number of components estimates. Empirically: (i) the effective complexity $\hat{K}$ adapts to scene complexity and recovers the true $K$ within $\pm 1$ on well-separated synthetic data with regime-appropriate concentration; (ii) a deconfounded comparison shows the DP prior's contribution is complexity selection, not per-component efficiency -- converged DP fits exceed single-pass fixed-$K$ VBGS by +2.7 dB at matched budgets yet tie an equally converged fixed-$K$ baseline, and on 3D scenes DP-Splat matches or exceeds VBGS's held-out color prediction with 5.9-7.6x fewer components; (iii) the posterior-predictive color variance is well calibrated on model-matched synthetic data; and (iv) the ordering suggested by exact-posterior asymptotics reverses under mean-field coordinate ascent: the DP prior resists over-splitting while the sparse finite mixture saturates its truncation, a gap between variational practice and posterior asymptotics documented across three orders of magnitude in $N$.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes replacing the fixed number of Gaussians in 3D Gaussian Splatting with a Dirichlet Process (stick-breaking) prior or sparse overfitted finite Dirichlet prior, enabling automatic adaptation of component count. The method maintains closed-form coordinate-ascent updates, provides theoretical guarantees (monotonicity, truncation-error bounds), and empirically shows DP-Splat matches/exceeds VBGS with 5.9-7.6x fewer components while achieving +2.7 dB gain over fixed-K baselines.
AI.motivation: Current 3D Gaussian Splatting methods require heuristic density control or user-specified component counts K, which is suboptimal - fixing K either wastes resources on unnecessary components or lacks capacity for complex scenes. The authors aim to automate complexity selection while maintaining computational tractability.
AI.method: Replaces the finite symmetric Dirichlet prior over mixture weights with a truncated stick-breaking Dirichlet process prior (and an overfitted finite Dirichlet alternative). All updates remain closed-form coordinate-ascent steps. A natural-gradient stochastic variant makes per-step cost independent of point count. Provides theoretical guarantees including exact monotonicity and rigorous truncation-error bounds.
AI.result: (i) Effective complexity K̂ adapts to scene complexity, recovering true K within ±1 on synthetic data. (ii) DP prior's contribution is complexity selection, not per-component efficiency - converged DP fits exceed single-pass fixed-K VBGS by +2.7 dB but tie converged fixed-K baseline. DP-Splat matches/exceeds VBGS with 5.9-7.6x fewer components. (iii) Posterior-predictive color variance is well-calibrated. (iv) DP prior resists over-splitting while sparse finite mixture saturates truncation, revealing a gap between variational practice and posterior asymptotics.
AI.conclusion: The Dirichlet Process prior effectively automates component count selection in 3D Gaussian Splatting while maintaining closed-form updates and theoretical guarantees. The work also reveals important discrepancies between variational practice and asymptotic theory regarding complexity control.

title: Omni-Decision: A Progressive Evidence-State Agent System for Omni-Modal QA
summary: Omni-modal evidence-seeking QA requires agents to answer questions whose evidence is sparsely distributed across videos, audio, images, web pages, and computation results. Existing agentic multimodal systems often leave evidence in scratchpads, tool trajectories, or free-form histories, making it difficult to track what has been grounded, what remains missing, and when the evidence is sufficient to answer. We propose Omni-Decision, a training-free evidence-state system that turns omni-modal QA into a query-scoped evidence-closure process. For each query, Omni-Decision maintains a structured evidence state containing confirmed evidence, unresolved conflicts, fact and computation dependencies, and open evidence needs. A shared state view conditions planning, evidence acquisition, validation, repair, and finalization. Heterogeneous observations from media, web, computation, and verification modules are normalized, judged, and committed through deterministic state updates. This design enables targeted evidence acquisition, preserves sparse cross-modal cues, and provides inspectable control over repair and stopping. Omni-Decision achieves 45.6% accuracy on OmniGAIA and 58.3% on WorldSense, improving over the baselines by +27.3 and +30.2 percentage points, respectively. No-state ablations and trajectory audits further support the role of explicit evidence-state control in multi-step omni-modal evidence seeking.

AI.tldr: Omni-Decision is a training-free system that uses a structured evidence state to track confirmed facts, conflicts, dependencies, and gaps, enabling targeted evidence-seeking across omni-modal inputs (video, audio, images, web, computation). It achieves 45.6% on OmniGAIA and 58.3% on WorldSense, outperforming baselines by +27.3 and +30.2 percentage points.
AI.motivation: Existing agentic multimodal systems leave evidence scattered across scratchpads, tool trajectories, or free-form histories, making it hard to track what has been grounded and when evidence is sufficient. This lack of structured state hinders effective omni-modal evidence-seeking QA.
AI.method: Omni-Decision maintains a structured evidence state with confirmed evidence, unresolved conflicts, fact/computation dependencies, and open evidence needs. A shared state view conditions planning, acquisition, validation, repair, and finalization. Heterogeneous observations are normalized, judged, and committed via deterministic state updates.
AI.result: Achieves 45.6% accuracy on OmniGAIA and 58.3% on WorldSense, improving over baselines by +27.3 and +30.2 percentage points respectively. Ablation studies confirm the importance of explicit evidence-state control.
AI.conclusion: Explicit evidence-state control significantly improves multi-step omni-modal evidence seeking by enabling targeted acquisition, preserving sparse cross-modal cues, and providing inspectable control over repair and stopping decisions.

title: Infrared Organization and Critical Cognitive Field Formation in Transformer Dynamics
summary: Large language models exhibit remarkable emergent behaviors, yet the physical mechanism governing their collective dynamics remains poorly understood. Cognitive Field Theory predicts that learning reorganizes the time-scale density of states (TDOS) through the infrared accumulation of slow relaxation modes, thereby enhancing the memory self-energy, reducing the cognitive forgetting gap, and strengthening the collective susceptibility. Using publicly available Pythia language models, we extract relaxation spectra directly from Transformer layer Jacobians throughout training, network depth, and model scale, allowing the TDOS, memory self-energy, forgetting gap, memory kernel, and infrared critical exponent to be measured quantitatively. The measurements reveal progressive infrared accumulation of slow relaxation modes, producing an approximately flat infrared TDOS with \( ρ(λ)\simλ^{-0.1} \) and scale-free memory kernels \( K(t)\sim t^{-1}. \) The memory self-energy exhibits a pronounced transient maximum during early optimization before relaxing toward a metastable near-critical regime, corresponding to the smallest cognitive forgetting gap and the largest collective susceptibility predicted by Cognitive Field Theory. These observations provide quantitative experimental evidence that Transformer dynamics are governed by infrared collective organization. The reproducibility of the same dynamical behavior across training, network depth, and model scales suggests that infrared slow-mode organization represents a universal collective principle of Transformer dynamics.

AI.tldr: This paper provides quantitative experimental evidence that Transformer language models exhibit infrared collective organization of slow relaxation modes, consistent with Cognitive Field Theory predictions, revealing universal dynamical principles across training, depth, and scale.
AI.motivation: The physical mechanism governing the collective dynamics of large language models remains poorly understood, despite their remarkable emergent behaviors. This work aims to bridge this gap by empirically testing Cognitive Field Theory predictions on real transformer models.
AI.method: Using publicly available Pythia language models, the authors extract relaxation spectra from Transformer layer Jacobians across training stages, network depth, and model scales. They quantitatively measure TDOS, memory self-energy, forgetting gap, memory kernel, and infrared critical exponent.
AI.result: Progressive infrared accumulation of slow relaxation modes is observed, yielding approximately flat infrared TDOS (~λ^{-0.1}) and scale-free memory kernels (~t^{-1}). A transient maximum in memory self-energy appears during early optimization, followed by relaxation to a near-critical regime with minimal forgetting gap and maximal collective susceptibility.
AI.conclusion: Transformer dynamics are governed by infrared collective organization, and the reproducibility of this behavior across training, depth, and scale suggests infrared slow-mode organization is a universal collective principle of Transformer dynamics.

title: Unsupervised Detection of Entry and Exit Regions from Vehicle Trajectories for Camera-Agnostic Turning Movement Counts
comment: 14 pages, 7 figures; Supplementary Material: 7 pages, 3 figures
summary: Turning movement counts are essential for intersection-level traffic management, yet their collection remains predominantly manual due to the cost of per-camera region annotation. This paper presents an unsupervised pipeline that identifies entry and exit regions directly from raw vehicle trajectories extracted via object detection and multi-object tracking, requiring no manual annotation, camera calibration, or prior knowledge of intersection geometry. Unlike trajectory clustering methods that classify individual trajectories using pairwise similarity and must be re-executed on every new batch, the proposed pipeline clusters initial and terminal point locations to produce persistent spatial region polygons that classify future trajectories by point-in-polygon containment at linear cost. The pipeline comprises six sequential steps, each with configurable parameters evaluated through a systematic statistical analysis spanning 19,152 pipeline executions across 25 surveillance cameras capturing dense heterogeneous traffic in Bengaluru, India, and 10 sequences from the UA-DETRAC benchmark dataset. Both parametric and nonparametric testing frameworks identify three consistently significant parameters and yield an empirically grounded recommended configuration. Under this configuration, the pipeline achieves a median classification error of approximately 3% across all 25 cameras, including 16 held-out locations, with GEH values within accepted engineering thresholds. Compared with two trajectory clustering baselines, the proposed pipeline exhibits greater stability across camera views and lower computational cost, at the expense of higher median error. Extended evaluation demonstrates that calibration clips of at least 60 minutes and peak-traffic selection further improve region estimation quality.

AI.tldr: This paper presents an unsupervised pipeline that automatically identifies entry/exit regions at intersections from raw vehicle trajectories, requiring no manual annotation or camera calibration, achieving ~3% median classification error across 25 cameras.
AI.motivation: Turning movement counts are essential for traffic management but remain manually collected due to high cost of per-camera region annotation. Existing methods require manual labeling, camera calibration, or costly re-execution on new data batches.
AI.method: An unsupervised 6-step pipeline that clusters initial and terminal vehicle trajectory points to create persistent spatial region polygons, then classifies new trajectories by point-in-polygon containment at linear cost. Evaluated via 19,152 pipeline executions with statistical parameter analysis.
AI.result: ~3% median classification error across 25 cameras (including 16 held-out), GEH values within engineering thresholds. More stable and computationally cheaper than trajectory clustering baselines, but with higher median error. Calibration clips ≥60 min and peak-traffic selection improve results.
AI.conclusion: The proposed pipeline offers a practical, zero-annotation solution for turning movement counting, with identified significant parameters and recommended configuration for real-world deployment.

title: The Ebb and Flow of Multimodal Focus: Scheduling Visual Relay Windows for Grounded VLM Reasoning
summary: Vision-language models increasingly succeed on multimodal reasoning benchmarks, yet their visual evidence often becomes unstable once it enters the language stack, weakening evidence-grounded reasoning. To understand this fragility, we examine the internal dynamics of VLMs through a mechanistic lens and uncover a stable three-stage redistribution of multimodal attention focus across depth: an early question-conditioned organization, a critical middle visual-dominant relay, and a late return to answer formation. We operationalize the middle phase as the Visual Relay Window (VRW), and show that its geometry varies with task demand, is causally tied to grounded generation, and distinguishes unsupported answers from stronger reasoning trajectories. Guided by this internal rhythm, we propose TRACE, a task-adaptive inference-time control framework with lightweight trained modules. It reshapes relay allocation during prefill and preserves assembled visual support after handoff during decoding. Across four open-weight VLM backbones and seven benchmarks, TRACE delivers large gains on grounding-sensitive settings, improving them by 4.33 points on average and by up to 6.6 points, while also improving reasoning-heavy tasks. These results show that explicitly controlling multimodal focus across depth offers a unified and effective mechanism for strengthening evidence-grounded multimodal reasoning.

AI.tldr: VLMs exhibit a three-stage attention redistribution across depth; the middle "Visual Relay Window" is critical for grounded reasoning. TRACE, a task-adaptive control framework, reshapes this relay to improve multimodal reasoning by up to 6.6 points.
AI.motivation: VLMs often lose visual evidence stability once it enters the language stack, weakening evidence-grounded reasoning. The authors aim to understand and fix this fragility.
AI.method: Mechanistic analysis reveals a three-stage attention pattern: early question-conditioned, middle visual-dominant relay (VRW), late answer formation. TRACE uses lightweight trained modules for task-adaptive inference-time control, reshaping relay during prefill and preserving visual support during decoding.
AI.result: Across 4 open-weight VLM backbones and 7 benchmarks, TRACE improves grounding-sensitive settings by 4.33 points on average and up to 6.6 points, while also improving reasoning-heavy tasks.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly controlling multimodal focus across depth provides a unified and effective mechanism for strengthening evidence-grounded multimodal reasoning.

title: The Spectral Structure of Latent Treatment Effects
summary: Identifying heterogeneous treatment effects under unobserved confounding is central in observational causal inference. In proxy models with a discrete latent confounder, prior Synthetic Potential Outcomes (SPO) [Mazaheri-Squires-Uhler '25] recover the mixture of treatment effects through recursively constructed scalar moments. We show that this sequence is one projection of a more fundamental object. Under the same population factorization assumptions, there is an exact compressed observable operator: after projecting onto the shared proxy signal subspace, the difference of two treatment-arm quotient operators is similar to the diagonal matrix of latent treatment effects. Its eigenvalues are the latent effects; its lifted left eigenvectors, after anchor normalization, recover the target-proxy feature matrix and then the latent mixture proportions. Every scalar SPO moment is a bilinear functional of a power of this operator. The resulting estimator handles overcomplete proxy systems, replaces high-order scalar inversion with finite-dimensional spectral analysis, and admits high-probability first-order perturbation bounds for treatment effects, feature rows, and simplex-projected mixture weights.

AI.tldr: The paper extends Synthetic Potential Outcomes (SPO) by showing that treatment effects under unobserved confounding can be recovered via a spectral operator defined on proxy variables, replacing high-order scalar moments with finite-dimensional eigenvalue analysis.
AI.motivation: To identify heterogeneous treatment effects under unobserved confounding in causal inference, particularly for proxy models with discrete latent confounders, without relying on high-order scalar moment inversion.
AI.method: Defines a compressed observable operator by projecting onto the shared proxy signal subspace; the difference of two treatment-arm quotient operators is similar to a diagonal matrix of latent treatment effects. Eigenvalues recover treatment effects; lifted left eigenvectors with anchor normalization recover feature matrix and mixture proportions.
AI.result: The estimator handles overcomplete proxy systems, replaces high-order scalar inversion with finite-dimensional spectral analysis, and admits high-probability first-order perturbation bounds for treatment effects, feature rows, and simplex-projected mixture weights.
AI.conclusion: The proposed spectral approach provides a more fundamental and computationally robust framework than prior SPO methods for heterogeneous treatment effect recovery under unobserved confounding.

title: Learning Anatomy-Grounded CT Vision-Language Representations with Organ-Hierarchical Report Knowledge
summary: Medical vision-language pretraining (VLP) from paired CT images and radiology reports enables scalable representation learning, but most existing methods align either whole scans with entire reports or local image regions with text fragments. These formulations underuse a key property of radiology reports: findings are organized around anatomical structures, with abnormalities described by organs, disease concepts, locations, and severity-related attributes. We propose OKA-CT, an organ-hierarchical knowledge-augmented framework for CT-report VLP. OKA-CT first converts free-text reports into organ-conditioned knowledge using radiology report parsing and LLM-assisted semantic structuring. The extracted hierarchy is used across two learning stages. Stage~1 injects anatomy-grounded evidence into the CT visual representation through fine-grained organ-conditioned supervision, while Stage~2 uses organ-specific report evidence to guide structured report-CT contrastive learning, where hierarchy-derived semantic soft targets treat non-paired cases with shared organ-level findings as weak semantic positives rather than uniform negatives. A lightweight query-based global branch further aggregates disease-relevant volumetric evidence for whole-scan representation. On CT-RATE and RAD-ChestCT datasets, OKA-CT achieves zero-shot abnormality diagnosis AUROCs of 84.9 and 72.2, outperforming prior CT VLP baselines. Retrieval and patch-occlusion analyses further show improved report-image alignment and stronger sensitivity to disease-associated anatomical regions.

AI.tldr: OKA-CT proposes an organ-hierarchical knowledge-augmented framework for CT-report vision-language pretraining, using parsed radiology reports to create organ-conditioned learning stages that achieve state-of-the-art zero-shot abnormality diagnosis on CT-RATE and RAD-ChestCT datasets.
AI.motivation: Existing CT VLP methods underuse the organ-structured nature of radiology reports, where findings are organized around anatomical structures with disease descriptions tied to specific organs, locations, and severity attributes.
AI.method: OKA-CT converts free-text reports into organ-conditioned knowledge via radiology report parsing and LLM-assisted semantic structuring, then uses two learning stages: Stage 1 injects anatomy-grounded evidence into CT visual representations via organ-conditioned supervision; Stage 2 uses organ-specific report evidence for structured report-CT contrastive learning with hierarchy-derived semantic soft targets that treat non-paired cases with shared organ findings as weak positives rather than uniform negatives.
AI.result: OKA-CT achieves zero-shot abnormality diagnosis AUROCs of 84.9 and 72.2 on CT-RATE and RAD-ChestCT datasets, outperforming prior CT VLP baselines with improved report-image alignment and stronger sensitivity to disease-associated anatomical regions.
AI.conclusion: Leveraging organ-level hierarchical knowledge from radiology reports significantly improves CT-report understanding and zero-shot diagnosis, suggesting this anatomy-aware approach is more effective than conventional whole-scan or local-region alignment methods.

title: Enhancing Query Efficiency for d-DNNF Representations Through Preprocessing
summary: In this paper, we investigate preprocessing techniques aimed at improving the efficiency of accessing models of propositional formulas represented in conjunctive normal form (CNF). We focus on three fundamental tasks: uniform sampling, direct model access, and model enumeration. Our analysis reveals that most state-of-the-art preprocessors, when they do not preserve formula equivalence, are generally unsuitable for these tasks. In contrast, we demonstrate that preprocessors which preserve model counts can be effectively leveraged, provided relevant preprocessing information is maintained. To validate our approach, we perform extensive experiments on a diverse suite of benchmarks from multiple domains. The experimental results show that our preprocessing methods are both efficient and robust, yielding significant performance improvements for model access queries when CNF formulas are compiled into d-DNNF representations.

AI.tldr: This paper studies preprocessing techniques for CNF formulas to improve efficiency in uniform sampling, direct model access, and model enumeration. It finds that model-count-preserving preprocessors are effective when preprocessing information is retained, leading to significant performance gains when compiling CNF into d-DNNF.
AI.motivation: To improve the efficiency of accessing models of propositional formulas in CNF for three key tasks: uniform sampling, direct model access, and model enumeration.
AI.method: The authors analyze state-of-the-art preprocessors, identifying that model-count-preserving preprocessors can be effectively used if relevant preprocessing information is maintained. They validate their approach through extensive experiments on diverse benchmarks.
AI.result: Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed preprocessing methods are efficient and robust, achieving significant performance improvements for model access queries when CNF formulas are compiled into d-DNNF representations.
AI.conclusion: Model-count-preserving preprocessors, when used with retained preprocessing information, are effective for model access tasks, unlike most state-of-the-art preprocessors that do not preserve formula equivalence.

title: EquiFusion: Kinematics-Agnostic Human Motion Prediction via Equivariant Latent Diffusion
comment: Accepted to ECCV 2026. Visit our webpage at https://ceveloper.github.io/publications/equifusion/
summary: Existing Stochastic 3D Human Motion Prediction models are fundamentally constrained by hard-coding the skeleton kinematics, severely limiting generalization, preventing cross-dataset training, and requiring complex data retargeting. We introduce EquiFusion, the first kinematics-agnostic model to solve this bottleneck, implementing a latent diffusion model with a permutation equivariant architecture. EquiFusion treats the kinematics' connectivity as an explicit input parameter, ensuring its internal computations are inherently agnostic to joint ordering and graph structure. This novel design enables truly cross-dataset generalization to unseen kinematics and unlocks novel zero-shot directions, such as motion prediction from partial or occluded observations and targeted limb generation. EquiFusion achieves state-of-the-art results on major benchmarks, being up to 75% more compact than previous kinematics-specific methods, while achieving faster training and inference. EquiFusion thus establishes a new, flexible standard for robust human motion prediction. Model and training code are available at https://ceveloper.github.io/publications/equifusion/.

AI.tldr: EquiFusion introduces a kinematics-agnostic, permutation-equivariant latent diffusion model for stochastic 3D human motion prediction that generalizes across unseen skeleton structures, enables zero-shot partial/occluded motion prediction, and achieves SOTA results with up to 75% smaller model size and faster training/inference.
AI.motivation: Existing models are constrained by hard-coded skeleton kinematics, limiting generalization across datasets, preventing cross-dataset training, and requiring complex data retargeting.
AI.method: A kinematics-agnostic latent diffusion model with a permutation equivariant architecture that treats kinematic connectivity as an explicit input parameter, making computations agnostic to joint ordering and graph structure.
AI.result: State-of-the-art results on major benchmarks; up to 75% more compact than prior kinematics-specific models; faster training and inference; enables zero-shot motion prediction from partial/occluded observations and targeted limb generation.
AI.conclusion: EquiFusion establishes a new flexible standard for robust human motion prediction, overcoming the fundamental bottleneck of kinematic hard-coding.

title: Comparative Analysis of GAT and BERT for Human-Like Playtesting
summary: Accurately modeling and understanding player experience is crucial for designing engaging puzzle games. To achieve this, a common approach involves collecting diverse user data to train predictive playtesting models that mimic player behavior. However, existing data-driven methods often lack the ability to capture the full range of player strategies and require extensive feature engineering and network architecture modeling. This limitation becomes particularly evident when new game mechanics or features are introduced, which necessitate continual adjustments to the models. To addrss these challenges, we propose a more generalized representation that reduces - or even eliminates - the need for ongoing feature-engineering maintenance. Specifically, we investigate two general-purpose network architectures: (a) a transformer-based model (BERT) and (b) a graph attention model (GAT), both of which are designed to effectively capture the relational structure of Candy Crush Saga (CCS) game boards. Our experiments compare these approaches to Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) baselines, revealing better performance on challenging board configurations and underscoring the benefits of our generalizable representation.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes transformer (BERT) and graph attention (GAT) models to replace CNNs for modeling player experience in Candy Crush Saga, reducing the need for feature engineering and better generalizing to new game mechanics.
AI.motivation: Existing data-driven methods for modeling player experience in puzzle games struggle to capture diverse player strategies, require extensive feature engineering, and need constant adjustments when new game mechanics are introduced.
AI.method: The authors investigate two general-purpose network architectures—a transformer-based model (BERT) and a graph attention model (GAT)—designed to capture the relational structure of Candy Crush Saga game boards, and compare them against CNN baselines.
AI.result: The proposed BERT and GAT models outperform CNN baselines, particularly on challenging board configurations, demonstrating better performance and generalization.
AI.conclusion: General-purpose architectures like transformers and graph attention networks can effectively reduce or eliminate the need for ongoing feature engineering in predictive playtesting models for puzzle games.

title: Event-RGB Adaptive Tracking for Nighttime Highway Perception
summary: Intelligent Transportation Systems deployed on highways predominantly rely on conventional RGB cameras for traffic perception and vehicle tracking. However, highway environments present unique challenges: the absence of artificial lighting infrastructure, combined with high vehicle velocities, results in severely degraded perception performance under low-light conditions. Specifically, nighttime scenarios suffer from motion blur, insufficient exposure, and poor signal-to-noise ratios, which catastrophically impair the reliability of RGB-based sensing systems. To address these limitations, we propose a novel Joint Event-RGB Adaptive Tracking (JEAT) framework. Unlike existing multi-sensor trackers constrained by rigid, hard-coded prioritization, JEAT merges asynchronous event streams and RGB frames into a unified joint data association optimization. By employing an Adaptive Extended Kalman Filter to continuously estimate measurement noise via NIS statistics, the framework dynamically weights and fuses both modalities, optimally harnessing event streams during dark or high-speed motion while leveraging RGB frames under bright or static conditions. Furthermore, given the absence of publicly available datasets tailored for event-based highway perception with diverse environmental conditions, we present SEHN, a large-scale synthetic dataset generated using the CARLA simulator. Our dataset encompasses diverse environmental conditions (daytime, nighttime, nighttime with out artificial lighting) and varying traffic densities, providing synchronized RGB imagery and event streams to facilitate multi-modal fusion research. Our code and datasets will be available at https://github.com/haidongwang96/SEHN.

AI.tldr: Proposes JEAT, a joint event-RGB adaptive tracking framework for highway perception that dynamically fuses event streams and RGB frames via an Adaptive Extended Kalman Filter, plus SEHN, a synthetic CARLA-based dataset for event-based highway perception research.
AI.motivation: RGB cameras fail in highway nighttime conditions due to motion blur, insufficient exposure, and poor signal-to-noise ratios. Existing multi-sensor trackers use rigid, hard-coded prioritization that cannot adapt to varying lighting and motion conditions.
AI.method: JEAT merges asynchronous event streams and RGB frames into a unified joint data association optimization, using an Adaptive Extended Kalman Filter with NIS statistics to dynamically estimate measurement noise and weight/fuse both modalities adaptively.
AI.result: The SEHN dataset is introduced as a large-scale synthetic benchmark with diverse environmental conditions (daytime, nighttime, with/without artificial lighting) and varying traffic densities, providing synchronized RGB and event data.
AI.conclusion: The JEAT framework addresses RGB limitations in highway nighttime perception by adaptively fusing event and RGB data; the SEHN dataset fills a gap for event-based highway multi-modal fusion research.

title: Bandit PCA with Minimax Optimal Regret
summary: We study the bandit-feedback version of online principal component analysis (Bandit PCA): in each round $t = 1,\dots,T$, the adversary selects a $d \times d$ symmetric gain matrix $G_t$ with spectrum in $[0,1]$ and rank at most $r$; the learner simultaneously selects a unit vector $w_t \in S^{d-1}$ and receives the reward $w_t^\top G_t w_t$. The learner receives no other feedback, and aims to minimize the regret against the best unit vector in hindsight. This problem was introduced by Kotlowski and Neu (2019), who gave an algorithm with regret $O(d\sqrt{rT \log T})$ and showed the lower bound of $Ω(r\sqrt{T/\log T})$. We improve upon both of these bounds and essentially bridge the gap between them, establishing the minimax regret of order $r\sqrt{dT}$ up to polylogarithmic factors in $d$ and $T$. The upper bound is attained by a novel algorithm, which combines online mirror descent on the spectrahedron of (real) density matrices with a multiscale exploration scheme in which the eigenspaces with different spectral magnitudes are updated at different rates. For the lower bound, we construct an adaptive adversary that refines a hidden large-reward subspace based on the learner's actions, in such a way that low regret is impossible without estimating the subspace; as a result, lower-bounding the regret reduces to studying the arising subspace estimation problem. Finally, we discuss connections of Bandit PCA with adaptive-measurement quantum tomography.

AI.tldr: This paper closes the gap between upper and lower bounds for Bandit PCA, establishing the minimax regret of order r√dT (up to polylog factors) via a novel algorithm combining online mirror descent on density matrices with multiscale exploration, and a matching lower bound via an adaptive adversary that reduces regret minimization to subspace estimation.
AI.motivation: The authors aim to resolve the open problem of closing the gap between existing upper bound O(d√(rT log T)) and lower bound Ω(r√(T/log T)) for Bandit PCA, which was introduced by Kotlowski and Neu (2019).
AI.method: The upper bound is achieved by a novel algorithm that combines online mirror descent on the spectrahedron of density matrices with a multiscale exploration scheme, where eigenspaces with different spectral magnitudes are updated at different rates. The lower bound is constructed via an adaptive adversary that hides a large-reward subspace and forces regret minimization to reduce to a subspace estimation problem.
AI.result: The minimax regret for Bandit PCA is established as Θ(r√(dT)) up to polylogarithmic factors, bridging the gap between previous upper and lower bounds.
AI.conclusion: The paper essentially settles the minimax regret for Bandit PCA and discusses connections to adaptive-measurement quantum tomography.

title: MED-DSLC: Multi-Expert-Domain Classification via Domain Supervision and Logit Calibration
comment: Accepted to ECCV 2026. Code is available at https://github.com/Leonard-Zeng/MED-DSLC
summary: Vision-language models (VLMs) such as CLIP enable zero-shot classification by comparing image features with text prompts in a shared embedding space. A fundamental property underlying this capability is the global comparability of logits across arbitrary candidate classes. However, VLMs are often adapted to fine-grained domains using techniques such as LoRA. While this improves in-domain accuracy, out-of-domain accuracy degrades. This leads to a highly fragmented model ecosystem, with thousands of specialized models. Multi-Expert-Domain classification seeks to address this problem, by merging LoRAs trained independently on specialized domains. However, due to the independent training, the various domain experts no longer produce globally calibrated logits. As a result, when evaluating over the union of multiple domain-specific class sets, heterogeneous logit scales induce cross-domain interference and artificially high confidence for out-of-domain classes, inducing prediction errors. In this work, we identify domain supervision and cross-domain logit miscalibration as the key issue to scalable multi-domain zero-shot recognition. We propose MED-DSLC, combining domain supervised training and domain-wise logit scaling, to explicitly restore global logit comparability. MED-DSLC is a lightweight solution for MED classification, which is shown to preserve within-domain discrimination while reducing cross-domain logit interference with minimal data. Extensive experiments across diverse fine-grained benchmarks demonstrate that it substantially improves mean accuracy (+15\%), cross-domain robustness, and scalability in the size of MED classification problem. Our results show that restoring output-level calibration is essential under highly data imbalanced settings for achieving a truly zero-shot VLM under multi-domain specialization.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes MED-DSLC, a method combining domain supervised training and domain-wise logit scaling to restore global logit comparability when merging multiple LoRA experts for multi-domain zero-shot classification, achieving +15% mean accuracy improvement.
AI.motivation: When multiple LoRA experts trained independently on specialized domains are merged for multi-domain classification, their logits become miscalibrated across domains, causing cross-domain interference and inflated confidence for out-of-domain classes. This undermines the global comparability that VLMs like CLIP originally possess.
AI.method: MED-DSLC combines 1) domain supervised training to align features with domain identity, and 2) domain-wise logit scaling to calibrate output scores across different domain experts, restoring global logit comparability with minimal data.
AI.result: Experiments on diverse fine-grained benchmarks show MED-DSLC substantially improves mean accuracy by +15%, enhances cross-domain robustness, and scales well with increasing number of domains, especially under data-imbalanced settings.
AI.conclusion: Restoring output-level calibration is critical for achieving truly zero-shot multi-domain VLM classification when merging specialized LoRA experts, and MED-DSLC offers a lightweight and effective solution.

title: Learning Residual Kinematic Corrections for Continuous Neural Decoding via Reinforcement Learning
summary: Decoding continuous three-dimensional (3D) motor imagery (MI) using non-invasive electroencephalography (EEG)-based brain--computer interfaces (BCIs) remains challenging due to signal variability and residual decoding errors. Deep learning architectures such as convolutional neural network--long short-term memory (CNN--LSTM) models can capture spatial and temporal dynamics for continuous kinematic decoding; however, systematic residual errors persist in predicted trajectories. We propose a two-stage decoding framework that applies reinforcement learning (RL) to perform residual kinematic correction on the outputs of a CNN--LSTM decoder (CNN--LSTM--RL). The RL agent is trained offline without direct EEG input and instead operates on predicted kinematic trajectories to optimize movement accuracy relative to target trajectories. Decoding performance was quantified using Pearson correlation coefficients ($r$) and Root Mean Square Errors (RMSE) along the $x, y$, and $z$ axes. Compared to CNN--LSTM applied alone, CNN--LSTM--RL improved the mean correlation from $0.5076$ to $0.7181$ ($p = 0.0005$) in 2D and from $0.6420$ to $0.7780$ ($p = 0.0059$) in VR, with relative gains of $41.5\%$ and $21.2\%$, respectively. Correspondingly, RMSE was reduced from $0.0890$ to $0.0532$ (2D, $p < 0.0001$) and from $0.0714$ to $0.0441$ (VR, $p < 0.0001$), representing relative reductions of $40.2\%$ and $38.2\%$. These findings demonstrate that this scalable framework enhances 3D BCI MI decoding by correcting kinematic errors via offline residual RL without extra neural data, advancing neurorehabilitation, prosthetics, and virtual interaction.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a two-stage decoding framework combining CNN-LSTM with reinforcement learning (CNN-LSTM-RL) to correct residual kinematic errors in 3D motor imagery EEG-based BCI decoding. The RL agent operates offline on predicted trajectories, significantly improving correlation and reducing RMSE compared to CNN-LSTM alone in both 2D and VR settings.
AI.motivation: Decoding continuous 3D motor imagery from non-invasive EEG remains challenging due to signal variability and persistent residual decoding errors, which limit practical BCI applications in neurorehabilitation, prosthetics, and virtual interaction.
AI.method: A two-stage framework: (1) a CNN-LSTM decoder extracts spatial-temporal features from EEG to predict kinematic trajectories; (2) a reinforcement learning agent receives predicted kinematic trajectories (no EEG input) and applies residual corrections to optimize movement accuracy against target trajectories, trained offline.
AI.result: In 2D: correlation improved from 0.5076 to 0.7181 (p=0.0005, +41.5%), RMSE reduced from 0.0890 to 0.0532 (p<0.0001, -40.2%). In VR: correlation improved from 0.6420 to 0.7780 (p=0.0059, +21.2%), RMSE reduced from 0.0714 to 0.0441 (p<0.0001, -38.2%).
AI.conclusion: The proposed CNN-LSTM-RL framework effectively corrects kinematic errors via offline residual RL without requiring additional neural data, offering a scalable approach to enhance 3D BCI MI decoding for neurorehabilitation, prosthetics, and virtual interaction.

title: Sticky Jump Diffusions: A Unifying View of Masked, Continuous, and Hybrid Diffusion
summary: We introduce Sticky Jump Diffusions (SJDs), continuous-time Markov processes on $\mathbb R^d$ whose discrete anchors are token embeddings. In forward time, anchors release their mass at a hazard rate and the released mass diffuses in the continuous ambient space; time reversal couples a score-driven SDE with a sticky jump kernel whose rate and destination are fixed by flux balance with the forward law. We estimate the score and the per-anchor reverse hazards from a single denoising classifier via Denoising Hazard Matching, the hazard analogue of denoising score matching, with simulation-free cross-entropy training. SJD recovers masked diffusion, continuous diffusion, and hybrid diffusion as limits. Its reversal explains features that each family treats as given: the mask of masked diffusion carries no evidence about the source token because the unsticking kernel of every anchor collapses to the same absorbing point; the terminal projection of continuous diffusion is required due to the absence of atoms in its forward marginal, without which flux balance yields no reverse jumps; and the update rules of hybrid diffusion (commit rate, destination, and drift) all follow from flux balance rather than from separate design. Beyond these limits, the unsticking kernel becomes a design space: a cross-position blending corrupts each position toward a blend of its neighbors' clean values or embeddings, turning dependency structure such as spatial locality or a constraint graph into an inductive bias of the corruption itself, and improves over the identity-kernel hybrid on CIFAR-10, Text8, and Sudoku.

AI.tldr: Sticky Jump Diffusions (SJDs) unify masked, continuous, and hybrid diffusion models through a framework where token embeddings serve as discrete anchors that release mass for diffusion. The method uses Denoising Hazard Matching for training and shows that hybrid diffusion update rules emerge from flux balance rather than separate design. Cross-position blending kernels improve performance on CIFAR-10, Text8, and Sudoku.
AI.motivation: To unify masked diffusion, continuous diffusion, and hybrid diffusion under a single theoretical framework (SJDs), and to explain design choices that each family previously treated as given (e.g., why mask carries no evidence, why terminal projection is needed in continuous diffusion).
AI.method: Introduces Sticky Jump Diffusions as continuous-time Markov processes with discrete anchors (token embeddings). Uses Denoising Hazard Matching to estimate score and per-anchor reverse hazards from a single denoising classifier via simulation-free cross-entropy training. Employs cross-position blending kernels that corrupt positions toward neighbors' clean values.
AI.result: SJD recovers masked, continuous, and hybrid diffusion as limiting cases. Cross-position blending kernels improve over identity-kernel hybrid on CIFAR-10, Text8, and Sudoku by turning spatial/dependency structure into an inductive bias of the corruption process.
AI.conclusion: Sticky Jump Diffusions provide a unified theoretical framework where design choices for various diffusion families emerge naturally from flux balance. The unsticking kernel becomes a flexible design space enabling inductive biases from structure (locality, constraints) to be baked into the corruption process, yielding empirical improvements.

title: MMA-Former: Multi-Window Mixture-of-Head Attention Transformer for Adaptive PNI Prediction in 3D MRI
comment: Published in the 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI 2026); accepted for oral presentation
summary: Perineural invasion (PNI) is a critical prognostic factor in cholangiocarcinoma. Non-invasive prediction from 3D MRI is challenging, demanding models that efficiently capture both fine-grained details and global context. We propose the Multi-window Mixture-of-Head Attention Transformer (MMA-Former), a novel end-to-end 3D architecture featuring a Coarse-Fine Transformer (CFT) structure for parallel multi-scale feature extraction. We advance this structure by integrating a novel Window-Specific Mixture-of-Head attention (WS-MoH) mechanism. Unlike standard Multi-Head Self Attention (MSA), WS-MoH generates a representation for each 3D window and dynamically routes the entire window to specialized or common attention heads. This enables spatially adaptive feature extraction tailored to the local context of each window, enhancing specialization and reducing redundancy without increasing parameters. Evaluated on a retrospective dataset of 168 T1-weighted MRI scans, MMA-Former achieved an AUC of 0.752, outperforming other 3D architectures, including the best CNN (AUC of 0.708) and Transformer baselines (AUC of 0.681).

AI.tldr: MMA-Former, a 3D Transformer with Multi-window Mixture-of-Head Attention, predicts perineural invasion from MRI with AUC 0.752, outperforming CNN and Transformer baselines.
AI.motivation: PNI is a critical prognostic factor in cholangiocarcinoma, but non-invasive prediction from 3D MRI is challenging, requiring models that capture both fine-grained details and global context effectively.
AI.method: Proposes MMA-Former with Coarse-Fine Transformer (CFT) for parallel multi-scale feature extraction, and a Window-Specific Mixture-of-Head attention (WS-MoH) mechanism that dynamically routes each 3D window to specialized or common attention heads for spatially adaptive feature extraction.
AI.result: On 168 T1-weighted MRI scans, MMA-Former achieved AUC 0.752, outperforming best CNN (0.708) and Transformer baselines (0.681).
AI.conclusion: WS-MoH enhances specialization and reduces redundancy without increasing parameters, enabling superior PNI prediction compared to existing 3D architectures.

title: HCRMap: Pressure-Aware Hot-Expert Residency Mapping for 3.5D MoE Chiplet Inference
summary:
Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) large language models (LLM) activate only a small number of experts during inference, but token routing introduces persistent expert hotness skew: a small set of hot experts continuously receives most tokens, while the remaining experts are lightly loaded. On 3.5D multi-chiplet systems, this skew not only causes compute imbalance but also amplifies pressure on communication, memory bandwidth, I/O, and execution queues. Therefore, the core problem is not simply to reduce token movement, but to dynamically place and reuse hot expert replicas across different memory tiers.
  This paper proposes HCRMap, a hot expert residency mapping framework for pressure-aware expert replica management in 3.5D MoE inference. Based on expert hotness, weight loading cost, migration overhead, and runtime resource pressure, HCRMap dynamically determines which experts should be promoted, retained, demoted, or evicted. It then maps routed token groups to suitable resident replicas, thereby jointly mitigating communication, memory, and queue bottlenecks.
  Experimental results show that HCRMap reduces end-to-end latency by 43.6% and 43.0% over Hydra in the prefill and decode stages, respectively; by 34.5% and 33.1% over MoEntwine; and by 46.7% and 46.0% over PIMoE.

AI.tldr: HCRMap is a mapping framework for 3.5D MoE inference that dynamically manages hot expert replicas across memory tiers, reducing end-to-end latency by 33-47% over existing methods.
AI.motivation: MoE LLMs suffer from expert hotness skew in multi-chiplet systems, causing compute imbalance and amplified pressure on communication, memory bandwidth, I/O, and execution queues.
AI.method: HCRMap dynamically promotes, retains, demotes, or evicts experts based on hotness, weight loading cost, migration overhead, and runtime resource pressure, then maps token groups to suitable resident replicas.
AI.result: End-to-end latency reduced by 43.6%/43.0% over Hydra, 34.5%/33.1% over MoEntwine, and 46.7%/46.0% over PIMoE in prefill/decode stages respectively.
AI.conclusion: Jointly mitigating communication, memory, and queue bottlenecks via dynamic expert replica placement effectively addresses the multi-faceted pressure issue in 3.5D MoE inference systems.

title: WSqD: A Horizon-Free Learning Rate Schedule for Large Model Training
summary: Standard learning rate schedules such as cosine annealing are tied to a fixed training horizon, limiting their ability to accommodate post hoc horizon extension. Warmup-stable-decay (WSD) partially addresses this issue by maintaining a long constant-rate phase before a short linear cooldown, allowing training to resume from a pre-decay checkpoint. However, its peak learning rate is still tuned based on the original training horizon and can become suboptimal when training is extended. Motivated by stochastic convex optimization, we propose WSqD (Warmup with Square-root base and linear Decay), a learning rate schedule that replaces WSD's constant stable phase with a shifted inverse-square-root base while retaining the final linear cooldown. In the stochastic convex setting, WSqD provably attains the minimax-optimal $O(1/\sqrt{T})$ last-iterate convergence rate. Importantly, its base learning rate schedule is horizon-independent, and the training horizon is needed only to determine when to begin the final cooldown. Empirically, on language-model pretraining using the SlimPajama corpus, WSqD matches or outperforms carefully tuned WSD and other baselines across multiple training horizons while reusing a single peak learning rate.

AI.tldr: WSqD is a new learning rate schedule that replaces the constant stable phase of WSD with a shifted inverse-square-root base, enabling horizon-independent peak learning rate selection and provably optimal convergence in stochastic convex settings, while matching or outperforming WSD in language model pretraining.
AI.motivation: Standard schedules (cosine annealing) are tied to fixed training horizons, limiting post hoc extension. WSD partially helps but its peak learning rate is still tuned to the original horizon, becoming suboptimal when training is extended.
AI.method: WSqD replaces WSD's constant stable phase with a shifted inverse-square-root base learning rate while retaining the final linear cooldown. The base schedule is horizon-independent; the horizon only determines when to start the cooldown.
AI.result: Provably achieves minimax-optimal O(1/√T) last-iterate convergence in stochastic convex optimization. Empirically matches or outperforms tuned WSD and baselines on SlimPajama language model pretraining across multiple horizons with a single peak learning rate.
AI.conclusion: WSqD offers a practical, horizon-agnostic learning rate schedule that simplifies hyperparameter tuning while maintaining strong performance across varying training durations.
