Interestingly, our experiments reveal that reducing memorization can either improve or impair generalization, depending on the dataset and training setup. These findings challenge the traditional assumption that overfitting is equivalent to memorization and suggest new hypotheses to reconcile these seemingly contradictory results. The source code is available at https://github.com/MarlonBecker/RandomLabelHeads

AI.tldr: This paper proposes using Random Label Prediction heads (RLP-heads) attached at various network depths to measure memorization in DNNs, revealing that reducing memorization can either help or hurt generalization depending on the dataset.
AI.motivation: To empirically study memorization in deep neural networks for classification tasks and understand how memorization capacity evolves across network layers.
AI.method: Augments training samples with auxiliary random labels predicted by RLP-heads attached at arbitrary network depths; uses RLP-head performance as an empirical estimate of Rademacher complexity to measure sample-level memorization and model capacity.
AI.result: Reducing memorization can either improve or impair generalization depending on the dataset and training setup; overfitting is not equivalent to memorization.
AI.conclusion: Challenges the traditional assumption that overfitting equals memorization and suggests new hypotheses to reconcile these contradictory findings.

title: SLVMBench: Skill Learning from Video Memory
summary: We introduce Skill Learning from Video Memory (SLVMBench), the first benchmark that jointly evaluates whether video large language models (video-LLMs) can learn skills from long video memory and apply them to real-time tasks. SLVMBench presents models with 2-3 hour video streams that contain a tutorial video embedded in a stream of arbitrary irrelevant videos, resembling real-world human learning practices. Video-LLMs are asked to apply the acquired skill to answer real-time questions about an ongoing video. Unlike long-video understanding benchmarks that emphasize passive comprehension and skill-learning benchmarks that rely on short, immediate demonstrations, SLVMBench tests the full pipeline of memorizing and extracting procedural knowledge, as well as transferring it to real-time tasks. Moreover, rigorous human annotations feature sub-second-level temporal calibration, manually engineered questions eliminating common-sense guessing, and collated tutorials to ensure coverage of the required skills. Evaluations on state-of-the-art proprietary and open-source video LLMs show that video-LLMs struggle substantially with learning and applying skill knowledge from videos. Moreover, performance degrades markedly when the skill knowledge is placed within a long video memory. These results reveal a key limitation of existing video LLMs and position SLVMBench as the first benchmark for studying real-time skill acquisition and application from long-context video memory.

AI.tldr: SLVMBench is the first benchmark evaluating whether video-LLMs can learn skills from long (2-3 hour) video streams containing embedded tutorials and apply them to real-time tasks, revealing that current models struggle significantly with skill acquisition and transfer from long video memory.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks focus on either passive long-video comprehension or skill learning from short demonstrations, but none evaluate the full pipeline of memorizing procedural knowledge from long videos and transferring it to real-time tasks, which resembles real-world human learning.
AI.method: SLVMBench constructs 2-3 hour video streams where a tutorial is embedded among irrelevant videos, then asks video-LLMs to apply learned skills to answer real-time questions. It features sub-second temporal calibration, manually engineered questions to avoid common-sense guessing, and collated tutorials ensuring skill coverage.
AI.result: State-of-the-art proprietary and open-source video-LLMs struggle substantially with learning and applying skill knowledge from videos, and performance degrades markedly when skill knowledge is placed within a long video memory.
AI.conclusion: Existing video-LLMs have a key limitation in real-time skill acquisition and application from long-context video memory; SLVMBench is positioned as the first benchmark to study this challenging problem.

title: Condition-Stratified Robustness Analysis of Post-Hoc Calibration Methods for Probabilistic Classifiers
summary: Post-hoc calibration is widely adopted to correct probability estimates from trained classifiers, yet most evaluations report aggregate performance without testing whether that performance holds across distinct operating conditions within a single dataset. We present a pre-registered, condition-stratified robustness analysis comparing temperature scaling (TEMP) and isotonic regression (ISO) across four controlled conditions (C1--C4). Four hypothesis groups are evaluated: discrimination deltas with Holm-corrected multiplicity control (H1), Brier score differences (H2), calibration slope outcomes (H3), and AUROC differences under best-condition setups (H4). TEMP-minus-ISO discrimination deltas remain small across all conditions (-0.0155 to 0.0139), with Holm-adjusted p-values of 0.9895 everywhere. TEMP Brier differences are consistently negative (C1: -0.0002 through C4: -0.0074), while ISO shows sign reversals. TEMP calibration slopes stay closer to unity in every condition (range 0.7597--0.9493) than ISO slopes (0.1364--0.2726). AUROC differences shift from near zero in C1 (-0.0004) to positive in C4 (0.0264). These results establish that in-dataset robustness is condition-dependent and metric-specific. No claim of external transportability is made.

AI.tldr: This paper evaluates post-hoc calibration methods (temperature scaling vs. isotonic regression) across different operating conditions within a single dataset, finding that performance varies by condition and metric.
AI.motivation: Existing calibration evaluations only report aggregate performance without testing whether results hold across different operating conditions within a single dataset.
AI.method: Pre-registered, condition-stratified robustness analysis comparing temperature scaling (TEMP) and isotonic regression (ISO) across four controlled conditions (C1-C4), testing four hypothesis groups: discrimination deltas (H1), Brier scores (H2), calibration slopes (H3), and AUROC differences (H4).
AI.result: TEMP-minus-ISO discrimination deltas are small across conditions (-0.0155 to 0.0139) with Holm-adjusted p-values of 0.9895. TEMP Brier differences are consistently negative (-0.0002 to -0.0074), while ISO shows sign reversals. TEMP calibration slopes remain closer to unity (0.7597-0.9493) than ISO (0.1364-0.2726). AUROC differences shift from near zero (-0.0004) to positive (0.0264).
AI.conclusion: In-dataset robustness is condition-dependent and metric-specific, and no claim of external transportability is made.

title: HierCAD: Hierarchical Text-to-CAD Design via Structure Alignment and Parameter Grounding
summary: Recent text-to-CAD approaches have shown promising results by leveraging large language models, but they often struggle with maintaining structural consistency in complex designs and accurately grounding geometric parameters. To address these issues, we propose HierCAD, a hierarchical text-to-CAD framework that improves both structural reasoning and parameter prediction. HierCAD reformulates CAD generation as progressive reasoning by decomposing CAD construction trees into object-level procedural reasoning and part-level topology reasoning trajectories. To further improve generation fidelity, we introduce a unified Structure Alignment and Parameter Grounding (SAPG) learning strategy. Structure alignment aligns topology reasoning trajectories with their corresponding parametric CAD spans, while parameter grounding mitigates shortcut learning through structure-preserving parameter perturbations and ranking-based supervision. Experiments demonstrate that HierCAD outperforms prior state-of-the-art methods on both CAD sequence generation and reconstructed CAD model evaluation. Our code is available at https://github.com/Collab-Gen/HierCAD.

AI.tldr: HierCAD is a hierarchical text-to-CAD framework that improves structural consistency and parameter grounding by decomposing CAD construction into object-level and part-level reasoning trajectories, paired with a Structure Alignment and Parameter Grounding (SAPG) learning strategy.
AI.motivation: Existing text-to-CAD approaches using large language models struggle with maintaining structural consistency in complex designs and accurately grounding geometric parameters.
AI.method: HierCAD reformulates CAD generation as progressive reasoning by decomposing CAD construction trees into object-level procedural reasoning and part-level topology reasoning trajectories. It also introduces SAPG learning strategy: structure alignment aligns topology trajectories with parametric CAD spans, and parameter grounding uses structure-preserving perturbations and ranking-based supervision to prevent shortcut learning.
AI.result: HierCAD outperforms prior state-of-the-art methods on both CAD sequence generation and reconstructed CAD model evaluation.
AI.conclusion: The hierarchical decomposition and SAPG strategy effectively improve structural consistency and parameter accuracy in text-to-CAD generation.

title: Advancing Optimal Subset Oracle via Learning Relaxation of Neural Set Functions
summary: Learning neural set functions is pivotal to a wide range of important applications, including compound selection in AI-driven drug discovery and product recommendation. Recent work has introduced optimal subset oracles to implicitly learn set functions under practical weakly supervised settings, where model parameters are optimized through mean-field variational inference. However, these frameworks rely on Monte Carlo sampling to estimate gradients of the evidence lower bound when updating the variational distribution. Repeated sampling across iterations incurs substantial computational overhead, while the resulting stochasticity can destabilize the optimization trajectory. In this work, we reinterpret the evidence lower bound as a continuous relaxation of the set function and learn a surrogate objective that replaces sampling-based ELBO gradient estimation during variational optimization. The learned surrogate provides stable and efficient gradients throughout the continuous domain, thereby reducing computational overhead and accelerating inference. Furthermore, we establish an approximation guarantee for the proposed framework under submodular maximization and characterize its connection to variational free energy. Experiments on a variety of real-world tasks demonstrate consistent improvements over existing baselines.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a continuous relaxation of the ELBO to replace Monte Carlo sampling for gradient estimation in learning neural set functions, achieving faster and more stable optimization with theoretical guarantees.
AI.motivation: Existing frameworks for learning neural set functions under weakly supervised settings use Monte Carlo sampling to estimate ELBO gradients during variational inference, which incurs high computational overhead and introduces stochasticity that destabilizes optimization.
AI.method: The authors reinterpret the ELBO as a continuous relaxation of the set function and learn a surrogate objective that replaces sampling-based ELBO gradient estimation during variational optimization, providing stable and efficient gradients throughout the continuous domain.
AI.result: Experiments on real-world tasks demonstrate consistent improvements over existing baselines in terms of computational efficiency and optimization stability.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework provides an approximation guarantee under submodular maximization, establishes a connection to variational free energy, and offers a practical alternative to sampling-based methods for neural set function learning.

title: Longitudinal Multi-View Breast Cancer Risk Prediction
summary: Accurate breast cancer risk prediction from screening mammography is critical for enabling personalized screening intervals and early detection. Recent deep learning methods have shown the value of longitudinal data and explicit temporal alignment. However, existing approaches either perform explicit alignment using a single mammographic view or model multiple views without explicit longitudinal alignment, limiting their ability to exploit the complementary spatial-temporal information used in clinical practice. To address this gap, we propose LMV-Net, a longitudinal multi-view breast cancer risk prediction model that jointly analyzes anatomically complementary CC and MLO views within an explicitly aligned longitudinal framework. We evaluate our approach on the public EMBED and CSAW-CC datasets, comparing it to state-of-the-art breast cancer risk prediction methods. Our model consistently outperforms existing approaches in overall risk prediction performance and across different breast density and cancer subgroups. Importantly, these improvements highlight the potential of longitudinal multi-view modeling to enhance risk stratification, paving the way for future work on personalized screening, earlier identification of high-risk patients, and more efficient screening resource allocation. The code is available at https://github.com/sot176/LMV-Net.

AI.tldr: LMV-Net is a deep learning model that jointly analyzes longitudinal CC and MLO mammographic views for breast cancer risk prediction, outperforming existing methods on public datasets.
AI.motivation: Existing deep learning methods for breast cancer risk prediction either use explicit temporal alignment with a single mammographic view or model multiple views without explicit longitudinal alignment, limiting their ability to exploit complementary spatial-temporal information used in clinical practice.
AI.method: LMV-Net is a longitudinal multi-view model that jointly analyzes anatomically complementary CC (craniocaudal) and MLO (mediolateral oblique) views within an explicitly aligned longitudinal framework for breast cancer risk prediction.
AI.result: Evaluated on EMBED and CSAW-CC datasets, LMV-Net consistently outperforms state-of-the-art breast cancer risk prediction methods, both in overall performance and across different breast density and cancer subgroups.
AI.conclusion: Longitudinal multi-view modeling enhances risk stratification and shows potential for enabling personalized screening, earlier identification of high-risk patients, and more efficient screening resource allocation.

title: Heuristic Learning for Active Flow Control Using Coding Agents
summary: Active flow control involves nonlinear dynamics, partial observations, and computationally expensive simulations, making controller design particularly challenging. Deep reinforcement learning (DRL) has emerged as a powerful framework for such problems, but its success typically relies on large numbers of simulator interactions and produces neural-network policies whose decision process often remains difficult to interpret. In this work, we investigate a different paradigm: instead of optimizing neural-network parameters, we use modern coding agents to search directly for explicit executable feedback laws. We introduce a constrained heuristic-learning protocol in which an agent iteratively proposes, evaluates, and revises controller implementations while interacting exclusively through the public benchmark interface. The proposed framework is evaluated on 13 active flow-control benchmarks spanning one, two, and three-dimensional problems and compared against the strongest available DRL baselines under identical simulation budgets. The discovered heuristic controllers match or outperform the best DRL policy in 10 of the 13 environments while remaining compact, interpretable, and directly inspectable. Beyond aggregate performance, the resulting controllers reveal physically meaningful feedback mechanisms, transfer successfully across more challenging configurations, and remain competitive under varying Reynolds and Rayleigh numbers, actuator counts, and observation sparsity. These results suggest that heuristic learning through coding agents constitutes a credible and complementary alternative to conventional reinforcement learning, combining competitive performance with physically interpretable controller representations. Prompts and source code are available at https://github.com/DonsetPG/fluid-heuristic-learning.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes using coding agents to directly search for explicit, interpretable feedback laws for active flow control, instead of training black-box neural-network policies via deep reinforcement learning. The method matches or outperforms DRL on 10 out of 13 benchmarks while producing compact, physically interpretable controllers.
AI.motivation: Active flow control is challenging due to nonlinear dynamics, partial observations, and expensive simulations. While DRL is powerful, it requires massive simulator interactions and produces uninterpretable neural-network policies.
AI.method: A constrained heuristic-learning protocol where a coding agent iteratively proposes, evaluates, and revises explicit feedback laws through a public benchmark interface, searching directly for executable controllers rather than optimizing neural-network parameters.
AI.result: Evaluated on 13 active flow-control benchmarks (1D, 2D, 3D). Heuristic controllers match or outperform the best DRL policies in 10/13 environments, while being compact, interpretable, transferable, and robust to varying Reynolds/Rayleigh numbers, actuator counts, and observation sparsity.
AI.conclusion: Heuristic learning through coding agents is a credible and complementary alternative to conventional DRL, combining competitive performance with physically interpretable controller representations for active flow control.

title: Benchmarking Edge Inference Strategies for Deep Learning Models in Industrial Machine Vision
comment: 6 pages, 6 figures, accepted at IEEE COINS 2026. Preprint version; camera-ready version to appear in the conference proceedings
summary:
Edge deployment is often the preferred solution for industrial machine vision systems when low latency, data security, or limited connectivity are critical requirements. Several frameworks are available to optimise inference on edge devices; however, relatively few studies have systematically compared their inference-time performance under industrial deployment conditions.
  In this work, we present a comparative study of four widely used approaches for machine vision inference in industrial settings: plain PyTorch, ONNX Runtime, OpenVINO, and TensorRT. The evaluation focuses on inference time, covers several CPU- and GPU-based hardware platforms, and includes both conventional convolutional neural networks and a transformer-based vision model. For the hardware platforms and models evaluated, the results show that OpenVINO achieves the lowest inference time on CPUs, while TensorRT achieves the lowest inference time on GPUs. However, TensorRT does not outperform plain PyTorch for the transformer-based model considered in this study.

AI.tldr: This paper compares four inference optimization frameworks (PyTorch, ONNX Runtime, OpenVINO, TensorRT) for industrial edge deployment, finding OpenVINO best on CPUs and TensorRT best on GPUs, though TensorRT underperforms on transformer models.
AI.motivation: Industrial machine vision systems require low latency, data security, and limited connectivity, but few studies have systematically compared inference-time performance of optimization frameworks under real industrial conditions.
AI.method: A comparative study of four frameworks (plain PyTorch, ONNX Runtime, OpenVINO, TensorRT) evaluated on multiple CPU/GPU hardware platforms using both CNNs and a transformer-based vision model, measuring inference time.
AI.result: OpenVINO achieves lowest inference time on CPUs; TensorRT achieves lowest inference time on GPUs; but TensorRT does not outperform plain PyTorch for the transformer-based model tested.
AI.conclusion: Framework choice for edge deployment depends on hardware (CPU vs. GPU) and model architecture (CNN vs. transformer), with no single framework universally optimal.

title: Structure-Feature Aligned Graph Learning via Alternating Constrained Optimization
summary: We introduce a constrained two-view framework for node prediction that aligns structure-conditioned GNN embeddings with a structure-free feature prior learned by an anchor model. Conventional Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) couple feature transformation and neighborhood aggregation, which renders them vulnerable to topology noise and heterophilous connections. To decouple this dependency, our framework utilizes an independent anchor network to capture intrinsic attribute features via a self-supervised reconstruction objective. Furthermore, we propose a Channel-Split Adaptive Gated GNN (CSAG-GNN) that dynamically routes representations between global spectral smoothing and local spatial discrimination through a node-wise gating mechanism. We propose a stable cyclic alternating optimization strategy to solve the resulting coupled bi-level objective, preventing mutual representation drift during training. Empirical results on both homophilous and heterophilous benchmarks show balanced performance gains and structural robustness over competitive baselines.

AI.tldr: A constrained two-view framework that decouples feature transformation and neighborhood aggregation in GNNs by using an independent anchor model and a Channel-Split Adaptive Gated GNN (CSAG-GNN) with a stable cyclic alternating optimization strategy, showing balanced performance on both homophilous and heterophilous graphs.
AI.motivation: Conventional GNNs couple feature transformation and neighborhood aggregation, making them vulnerable to topology noise and heterophilous connections. The authors aim to decouple this dependency to improve robustness.
AI.method: (1) A constrained two-view framework aligning structure-conditioned GNN embeddings with a structure-free feature prior from an anchor model; (2) CSAG-GNN with a node-wise gating mechanism that dynamically routes between global spectral smoothing and local spatial discrimination; (3) A stable cyclic alternating optimization strategy to solve the coupled bi-level objective.
AI.result: Balanced performance gains and structural robustness over competitive baselines on both homophilous and heterophilous benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework effectively decouples feature transformation from neighborhood aggregation, achieving robustness across diverse graph structures. The CSAG-GNN and cyclic alternating optimization strategy are key enablers.

title: Efficient Tuning Before Low-Bit Post-Training Quantization for Stochastic Gradient Descent-optimized Models
comment: 12 pages, 10 figures, submitted to IEEE Transactions on Neural Networks and Learning Systems (TNNLS). Code available at https://github.com/xpxpxp2001xpxpxp/ETBQ
summary: Post-training quantization (PTQ) compresses deep neural networks for deployment under limited memory and computational budgets. However, low-bit (i.e., 2-bit or 4-bit) PTQ often suffers from substantial performance degradation. Most existing PTQ methods operate on an unconstrained full-precision (FP) model and primarily address quantization errors through post-hoc reconstruction. We argue that low-bit PTQ accuracy is limited not only by post-quantization error minimization, but also by the quantization-error tolerance of a FP model itself. In this paper, we propose Efficient Tuning Before Quantization (ETBQ), a pre-conditioning tuning stage for Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD)-optimized models before PTQ. During tuning, the FP model is optimized under perturbations sampled from the error distributions of weight and activation quantization, guiding the model toward a loss-landscape region that is less sensitive to the subsequent PTQ. Unlike QAT, ETBQ does not train a fake-quantized deployment model, which is computationally and memory intensive. Instead, ETBQ outputs a FP model that can be used by any PTQ backend. Experiments on CIFAR-100, Tiny-ImageNet, ImageNet, and Cityscapes provide consistent evidence that ETBQ improves low-bit PTQ across diverse tasks. Under W2A4 settings, e.g., ETBQ improves over naive PTQ by 2.14\% top-1 accuracy on Tiny-ImageNet and by 5.80\% mIoU on Cityscapes. Code is available at https://github.com/xpxpxp2001xpxpxp/ETBQ.

AI.tldr: ETBQ is a pre-conditioning stage that tunes full-precision models under simulated quantization perturbations before PTQ, improving low-bit quantization accuracy without the computational cost of QAT.
AI.motivation: Existing low-bit PTQ methods focus on post-hoc error reconstruction but ignore the inherent quantization-error tolerance of the FP model. The authors argue that both factors limit PTQ accuracy.
AI.method: ETBQ tunes the FP model under perturbations sampled from the error distributions of weight and activation quantization, steering the model toward a loss-landscape region less sensitive to subsequent PTQ.
AI.result: ETBQ improves over naive PTQ by 2.14% top-1 accuracy on Tiny-ImageNet (W2A4) and 5.80% mIoU on Cityscapes, with consistent gains across CIFAR-100, Tiny-ImageNet, ImageNet, and Cityscapes.
AI.conclusion: ETBQ is an efficient, QAT-free pre-tuning method that boosts low-bit PTQ performance and is compatible with any PTQ backend.

title: Self-supervised training for high-resolution close-range multispectral remote sensing imagery
summary: Although self-supervised learning (SSL) offers a promising way to reduce annotation effort in close-range remote sensing, its effectiveness for high-resolution multispectral unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) imagery remains underexplored due to limited data. This study evaluated SSL pretraining for precision agriculture using cm-scale multispectral drone imagery collected across multiple sensors, years, and regions. Transformer-based encoders were pretrained with Momentum Contrast v3 (MoCo-v3) and Masked Autoencoders on a harmonized dataset combining msuav500K with newly collected multi-year UAV imagery from agricultural fields in Finland. Pretraining used four spectral bands (Green, Red, Red-Edge, Near-Infrared) for cross-sensor compatibility. The models were evaluated on crop-weed semantic segmentation using the WeedMap dataset with 5--100% training data. The following two subsets served as downstream tasks: Task A (Germany, RedEdge-M), where all pretrained models were compared under partial and full fine-tuning, and Task B (Switzerland, Sequoia), where the best encoder from Task A was assessed. Our Swin Transformer pretrained with MoCo-v3 achieved the strongest performance on both tasks, surpassing the Swin Transformer model of Doornbos et al. pretrained on a pre-release of msuav500K. Our pretrained Swin Transformer further demonstrated cross-sensor and cross-region generalization. We additionally provide a public multi-year multispectral UAV dataset from Finland to support future research.

AI.tldr: This study demonstrates that SSL pretraining (particularly MoCo-v3 with Swin Transformer) effectively improves crop-weed segmentation in multispectral UAV imagery across different sensors and regions, using a harmonized multi-year dataset from Finland.
AI.motivation: SSL's potential for reducing annotation effort in close-range remote sensing is underexplored for high-resolution multispectral UAV imagery due to limited data availability.
AI.method: Transformer encoders pretrained with MoCo-v3 and Masked Autoencoders on a harmonized multispectral UAV dataset (four bands: Green, Red, Red-Edge, NIR), evaluated on crop-weed segmentation tasks across two different sensor/region subsets.
AI.result: Swin Transformer with MoCo-v3 outperformed all other models on both tasks, including the prior Swin model by Doornbos et al., demonstrating cross-sensor and cross-region generalization.
AI.conclusion: SSL pretraining with MoCo-v3 on harmonized multispectral UAV data effectively improves downstream segmentation performance and generalizes across sensors and regions; a public multi-year UAV dataset from Finland is released.

title: Privacy-Aware Collaborative and Distributed Bayesian Optimization
summary: We propose a collaborative meta-learning framework for distributed Bayesian optimization matching centralized performance without raw-data exchange. We show gradient sharing leaks client observations, with leakage worsening as the search converges and queries concentrate near the optimum. We evaluate a differentially private defense and characterize its privacy-utility trade-off.

AI.tldr: Proposes a collaborative meta-learning framework for distributed Bayesian optimization that matches centralized performance without sharing raw data, demonstrates that gradient sharing leaks client observations (worsening near the optimum), and evaluates a differentially private defense with its privacy-utility trade-off.
AI.motivation: To enable distributed Bayesian optimization that matches centralized performance while preventing leakage of client observations, which becomes more severe when queries concentrate near the optimum during convergence.
AI.method: Collaborative meta-learning framework for distributed Bayesian optimization; analysis of gradient sharing leakage; evaluation of differentially private defense.
AI.result: Gradient sharing leaks client observations, with leakage worsening as search converges near the optimum. A differentially private defense provides a privacy-utility trade-off.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework achieves centralized-level performance without raw-data exchange, but gradient sharing introduces privacy risks that can be mitigated with differential privacy at the cost of some utility.

title: Uncertainty Quantification for EO Regression Tasks: Building Height, Tree Canopy Height and Above-ground Biomass Estimation
summary: Earth Observation regression tasks such as building height, canopy height, and above-ground biomass estimation underpin critical applications in urban planning, forest monitoring, and climate policy, where both accuracy and reliability are critical. Yet most deep learning models yield only deterministic predictions, providing no indication of per-pixel reliability. These regression tasks are inherently challenging due to heterogeneous land surfaces, skewed target distributions, sensor noise, and signal saturation at high target values, making uncertainty (UC) estimation essential for reliable inference. We address this gap by modeling aleatoric uncertainty using year-long Sentinel-1 SAR and Sentinel-2 MSI time series, proposing two complementary approaches: (i) Gaussian UC, which jointly predicts mean and standard deviation under a Gaussian assumption, and (ii) Quantile UC, which estimates the 10th, 50th, and 90th quantiles to capture asymmetric and heteroscedastic error distributions. Both models are evaluated on three representative EO regression tasks at 10 m spatial resolution. Results show that both approaches match or surpass deterministic benchmarks and existing global products, while delivering well-calibrated, interpretable, and operationally useful confidence estimates. Notably, both models outperform the current 10 m state-of-the-art uncertainty-aware model for canopy height estimation. Our implementation will be available at: https://github.com/RituYadav92/EO-Regression-Uncertainty-Estimation

AI.tldr: This paper proposes two uncertainty estimation approaches (Gaussian UC and Quantile UC) for Earth Observation regression tasks using Sentinel-1 SAR and Sentinel-2 MSI time series, achieving state-of-the-art performance on building height, canopy height, and biomass estimation while providing interpretable per-pixel confidence estimates.
AI.motivation: Current deep learning models for EO regression tasks only provide deterministic predictions without per-pixel reliability estimates, yet applications in urban planning, forestry, and climate policy require both accuracy and uncertainty quantification due to challenges like heterogeneous land surfaces, skewed distributions, sensor noise, and signal saturation.
AI.method: Two complementary uncertainty modeling approaches: (i) Gaussian UC - jointly predicts mean and standard deviation under a Gaussian assumption for aleatoric uncertainty; (ii) Quantile UC - estimates 10th, 50th, and 90th quantiles to capture asymmetric and heteroscedastic error distributions. Both use year-long Sentinel-1 SAR and Sentinel-2 MSI time series data at 10m resolution.
AI.result: Both approaches match or surpass deterministic benchmarks and existing global products while delivering well-calibrated, interpretable confidence estimates. They outperform the current 10m state-of-the-art uncertainty-aware model for canopy height estimation.
AI.conclusion: The proposed uncertainty estimation methods are effective for EO regression tasks, providing both high accuracy and reliable per-pixel uncertainty estimates essential for operational applications.

title: Fundamental Limitations of Fixed-Budget Best-Arm Identification
summary: In fixed-budget best-arm identification, also known as ranking and selection, an algorithm has a sampling budget to distribute across $K$ arms. Each sample provides noisy feedback about that arm's mean, and the goal is to identify the arm with the largest mean. A common performance benchmark is the static oracle: a non-adaptive strategy that knows the means in advance and chooses fixed sampling proportions to maximize the exponential decay rate of the probability of incorrect identification. Several adaptive algorithms have been constructed such that their sampling proportions converge to the static oracle proportions. However, it has remained open whether any algorithm could match the static oracle's error decay rate uniformly across all problem instances. We answer this in the negative. For any $K\ge 3$ and for rewards drawn from any one-parameter natural exponential family, we show that for any algorithm, there is at least one instance where the error decay rate is at most $\left(1 + \frac{\log(K)}{8}\right)^{-1}$ times that of the static oracle. This also answers the open question posed by Qin (2022), showing that fixed-budget best-arm identification does not admit a complexity.

AI.tldr: This paper proves that no adaptive algorithm can uniformly match the static oracle's error decay rate in fixed-budget best-arm identification for K≥3 arms with rewards from any one-parameter natural exponential family. The best possible error decay rate is at most (1 + log(K)/8)^{-1} times the oracle rate, answering an open question by Qin (2022).
AI.motivation: To determine whether any adaptive algorithm can uniformly match the static oracle's exponential error decay rate in fixed-budget best-arm identification, a question that has remained open.
AI.method: The paper provides a negative answer by proving a lower bound: for any algorithm and K≥3, there exists at least one problem instance where the error decay rate is at most (1 + log(K)/8)^{-1} times that of the static oracle. The result holds for rewards from any one-parameter natural exponential family.
AI.result: No algorithm can uniformly match the static oracle's error decay rate. The lower bound shows a factor of (1 + log(K)/8)^{-1} degradation compared to the oracle. This also shows that fixed-budget best-arm identification does not admit a complexity (in the sense of Qin, 2022).
AI.conclusion: The problem of fixed-budget best-arm identification has an inherent limitation: adaptive algorithms cannot achieve the static oracle's performance uniformly across all instances, establishing a fundamental gap between adaptive and non-adaptive strategies.

title: Video Transformer for Remote Identity Document Hologram Detection
comment: Accepted to SMC2026 conference, 6 pages, 5 figures, 5 tables
summary: Remote identity authentification using Identification Documents has been a major challenge for several years. DeepFakes advent and the development of AI-guided tools helps fraudsters creating counterfeit ID Documents. Ensuring the authenticity of ID Documents has become a real clue in the seurization of remote authentification. This need is all the more pressing given the increasing digitization of administrative and transactional processes. To ensure widespread accessibility, the system should rely solely on video captured via mobile devices. In this specific context, confirming the authenticity of ID is a real challenge as many security features needs specific device like infrared sensor for instance. Among underutilized but promising security features, holographic printings hold a special place. Difficult to counterfeit, they produce distinctive visual effects according enlightment, making them both detectable in a video captured by a smartphone camera and difficult to imitate. In this paper, we propose a Remote Identity Document Verification System (RIDVS) and an approach based on a video transformer for detecting holograms in simple videos captured by smartphones. Our system is designed for a smartphone-based capture process, followed by a server-side verification. The hologram detection method builds on a robust model previously validated in a related research domain. We demonstrate that it outperforms existing SotA methods, achieving near-perfect accuracy even when trained on medium- to small-sized datasets. In particular, we report improvements of +26.86\% in Recall and +17.93\% in accuracy over the best MIDV-Holo baseline. This study includes several experiments that evaluate the model adaptation to frugality, both for training samples and computational resources.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a Remote Identity Document Verification System (RIDVS) using a video transformer to detect holograms in smartphone-captured videos, achieving near-perfect accuracy and outperforming existing methods by up to +26.86% in Recall and +17.93% in accuracy.
AI.motivation: Remote identity authentication using ID documents faces increased fraud risks from DeepFakes and AI tools. With growing digitization of processes, there is urgent need for accessible, smartphone-based ID verification systems that can detect security features like holograms without specialized hardware.
AI.method: The system uses a smartphone-based capture process with server-side verification. A video transformer-based approach is employed for detecting holograms in videos, building on a previously validated robust model and adapted for frugal training and computational resource usage.
AI.result: The proposed hologram detection method outperforms existing state-of-the-art methods, achieving near-perfect accuracy even with medium-to-small datasets. It reports improvements of +26.86% in Recall and +17.93% in accuracy over the best MIDV-Holo baseline.
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates effective remote ID document verification via smartphone video, with hologram detection as a key security feature. The system shows strong performance and adaptability to limited training data and computational resources.

title: Bet on Features: Anytime-Valid and Feature-Aware Auditing of Conditional Quantile Forecasters
summary: Black-box conditional quantile forecasts are widely used for sequential decisions under asymmetric costs, such as inventory planning in supply chain management. Once deployed, such forecasters must be monitored continuously as data streams drift and regimes change; this invalidates standard, fixed-horizon backtests for calibration. Further, existing backtests do not take into account that the notion of calibration is, in fact, information-dependent: forecasts can look calibrated to an auditor with coarse information while being miscalibrated to an auditor with richer information. We develop a distribution-free and game-theoretic testing framework for continuously auditing black-box conditional quantile forecasters with non-i.i.d. losses, such that the resulting evidence process is powerful against predictably chosen alternatives specified by the features available to the auditor. We first formalize notions of conditional quantile calibration when different sets of features are available to the auditor, establishing that the coarseness of the auditor's information set determines the hardness of the testing problem. We then identify the sets of alternatives for which the auditor can achieve power, and focusing on contextual bets linear in the features, we derive finite-time detection guarantees for such alternatives, all without an i.i.d. assumption. The resulting evidence processes are interpretable at the feature level, as they quantify fine-grained, "feature-aware" evidence for miscalibration. We empirically validate these methods on simulated and real data, finding that a popular time series forecaster (Chronos-2) is highly miscalibrated w.r.t. multiple relevant features.

AI.tldr: The paper develops a distribution-free, game-theoretic testing framework for continuously auditing black-box conditional quantile forecasters under non-i.i.d. data streams, establishing that calibration is information-dependent and providing finite-time detection guarantees for feature-aware alternatives.
AI.motivation: Existing backtests for conditional quantile forecasts fail under drifting data streams and ignore that calibration is information-dependent—a forecaster can appear calibrated to a coarse-information auditor while being miscalibrated to a richer-information auditor.
AI.method: A distribution-free, game-theoretic testing framework for continuous auditing, formalizing conditional quantile calibration under varying auditor information sets, identifying powerful alternatives, and deriving finite-time detection guarantees for linear contextual bets without i.i.d. assumptions.
AI.result: The evidence processes are interpretable at the feature level ("feature-aware"), and empirical validation on simulated and real data shows that the popular forecaster Chronos-2 is highly miscalibrated with respect to multiple relevant features.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides a rigorous, information-dependent approach to auditing quantile forecasts in non-stationary environments, with practical evidence of miscalibration in state-of-the-art time series models.

title: Towards Efficient Convolutional Neural Network for Embedded Hardware via Multi-Dimensional Pruning
comment: Author's accepted version. Published in Proceedings of the 60th ACM/IEEE Design Automation Conference (DAC 2023)
summary: In this paper, we propose TECO, a multi-dimensional pruning framework to collaboratively prune the three dimensions (depth, width, and resolution) of convolutional neural networks (CNNs) for better execution efficiency on embedded hardware. In TECO, we first introduce a two-stage importance evaluation framework, which efficiently and comprehensively evaluates each pruning unit according to both the local importance inside each dimension and the global importance across different dimensions. Based on the evaluation framework, we present a heuristic pruning algorithm to progressively prune the three dimensions of CNNs towards the optimal trade-off between accuracy and efficiency. Experiments on multiple benchmarks validate the advantages of TECO over existing state-of-the-art (SOTA) approaches. The code and pre-trained models are available at https://github.com/ntuliuteam/Teco.

AI.tldr: TECO is a multi-dimensional pruning framework that collaboratively prunes depth, width, and resolution of CNNs for embedded hardware efficiency, using a two-stage importance evaluation and heuristic pruning algorithm.
AI.motivation: To improve CNN execution efficiency on embedded hardware by jointly pruning three dimensions (depth, width, resolution) rather than treating them independently.
AI.method: A two-stage importance evaluation (local + global importance) followed by a heuristic pruning algorithm that progressively prunes across dimensions to balance accuracy and efficiency.
AI.result: Outperforms existing state-of-the-art methods across multiple benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Multi-dimensional collaborative pruning is more effective than single-dimension approaches, and TECO provides a practical framework for deploying efficient CNNs on embedded hardware.

title: How to Tame Grokking: Representation Geometry as a Control Signal
summary: Grokking is a phenomenon in which neural networks initially memorize training data and only later exhibit strong generalization after prolonged optimization. Despite extensive recent study, the factors influencing the emergence and timing of grokking remain incompletely understood. We investigate the relationship between representation geometry and delayed generalization. We find that dimensionality collapse consistently precedes the onset of grokking in all evaluated settings. Motivated by these observations, we introduce Geometric Dimensionality Regularization (GeomDR), a simple spectral regularizer that modifies the effective dimensionality of hidden representations during training. Across modular addition, modular division, and permutation composition tasks, GeomDR consistently alters grokking dynamics and can substantially accelerate the onset of generalization depending on the intervention schedule and target dimensionality. In several settings, grokking is accelerated by up to 52 times relative to standard AdamW training. Similar qualitative effects are observed in both multilayer perceptrons and transformers. Together, these results suggest that representation geometry can serve as an effective control signal for grokking and provide evidence that geometric interventions offer a practical approach for studying and influencing delayed generalization in neural networks.

AI.tldr: This paper finds that dimensionality collapse precedes grokking and introduces GeomDR, a spectral regularizer that modifies hidden representation dimensionality to accelerate generalization by up to 52x across various tasks and architectures.
AI.motivation: To understand the factors influencing the emergence and timing of grokking—the phenomenon where neural networks transition from memorization to delayed generalization.
AI.method: The authors first observe that dimensionality collapse consistently precedes grokking. They then propose Geometric Dimensionality Regularization (GeomDR), a spectral regularizer that controls the effective dimensionality of hidden representations during training.
AI.result: GeomDR consistently alters grokking dynamics across modular addition, division, and permutation tasks, accelerating generalization by up to 52x compared to standard AdamW training. Effects hold for both MLPs and transformers.
AI.conclusion: Representation geometry serves as an effective control signal for grokking, and geometric interventions offer a practical approach for studying and influencing delayed generalization in neural networks.

title: HyperGS: Fast and Generalizable Gaussian Video Representation
summary: Gaussian Splatting has emerged as an effective representation for video, but existing methods rely on per-video optimization. This leads to slow encoding and limits generalization across videos. To amortize this optimization, we propose HyperGS, a feedforward, optimization-free approach that directly predicts Gaussian representations from any video in a single forward pass, speeding up encoding and decoding by orders of magnitude while generalizing to out-of-distribution videos at higher resolutions. In HyperGS, we design a factorized spatiotemporal Transformer to extract tokens from video, and a learnable query-based Transformer to obtain 8-parameter Gaussian representations for each video frame. We find that naively predicting Gaussians across diverse videos induces a needle-like degeneration that collapses training, and address this with a rank-based geometric regularizer whose strength adapts dynamically to stabilize optimization. HyperGS achieves encoding at $10^4$--$10^5\times$ the speed of per-video Gaussian optimization at matched reconstruction quality while generalizing zero-shot to $720p$ video, enabling higher-resolution rendering without re-encoding. HyperGS improves PSNR by +2.9--3.1 dB over the prior video encoders on K400, SSv2, and UCF101 at a smaller video representation size. By predicting explicit 2D Gaussians in a single forward pass, HyperGS combines the fast, flexible rendering of Gaussian Splatting with the speed and generalization of feedforward prediction, advancing Gaussians as a practical direction for fast and generalizable video representation.

AI.tldr: HyperGS is a feedforward, optimization-free approach that directly predicts Gaussian representations from any video in a single forward pass, achieving orders-of-magnitude faster encoding/decoding while generalizing zero-shot to high-resolution out-of-distribution videos.
AI.motivation: Existing Gaussian Splatting methods for video rely on slow per-video optimization and lack generalization across videos, limiting practical adoption.
AI.method: HyperGS uses a factorized spatiotemporal Transformer to extract video tokens and a learnable query-based Transformer to predict 8-parameter 2D Gaussian representations per frame, with a rank-based geometric regularizer to prevent needle-like degeneration during training.
AI.result: Achieves 10^4-10^5× faster encoding than per-video optimization at matched quality, zero-shot generalization to 720p video, and +2.9-3.1 dB PSNR improvement over prior video encoders on K400, SSv2, and UCF101.
AI.conclusion: HyperGS combines the fast rendering of Gaussian Splatting with the speed and generalization of feedforward prediction, advancing Gaussians as a practical direction for fast, generalizable video representation.

title: A multi-scale feature enhanced graph neural network for fluid dynamics prediction in complex geometries
summary: Industrial design in fields such as vehicle and aerospace engineering often relies on large-scale numerical simulations to evaluate fluid dynamics performance, which can incur substantial computational costs. Deep neural networks have shown promise in improving simulation efficiency, especially graph neural networks (GNNs), which demonstrate great potential due to their flexibility with unstructured data. However, GNNs face challenges when dealing with tasks involving complex geometries and large-scale meshes. In this paper, we propose the Multi-scale Feature Enhanced Graph Neural Network (ME-GNN) to tackle these challenges. ME-GNN employs a graph neural network with a two-step message-passing mechanism to capture detailed local features effectively. Additionally, it integrates an Attention U-Net with uniform grid discretization, enabling the extraction of both fine and coarse features. The model also utilizes K-hop sampling to construct subgraphs, facilitating efficient training on large datasets while preserving detailed local features. We evaluated ME-GNN on three benchmark datasets and achieved state-of-the-art results: a relative L2 error of 0.0196 for the velocity field and 0.0556 for the surface pressure on ShapeNet-Car, a normalized mean squared error of 0.0033 for the flow field on AirfRANS, and a relative L2 error of 0.1416 for the surface pressure on DrivAerNet.

AI.tldr: ME-GNN is a graph neural network with multi-scale feature enhancement that achieves state-of-the-art results on complex aerodynamic simulation tasks by combining two-step message passing, Attention U-Net with uniform grid discretization, and K-hop sampling for efficient large-scale mesh processing.
AI.motivation: To reduce the high computational costs of large-scale numerical simulations in vehicle and aerospace engineering by improving deep neural network efficiency, specifically addressing GNNs' limitations with complex geometries and large-scale meshes.
AI.method: ME-GNN uses (1) a two-step message-passing mechanism for capturing detailed local features, (2) an Attention U-Net with uniform grid discretization for multi-scale feature extraction, and (3) K-hop sampling to construct subgraphs for efficient training on large datasets while preserving local details.
AI.result: State-of-the-art results on three benchmarks: relative L2 error of 0.0196 (velocity) and 0.0556 (surface pressure) on ShapeNet-Car; NMSE of 0.0033 on AirfRANS; relative L2 error of 0.1416 on DrivAerNet for surface pressure.
AI.conclusion: The proposed ME-GNN effectively addresses GNN limitations in complex geometry and large-scale mesh tasks, achieving superior performance for fluid dynamics simulation across multiple benchmark datasets.

title: CFR-Net:Collaborative Feature Refnement Network for Medical Image Anomaly Detection
summary: Medical image anomaly detection remains challenging because networks pretrained on natural images often exhibit limited adaptability to medical images, where abnormal patterns appear as fine-grained local shifts, multi-scale contextual mismatches, and orientation-sensitive structural deviations. To address this, we propose the Collaborative Feature Refinement Network (CFR-Net), which combines shared teacher-student feature refinement before decoding with cross-space consistency after decoding. CFR-Net refines frozen teacher features and trainable student features using a Multi-Path Feature Refinement Module (MPFRM) with shared parameters, imposing common multi-path refinement rules on generic visual references and representations adapted to the medical domain, thereby mitigating domain discrepancy while modeling local, multi-scale, and orientation-sensitive feature characteristics. A variance-sensitive objective and dynamic ``homework set'' reorganization further support layer-adaptive consistency learning. Experiments on medical benchmarks show that CFR-Net achieves competitive anomaly classification and strong anomaly localization performance when trained on normal data.

AI.tldr: CFR-Net is a Collaborative Feature Refinement Network for medical image anomaly detection that addresses domain adaptation issues by using shared teacher-student feature refinement before decoding and cross-space consistency after decoding, achieving competitive classification and localization performance on normal training data.
AI.motivation: Medical image anomaly detection is challenging because pretrained networks on natural images fail to adapt well to medical images, where anomalies appear as fine-grained local shifts, multi-scale contextual mismatches, and orientation-sensitive structural deviations.
AI.method: CFR-Net combines shared teacher-student feature refinement (Multi-Path Feature Refinement Module with shared parameters) before decoding with cross-space consistency after decoding. It uses a variance-sensitive objective and dynamic "homework set" reorganization for layer-adaptive consistency learning.
AI.result: CFR-Net achieves competitive anomaly classification and strong anomaly localization performance on medical benchmarks when trained on normal data.
AI.conclusion: The proposed CFR-Net effectively mitigates domain discrepancy between natural and medical images while modeling local, multi-scale, and orientation-sensitive feature characteristics, leading to improved anomaly detection performance.

title: CatRetriever: Contrastive Representation Learning for Slab-to-Bulk Retrieval in Generative Catalyst Discovery
summary: Inverse design is an emerging data-driven paradigm for efficiently navigating vast chemical spaces to discover new materials with targeted properties, and in the context of heterogeneous catalysis, surface generative models have recently advanced this goal by directly generating catalyst surface-adsorbate structures. However, these models typically operate at the slab level and do not provide the corresponding parent bulk structure, making it difficult to assess bulk-dependent properties such as formation energy, surface energy, crystallographic symmetry, and synthesizability. Here, we address this missing slab-to-bulk connection as a retrieval problem and introduce CatRetriever, a contrastive representation learning model that aligns slab and bulk crystal representations in a shared latent space. From a slab query, CatRetriever accurately retrieves plausible parent bulk candidates with R@1 > 91% and R@3 > 98% on both the in-distribution and holdout evaluation sets. We further extend the CatRetriever framework into an adsorption energy targeted bulk discovery pipeline that combines bulk retrieval, generative search space expansion, and adsorption energy distribution analysis. This workflow evaluates candidates by both structural compatibility with the query slab and their ability to access the target adsorption energy range across diverse surface environments. CatRetriever therefore provides a scalable route for connecting catalyst generative models with physically plausible and adsorption energy compatible bulk catalyst discovery.

AI.tldr: CatRetriever is a contrastive learning model that retrieves plausible parent bulk structures from slab-level catalyst surface representations, achieving >91% R@1 and >98% R@3 accuracy, and enables adsorption-energy-targeted bulk discovery by bridging generative surface models with bulk-dependent property assessment.
AI.motivation: Existing surface generative models for catalyst design produce slab-level adsorbate structures but fail to provide corresponding parent bulk structures, hindering evaluation of bulk-dependent properties like formation energy, surface energy, and synthesizability.
AI.method: CatRetriever uses contrastive representation learning to align slab and bulk crystal representations in a shared latent space, treated as a slab-to-bulk retrieval problem. It is extended into a pipeline combining bulk retrieval, generative search space expansion, and adsorption energy distribution analysis.
AI.result: CatRetriever achieves R@1 > 91% and R@3 > 98% for retrieving plausible parent bulk candidates on both in-distribution and holdout sets.
AI.conclusion: CatRetriever provides a scalable route to connect catalyst generative models with physically plausible and adsorption-energy-compatible bulk catalyst discovery, enabling targeted materials design.

title: Vinci2: Providing Proactive Assistance in Continuous Egocentric Videos
summary: When should an intelligent assistant speak up without being asked? Continuous egocentric video offers rich, evolving context that enables a new form of assistance: one that is proactive rather than merely reactive. Yet existing approaches either wait passively for user queries or treat every detected event as requiring a response, without considering the user's history, current activity, or whether assistance would actually be welcome. We reframe proactive assistance as a context-dependent decision problem: the agent must not only perceive what is happening, but reason over accumulated temporal context to determine when and whether to intervene. To this end, we present Vinci2, a proactive egocentric assistance system that advances the on-device assistant Vinci from reactive response toward proactivity. On the evaluation side, we present EgoServe, the first large-scale benchmark for proactive assistance in continuous egocentric video. EgoServe comprises over 3,000 service instances organized along 4 temporal memory horizons, ranging from immediate safety alerts to long-term habit coaching, across 10 service categories. On the modeling side, we propose EgoMemo, a training-free, memory-augmented agent that maintains three complementary memory representations: multi-scale temporal summaries, a semantic knowledge graph, and visual embedding archives. At each timestep, EgoMemo performs retrieval-augmented reasoning to determine whether assistance is warranted and, if so, produces contextually grounded responses. Experiments demonstrate that EgoMemo establishes strong baselines on EgoServe while remaining competitive on existing egocentric benchmarks. Our benchmark and code are publicly available at \href{https://sitonggong.github.io/EgoServe-page/}{Vinci2}.

AI.tldr: Vinci2 is a proactive egocentric assistance system that uses memory-augmented reasoning (EgoMemo) to decide when to intervene in continuous video, supported by the new EgoServe benchmark with 3,000+ service instances across 10 categories.
AI.motivation: Existing intelligent assistants are purely reactive—they wait for user queries or respond to every detected event—without considering user context, history, or whether assistance is welcome. The authors argue for proactive assistance as a context-dependent decision problem.
AI.method: The paper presents Vinci2 (proactive assistant), EgoServe (benchmark with 3K+ instances, 4 temporal horizons, 10 categories), and EgoMemo (training-free memory-augmented agent with three memory representations: multi-scale temporal summaries, semantic knowledge graph, visual embedding archives). EgoMemo uses retrieval-augmented reasoning at each timestep.
AI.result: EgoMemo establishes strong baselines on EgoServe and remains competitive on existing egocentric benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Proactive assistance in continuous egocentric video is feasible via memory-augmented, context-aware reasoning. The benchmark and code are publicly released to advance this research direction.

title: Active Offline-to-Online Reinforcement Learning
summary: Background: Offline reinforcement learning (RL) enables effective policies to be trained from large, previously collected datasets and subsequently improved through limited online interaction. This offline-to-online RL (O2O-RL) paradigm is particularly promising in nonstationary domains where interaction is costly or potentially hazardous. Standard O2O-RL pipelines train multiple candidate policies offline, evaluate them using off-policy or online evaluation, and then deploy and fine-tune the policy with the highest estimated value. However, as in offline pretraining, fine-tuning performance is highly sensitive to the choice of algorithm and hyperparameters, making it risky to commit to a single policy. Objectives: We study active policy selection for fine-tuning under a limited interaction budget in O2O-RL settings. To our knowledge, this is the first work to address this problem. Methods: We formulate the problem by identifying a fundamental trade-off between allocating online interactions to policy evaluation, which helps identify high-performing policies, and allocating them to fine-tuning, which improves policy performance. We then propose an approach that balances this trade-off by actively selecting policies for fine-tuning based on upper-confidence bounds on their future performance. These bounds are derived from locally linear performance forecasts fitted to observations obtained through online evaluation. Results: Across a diverse range of experiments, the proposed approach consistently outperforms existing O2O-RL baselines. Conclusions: Actively selecting and fine-tuning policies uses limited online interaction budgets more effectively than either committing to a single policy or dividing the budget equally among all policies. Our framework also advances offline RL toward practical deployment in real-world systems where online interaction is costly or risky.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces an active policy selection framework for offline-to-online RL that optimally balances the trade-off between evaluating and fine-tuning policies under limited online interaction budgets, consistently outperforming existing baselines.
AI.motivation: Standard O2O-RL pipelines commit to a single policy for fine-tuning, which is risky due to high sensitivity to algorithm/hyperparameter choices. The authors aim to actively select which policies to fine-tune under limited interaction budgets.
AI.method: The authors formulate a trade-off between allocating budget to policy evaluation (identifying high-performing policies) vs. fine-tuning (improving performance). They propose an active selection approach using upper-confidence bounds on future performance, derived from locally linear forecasts fitted to online evaluation observations.
AI.result: Across diverse experiments, the proposed approach consistently outperforms existing O2O-RL baselines, using limited online interaction budgets more effectively.
AI.conclusion: Actively selecting and fine-tuning policies is more effective than committing to a single policy or splitting budget equally. The framework advances offline RL toward practical deployment in real-world systems with costly/risky online interaction.

title: Parse, Search, and Confirmation: Training-Free Aerial Vision-and-Dialog Navigation with Chain-of-Thought Reasoning and Structured Spatial Memory
summary: In this paper, we tackle the Aerial Vision-and-Dialog Navigation (AVDN) task in the training-free setting for resource-efficient high-altitude UAV navigation.Naively applying MLLMs leads to unreliable navigation due to weak directional grounding and the lack of explicit spatial memory.To address these issues, we propose PSC-AVDN, a training-free framework that tightly couples a three-stage Parsing-Search-Confirmation reasoning pipeline with a Structured Spatial Memory (SSM).The parsing stage uses an LLM to convert ambiguous dialogue instructions into stable geometric directional and destination cues.A Search Chain-of-Thought (S-CoT) then performs stepwise target exploration under high-altitude observations, and a Confirmation Chain-of-Thought (C-CoT) conducts fine-grained verification around candidate regions to resolve visual ambiguity.Meanwhile, SSM integrates three complementary sources of spatial cues, including multi-scale visual observation, spatial visual memory, and structured geometric memory to provide global spatial context and long-horizon consistency.Extensive experiments on ANDH and ANDH-Full show that PSC-AVDN establishes new state-of-the-art performance in the training-free setting, matching or surpassing several finetuned methods.Code will be publicly available at: https://github.com/QY6616/PSC-AVDN

AI.tldr: PSC-AVDN is a training-free framework for Aerial Vision-and-Dialog Navigation that combines a three-stage Parsing-Search-Confirmation reasoning pipeline with Structured Spatial Memory (SSM), achieving SOTA performance on ANDH benchmarks without training.
AI.motivation: Existing MLLMs struggle with AVDN due to weak directional grounding and lack of explicit spatial memory, especially in training-free settings needed for resource-efficient UAV navigation.
AI.method: PSC-AVDN uses a three-stage pipeline: (1) Parsing stage with LLM to convert ambiguous dialogue into geometric cues, (2) Search Chain-of-Thought (S-CoT) for stepwise target exploration, (3) Confirmation Chain-of-Thought (C-CoT) for fine-grained verification. SSM integrates multi-scale visual observation, spatial visual memory, and structured geometric memory.
AI.result: PSC-AVDN establishes new SOTA in training-free setting on ANDH and ANDH-Full, matching or surpassing finetuned methods.
AI.conclusion: The PSC-AVDN framework effectively addresses directional grounding and spatial memory limitations in training-free AVDN, demonstrating that explicit reasoning and memory structures can rival finetuned approaches.

title: Time-Lag-Aware Deep Reinforcement Learning for Flexible Job-Shop Scheduling in PPVC Module Factories
comment: 10 pages,5 figures, UR by IEEE TII
summary: Prefabricated prefinished volumetric construction moves most building work into module factories, whose production floor operates as a flexible job shop. A major complication is decisive: long post-operation time-lags caused by concrete curing, watertightness ponding tests, and paint drying, during which a module is blocked while its workstation stays free. On benchmark instances grounded in an official national prefabrication guidebook, these lags inflate even the optimal reference makespan by about 67% on average, and ignoring them at decision time, then repairing to feasibility, is worse than every dispatching rule. We adapt a state-of-the-art dual-attention deep reinforcement learning solver through three minimally invasive, individually ablatable extensions: lag-aware dynamics with an admissible reward bound, two anticipatory lag feature channels, and liveness-masked operation- and station-type embeddings. With every extension disabled the implementation reproduces the original solver exactly, so all gains are attributable to the adaptations. We release a public, guidebook-grounded benchmark generator. On held-out instances the learned policy is the strongest solver-free scheduler: it reaches within about 4% of a constraint-programming reference and beats every dispatching rule and a genetic-algorithm metaheuristic, with its advantage widening under capacity contention, and a single size-mixed policy carries this lead across the trained range of factory sizes. It needs no solver, model, or license in the loop and re-plans within seconds of a disruption; where an exact solver can be deployed, that solver remains the quality ceiling, a boundary we map explicitly.

AI.tldr: A deep reinforcement learning solver is adapted with three minimally invasive extensions (lag-aware dynamics, anticipatory lag feature channels, and liveness-masked embeddings) to handle long post-operation time-lags in prefabricated volumetric construction scheduling, achieving near-optimal performance without needing an exact solver.
AI.motivation: Long post-operation time-lags (e.g., concrete curing, ponding tests, paint drying) significantly inflate makespan in prefabricated construction flexible job shops, and ignoring them leads to worse performance than simple dispatching rules.
AI.method: Adapted a dual-attention deep reinforcement learning solver with three extensions: lag-aware dynamics with admissible reward bounds, two anticipatory lag feature channels, and liveness-masked operation- and station-type embeddings, all individually ablatable.
AI.result: Learned policy reaches within ~4% of constraint-programming reference, beats all dispatching rules and genetic-algorithm metaheuristic, with advantage widening under capacity contention; a single size-mixed policy works across trained factory sizes.
AI.conclusion: The adapted DRL approach is the strongest solver-free scheduler for this problem, replans in seconds, and maps the boundary where exact solvers remain the quality ceiling.

title: Adaptive Routing for Efficient Diffusion Transformer-Based PNI Prediction
summary: Perineural invasion (PNI) is a critical prognostic factor in cholangiocarcinoma. However, its preoperative prediction from magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) remains challenging due to subtle imaging features that extend beyond tumor boundaries into surrounding regions. Conventional convolutional neural networks are limited in capturing long-range spatial dependencies. Transformer-based architectures improve global modeling of volumetric MRI by aggregating spatially distributed contextual cues, yet capturing subtle and noise-sensitive patterns in peritumoral regions remains challenging. Diffusion-based classifiers offer an alternative formulation by leveraging denoising-based class scoring to better capture such subtle patterns. However, these approaches introduce substantial computational overhead due to the combination of transformer-based modeling and iterative denoising processes. To address these challenges, we formulate PNI prediction as a diffusion-based classification problem and implement the denoising network using a transformer-based representation. To improve computational efficiency, we introduce adaptive routing across attention heads, spatial tokens, and MLP width. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed approach achieves an AUC of 0.731 with 257.57 GFLOPs.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a computationally efficient diffusion-based transformer model for predicting perineural invasion (PNI) from MRI in cholangiocarcinoma, achieving an AUC of 0.731 with 257.57 GFLOPs using adaptive routing across attention heads, spatial tokens, and MLP width.
AI.motivation: PNI is a critical prognostic factor in cholangiocarcinoma, but preoperative MRI prediction is challenging due to subtle imaging features beyond tumor boundaries. Existing CNNs lack long-range context, transformers improve global modeling but miss subtle peritumoral patterns, and diffusion-based classifiers are computationally expensive when combined with transformers.
AI.method: The authors formulate PNI prediction as a diffusion-based classification problem with a transformer-based denoising network. To improve efficiency, they introduce adaptive routing across attention heads, spatial tokens, and MLP width.
AI.result: The proposed approach achieves an AUC of 0.731 with 257.57 GFLOPs.
AI.conclusion: The adaptive routing mechanism successfully reduces computational overhead while maintaining competitive predictive performance for PNI classification from MRI.

title: HiFi-LLP: High-Fidelity, Low-Cost Latency Predictors with Confidence for Robust HW-NAS
comment: Published in the Proceedings of the 2025 IEEE 38th International System-on-Chip Conference (SOCC)
summary: With deep neural networks (DNNs) increasingly deployed on edge devices, hardware (HW)-aware optimization techniques--such as HW-aware compression and HW-aware neural architecture search (HW-NAS)--have become essential. These methods rely on real feedback from the target hardware to tailor DNN architectures for efficient deployment. While the search can be parallelized, latency measurements via hardware-in-the-loop (HIL) remain a bottleneck due to their sequential nature. Recent approaches use latency predictors to replace costly HIL feedback, but challenges persist: (1) platform-specific predictors often require tens of thousands of samples, and (2) inaccurate predictions can mislead the NAS process. To address this, we introduce HiFi-LLP, a high-fidelity, low-cost latency predictor based on graph attention networks, augmented with a confidence metric. HiFi-LLP outperforms prior platform-specific predictors by up to 9 percentage points (p.p.) in the 10% accuracy bound and achieves a Spearman's rank correlation of up to 0.996 across six devices in the LatBench dataset. We further propose a hybrid NAS framework that routes low-confidence predictions to HIL, achieving up to 8.6$\times$ speedup compared to typical NAS while maintaining a competitive Pareto front.

AI.tldr: HiFi-LLP introduces a high-fidelity latency predictor using graph attention networks with confidence metrics, achieving up to 0.996 Spearman correlation across devices and enabling 8.6× NAS speedup via hybrid low-confidence routing to hardware-in-the-loop.
AI.motivation: Hardware-aware NAS relies on costly sequential hardware-in-the-loop (HIL) latency measurements. Existing latency predictors require many samples and can mislead NAS with inaccurate predictions.
AI.method: HiFi-LLP uses graph attention networks with a confidence metric for latency prediction. A hybrid NAS framework routes low-confidence predictions to HIL while using high-confidence predictions directly.
AI.result: Outperforms prior predictors by up to 9 p.p. in 10% accuracy bound; achieves Spearman's rank correlation up to 0.996 across six devices; hybrid NAS achieves up to 8.6× speedup over typical NAS.
AI.conclusion: HiFi-LLP provides accurate, low-cost latency predictions and enables efficient HW-aware NAS without sacrificing Pareto-front quality.

title: Training-Free Off-Screen Player Imputation for Broadcast-Based Spatial Football Analytics
comment: 10 pages, 2 figures, 3 tables. Code and data: https://github.com/nowayfootball/offscreen-impute
summary: Spatial football metrics such as pitch control assume access to the positions of all 22 players, yet the most widely available source of positional data -- the broadcast main camera -- shows only 10-16 of them at any moment. We quantify the resulting distortion with an open, reproducible benchmark: a simulated broadcast viewport applied to open full-pitch tracking data (Metrica Sports; three matches, one held out from method development). Ignoring off-screen players -- the visible-only baseline implied whenever a video-based game-state-reconstruction (GSR) pipeline adds no imputation layer -- inflates hidden-zone pitch-control error to 25.1-26.9 percentage points and a mean absolute control-share error of 11.1-13.4 points across the three matches. We then evaluate a ladder of training-free, online imputation baselines that use only observations from the match being analysed. The best overall on these decision-relevant metrics, role-anchored centroid voting (each visible player votes for the full-team centroid by subtracting its running role offset, attenuating the viewport-induced subset bias), roughly halves hidden-zone error (to 12.2-13.8 points) and cuts control-share error to 28-48% of the ignore policy at every viewport width from 36 m to 60 m in all three matches. For occlusions <=9.6 s -- the regime of the closest learned prior work -- it reaches binwise median position errors of 3.3-8.9 m; but 50-57% of hidden-player observations lie beyond that regime. Integrated end-to-end into a broadcast-video GSR pipeline, imputation moves a downstream possession-quality score (Space-Creation Index) by 15.6 and 17.2 points on two real World Cup broadcast windows, flipping the verdict class in one.

AI.tldr: This paper quantifies the distortion caused by missing off-screen players in broadcast-view football data and evaluates imputation methods that use only in-match observations, finding that simple role-anchored centroid voting roughly halves error in hidden-zone pitch control compared to ignoring off-screen players.
AI.motivation: Broadcast main camera footage (the most widely available football positional data source) captures only 10-16 of 22 players at any time, but spatial metrics like pitch control assume all players are visible. The authors aim to quantify the resulting error and find practical, training-free imputation solutions.
AI.method: The authors create an open benchmark using full-pitch tracking data with simulated broadcast viewports. They evaluate a "visible-only" baseline (ignoring off-screen players) and a ladder of online, training-free imputation methods, with role-anchored centroid voting being the best performing approach.
AI.result: Visible-only baseline inflates hidden-zone pitch-control error to 25.1-26.9 percentage points. Role-anchored centroid voting roughly halves hidden-zone error (to 12.2-13.8 points) and reduces control-share error to 28-48% of the ignore policy. For occlusions ≤9.6s, median position errors are 3.3-8.9m, but 50-57% of occlusions exceed this duration.
AI.conclusion: Simple imputation methods can substantially reduce errors from missing players in broadcast football footage, with role-anchored centroid voting being effective despite its simplicity. However, long occlusion durations remain challenging, and imputation can flip downstream decision-relevant metrics like possession-quality scores.

title: From Global to Factor-Wise Expert Composition in Discrete Diffusion Models
