Rather than assuming a fixed fusion structure, the framework first integrates more closely related modalities and then incorporates additional heterogeneous modalities, supporting scalable adaptation across datasets with different modality sets.We evaluate the framework on two healthcare-oriented training environment datasets: NurViD and the Nurse Training dataset. Across these datasets, preliminary results suggest that the proposed cascaded fusion strategy improves over individual modality models and provides competitive performance relative to previously reported dataset-specific baselines. Overall, these findings indicate that cascaded LoRA-based fusion is a promising parameter-efficient approach for integrating heterogeneous modalities in medical training action and activity recognition tasks. github: https://github.com/anonymous0-ai/LoRA-Based-Cascaded-Multimodal-Fusion-.git.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a cascaded LoRA-based multimodal fusion framework for action/activity recognition in healthcare training environments. It sequentially integrates modalities without retraining previous components, achieving competitive results on NurViD and Nurse Training datasets.
AI.motivation: To address the challenge of integrating heterogeneous modalities (e.g., video, sensor data) for healthcare training action recognition using a parameter-efficient approach that supports scalable adaptation across datasets with varying modality sets.
AI.method: A cascaded Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA)-based multimodal fusion framework that first integrates more closely related modalities, then incorporates additional heterogeneous modalities sequentially, without retraining previously learned components.
AI.result: The cascaded fusion strategy improves over individual modality models and achieves competitive performance compared to previously reported dataset-specific baselines on NurViD and Nurse Training datasets.
AI.conclusion: Cascaded LoRA-based fusion is a promising parameter-efficient approach for integrating heterogeneous modalities in medical training action and activity recognition tasks.

title: Beyond the Single Camera: Agentic Multi-View Reasoning in Sports Video Understanding
summary: Recent Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) achieve strong performance on single-view video understanding benchmarks. However, sports videos involve dense occlusion, rapid motion, and complex interactions that are difficult to resolve from a single viewpoint. In practice, sports events are recorded from multiple camera angles, providing complementary evidence used by referees. Yet, no existing benchmark evaluates MLLMs on multi-view sports video understanding. To address this gap, we introduce SportMV-Bench, a comprehensive benchmark built from official match recordings, through a dedicated pipeline combining LLM-based generation, MLLM-based verification, and human filtering to ensure quality and consistency. SportMV-Bench containing 787 multi-view video bundles and 2592 question-answer pairs across three categories: Perception-Aware Recognition (PAR), Rule-aware Event Interpretation (REI), and Adjudicative Decision Reasoning(ADR). Our analysis shows that current MLLMs fail to effectively exploit multi-view information, with the bottlenecks lying in fine-grained visual perception and view selection rather than logical reasoning or domain knowledge. We propose SportMV-Agent, an agentic framework that orchestrates an iterative loop of active view selection, perception tool execution, and evidence-grounded reasoning, achieving a significant 14.46% relative improvement over the strongest MLLM baseline.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces SportMV-Bench, the first benchmark for evaluating MLLMs on multi-view sports video understanding, and proposes SportMV-Agent, an agentic framework that improves multi-view reasoning by 14.46% over baselines.
AI.motivation: Existing MLLMs are strong on single-view videos but fail in sports scenarios involving occlusion, rapid motion, and complex interactions. No benchmark exists to evaluate multi-view sports video understanding despite real-world multi-camera setups.
AI.method: SportMV-Bench was built from official match recordings via LLM-based generation, MLLM-based verification, and human filtering, yielding 787 multi-view bundles and 2592 QA pairs across three categories. SportMV-Agent uses an iterative loop of active view selection, perception tool execution, and evidence-grounded reasoning.
AI.result: Current MLLMs fail to exploit multi-view information; bottlenecks are fine-grained visual perception and view selection. SportMV-Agent achieves a 14.46% relative improvement over the strongest MLLM baseline.
AI.conclusion: Multi-view understanding in sports videos remains challenging; view selection and perception are more critical than reasoning or domain knowledge, and agentic frameworks like SportMV-Agent show promising gains.

title: Evidence-Backed Video Question Answering
summary: Current Video Large Language Models (Video LLMs) excel in question answering (QA) but largely operate as black boxes, providing textual answers without verifiable visual grounding. Existing explainability efforts rely on textual rationales or sparse bounding boxes, which struggle to capture complex video dynamics such as occlusions and non-rigid deformations. We propose Evidence-Backed Video Question Answering (E-VQA), a novel task requiring models to jointly output a semantic answer and precise spatio-temporal evidence: temporal segments and dense, tracked object segmentation masklets. To support this, we introduce ST-Evidence, the first human-verified benchmark for both discriminative and generative pixel-level grounding. Evaluations of state-of-the-art models reveal a critical decoupling between QA accuracy and true visual perception that scaling alone fails to bridge. To address this, we develop scalable, automated generation pipelines to create ST-Evidence-Instruct, a 160k-scale dataset bridging high-level reasoning with fine-grained grounding. Fine-tuning grounded Video LLMs on this data yields substantial gains over the corresponding size-matched UniPixel baselines (e.g., +27.2 t-mean and +13.8 J&F on a 7B model), establishing a robust baseline for explainable, evidence-backed video understanding. Code and data are available at https://github.com/SalesforceAIResearch/EVQA.

AI.tldr: E-VQA proposes a new video QA task requiring models to output answers along with spatio-temporal evidence (tracked segmentation masklets). The authors introduce ST-Evidence benchmark, find a decoupling between QA accuracy and visual grounding, create a 160k-scale dataset, and fine-tune models to achieve significant improvements in grounding performance.
AI.motivation: Current Video LLMs excel at QA but lack verifiable visual grounding. Existing explainability methods (text rationales, sparse bounding boxes) fail to capture complex video dynamics like occlusions and non-rigid deformations.
AI.method: (1) Define E-VQA task requiring semantic answer + temporal segments + dense tracked segmentation masklets. (2) Build ST-Evidence benchmark with human-verified pixel-level grounding. (3) Develop automated pipelines to generate ST-Evidence-Instruct (160k-scale dataset). (4) Fine-tune grounded Video LLMs on this data.
AI.result: State-of-the-art models show decoupling between QA accuracy and visual grounding that scaling alone cannot fix. Fine-tuning on ST-Evidence-Instruct yields substantial gains (e.g., +27.2 t-mean and +13.8 J&F on a 7B model) over UniPixel baselines.
AI.conclusion: The decoupling between QA and visual grounding is a fundamental issue not resolved by scaling. The proposed dataset and methods establish a strong baseline for explainable, evidence-backed video understanding.

title: Latent-Identity Tuning in Text-to-Image Personalization Models
summary: Generating and editing a person's face demands high precision, as even minor modifications can significantly alter a subject's perceived identity. Current personalization and editing methods built on general-purpose text-to-image models, however, often lack the precision required for fine-grained facial edits. We present a method for fine-grained identity tuning in text-to-image personalization models. Unlike standard image editing, which operates on a given image, identity tuning modifies the latent representation of a specific identity, enabling the generation of diverse images that consistently depict the same edited identity. To enable fine-grained latent identity tuning, we explore the latent space of a pre-trained, frozen encoder for text-to-image personalization. Our approach requires no additional training. Instead, it leverages the existing architecture of a frozen encoder to uncover latent semantic directions. This space consists of a set of latent tokens that play distinct roles in capturing different aspects of an identity and often correspond to specific spatial or semantic facial regions. We show that meaningful directions can be identified within this space and within subspaces defined by selected tokens, enabling localized, fine-grained, and semantically coherent edits. We validate our approach through qualitative and quantitative experiments that demonstrate diverse localized facial edits while preserving cross-image identity consistency. Project page at: https://garibida.github.io/IdentityTuning/

AI.tldr: A method for fine-grained facial identity editing in text-to-image personalization models using latent semantic directions from a frozen encoder, requiring no additional training.
AI.motivation: Current personalization and editing methods lack precision for fine-grained facial edits, as even minor modifications can significantly alter perceived identity.
AI.method: Explores latent space of a pre-trained frozen encoder for text-to-image personalization; identifies semantic directions within latent tokens corresponding to facial regions; enables localized edits without additional training.
AI.result: Demonstrates diverse localized facial edits while preserving cross-image identity consistency, validated through qualitative and quantitative experiments.
AI.conclusion: Latent semantic directions from frozen encoders can enable precise, localized identity tuning for consistent facial editing across diverse generations.

title: Read It Back: Pretrained MLLMs Are Zero-Shot Reward Models for Text-to-Image Generation
summary: In this paper, we propose SpectraReward, a training-free reward function that turns pretrained MLLMs into off-the-shelf reward models for image-generation reinforcement learning. Instead of asking the MLLM to judge a generated image or answer decomposed verification questions, SpectraReward measures how well the original prompt can be recovered from the generated image through a single image-conditioned, teacher-forced forward pass. We use the average image-conditioned prompt log-likelihood as the reward, directly reusing the MLLM's pretrained image-text alignment ability without preference labels, reward-model fine-tuning. We further introduce Self-SpectraReward, a special case for unified multimodal models where the policy's own understanding branch serves as the reward model for its generation branch, forming a closed-loop self-improving framework without external reward models or external knowledge. Extensive experiments validate SpectraReward through a broad image-generation RL study covering two diffusion models, three RL algorithms, nine reward MLLM backbones from four MLLM families spanning 4B to 235B parameters, and five out-of-distribution text-to-image benchmarks. Results show that both SpectraReward and Self-SpectraReward significantly and consistently improve generation performance and outperform prior MLLM-derived reward training methods. Further analysis reveals that larger reward MLLMs are not always better, while Self-SpectraReward can match or surpass much larger external reward models, suggesting that reward-policy alignment is a key factor for effective image-generation RL. Project Page: https://huangrh99.github.io/SpectraReward/

AI.tldr: SpectraReward is a training-free reward function that uses pretrained MLLMs to score generated images by measuring how well the original prompt can be recovered from the image via log-likelihood, enabling effective image-generation reinforcement learning without reward model fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: Existing MLLM-derived reward methods require preference labels or reward-model fine-tuning, which is costly and lacks scalability. The authors aim to directly leverage pretrained MLLMs' image-text alignment ability as a reward signal without additional training.
AI.method: SpectraReward computes reward as the average image-conditioned prompt log-likelihood via a single teacher-forced forward pass through a pretrained MLLM. Self-SpectraReward extends this by using the policy's own understanding branch as the reward model for its generation branch, creating a closed-loop self-improvement framework without external models.
AI.result: Extensive experiments across two diffusion models, three RL algorithms, nine MLLM backbones (4B-235B), and five benchmarks show significant and consistent improvements over prior MLLM-derived reward training methods.
AI.conclusion: Larger reward MLLMs are not always better; Self-SpectraReward can match or surpass much larger external reward models, highlighting that reward-policy alignment is a key factor for effective image-generation RL.
